question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to change maternity services provided in the (i) Daisy Hill Hospital; and (iI) Craigavon Area Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether there has been a central budgetary assessment of the known and potential costs associated with net zero climate policies and funding streams for each Department and arm's-length body to inform future financial planning.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to introduce legislation, similar to Jade’s Law, to automatically suspend parental responsibility where a parent is convicted of murdering or committing voluntary manslaughter of the other parent, in order to protect children from undue influence.
answer_text: Courts have a range of orders at their disposal to effectively suspend parental responsibility in such circumstances, but I recognise that the process involved usually places the onus on the family of the victim of the crime or a statutory agency to apply for such an order. I have therefore asked my officials to undertake some preliminary work on this issue with a view to determining the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of the 2025-2026 Capital budget unpent at financial year end, broken down by Department.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) is not yet in a position to provide details of the amount of unspent capital for the 2025-26 financial year. This information will be available following the receipt of Provisional Outturn returns from Departments, which are anticipated in mid-May. This information is provisional until Final Outturn, based on audited year-end accounts, is reported in the autumn. However, the changes between Provisional and Final Outturn are usually minimal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of those using public transport who pay full ticket price for their journey, in each of the last four years.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any criteria applied by the Public Prosecution Service when determining which cases should progress through the courts during the current restrictions of the Criminal Bar strike.
answer_text: I understand PPS decisions on derogations are not based on a prescriptive set of criteria but, as we would want and expect, are instead based on the individual facets of each case. Factors informing decisions rightfully include the nature of the offence and the risk of further offending towards the complainant and others while the case as is ongoing or in the event it is impacted by witness attrition. I have been reassured by the compassionate and trauma informed risk-based approach being applied by PPS but of course the dialogue should not be about exceptions, and I hope the Accelerated Review I have commissioned will soon result in a full return to service and all cases will begin to proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 43314/22-27, whether these costs are recouped from prisoners via repayment plans or whether they represent a direct cost to her Department.
answer_text: The costs referred to in AQW 43314/22-27 are not recouped from prisoners. Where financial support is provided for Higher Education in accordance with the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) distance learning policy, these costs represent a direct cost to the Department of Justice. NIPS has a statutory obligation to enable prisoners to participate in education, as far as is practicable. In line with the distance learning policy, NIPS provides financial support for Higher Education where eligibility criteria are met. Prisoners who do not qualify for NIPS support may choose to self-fund their studies; however, student loans are not currently available to prisoners in Northern Ireland.The Open University is the principal provider of higher education within the prison estate. Research by the Ministry of Justice indicates that prisoners who engage in Open University study are less likely to reoffend following release.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been made of the gender balance of staff within Tourism NI.
answer_text: In accordance with our statutory obligations, Tourism NI completes and submits the annual Fair Employment Monitoring Return to the Equality Commission. In doing so, the gender balance of our workforce is assessed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the value of expenditure incurred by Tourism NI during the period in which Internal Audit assessed and issued a limited opinion audit report on Procurement and Contract Management in 2024-25.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff in internal audit roles within the Civil Service, broken down by Department.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cost of welfare mitigation to the Northern Ireland block grant, in each financial year from 2016-2017 to date.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any process in place in the Civil Service office estate to dispose of office furniture, including any receipts received by his Department for disposals, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: Over the last three financial years two frameworks have been used to dispose of furniture from the DoF office estate.  The furniture which cannot be readily reused within the office estate, has been disposed in accordance with those frameworks.  The majority of furniture removed from the DoF office estate is not fit for re-use and therefore attracts a cost for recycling or disposal. No income has been derived from the disposal of this furniture. The office furniture supplier advised that in 2025/2026 the contract was used to divert 124,208 kg of furniture from landfill, with an emissions saving of 178,741 kg/Co2e.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of complaints received on how the iPad monitoring system is operating from domiciliary care workers, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in the last three years.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to introduce legislation, similar to Jade’s Law, to automatically suspend parental responsibility were a parent is convicted of murdering or committing voluntary manslaughter of the other parent, in order to protect children from undue influence.
answer_text: Whilst I have huge sympathy for the policy intent of Jade’s Law, the policy surrounding parental rights and family law lies with the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail, of the 72 people dismissed from the Northern Ireland Civil Service in 2024-25, the number who received a compensation or settlement payment on their dismissal, including the value of the payments, in the last three years.
answer_text: Our records show that, including NI Prison Service dismissals, there were a total of 89 dismissals from the NICS in 2024-25. Of these, 76 received a compensation payment. The total compensation paid over this last three years was £2,905,325.24. 	The amount of compensation payable is calculated by applying a percentage to the maximum that could be paid under the Civil Service Compensation Scheme (Northern Ireland), Section 11 and Rule 3.3 of the Civil Service Compensation Scheme (Northern Ireland). A payment would be made only if there is clear evidence to justify the level of the award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any procurement process, applied to the Northern Ireland Civil Service and its arm's-length bodies when engaging social media influencers using public funds, including ethical guidance.
answer_text: The Executives procurement policy is contained within the Public Procurement Policy Statement and this sets out the principles and strategic objectives for public procurement to ensure public spend is used to:create sustainable jobs, skills and businesses;support the three pillars of Green Growth (i.e. climate, environment and the green economy);stimulate innovation; andensure supply chains are resilient and ethical.The Ethical Procurement Policy is guided by International best practice set out in the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, and the Global Sullivan Principles (1999).Procurement Policy Notes (PPNs) are the means by which departments and arms-length bodies are advised of procurement policy. PPN 04/21 Procurement Control Limits specifically details the procurement process to follow based on the estimated value of a contract.These Policies must be considered by Departments and Arms-length bodies when commissioning any goods or services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of bank accounts held by the Civil Service.
answer_text: There are currently 188 live bank accounts held by the nine departments and one non-ministerial body (PPS) that fall under the scope of the Civil Service.The number of bank accounts vary by department and depend on the business needs of each respective department. Departments handle a significant amount of public money and carry out many financial transactions every working day. It is essential that these are handled in a way which is efficient and safe for the public sector as a whole.The range and number of bank accounts are reviewed when the banking contract is considered for renewal and any new bank accounts can only be opened with the approval of the Government Accounts team within my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether overgrown branches and hedges can be cut by members of the public if they are overhanging a road way from (i) private; or (ii) publicly owned land.
answer_text: The Department has a statutory duty to maintain the public road network, which includes ensuring that boundary trees or hedges do not overgrow the roadway or footway to the extent that they impede road users or pedestrians. Maintenance of trees and hedges on the adopted road network is therefore undertaken only where it is necessary in the interests of road safety and is carried out in accordance with relevant environmental legislation.Trees and hedges on private or unadopted land remain the responsibility of the landowner, who is responsible for ensuring that they do not endanger or obstruct road users.Where hedges or overhanging vegetation present a road safety concern, this should be reported to my officials, who will assess the matter and take appropriate action in line with the Department’s statutory responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any Civil Service properties that were disposed of, in the last five years, including (i) the Land and Property Services Red Book valuations of the property; and (ii) the final sale price agreed on the property.
answer_text: There were 146 Civil Service disposals completed in the last 5 years (up to 31 March 2026).  The table below summarises the total Red Book Valuation, Sale Price and the variation between Sale Price and Market Value. Asset TypeNo SalesRed Book Valuation TotalSale PriceTotalVariance(Sale Price-MV)Land79£7,772,902£8,559,843£786,941Buildings67£40,827,500£43,424,026£2,596,526Totals146£48,600,402£51,989,869£3,389,467Open market sale prices for land and buildings reflect market conditions at the time of sale and are influenced by many factors such as supply and demand, property characteristics, planning and legal matters, market competition, funding conditions, construction and abnormal costs and other due diligence identified risks. A Red Book valuation is an assessment at a point in time based on critical assumptions and cannot predict these variable factors conclusively.  Property market conditions over the past 5 years have been affected by, but not limited to, the COVID19 pandemic, fluctuations in interest rates, wider economic uncertainty, rising construction costs and changes in investor behaviour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any engagement he has undertaken in response to the immediate impact of fuel price hikes on the farming and fishing industries.
answer_text: I understand the challenges that both farmers and fishermen are facing given the fuel price hikes due to the Iran War and I have had discussions with representations of both industries on the issue. Unfortunately, reliance on fossil fuels exposes local industries to global events and resultant price volatility. At this stage it is not possible to determine the longer term impacts of the fuel price hikes but I will continue to monitor the market situation and my engagement with industry.  My Officials have also been discussing the potential impacts on the farming and fisheries industries with UK Government officials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action his Department is taking in its role as shared service HR to the Civil Service to (i) support staff experiencing violence against women; and (ii) raise awareness of the issue.
answer_text: The Civil Service as an employer is committed to supporting the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) 2024–31 and signed the White Ribbon Charter in 2019 and 2023, pledging to raise awareness of violence against women and girls and the importance of preventing its occurrence. To date, my department on behalf of the Civil Service, has delivered several actions as part of its wider programme of work on domestic and sexual abuse including: launched an updated Civil Service Domestic and Sexual Abuse Policy with new Supporting Guidance in December 2025;  implemented policy support measures for staff who are victims of domestic and/or sexual violence and abuse to provide up to 10 days paid special leave to facilitate practical arrangements, such as seeking advice or securing alternative accommodation; and appointed 83 Safe Place Advocates across the Service who are staff volunteers trained to listen and signpost to sources of support and trained over 600 front line staff in the Department for Communities in “Supporting Customers affected by Domestic Abuse”.  Ongoing and future actions in 2026/27 by my Department include: a Civil Service wide awareness campaign promoting the internal and external sources of support available; participating in the EVAWG Workplace Sector Forum constituted as a part of the EVAWG Strategic Framework; and reviewing the existing domestic abuse e-learning module and developing new training products on EVAWG and Trauma Informed Approach.In recognition of the work delivered to date, the Civil Service attained the Platinum Workplace Charter Award by ONUS (a social enterprise offering training and consultancy services on domestic violence and abuse).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the parties who formally agreed to the Terms of Reference of the Open Book Exercise with UK Treasury.
answer_text: The Terms of Reference for the Open Book exercise were shared with Executive colleagues asking if they had any comments. No responses were received from colleagues disagreeing with the terms of reference. My officials therefore indicated to Treasury officials on the 25 February that we were content to agree the terms of reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any proposals submitted to his Department by any other Department to increase the use of Financial Transactions Capital during the proposed multi-year budget period.
answer_text: Executive discussions are continuing in a bid to finalise the multi year budget. Once agreed the published Budget will include details of FTC expenditure by each department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when the outcome of the Open Book Exercise with UK Treasury will be made public.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans for training, and awareness raising for local councils and public representatives to support the rollout of Baby Loss Certificates, in June 2026.
answer_text: My officials are preparing a suite of guidance documents for both applicants and staff along with awareness raising materials to accompany the launch of the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme.  The documentation will be distributed as appropriate across District Registration Offices, local councils, and medical settings such as General Practitioners Offices, maternity units and Hospital waiting areas.  All staff in the General Register Office involved in producing the certificates will be fully trained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of women who reported a violent crime against them, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the specific information requested in the question.  The recording of information on offences reported to the police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.   I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any further query relating to the number of women who reported a violent crime against them to the PSNI.  However, the Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on offences reported to them where the victim gender was recorded as female as part of their Police recorded crime bulletin.  In that bulletin, the PSNI definition of 'Violence against women and girls' (VAWG) includes both Violence against the Person and Sexual offences with a female victim.  Based on this definition, figures for the number of police recorded crimes where the victim was a female in the years 2022/23 to 2024/25 have been provided in the following table.Police recorded crime where victim was recorded as female for Violence Against the Person and sexual offences, 2022/23 to 2024/25Offence type2022/232023/242024/25Violence with injury (including homicide & death/serious injury by unlawful driving)6,0915,9905,413Violence without injury9,4658,1287,663Stalking and Harassment8,8096,2845,886Sexual offences3,2773,0673,142Source: PSNI.Note:Full detail of counting rules for these figures can be found within the police recorded crime bulletin produced by PSNI.Figures relate to offences under the categories specified that were reported to police during the years included in the table.Individuals may have been the victim of more than one of the offence categories listed within a particular time period, i.e., the figures provided should not be interpreted as the overall number of individuals who were a victim of at least one of these offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the topics discussed in his meeting with the UK Treasury this week; and (ii) any agreements reached.
answer_text: On 19 March 2026 I met the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) alongside my Scottish and Welsh counterparts.I would refer the Member to the communiqué available below:https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/finance-interministerial-standing-committee-19-march-2026Following the F:ISC meeting, I also held a bilateral meeting with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, during which the following topics were discussed:Childminder 10% Tax Allowance and Tax-Free ChildcareLocal Growth FundTransformation FundingOpen Book Exercise and Treasury’s final reportExecutives financial positionFiscal FrameworkI also pressed the Chief Secretary to the Treasury for further support to be provided in response to the significant rise in global oil prices and the widespread reliance here on home heating oil.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when the report on the Open Book Exercise with UK Treasury will be (i) completed; and (ii) shared with the NI Assembly.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has received the Treasury report in relation to the Open Book exercise; this has been shared with the wider Executive. The report represents high level Treasury analysis, informed, in part, by data provided by local departments. It will help support future discussions with the Treasury.  A response from the Executive has issued to Treasury and further engagement regarding the Executive’s multiyear budget will be required. Decisions on whether, when and in what form to publish any outputs from the review will be a matter for the Treasury and Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions he has taken to support (i) farmers; and (ii) fishermen, who have been affected by the rise in fuel prices.
answer_text: I understand the challenges that both farmers and fishermen are facing, and I share your concerns regarding rising fuel prices. I have had discussions with representatives of both sectors on this issue. These market developments are outside the control of the NI Executive, but I and my officials will continue to liaise with the UK Government.  Whilst, at this stage, a support package for farmers and fishermen affected by the increase in fuel costs is not planned, I am continuing to monitor the situation. The implementation of any future financial support package would be dependent on the allocation of additional funds to my Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of the impact of the announcement by the Competition and Markets Authority that written prescription fees from vets will be capped at £21 and practices will have to publish price lists for services.
answer_text: The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) has completed its final report on its market investigation into veterinary services for household pets in the UK. The cap on prescriptions charges is one of several recommendations made by the CMA. Key recommendations include improving price transparency by requiring veterinary practices to publish standard fees, enhancing consumer information through clearer communication about treatment options, and fostering greater competition within the sector.  The CMA also suggests establishing mechanisms to ensure that pet owners receive impartial advice and can make informed decisions regarding their pets’ care.In relation to prescription charges, the CMA has concluded that some veterinary practices charge widely differing fees for issuing prescriptions and that these charges may discourage pet owners from purchasing medicines elsewhere. The CMA has therefore decided to cap prescription charges at £21 for the first medicine and £12.50 for each additional medicine. The CMA found that vet fees for prescriptions have risen at nearly twice the rate of inflation.Implementation of these recommendations will involve collaboration between the CMA, the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS), and industry stakeholders. The CMA will oversee the regulatory changes and monitor compliance, while the RCVS will be responsible for updating professional standards and ensuring that veterinary practices adhere to new requirements. I have asked my officials to continue engaging with the UK Government, the CMA and the veterinary profession to ensure that the interests of pet owners and veterinary practices in Northern Ireland are properly reflected as these reforms are taken forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work that is being done to reduce (i) autism spectrum disorder; and (ii) ADHD, diagnosis waiting lists.
answer_text: Waiting lists for assessment of autism and ADHD are lengthy and growing for children, young people and adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors including funding that is short of objectively assessed need. My Department is undertaking a number of actions in an effort to improve waiting times within a financially constrained environment.In May 2025, my Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. The Framework was issued for public consultation and this exercise concluded on 26th September 2025. This Framework proposes an approach to neurodiversity which is inclusive and aligned to the needs of children and their families. My Department is currently working with partners to develop a regionally consistent neurodevelopmental pathway. This work will also assist in evaluating service capacity, scoping out workforce requirements and clarifying areas for future transformational investment.A dedicated Project Lead for Adult Autism was appointed in March 2025. Since recruitment, they have worked with Trusts to map current diagnostic and support pathways, and explore steps that can be taken to manage waiting lists and reduce variation across the region within existing resources. This builds on recommendations identified following the 2023 review of adult autism services and will help to optimise existing delivery and standardise service provision across Northern Ireland.Department officials meet monthly with HSC Trust Heads of ASD Services. These meetings are structured separately for adult and children’s services and provide a forum where professionals can participate in regional discussion to explore demand-capacity challenges and consider further opportunities to optimise existing pathways.In respect to ADHD assessment waits, my Department commissioned and recently published a regional needs assessment covering both adults and children across all Trusts. The primary objectives associated with this exercise were to understand overall demand across the lifespan and to identify detailed recommendations to support future planning and inform the development of a future commissioning model for ADHD services, subject to future budget availability.Officials have commenced an options appraisal process to identify service development opportunities that could potentially be implemented in the short to medium term to address the need for ADHD services while taking account of the significant financial pressures facing the health and social care sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) recent plans for; and (ii) progress made in, addressing mental health services in hospital Emergency Departments.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures that are being taken to reduce waiting times following breast screening referrals in the (i) Southern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Suspect breast cancer referrals operate through a single regional breast waiting list, introduced in May 2025 to ensure that every patient is offered the earliest available appointment, regardless of the Trust from which their referral originates. This change was necessary because waiting times previously varied significantly across the five Trusts, creating inequity of access. Under the regional system, patients in the Southern and South Eastern Trusts draw from the same appointment pool as patients elsewhere, and for that reason it is not meaningful to report actions or capacity at individual Trust level.  Regional efforts to improve breast assessment waiting times include maximising core capacity, delivering additional evening and weekend clinics, and securing sessional input from NHS Scotland.These immediate actions sit alongside a programme of longer-term reform supported through the Elective Care Framework (ECF), which provides £5 million in recurrent funding to modernise and stabilise breast services. The current focus is on the planning and implementation of a consistent, modernised service model for Northern Ireland that operates 50 weeks per year, is consultant-led (rather than consultant-delivered), and makes greater use of an expanded multidisciplinary skill mix. Key principles for this model have been agreed through the newly established Breast Operational Delivery Network.  A regional workforce gap analysis is underway, aligned to the agreed model, and will inform the investment plan associated with the recurrent £5 million.Through this combination of regional coordination, targeted short-term interventions and longer-term service reform, the Department remains committed to reducing breast assessment waiting times and ensuring that patients across Northern Ireland have equitable and timely access to high-quality care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current waiting time for the Autism Advisory & Intervention Service to engage with children who have been referred from Education Services.
answer_text: The Autism Advisory and Intervention Service (AAIS) is delivered by the Education Authority as part of its wider Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) support system through the Local IMPACT Team (LIT) model.  AAIS no longer exists as a stand alone service.As of the end of February, there are 1,575 children and young people awaiting support across all SEND categories within Education Authority services. This figure reflects demand across the full range of SEND needs and is not specific to autism alone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any of the 23 Northern Ireland companies named on 19 March 2026 by HMRC for not paying minimum wage have been receiving financial support from his Department through rates relief schemes.
answer_text: Of the 23 companies named by HMRC on 19th March 2026 for not paying minimum wage, 13 received some form of rate relief in 2025/26.It is totally unacceptable for any company not to pay their employees at least the minimum wage, and I welcome the exposure of those who do not. Employees can get more information about their rights and the minimum wage through the NI Direct website by following the link https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/national-minimum-wage-and-living-wage#toc-2Rates are a property tax and relief is awarded on the basis of statutory criteria, the criteria focuses primarily on property use or class.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to identify the number of households heated by oil.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the proposed active travel and greenway scheme for the Newry Road into Warrenpoint; and (ii) to detail how the design is being progressed to support both active travel and the smooth flow of traffic for local users and businesses.
answer_text: I can confirm that proposals to improve pedestrian and cycling infrastructure on the A2 Newry Road between Warrenpoint and the new Narrow Water Bridge are currently being developed. This forms part of a broader initiative aimed at increasing active travel provision in the area to unlock benefits associated with improved connectivity to Newry and the Carlingford Greenway.These improvements will require a reallocation of road space along the corridor to provide safe, high quality cycling infrastructure. Officials are cognisant of concerns about the capacity of the road network, which will be considered as part of the design process.My Department is currently developing options to deliver the scheme. The local community, businesses, and other key stakeholders will be invited to feed into the design process through public engagement on emerging options in the coming months. I would urge all those in the area with an interest to share their views through this upcoming engagement exercise, which will help inform development of the detailed design.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the introduction of new measures for meningitis vaccination for those born prior to 2015.
answer_text: The Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI), an independent, expert scientific advisory committee, advises the four UK Health Departments on all vaccination and immunisation matters.  At present, JCVI has not recommended any new measures for meningitis vaccination for those born prior to 2015.  JCVI is currently reviewing evidence following a recent outbreak of meningitis B in Kent and my Department will consider any updated JCVI advice once it becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the criteria used when decisions are made on which cases should progress through the courts during the current restrictions of the Criminal Bar strike.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had on the potential to stage a leg of the FIA World Rally Championship in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department and Tourism NI had significant engagement with Motorsport UK in 2021 around the possibility of hosting a round of the FIA World Rally Championship  here.  At that time Motorsport UK took the decision not to host the event in the north the following year.I have not been approached recently to engage in any discussions on hosting this event in future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff vacancies within the Northern Ireland Civil Service that are being filled by (i) a person employed in a temporary promotion; and (ii) agency staff.
answer_text: NICSHR does not maintain central records identifying the number of vacancies across the Civil Service that are currently being filled by either (i) individuals serving on temporary promotion or (ii) agency staff.

To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 30818/22-27.

answer_text: The management of temporary promotions is the responsibility of line managers in Departments. Temporary promotions are designed primarily to meet business and management needs and can be put in place for several different reasons: e.g. covering sick absence, maternity leave, special exercises (time bound projects), and to fill vacancies.As at the end of March 2026, a total of 3,726 NICS employees were on temporary promotion. This represents 15% of the total 24,643 NICS employees. This indicates that approximately threequarters of temporary promotions are attributable to vacancy pressures.There are a number of volume competitions ongoing and planned which should provide supply and therefore help reduce the number of temporary promotions.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the A2 Newry Street from Warrenpoint town centre to the roundabout at the dual carriageway, is under consideration to be reduced down from four lanes of traffic.
answer_text: I can confirm that proposals to improve pedestrian and cycling infrastructure on the A2 Newry Road between Warrenpoint and the new Narrow Water Bridge are currently being developed. This forms part of a broader initiative aimed at increasing active travel provision in the area to unlock benefits associated with improved connectivity to Newry and the Carlingford Greenway.The scheme will require a reallocation of road space along the corridor to provide safe, high quality cycling infrastructure. Officials are cognisant of concerns about the capacity of the road network, which is informing the design process.My Department is currently developing options to deliver the scheme. The local community, businesses, and other key stakeholders will be invited to feed into the design process through public engagement on emerging options in the coming months. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when there will be a new round of Your School Your Club opportunities.
answer_text: My Department is investing £3 million in eight schools as part of Your School Your Club (YSYC) in the most recent round.The Chief Executive of Sport NI advises me that subject to budget confirmation, they plan to invite Stage 1 applications to YSYC in April.In the meantime, interested schools should complete the “Project Register” to inform Sport NI about their community use project proposals and associated progress.	The link to the Project Register and Programme Guidance can be accessed at https://www.sportni.net/funding/your-school-your-club/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions his Department has taken to assess the regional spread of support to football clubs at all levels.
answer_text: Football clubs exist in every city, town and rural area, providing valuable benefits for communities across Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland Football Fund is designed to provide funding to modernise clubs’ facilities. The Fund is designed to give all clubs an opportunity to make their case for investment. Funding awards were recently made to performance clubs to assist them to develop business cases for their projects. These clubs were selected through open competition. The pilot stage of TNIFF Grassroots Facilities Programme has been designed to spread successful projects across Northern Ireland ensuring investment reaches different areas, while helping my Department understand what is needed for future stages of the programme.  In Stream 1 at least one applicant from each of Northern Ireland’s Divisional Associations will be supported.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been made of the level of investment into football clubs in south Down, at all levels of football, over the past ten years.
answer_text: A Strategic Outline Case was completed for The Northern Ireland Football Fund, which examined the need for capital investment in the football sector in Northern Ireland. However, my Department has not undertaken a specific assessment of investment in football clubs within South Down, over the past ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Olympic Legacy Fund will be a rolling funding opportunity for clubs across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the positive impact that the Olympic Legacy Fund has had on grassroots sports within our communities, delivering significant community benefits throughout the past year through investment in equipment and modernisation of club and community sports facilities across Northern Ireland. In total, £2.136 million has been disbursed during 2025/26 to support 82 projects.The innovative approach adopted through its community crowdfunding model has led to greater local ownership and pride in facilities and has increased community participation in shaping and supporting projects. It has been my intention that this would not be a oneoff investment and I plan for this to reopen for applications in 2026/27.
