answer_text: Each individual department is responsible for managing their own expenditure, on oil and gas, within the Civil Service estate.Expenditure on oil and gas fuel by my Department in each of the last 3 financial years is provided in the table below.  2024-25£0002023-24£0002022-23£000Oil and Gas Fuel   1,416    1,402   2,304

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether all Health and Social Care Trusts apply the same travel policy for staff.
answer_text: Mileage rates are set regionally, and all HSC trusts pay the same mileage rates to staff employed under Agenda for Change terms and conditions.As part of negotiations to conclude industrial action undertaken during 2023, my Department, employer representatives and TUS undertook to consider a regional system of mileage reimbursement which might better reflect the integrated nature of the HSC service and the rurality of many of our population requiring care at home.Circular HSC (AfC) (5) 2024, issued on 20th May 2024, set out an increase in the primary rate of mileage to 58 pence per mile. The primary rate shall be payable for the first 4,500 miles travelled in any one financial year. The secondary mileage rate, payable for each mile thereafter, was maintained at 30p per mile.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total cost of staff travel to the Civil Service, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The total cost of staff travel incurred by my Department in each of the last three financial years is provided in the table below.  2024-25£0002023-24£0002022-23£000Total£000Staff Travel553       514   619     1,686 My Department can only provide information relating to its own expenditure, as each department is responsible for managing and reporting its own staff travel costs.These figures mainly cover mileage, parking and subsistence claims as well as travel and accommodation booked through the collaborative Travel Management Services Framework.   All expenditure relates to official business carried out by staff in line with the Travel and Subsistence Policy, which permits reimbursement only where travel is necessary, economic and directly connected to departmental duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to whether the mileage rate for domiciliary care workers should be reviewed, in light of the recent rise in fuel prices.
answer_text: As part of negotiations to conclude industrial action undertaken during 2023, my Department, employer representatives and Trade Union Side undertook to consider a regional system of mileage reimbursement which might better reflect the integrated nature of the HSC service and the rurality of many of our population requiring care at home.Following these negotiations, a Departmental circular, which issued on 20 May 2024, informed HSC employers that the primary rate of mileage had increased, with effect from 1 April 2024 to 58 pence per mile for staff employed within HSC under Agenda for Change (AfC) terms and conditions.It is important to note that when AfC mileage rates were increased in 2024, fuel prices were at similar levels to those currently prevailing. Staff have, to some extent, benefited from lower fuel prices over the past couple of years as the mileage rates paid have remained the same.	 The Department is not considering any further revision of the published rates at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any process by which rate of fuel charge incurred by staff would be reviewed in the event of an increase in fuel costs.
answer_text: Mileage allowances for official travel by civil servants are set in accordance with current HMRC-approved rates for non-taxable mileage. Any indication of a sustained change in global fuel prices will be taken into account when considering whether adjustments to mileage rates are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any processes followed by his Department when UK Government schemes are announced with associated increased funding available for Northern Ireland through Barnett consequentials, Departmental Expenditure Limit or annually managed expenditure, to ensure that Northern Ireland applies for and draws down all available funds in a timely manner.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current number of vacant posts, broken down by grade across the Civil Service.
answer_text: As at the end of February 2026, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 5300 vacancies across the Civil Service on behalf of Executive departments, each of which is responsible for determining its workforce requirements. The breakdown by grade (including analogous grades) is shown in the table below. Analogous GradeNumber of vacanciesSCS24G663G7278DP525SO665EO1528EO2990AO1651AA367Industrial144Uniformed 65Grand Total5300		Source: NICSHRDetails of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any (i) ongoing; and (ii) completed, processes to downsize the senior Civil Service.
answer_text: Each Department is responsible for managing its own staffing requirements, including for the Senior Civil Service (SCS), and each Minister and Accounting Officer must ensure that its workforce is affordable within its departmental budget.  Decisions on the size and structure of their SCS cohorts are therefore a matter for individual Departments and may be influenced by future Executive priorities.The NICS People Strategy 2025–2030 sets out the planned shift to Strategic Workforce Planning (SWP). Strategic Workforce Planning is about ensuring Departments have the right people, with the right skills, in the right roles, in the right location, at the right time and at the right cost to deliver its priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how the accuracy and validity of Land and Property Services Red Book valuations of properties are audited.
answer_text: All Land and Property Services (LPS) valuers providing valuation advice to public sector clients are either Members (MRICS) or Fellows (FRICS) of the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors and as such are required to deploy the appropriate skills, knowledge, experience and ethical behaviours both to form sound judgements and to report opinions, that are consistent, accurate, objective and transparent.Valuers delivering in compliance with RICS Valuation - Global Standards (the Red Book) are also registered for regulation under the RICS Valuer Registration Scheme (VRS).LPS, as a Sponsoring Non-Regulated Organisation with RICS, is subject to RICS internal regulatory audit, monitoring obligations and compliance checks to ensure adherence with Rules of Conduct and mandatory service and regulatory standards which includes a requirement to operate a RICS-compliant complaints-handling procedure. Each LPS VRS regulated valuer provides an annual return to RICS as a summary of valuation work they have undertaken in the previous 12 month period and details of all continuing professional development (CPD) completed.To assist valuers in meeting CPD minimum requirements, LPS delivers regular training, both in-house and through RICS compliance and training teams to inform in respect of changes or updates to valuation standards, processes and procedures.LPS has in place internal processes that enable sampled casework to be reviewed to ensure compliance with mandatory Valuation Standards, professional guidance and governance procedures, including implementation of necessary senior grade approvals. LPS client casework processes are subjected to regular Department of Finance Internal Audits.Valuations undertaken for financial reporting purposes (asset valuations) are subject to annual audit and review by the NI Audit Office including a 3-yearly assurance audit that LPS has the professional skills and expertise to act as an expert in accordance with the International Standard on Auditing (UK and Ireland) 500 – Audit Evidence (ISA 500). This audit is conducted by an external valuation expert.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the geographical locations of where the new 120 Deputy Principal level staff, currently being recruited for, will be based.
answer_text: The Civil Service has recently launched a competition to recruit approximately 120 staff at Deputy Principal grade. This recruitment competition will be used to fill current vacancies across all Civil Service departments as well as any additional vacancies that may arise over the duration of the competition. The vast majority of vacancies at Deputy Principal grade are within the Greater Belfast area, although there are opportunities throughout the North.As at the end of February 2026, (the most recent figures available at time of the request), the NICSHR caseload for vacancies which may be filled from this Deputy Principal recruitment competition was 131.  The breakdown of the vacancies by city/town is shown in the table below. It should be noted that the locations are determined by whichever Department(s) needs to fill the vacancies.City/TownNumber of DP vacanciesAntrim1Ballykelly11Ballymena1Bangor16Belfast88Carrickfergus1Coleraine1Cookstown1Derry-Londonderry3Downpatrick4Enniskillen1Lisburn2Omagh1Total131Source: NICSHRDetails of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacancy that needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the new 120 Deputy Principal level staff currently being recruited will be required to work full-time, on site upon commencement of their roles.
answer_text: As outlined in the Candidate Information Booklet, this competition is being used to fill full time vacancies throughout the North.  It also outlines that some elements of home working and/or remote working may be possible, subject to business needs following a specific training period.  This is the same across all grades.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps he has taken to address the areas identified as lacking in skills, as stated in the Northern Ireland Audit Office's Capacity and Capability review.
answer_text: Departments are using findings from the NICSHR’s highlevel Strategic Learning Needs Analysis to shape updated learning and development plans, supported by refreshed leadership and management programmes and new products focused on team effectiveness and highperforming teams.In addition, a corporate Strategic Workforce Planning guide and toolkit are already in place, and the 2026 NIAO report requires departments to complete SWP plans by 31 March 2028 to enable a NICSwide aggregated plan. The new Civil Service workforce model developed through the Job Families Framework will standardise job architecture and career pathways, improving skills matching so the Civil Service has the right people, in the right roles, with the right skills at the right time: full implementation will align with Integr8, the new integrated HR and Finance IT transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the level of hybrid working within her Department, including any progress against the Minister of Finance's target of staff attending their workplace for at least 40 per cent of their contractual working hours each week.
answer_text: My Department operates in line with the NICS Hybrid Working Policy. Where a role is assessed as suitable for hybrid working based on business needs, staff are required to attend their designated workplace for at least 40% of their contractual working hours each week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the reason he launched the Civil Service campaign to recruit approximately 120 generic Deputy Principal level staff for roles across a range of departments and locations, rather than recruiting for specific skills or technical qualified posts.
answer_text: This competition aims to fill general service DP posts, which are assigned based on departmental vacancies reported to NICSHR for recruitment. It also establishes a merit list for future vacancies declared by departments.  Any posts at this grade or analogous grade with specific eligibility would require individual competitions.  In tandem, at the request of a range of Departments NICSHR is also in the process of recruiting to DP posts that require specific skills including professional and technical posts.As part of the transition to the Civil Service Job Families Framework, all General Service roles are being mapped into the new job families and professions, ensuring they are fully integrated into the future workforce model and supporting more targeted, skillsbased recruitment over time.In the interim, departments still require NICSHR to maintain a reliable supply of Deputy Principal staff to maintain business continuity, from which they can draw staff throughout the life cycle of the merit list. The strategic direction remains towards recruitment aligned to Job Families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his Department’s current arrangements with Lisney, including (i) the length of contract; (ii) value of contract; and (iii) terms of reference for property services provided.
answer_text: The current Agent’s Framework Agreement is for an initial 3 year term, from 7 October 2022 until 6 October 2025, with the option to extend by up to 2 years. The Framework was extended for a further year from 7 October 2025.Following a competitive procurement process, Lisney was appointed the lead agent and McKibbin the reserve agent. It has not yet been necessary to instruct McKibbin to undertake any disposals.The Framework provides an end to end professional estate agency function for the disposal and letting of public sector land and property assets, while also allowing for additional professional services or specialist advice relating to the sale, letting or management of such assets.Total fees paid to Lisney for completed instructions up to 31 March 2026 amount to £133,330 plus VAT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with UK Treasury regarding their emergency meetings on rising oil prices and the impact on homes and businesses in the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The rising price of home heating oil and fuel as a result of the situation in the Middle East is a major concern for both households and businesses, many of whom are already facing cost of living and cost of doing business pressures. I have written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury setting out my concerns and highlighting the need for the British Government to provide support, as well as raising the matter directly with him at the recent F:ISC meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the projected ongoing costs of encompass, including support, for the next seven years or for the duration of the contract.
answer_text: The total projected ongoing costs of encompass, including support, for the next seven years to the end of the Business Case in 2033/34 is £238.3m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total cost of integrating medical devices to encompass.
answer_text: The total cost of integrating medical devices at the installation and implementation phase of encompass was circa £4.6m, which included hardware, software and support costs to connect to encompass.  Annual ongoing support costs are projected at £2.5m across the remainder of the programme lifetime (to 2033).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total expenditure for the installation and implementation of the encompass project.
answer_text: The total expenditure for the installation and implementation phase of encompass to March 2026 is £343.6m capital and £56.2m revenue.Significant spend on updating our systems was unavoidable with legacy systems coming to the end of their lifespan. The world’s best healthcare providers all have an electronic health record at their centre. We want the best for our population, and, in today’s world, that requires world-class systems based around patients and clinicians.Investment in encompass reflects the scale and complexity of replacing regionwide core clinical and social care systems, which are critical to daytoday service delivery across hospitals, community services and social care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of premises that have received the Back in Business rates relief scheme, since its reinstatement in 2024, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The information requested is set out in Annex A, which provides the number of businesses that have received the Back in Business relief since the scheme’s reinstatement in 2024, broken down by Parliamentary Constituency.Annex AParliamentary ConstituencyBusinesses awarded Back in Business reliefBELFAST EAST4BELFAST NORTH8BELFAST SOUTH12BELFAST WEST11EAST ANTRIM10EAST LONDONDERRY8FERMANAGH AND SOUTH TYRONE9FOYLE4LAGAN VALLEY4MID ULSTER7NEWRY AND ARMAGH7NORTH ANTRIM8NORTH DOWN5SOUTH ANTRIM2SOUTH DOWN3STRANGFORD3UPPER BANN6WEST TYRONE2 113

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 41098/22-27, to detail the (i) number; and (ii) grade of staff, that can add or remove items on the Insolvency Service Application for Administering Cases system.
answer_text: The Insolvency Service Application for Administering Cases (ISAAC) is the Insolvency Service’s digital case management system that provides a secure and structured environment for administering insolvency cases.  It ensures statutory compliance, accurate financial processing and complete auditability.Documents, however, are not stored within ISAAC.  They are held in the NI Civil Service records management system, Content Manager, and are accessible through ISAAC.  Access to ISAAC is restricted through defined role-based and user groups permissions.All staff within the Insolvency Service must have access to ISAAC in order to carry out their duties effectively.  There are currently eighty-four Insolvency Service staff members, across grades AO, EO11, EOI, SO, DP, Grade 7 and Director, who can add or remove specific information through ISAAC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the proportion of businesses that availed of the Back in Business rates relief scheme, and have remained in business after the period of relief ends, since the scheme was relaunched in 2024.
answer_text: Since the Back in Business scheme was re-introduced, 113 businesses have received an award, and I am pleased to note that 106 of these continue to benefit from the relief, that has been designed to support businesses that establish themselves in previously vacant premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of people that have had a bankruptcy fully discharged, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of people that have been fully discharged from bankruptcy in the last ten financial years is detailed in the table below.Financial YearNumber of People Fully Discharged from Bankruptcy2016-179432017-189072018-197332019-205642020-215212021-221042022-231082023-24802024-252052025-26*188*financial year to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of people involved in insolvency proceedings, that can gain access to the records of hearings of their cases.
answer_text: There are eleven types of formal proceedings that deal with corporate and personal insolvency.Each will involve a range of individuals and organisations, which will also vary dependent on the complexity of the case.  Consequently, it would not be possible to provide definitive numbers of those people that can gain access to the records of hearings in each case.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any timescale for legislative action to provide them with legal powers to suspend certain public office-holders.
answer_text: As legislation currently stands, we have no lawful basis to impose a suspension on statutory officeholders whose terms and conditions are laid out in the statute governing their role.We have tasked officials to urgently identify an appropriate legislative vehicle by which to introduce such a power, and the range of offices for which it is required. Once the appropriate legislative vehicle is identified and full Bill instructions prepared, timescales will be clearer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any progress made on digitisation projects within his Department.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking work across several business areas on the digitisation of services.Land & Property Services (LPS)Land & Property Services is delivering a multi-year transformation programme to modernise systems and services, including:launching Rates Online, which offers self-service facilities including the ability to view rates accounts, report changes, make payments, raise queries and opt to have paperless bills;digital access to draft valuations;moving all Land Registry registers online, providing a fully digital application process for solicitors, enabling online citizen access and paperless processing; and enhancing the Government Land and Property Register as a single digital record of central government land and property assets. NICSHRNew cloud-based software has been procured to manage occupational health and welfare work, supporting Occupational Health and Wellbeing to become fully digital; the project will launch on 30 April 2026.The development of the Candidate Interview Assessment Booklet to go digital is in its final stages with the aim to launch in April.A user-friendly case-management dashboard has been developed to streamline case management and reporting for various HR processes. The first process to launch on the dashboard was Hybrid Working requests in September 2025, allowing staff and line managers to track progress of their requests throughout the entire process. This has recently been further rolled out to support the implementation of the new grievance policy.NISRAAn online Labour Market Survey to measure key aspects of the labour market – including employment, unemployment and economic inactivity rates – is currently being piloted.NISRA aims to automate significant portions of its statistical production processes by developing ‘Reproducible Analytical Pipelines’ (RAP). These increase efficiency by reducing the time taken to produce statistics and increase production precision: creating space for further quality assurance and for higher value adding work. Almost half (48%) of NISRA’s statistical publications have been converted to RAPs, compared with 28% in 2022/23.Integr8 ProgrammeThe Integr8 Programme has been established to deliver modern, flexible and customer-focused Finance and HR services for the NI Civil Service by delivering a new operating model and a supporting technology solution.Digital, Security and Finance Shared Services (DSF)DoF provides a central NI Identity Assurance (NIDA) service, to support digitised government services, that enables citizens to access a wide range of NI Direct online services across multiple public bodies. The Digital Workplace Programme has been set up to provide a renewed, organisation wide approach to Information & Records Management. The planned new approach should start rolling out in 2027-28.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been undertaken on the the level of staffing within her Department working on the Energy Strategy.
answer_text: Decarbonisation is one of my key priorities, reflecting its potential to deliver longterm economic and social benefits. Recent price volatility has reinforced the importance of increasing the generation and use of local renewable energy which will deliver a stable, affordable price for energy. The Energy Strategy is a pathway to delivering this.My Department continues to operate within a challenging resource context which places pressures on staffing and funding across the NICS. In response, my Department, including Energy Group, continuously reviews how to best use the resources available and is focussed on strong leadership, and ensuring the right people with the right skills are delivering priority policies and services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement, or associated commitments, with the UK Treasury regarding challenges facing small businesses as a result of the new Making Tax Digital system requirements.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQO 3182/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has carried out an assessment of the need to re-establish the High Street Task Force.
answer_text: The High Street Task Force was established and led by The Executive Office,  and they retain responsibility for any decision to reestablish the Task Force.  My Department has responsibility for one recommendation from the High Street Task Force Report, namely ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’.My Department continues to engage with partners across government, councils and business on the challenges facing high streets, and are progressing our responsibility from the Task Force Report through work on employment rights, the Real Living Wage and the Good Employment Charter. Local Economic Partnerships also provide a mechanism to address local economic issues, including town centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement, or associated commitments, with the UK Treasury regarding challenges facing childcare businesses as a result of the removal of the 10 per cent wear and tear allowance.
answer_text: The removal of the 10 per cent ‘wear and tear’ allowance for childcare providers was first raised with me in correspondence in January. Specifically in relation to the removal of this allowance for childminders with income of over £50,000, from April of this year. While affected childminders will still be able to claim tax relief on business expenses, to levels over 10 per cent where that is applicable, there is concern from the sector as to how the removal of this allowance might impact on some childminders, and the practicalities of how the new approach will be implemented. My officials previously raised these issues with HMRC. However, given the ongoing concerns, I asked for this matter to be escalated and for senior officials in my department to raise the matter with their senior counterparts in HMRC. A senior level meeting took place on 4 March, where my officials pressed HMRC on the importance of recognising and acting on concerns from the sector ahead of the proposed changes coming into effect. It is important that HMRC alleviate any impact on the sustainability of the sector. My department has also been liaising with the Department of Education on this matter, given its responsibilities for childcare provision. I understand that Education officials are monitoring any impact the changes may have on childminder provision at a strategic level. My officials will continue to engage with the Department of Education and HMRC to understand how the sector’s concerns are being responded to.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) volume; and (ii) cost, of missed MOT appointments, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) publishes quarterly statistics in the ‘Driver & Vehicle Agency Activity Statistics’ which are available at Driver & Vehicle Agency Activity Statistics | Department for Infrastructure. The number of Failed to Attend (FTA) appointments is detailed in Section 1 – Vehicle Testing, Table 1.10.  The most recent publication can be accessed via the following link  DfI, Driver, Vehicle, Operator, and Enforcement Statistics - Quarter Two; July 2025 to September 2025. The table below details the number of missed vehicle test appointments in each of the last three financial years.  It also separately identifies private cars from all vehicle tests. Financial YearPrivate Car FTAsVehicle Test FTAs2022-2339,20550,7702023-2437,48749,0972024-25	36,18148,459For the most part, there is no cost to the DVA for a missed appointment, as the customer pays the appropriate test fee at the time of booking.  If they fail to attend a booked appointment their fee is forfeited. Where a customer successfully appeals an FTA, for example due to severe weather, bereavement, medical emergency etc; they are provided with a new appointment slot or a refund. The value of these replacement appointments is estimated to be around £18,000 across the three financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been carried out of the level of students from the Mournes area taking up courses in the Southern Regional College, since the closure of the Kilkeel campus.
answer_text: An analysis of enrolments confirms that students from the Mourne locality continue to access all levels, modes of attendance and pathways across all Southern Regional Collegen(SRC) faculties.  In the academic year following closure of the Kilkeel campus, student numbers from the Mournes area increased.  In the two subsequent years there was a slight decrease, but the numbers of students in 2024/25 was higher than in 2020/21.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the total number of unpaid court fines, in the last five years, broken down by year.
answer_text: The total number of unpaid court fines at the end of each of the last five financial years is set out below:Financial YearNumber of Unpaid Court Fines at the end of each financial year2020/21109,4072021/22114,6462022/23116,4862023/24110,175*2024/25101,332**The volumes of unpaid court fines for the financial years 2023/24 and 2024/25 are provisional until audited accounts are finalised and signed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the total amount in unpaid court fines, in the last five years, broken down by year.
answer_text: The term “court fine” is used to cover court-imposed monetary penalties, and juror fines which have been registered with the court.Financial YearNumber of Unpaid Court Fines at the end of each financial year2020/21£16.3m2021/22£17.1m2022/23£17.3m2023/24£17.0m*2024/25£15.6m**The monetary value of unpaid court fines for the years 2023/24 and 2024/25 are provisional until audited accounts are finalised and signed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any action her Department is taking to recover unpaid court fines.
answer_text: The Fine Collection and Enforcement Service (FCS), which was established in June 2018 is part of the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) and is responsible for the enforcement and recovery of unpaid fines. When a court makes a collection order, this affords collection officers within FCS a range of powers to collect and enforce unpaid court fines. These powers are set out in Article 6 of the Justice Act (NI) 2016 and include deductions from benefits, applying deduction from earnings and subject to the order of the court, vehicle seizure orders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 41098/22-27, to detail what happens to the data related to a person’s case once their creditors have been discharged.
answer_text: In a bankruptcy case, the bankrupt will normally be discharged after one year, unless that period has been extended by the High Court.  Creditors will not be discharged unless, or until, they have been paid in full.  The administration of a bankruptcy will frequently extend beyond the date of bankrupt’s discharge, especially if assets are disputed, or other matters remain before the courts.  As a result, the Trustee in bankruptcy can continue to realise assets within the bankruptcy estate and make distributions to creditors until all matters are resolved.  If new information relating to the bankruptcy comes to light in future years, especially relating to previously unrealised assets, it is important that sufficient detail is retained to allow the case to be re-opened and re-examined.  As a result, documents relating to bankruptcies are retained for 25 years.  This is in line with my Department’s published Retention and Disposal Schedule which sets out its policy for retaining documents in all business areas, and which can be found at: DfE Retention and Disposal Schedule - Internet Version.PDF

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the regional breakdown of where the additional £2.5 million for essential roads maintenance she announced on 24 February 2026, will be allocated.
answer_text: I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and was pleased to announce additional funding of £2.5m to be allocated for essential roads maintenance in this financial year. We are all too aware of the toll on our road network due to recent severe conditions.This funding was allocated based on need and capacity to deliver, with almost £2m being allocated to Southern Division, just over 0.5m to Western and a small amount to Eastern.This amount is in addition to the £7.85m Winter Recovery Fund, which I announced to help address the worst affected areas as quickly as possible, and contributes to the £40m my Department has invested in our road network since December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any criteria her Department attached to the £45 million Regional Economy Fund, allocated to local councils for distribution.
answer_text: The establishment of Local Economic Partnerships (LEPs) across 11 councils is a key part of the Sub Regional Economic Plan.In order to draw down allocated funding from the £45m Regional Balance Fund, each LEP must submit an Action Plan to the Department for approval and sign both a Memorandum of Understanding and a Letter of Offer. Guidance has been provided to Partnerships to help shape these plans and my officials and Invest NI are part of all partnerships and continue to provide guidance and support as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the £45 million Regional Economy Fund, which was announced on 27 January 2025, including (i) the amount drawn down to date; and (ii) the profile of any anticipated spend on this, in the incoming financial years.
answer_text: All Councils have been incurring expenditure on Local Economic Partnerships as part of their work to date in developing and finalising action plans. In accordance with the usual practice, they will claim in arrears and can commence drawing down funding  once they have an  approved Action Plan and a signed Letter of Offer. All  Action Plans have now been submitted and Letters of Offer issued to eight Councils with the remainder to be issued in the coming weeks.  Two councils have submitted claims totalling just under £70K  in the period up to February 2026 and these claims have been paid. Councils are forecasting further expenditure of £605K in this financial year and we expect claims to be submitted for this period  by  councils in the coming weeks.  My officials continue to work with Councils around forecast for remaining years and updating this as action plans are agreed and projects delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the level of projects supported by the Investment Fund, in each of the past three financial years.
answer_text: The table below shows the projects supported by the Investment Fund in each of the last three financial years, including the current financial year to date, as determined by the date each loan was formally signed:Loan Value Year loan signed York Street Student Accommodation£19.50m 2023-24 Bradbury Place Student Accommodation£10.83m 2023-24 Derry Aparthotel £3.80m 2023-24 Hamilton Dock Hotel£28.00m 2023-24 Dunluce Lodge Hotel £6.00m 2023-24 King's Hall Phase 2 £17.86m 2023-24 Gronan Windfarm£8.58m 2025-26

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) her assessment of the economic impact the Carlingford ferry has on the local economy; and (ii) the level of engagement she has had with Carlingford Lough Ferry.
answer_text: The Carlingford Lough Ferry plays a strategic economic role linking County Down and County Louth.  The ferry supports crossborder tourism, regional mobility, and local economic development. Its continued operation aligns strongly with ongoing Shared Island and crossborder investment programmes.While I haven’t met directly with the operators of the Carlingford Lough Ferry, I did meet with Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in November 2025 and we discussed the tourism and economic potential including opportunities in the Carlingford Lough region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of compensation claims (i) made; and (ii) which were successful, against his Department, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: Compensation claims lodged in last 3 financial years – 673Public liability claims lodged in last 3 financial years – 8(ii) 	Out of these claims:• 	the courts determined that the Department was liable to pay compensation in 0 cases.• 	13 cases settled out of court for a figure less than the professionally assessed value of the claim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of employer liability claims (i) made; and (ii) which were successful, against his Department, in each of the last three financial years, broken down by (a) accidents or injury caused due to faults in the fabric of a departmental building; and (b) other damages, including fair employment and industrial tribunal cases.
answer_text: There have been 6 employer liability claims made against my Department within the last 3 years. Four claims were for ‘accidents or injuries caused due to faults in the fabric of a departmental building’; and two claims were for ‘other damages’ from injuries caused in the area surrounding an office building. There were no fair employment or industrial tribunal cases as a result of these claims.One claim for ‘accidents or injuries caused due to faults in the fabric of a departmental building’ and one claim for ‘other damages’ were both successful and settled in 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of contract claims, paid by his Department, to contractors for unforeseen delays in project completion or cost overruns, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: My officials have advised that the Department has not paid any contract claims to contractors for unforeseen delays in project completion or cost overruns over the last three financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any reasons his Department has paid contract claims to contractors for unforeseen delays in project completion or cost overruns, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: My officials have advised that the Department has not paid any contract claims to contractors for unforeseen delays in project completion or cost overruns over any of the last three financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of public liability claims (i) made; and (ii) which were successful, against his Department, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: (i) As public liability claims are compensation claims this question has been dealt with in the response to AQW 41190/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when care workers in (i) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the Western Health and Social Care Trust, will move from task or zero hour contracts to contracted hours, in line with the other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Southern Trust has advised that in preparation for the implementation of a new HR and Payroll system in Autumn 2026, the Trust Home Care service consulted with staff about moving to contracted hours and expects to have completed this work by 31 March 2026.The Western Trust has confirmed that it does not have any home care workers on zero-hour contracts, the Trust does operate a bank to deal with urgent and unforeseen circumstances and these staff have bank contracts.  These bank contracts allow workers to choose to accept or decline shifts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average cost to her Department to repair a pothole.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 26404/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the data management controls her Department has in place for the Insolvency Service's Application for Administering Cases; and (ii) when these controls were last tested or audited.
answer_text: Access to the Insolvency Service Application for Administering Cases (ISAAC) is restricted to authorised Insolvency Service staff through password-protected access to the NIGov network. Additional controls are applied through defined role-based and user groups permissions.Insolvency Practitioners (IPs) and their assistants access a dedicated IP Portal and must authenticate using Government Gateway. When new IP users are registered, their verified personal details are recorded to support the authentication process. Routine compliance checks are carried out to ensure that all user profiles remain accurate and up to date. These checks also verify that access for IPs is removed for cases where they have completed their administration. The most recent check was completed on 23 February 2026.   An annual review is also undertaken to confirm that staff who have left the Insolvency Service no longer retain access to ISAAC, the latest review took place on 23 July 2025.ISAAC is also subject to an annual independent IT Health Check. The 2025 assessment was conducted during week commencing 12 August 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any reviews are being carried out on different pay bands for care workers across the Health and Social Care Trusts, despite having similar duties.
answer_text: Banding of all Agenda for Change roles, including those of care workers, is determined by the duties and responsibilities of a role assessed in partnership with Trades Unions using an agreed job evaluation scheme. Across the UK, jobs are matched to nationally evaluated profiles, based on the roles undertaken. These profiles work on the basis that similar posts and responsibilities across the UK health services reflect the demands of the job and to ensure equality of pay. This process ensures that the level of post is dependent on the role, not the environment, and as there is a difference between roles, a skills mix is necessary.All HSC Trusts have also confirmed that no reviews are currently being undertaken within their Trust of the different pay bands for care workers. If an individual member of staff believes that their role has changed significantly, they should discuss this with their employer, who can then submit a new job description through the Job Evaluation process to have a Job Evaluation panel consider if their band should be changed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on iPads for domiciliary care workers, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Spending on iPads for the Statutory Trust Home Care service was across 2 financial years as follows:2023/24 - iPads via Care Line Live - £452k (Acquired 969 iPads) and2024/25 – iPads via Domiciliary Live Monitoring (Care Line Live) - £108k (Acquired an additional 231 iPads).The introduction of iPads has allowed the Trust to realise a number of service improvements in terms of efficiency and quality of care offered, including:The commencement of an implementation programme to introduce a real time home care management system from December 2024 to assist with improving efficiency of the Statutory Home Care service. This is currently operational in 2 out of 3 localities in the SHSCT.Staff now able to receive electronic ”live” updates to their work schedules as opposed to printed and posted schedules. Improved connectivity of this large staff workforce to the wider organisation. All staff now have a Trust email account.Improved service user safety, as care staff “check in” when entering service users homes, enabling the office to monitor this in real time and raise an alert if a call appears that it has not taken place. Improved staff safety as the iPads facilitate increased Lone Worker safety as the office have visibility whether a visit has taken place or has not occurred or that the staff member appears to be delayed leaving the visit. Increased efficiencies as the iPads have facilitated real time check in / out and provides detail and insight into productivity and capacity that can potentially be freed up to be allocated to new service users requiring a care package. Proper support of our staff via the iPads, to access e-learning opportunities and to attend virtual Team meetings andImprovements in terms of the ability to share communications and assessments in a more timely manner with relevant staff immediately, increasing staff and service user safety, as well as improving the quality of the care delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of pothole repairs, in each of the last three financial years, for each regional division.
answer_text: The table below details my Department’s spend on structural maintenance, which includes pothole repairs, in each of the last three financial years, for each Roads Division.  Roads DivisionStructural Maintenance22-2323-2424-25£’m£’m£’mEastCapital14.914.119.8Resource2.83.75.6NorthCapital26.322.725.7Resource3.555.7SouthCapital29.231.632.7Resource5.17.17.4WestCapital40.629.128.9Resource4.96.99.8Expenditure reflects budget availability and capacity in any given year.  Capital Structural Maintenance includes resurfacing and reconstruction, asphalt patching, surface dressing, capital investment in both drainage infrastructure and in the structural stability of embankments. Resource Structural Maintenance covers patching of carriageway and footways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail where within his Department he will propose efficiencies and savings, in the open book exercise, in response to the Reserve Claim.
answer_text: This exercise is intended to provide more clarity on the Executive’s spending and pressures. Each department will play a crucial role in articulating the challenges in their area. It will support a greater understanding of the challenges the Executive is facing. The exercise does not explicitly require departments to identify efficiencies.I will continue to explore every opportunity to identify and achieve any potential efficiencies and savings, where possible, in the management of my departmental budget, as I seek to deliver value for money public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether North South Bodies will be included in any attempts to drive efficiencies, in the open book exercise, in response to the Reserve Claim.
answer_text: The exercise being undertaken in conjunction with the Treasury will cover all spend by departments and arms-length bodies and will therefore include that which pertains to spend on North South Bodies, where relevant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether (i) increased funding allocations to repair roads will be included in existing sub-contracts with providers; and (ii) new bids will be opened for the short notice nature of the work.
answer_text: The works associated with the increase in funding I recently announced will be delivered through the contracts current in place. The arrangements between the main contractor and their sub-contractors are governed by agreements between these parties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the volume of (i) pothole repairs; and (ii) resurfacing works, that took place in each month, over the last 12 months.
answer_text: My department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.  Defects are categorised using a range of options and a priority is set based on the severity of safety risk to the public. This information is published and can be viewed using the link: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defects.  The information can also be sorted to show if the defect has been instructed for repair and completed.As resurfacing works are planned and delivered as schemes that can extend over several months, the Department does not hold this information broken down by month. Instead, completed schemes are reported annually in the Department’s 2025/26 annual report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the level of poor quality repairs to potholes, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold centrally recorded information specifically categorised as “poor quality” pothole repairs.Pothole repairs are inspected as part of routine operational and safety processes, and where defects are identified following repair, remedial action is taken in line with contractual and operational requirements. Records are maintained to support the management of the road network and to ensure that repairs meet the required standards; however, these records are not collated in a way that allows the Department to report separately on the number or level of repairs deemed to be of poor quality over the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of complaints made regarding the Civil Service recruitment process, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Complaints data relating to the NICS external recruitment process is published in the Recruitment to the NICS Annual reports. These reports can be accessed via the NICS Recruitment website at NICS Recruitment - Annual Reports. The 2025 report will also be published on this site in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of fines issued; and (ii) the total amount fined, in relation to marine licensing, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Two fines have been issued in relation to marine licensing in the last three years. Both were in 2023 for £1388.00 and £1124.50, totalling £2,512.50. There were no fines issued in 2024 and 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that referrals to debt companies, arising from pay errors, have had on health staff.
answer_text: HSC Trusts do not use debt companies for the recovery of funds including those arising from pay errors. There is a regionally agreed HSCNI Employee Remuneration and Expenses Overpayment Recovery Policy which outlines the actions taken where an overpayment is identified. Repayment plans and financial hardship reviews are available to all HSC staff in line with the agreed policy with staff also able to access extensive financial wellbeing support via regional partner organisations. The use of internal HSC legal action may be considered where an employee fails to re-pay an overpayment of remuneration or expenses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any methods of communication used by (i) his Department; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trusts, to notify patients of upcoming appointments.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who missed hospital appointments due to missed postal communications and have lost their place on a waiting list, in each of the last three years.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any university courses which were oversubscribed, in each of the last three years, broken down by faculty.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of missed appointments recorded as due to missed or late postal communication of appointments, in each of the last three years.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has plans to introduce the option to register births in Ulster Scots.
answer_text: The New Decade New Approach (NDNA) document contained a commitment to ‘make any necessary statutory provision for births, marriages, and deaths to be registerable in Irish’. There are currently no plans to introduce the option to register births in Ulster Scots.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department recovered from landowners, who have not complied with tree and hedge cutting notices, in the South Down constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: During regular routine maintenance inspections, officials will note trees and hedges, within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction; obscuring road signs; or have the potential to create danger for the road user. In those instances, landowners will be contacted and asked to address the issue.  If the landowner fails to respond, the Department can instigate legal action to have the danger addressed, although this action is not usually required. The Department also places notices in the local press and in farming journals reminding landowners of their responsibility and the need to carry out maintenance during the early part of the year, avoiding the bird nesting season from 1 March to 31 August. My Department has issued a total of seven written notifications to landowners along the A2 between Annalong and Newcastle urging them to carry out the necessary remedial work. In addition, officials have also spoken directly with landowners seeking their co-operation and to clarify their responsibilities, which has generally resulted in the necessary work being undertaken. Where a notice has been served on private landowners regarding trees or hedges which pose a danger to vehicles using the highway and such notice has not been complied with and a vehicle or vehicles have been damaged, the Department will not normally be held liable. My Department has not sought to recover any costs from landowners in respect of cutting trees and hedges in the South Down area recently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the new rules introduced to protect Atlantic salmon.
answer_text: On 13 February 2026 Loughs Agency introduced measures within the Foyle and Carlingford catchment areas to ease pressure on already vulnerable salmon populations.The Agency has announced a reduction in salmon tag allocations for the 2026 season following new fish count data showing exceptionally low salmon returns.No blue (spring) tags will be issued, meaning all rivers in Loughs Agency's area will operate on a catch-and-release basis until 1 June 2026. From 1 June to 31 October 2026, anglers will be issued with one black tag per licence, a reduction from two.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the cost of borrowing by his Department, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The cost of borrowing by my Department in each of the last five financial years is £nil.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Belfast Region City Deal reallocation of funding for the Mourne Mountains Gateway Project.
answer_text: A conceptual proposal was presented by Newry, Mourne and Down District Council and approved by the Belfast Region City Deal Executive Board at its meeting last week. Subject to approval through the Council’s governance processes, the Council will begin to further develop the proposal and prepare an Outline Business Case.  Reallocation of the funding will be subject to business case approval by the BRCD Executive Board in the first instance, and ultimately Executive approval. I am committed to the retention of the funding in the Newry, Mourne and Down region and will ensure that my officials and Tourism NI work in partnership with the Council to secure a viable proposal that supports regional economic growth and job creation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount of the Public Sector Transformation funding that will be spent in the 2025-2026 financial year.
answer_text: It is estimated that over £14m of ringfenced public sector transformation funding will be spent in the 2025/26 financial year. Our Delivery Unit is overseeing implementation of public sector transformation projects to ensure they fulfil their potential and that connections are made across individual projects; and is advising the Public Sector Transformation Board on progress across the fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of dilapidations capitalised in his Department's 2024-2025 annual accounts.
answer_text: There were no dilapidations capitalised in my Department’s 2024-25 annual accounts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of what the £214.6 million allocation to his Department, as part of the £400 million Reserve Claim detailed in the Written Ministerial Statement on 11 February 2026, will mean to his 2025-2026 final position.
answer_text: The allocation of £214.6m from the Reserve Claim will enable my Department to address a number of the unavoidable financial pressures faced this year, including pay, Special Educational Needs, and essential schools’ maintenance. However, even with this allocation, a funding gap remains, leaving the Department, the EA and schools needing to make savings of £23.4m between now and the end of the current financial year.  Both my Department and the EA have made and continue to make every effort to realise savings at pace.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the £400 million Reserve Claim detailed in his Written Ministerial Statement on 11 February 2026 will cover the projected overspend for 2025-2026.
answer_text: Prior to the January In-Year Technical Exercise, the combined forecast pressure for the Department of Education and the Department of Health was £481.8 million. The further allocations agreed by the Executive on 6 February reduced this to £456.7 million.The £400 million Reserve claim allocations announced on 11 February brought the forecast overspend down to £56.7 million. Based on the latest departmental forecasts, the total forecast overspend for 2025-26 has now reduced again to £49.7 million. I have made it clear to all Ministers that we must continue our efforts to reduce the overspend. I remain committed to working with Ministerial colleagues to manage the remaining pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he plans to provide detail of how the Reserve Claim detailed in his Written Ministerial Statement on 11 February 2026 will interact with his proposed multi-year budget, in advance on the consultation closing to inform responses.
answer_text: As set out in my Oral Statement of 12 January, I did not include the impact of any overspend in 2025-26 in my draft Budget 2026-2029/30 proposals as we do not yet know what the quantum of the overspend will be. I also indicated that it was my intention to press Treasury to allow us to repay any overspend over a longer period to minimise the detrimental impact that an immediate reduction would have on public services.In my Written Ministerial Statement of 11 February, I welcomed Treasury agreement to a Reserve claim of £400 million for 2025-26 which is repayable over three years, £80 million in 2026-27, and £160 million in both 2027-28 and 2028-29. Consideration of how these repayments will be funded will form part of the discussions and  decisions by the Executive on a final Budget 2026-2029/30. The Executive will work with the Treasury to review the Executive’s spending and pressures. This will support a fuller understanding of the significant challenges we are facing and help strengthen the case for fairer funding for public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following his Written Ministerial Statement of 11 February 2026, to detail the timetable for the Executive to work with the Treasury on the 2026-2027 budget.
answer_text: To strengthen our case for fairer funding for public services, the Executive will work with Treasury to review the Executive’s spending and pressures. This will support a fuller understanding of the significant challenges we are facing. Each department will have a crucial role in this work, as they are best placed to articulate the challenges within their areas. This process will be firmly grounded in the principles of devolution and will respect Executive’s autonomy in making funding decisions.The exercise is intended to conclude in mid-March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of what the £185.4 million allocation to his Department as part of the £400 million Reserve Claim detailed in the Written Ministerial Statement of 11 February 2026 will mean to his 2025-26 final position.
answer_text: My Department is currently facing a funding gap of some £211.6 million. The allocation of £185.4 million from the Reserve Claim is very welcome as it will make a substantial contribution towards reducing our projected overspend. While it will remain considerably challenging to breakeven this year I will continue to make every effort to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of Civil Service staff required to wear a uniform to work; and (ii) this number expressed as a percentage of total Civil Service staff.
answer_text: Data on the number of Civil Service staff who are required to wear a uniform is not held. As a result, it is not possible to express this as a percentage of the total Civil Service workforce. The Civil Service Staff Handbook includes a chapter on “Uniforms and Protective Clothing”, which sets out the roles for which uniform items, protective clothing and, where appropriate, equipment are provided. In addition, there are other Civil Service roles, not listed in this chapter, where staff wear uniforms, including uniformed Prison Officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any progress on the new Legal Services Oversight Commissioner, following recent legislation approving the set up the Commissioner's powers and office.
answer_text: Since the commencement of the Legal Complaints and Regulation Act (NI) 2016 on 1 September 2025, good progress has been made. A fundamental element of the new arrangements is the agreement by the Commissioner of a complaints handling plan with both professional bodies; that agreement has been secured. New Lay Chairs and Members have been appointed to both the Bar Complaints Committee and the Solicitors Complaints Committee, and both Committees are actively operating the new arrangements.The Commissioner has also regularly met with the Lay Chairs, discussing and providing advice on areas such as Member training, Committee procedures and the Commissioner’s data requirements for reporting purposes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the regional spread of the £7.85 million Winter Recovery Road Fund allocation.
answer_text: The table below details the regional spread, by Roads Division, of the £7.85 million Winter Recovery Road Fund allocation to help mitigate the impacts that recent winter weather conditions have had on our road network.Roads Division£mNorthern0.25Southern3.3Eastern0.7Western3.6Total7.85This additional funding will deliver an enhanced structural maintenance programme including large-scale patching and resurfacing targeted at those areas of greatest need. This work will commence without delay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the finances in NI Water; and (ii) whether she will be engaging forensic accountants to review their finances this year.
answer_text: This year, I have allocated over £500 million of public money for water and sewerage services, which is over 90% of the funding NI Water requested in its 25/26 operating plan and budget. NI Water is currently reporting a potential budget pressure of c£1.5m resource expenditure caused by increased operational costs and is on track to spend all its capital budget allocation this year. There are no plans at present to instigate a forensic accountancy review of NI Water’s finances. My department continues to monitor the position closely.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he will take to deliver the £6.5million allocated to help people with disabilities adapt their homes in the January Technical Exercise, by the end of the financial year.
answer_text: I have allocated this funding to the Housing Executive for adaptations to its homes for disabled tenants, to be delivered by the end of this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the February 2026 Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman report The Removal of Patients from their General Practice Service; and (ii) to detail any actions he has taken in response.
answer_text: I welcome the Northern Ireland Public Service Ombudsman’s publication of the Overview Report on the Removal of Patients from their General Practice Service and the consideration given to this important issue.I fully recognise the importance of appropriate access to General Medical Services provided by GP practices under the GMS Contract, and the vital service delivered by GPs across Northern Ireland.I acknowledge NIPSO’s recognition of steps already taken by my Department to address some of the concerns identified in the report. My Department will give due regard to the three recommendations detailed in the NIPSO report as part of its ongoing work to ensure that all patients have access to the General Practice services they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any progress to improve safety measures around school bus stops.
answer_text: Road safety - and the safety of our school children - is a priority for me as Minister. My Department’s Safer Journeys to School programme is progressing at pace towards a comprehensive package of measures, including legislation.Work is underway preparing a consultation on the legislation. I will make an announcement in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of deaths of children, in each of the last five years, including the number of these children who were (i) in foster care at the time of their death; or (ii) adopted.
answer_text: Official statistics on death registrations are produced by the NISRA, using registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO).The number of deaths of children, in each of the last five years that are currently available, can be found in Table 5.2a of the Registrar General Annual Report. Regarding parts (i) and (ii), NISRA do not hold information on the care or adoption status of individuals who have died, as this is not collected as part of the death registration process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) inspections carried out on utility company reinstatement works on roads and footways, in South Down, over the last five years; and (ii) times further work was required, following the inspection.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a utility company fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the utility company is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.My Department does not maintain information by constituency area rather by council area. The number of inspections carried out on utility company reinstatement works on roads and footways and the times at which further work was required in the Newry Mourne & Down Council area, over the last five years, are included the table below: 2024/252023/242022/232021/222020/21Number of Inspections12101282150514481557Number of occasions further work was required1719374438

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support his Department provides to the NI Air Ambulance.
answer_text: I strongly support the work of the NI Air Ambulance service and offer my sincere thanks to all those involved in providing it.  In reaching people quickly, it provides fast access on the ground to urgent and emergency treatment, particularly in remote and rural areas.The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), Helicopter Emergency Medical Service (HEMS) operates in partnership with NI Air Ambulance.  Although two separate entities, they are co-dependent upon each other; the NI Air Ambulance provides and funds the aviation service, through charitable donations, and HEMS, through NIAS, provides and funds the clinical and operational aspects of the service.  My Department, through NIAS, has responsibility for all aspects of the delivery of clinical care, and clinical governance including the provision of the Paramedics and Consultants. The service is led by an Operational Lead with a team of eight Advanced Paramedics, Critical Care and a Clinical Lead working with a team of 15 consultants from across five Health and Social Care Trusts. The team of doctors have specialities in Emergency Medicine, Anaesthetics and Intensivists. NIAS also ensures the most appropriate medical equipment and medicines are available for use and provides the air desk and all dispatch facilities in the Emergency Ambulance Control room.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans for (i) a review of; or (ii) changes to, the process for registering a death.
answer_text: There are currently no plans for a review of or changes to the death registration process. Powers introduced by the Coronavirus Act 2020 made it possible to register a death remotely, without the signature of the informant. In the years that have passed since the start of the pandemic, these arrangements have become the normal means by which the majority of death registrations take place. The powers have created a more empathetic and efficient death registration process, easing the burden on grieving families and receiving wide support from stakeholders.The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill will put this process on a permanent statutory footing and is currently awaiting Royal Assent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the ability of Departments to spend their full allocation of capital funding, redistributed in January 2026 allocations, by the end of this financial year.
answer_text: As part of the January InYear Technical exercise, Departments were asked to notify the Department of Finance (DoF) of any capital easements that had arisen since December Monitoring, and to confirm whether they were able to spend any additional capital within this financial year.The further capital allocations agreed by the Executive were therefore only provided on the basis that Departments could fully utilise this funding within this financial year.Departments have been asked to notify DoF immediately of any further Capital DEL easements that may arise.
