question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any services that have moved to being delivered on a regional or cross-Health and Social Care Trust basis, since he came into office.
answer_text: I welcome any opportunity to increase cross-Trust and regional working within our health service. The implementation of single regional waiting lists is a key component of our strategy to streamline patient care and reduce waiting times across Northern Ireland. The implementation of the Encompass programme has provided the opportunity to develop single, regional waiting lists which will ensure patients are assessed in chronological order on a regional basis, rather than within their local Trust area. There are already single waiting lists for specialist services including spinal surgery, vascular surgery, neurosurgery and paediatrics. Over the last 2 years work has also progressed to establish a single red flag breast assessment waiting list, which launched on 8 May 2025. Other single waiting lists have been developed in subspecialty areas such as bladder outflow procedures, complex stone surgery and penile cancer.Cross-Trust working has also been expanded via the use of the Day Procedure Centres and Elective Overnight Centres with general surgery, ENT, gynaecology and paediatric surgery all being delivered across Trust boundaries. Regional endoscopy centres, cataracts centres and Rapid Diagnosis Centres also allow for regional working and learning. These centres have already served our population with tens of thousands of patients receiving treatment to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) quantity; and (ii) nature, of any financial efficiencies achieved by his Department, since he came into office.
answer_text: Since the return of devolution, my Department has delivered efficiencies across a range of areas, alongside having to manage increasing cost pressures, including pay awards.Through the delivery of these efficiencies my Department has had the ability to return resource money to the centre in recent years, which reflects the progress driven partly by the efficiencies made.In line with the business plan and our published estate, social policy and corporate plans in NISRA and LPS and in line with NICS wider work led by DoF (People Strategy) we have delivered efficiencies in the following areas.Estate ManagementThe Department has disposed of 14 buildings (sales, lease ends or demolitions) and vacated a further 8 buildings, which has resulted in running costs savings, including rent, rates and service charge, in the region of £5.2m since 2024-25. Capital receipts of £8.1m also resulted from these building sales and the related savings have been recognised and reported since February 2024.The installation of a new security on the Stormont Estate in March 2025 has produced savings of approximately £36k per annum.Staff EfficienciesBusiness areas have reduced staff numbers by 69 resulting in a saving of £3.7m over the period.  ChargingNISRA through leveraging its data collection platform, new analysis of data and working with Office for National Statistics has generate increased income of over £500k.Innovation & Consultancy Services (ICS) has moved to a full cost recovery model. DSO introduced a new charging model in April 2025 whereby DSO salaries and overheads associated with all legal work provided are now hard charged on a bi-annual basis.  LPS (OSNI) has increased its income by £712k from its commercial mapping products. Reducing IT CostsIT Assist has reduced its costs by streamlining the use of both hardware and software reducing costs by c£1.2m.New Ways of WorkingNISRA automation of processes by removing paper survey forms, generating c£160k of savings. TransformationLand & Property Services (LPS), installed a more efficient heating system in Lanyon Plaza during 2025/26 and will roll out efficient lighting sensors by the end of the financial year.Looking forward LPS expects its Transformation Programme to deliver significant efficiencies by 2030 through automation and digitalfirst services. Greater selfservice, online payments and improved communication will reduce staff demands, backlogs and operating costs. Automated workflows will cut manual processing and phone calls, while reduced paper use will create longterm savings in printing, postage and storage, supporting sustainability goals.Policy ChangeOn the 1st September 2025 a change to the NICS recruitment advertising policy was implemented which removed the mandatory requirement to advertise all externally recruited NICS job opportunities in the three daily newspapers, this can result in potential future savings in recruitment advertising spend.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any changes that will come about under the new Rates Online Service.
answer_text: The new Rates Online service is designed to enhance public service delivery by expanding opportunities for customers to self-serve, increasing transparency around rating information, and providing clearer digital channels for customers to engage with Land & Property Services (LPS). It modernises how ratepayers can access and manage their information online.Through Rates Online, customers can manage their personal, property and billing details and choose from a range of payment options. They can receive new rate bills and view previous bills electronically, reducing reliance on paper and improving accessibility.   Where customers face financial difficulty, the service provides a straightforward way to access support from LPS and obtain guidance from third-party advice organisations. Further development will introduce automation in rate relief applications, enabling customers to submit applications and track progress online. This will significantly improve transparency throughout the process. As more ratepayers move to the digital service, Rates Online will make it easier to access key services, reduce the dependency on printing rate bills, and contribute to a better environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the impact of cuts to the Local Growth Fund, on the social enterprise sector.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether there has been any reduction in Civil Service sick leave levels during the 2025-2026 financial year, to date.
answer_text: Official sickness data published by NISRA is not yet available for 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) an update on any progress on the development of a fiscal framework; and (ii) whether this enables its inclusion in a draft multi year budget from 2026.
answer_text: The agreements already secured with the Treasury through the Interim Fiscal Framework and at the 2025 Spending Review on the application of a relative need factor in our funding arrangements mean that over £1.4 billion in additional funding has been allocated for public services here to date, of which over £820 million has been included within the proposed draft Budget for 2026-2029/30.I have also presented a paper to my Ministerial colleagues, at the Executive meeting on 6 November, setting out my approach to progressing further discussions with the Treasury, on a full Fiscal Framework. My officials have also been engaging with their Treasury colleagues, to consider the scope of a full Fiscal Framework.Similarly to the process in Scotland and Wales, negotiating and delivering a full Fiscal Framework will take time. It will require agreement with Executive colleagues and the British Government. My Written Ministerial Statement on the draft Budget, on 6 January, references my department’s work to progress a full Fiscal Framework, however it is too early to include detail in the multiyear budget. Rest assured I want to make progress as quickly as possible, to ensure the Executive has the funding arrangements and fiscal levers necessary to meet the needs of our people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment he has made of the impact of housing pressures, arising from issues concerning asylum seeker homelessness, on existing households in need of housing.
answer_text: The provision of accommodation for asylum seekers is an excepted issue and the responsibility of the Home Office, however, if an asylum seeker is granted leave to remain, the Housing Executive will consider their application and homelessness risk like any other client group.		During 2024/25, of the 15,905 households who presented as homeless, 660 (4%) presented as No Accommodation in Northern Ireland, Section S95 and, of the 10,855 accepted as homeless, 589 (5%) were recorded as No Accommodation in Northern Ireland, Section S95.		Tables 1.1E and 2.1E within the Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin respectively refer:		https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2025

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of studies that show lower reoffending rates for drivers who take the Speed Awareness Course, compared to those who receive fines, whether she has any plans to make this course mandatory for new learner drivers, as part of her strategy to tackle speeding.
answer_text: Preparation for the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) here is progressing well. Once operational, one of the key elements will be the requirement for learner drivers to complete a set Programme of Training. My intention is to include a speed awareness module as part of that Programme of Training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any fiscal impact assessments that have been commissioned regarding the increased demand on health services due to population growth from immigrant settlers.
answer_text: My Department recognises that population growth, from all sources including immigration, can influence demand for Health and Social services.  Demographic trends and service pressures are regularly monitored to inform planning and resource allocation.At present, no specific impact assessments have been completed to solely examine the impact of population growth arising from immigrant settlers on health service demand. My Department’s planning and financial modelling considers population changes more broadly, using data from the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) and other official demographic sources, alongside projected service needs.My Department continues to review and update these projections to ensure that HSC services are appropriately resourced and able to meet future demand across all population groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any recent estimate of the number of small and medium-sized enterprises that have ceased trading during 2025; and (ii) any measures her Department is taking to avoid further closures.
answer_text: (i)The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) collects data on business closures as part of its Inter Departmental Business Register (IDBR). Figures for 2025 are not currently available but an update is expected in February 2026. You can find the register at the following link: https://www.nisra.gov.uk/statistics/business/inter-departmental-business-register (ii) Businesses across the North face multiple challenges and in my previous role as Finance Minister, I commissioned the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre to examine the cost of doing business in the North. That research was published in May 2025 and while many of the cost challenges fall outside the remit of my Department, and indeed of the Assembly, I am committed to supporting small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in a number of ways. My Department provides a range of support for SMEs through Intertrade Ireland and Invest NI who offer a broad mix of export, innovation, capability building, and funding supports designed to help businesses grow sales, modernise operations, develop new products, and expand into new markets. In addition, support and advice for SMEs is available from Go Succeed, the council-led business support service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any systemic changes he is introducing to eliminate the reliance on corridor care within the health system.
answer_text: I recognise the significant challenges faced by Emergency Departments (EDs) and I am acutely aware that the current level of use of non-designated clinical spaces to provide care is too high and must be reduced. However, Emergency Departments (EDs) operate in a highly dynamic environment where escalation spaces have their place in being used flexibly by clinicians to maintain patient safety during periods of exceptional demand. My Department requires all Trusts to adhere to strict clinical governance standards when using non-designated spaces for patient care. This includes:Risk assessments and infection prevention measures for any escalation area;Senior clinical oversight to ensure patient safety and dignity; andContinuous monitoring of patient flow and escalation triggers as part of Trust surge plans.While I accept corridor care is reflective of the current pressures on hospital and the wider health systems, the impact on those providing and receiving care in that environment is not acceptable as routine, nor is it sustainable. I want to provide reassurance that work is already ongoing that will help address this, subject to the resources available to me. For example, by diverting patients to more appropriate pathways, timely discharge, an increased focus on self-care, utilisation of Pharmacy support and GP services. These efforts are part of a broader hospital and system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care, both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system, but ultimately there is no system lever that of itself can address the overcrowding and corridor care in EDs. The role of the public is critical and recognises the shared responsibility for ensuring ED services are available for those who need them most.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of elective operations that have been cancelled, in the last three months, due to (i) staff shortages; and (ii) bed pressures.
answer_text: There were 214 elective operations cancelled over the last three months, due to staff shortages and bed pressures. This is approximately 1.8% of the total elective procedures scheduled over the same time period. The breakdown of cancellations by Trust is detailed below.HSC Trust	Staff Shortages	Bed PressuresBHSCT	59	60NHSCT	9	18SEHSCT	18	0SHSCT	10	0WHSCT	21	19Total	117	97Source: Health and Social Care Trusts & PSSID Information TeamUnscheduled care pressures continue to have a significant impact on bed capacity across the system, which in turn affects the ability of Trusts to protect and deliver elective care. Learning from winter 2024/25 informed the development of this year’s Winter Preparedness Plan, shaped by insights from the ‘Big Discussion – Whole System Flow’ series and reinforces the importance of a whole system approach to addressing these pressures. My Department’s overarching plan sets out a range of measures designed to mitigate the additional seasonal pressures experienced across the HSC. These include actions to:•	reduce ambulance handover delays; •	extend vaccination and community pharmacy initiatives; •	enhance support for GP practices; •	improve mental health and learning disability bed capacity; •	strengthen community based social care to improve system flow; and, •	avoid unnecessary emergency department attendances and admissions, particularly for those preferring end of life care at home. Together, these measures aim to stabilise unscheduled care demand and limit the knock on effects on elective services over the winter period.My Department is also working with Trusts to expand the number of procedures which are undertaken as day surgery. This change in clinical management not only benefits the patient but will also help to minimise the risk of cancellations due to bed pressures and staff shortages. The use of the regional day procedure centres and local day surgery units is supporting this work

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of people who retired from the Civil Service on ill health grounds, and subsequently returned to Civil Service employment (i) directly; or (ii) via agency work, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of staff vacancies of home care workers, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on Health and Social Care (HSC) vacancies actively being recruited by staff group and organisation is published by my Department on a quarterly basis at the link below. The latest information available refers to 30 September 2025.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/staff-vacanciesA breakdown of domiciliary care worker vacancies actively being recruited in each HSC Trust at 30 September 2025 is detailed in the table below.HSC Domiciliary Care Worker Vacancies Actively Being Recruited, as at 30 September 2025HSC TrustVacancies Actively Being RecruitedBelfast8Northern0South Eastern0Southern191Western17Total216Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoHThe level of vacancies being actively recruited to has fallen by 309 since September 2024, 59%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given there is no public dashboard, whether there any plans for the publishing of regular public statistics for cancelled elective operations, as is the case in NHS England.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans for (i) the investment of; and (ii) improvement of service of, home care workers to help alleviate pressures on hospital beds and treating those in need of urgent care.
answer_text: As you are aware, in 2023/2024 my predecessor, Minister Swann had approved a 7.93% uplift for the Independent Sector Home Care providers, which increased the hourly rate for home care to £18.54 per hour.  Subsequent to this, in 2024/2025 a £70 million support package for social care providers and hospices was agreed.  This led to a further uplift in the hourly rate for home care to £20.01.  This decision had been made in recognition of the important role played by Independent Sector providers and their requirement as an employer to pay the National Living Wage.In March 2025, I approved a 6.7% uplift for the Independent Sector Home Care minimum hourly rate which brought the minimum hourly rate for homecare to £21.35.  There is a continuing focus on the need to further uplift this rate in line with the Real Living Wage.  This is based upon a recognition of the valued work that homecare workers carry out for many of our most vulnerable people and also in recognition of the need to both stabilise the current homecare workforce and to attract more people to work in this sector.There do however continue to remain unsourced packages of care in each of the five HSCTs. This unmet need is partially associated with lack of sufficient capacity in the system, however other factors also impact this situation including, but not limited to, lack of availability in particular geographical areas or circumstances where individuals require a home care worker to call at a very specific time of day.  My Department continues to carry out a performance management role with the Trusts in relation to reducing this unmet need.  Through the work of the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board a focus has been adopted upon the community and what reforms and initiatives can be driven forward to assist in alleviating the pressures in our hospitals. Initiatives taken forward through the work of the Social Care Collaborative Forum include;Right-sizing packages of care for individuals and re-cycling care hours back into the system to create more capacity.Focus on prevention and early interventionProvision of support through Multi-Disciplinary Teams attached to an individual’s GP and local neighbourhood networks.Promotion of all elements of Self-Directed Support to empower individuals to manage their own care needs.Despite the work being carried forward in this area, the challenges continue to be considerable. I do appreciate the stress and apprehension encountered by individuals and families, on occasions when a package of care may not be available when they are medically fit to be discharged from hospital.I would ask that you and other MLAs continue to offer assurance to your constituents that these tensions and areas of unmet need are recognised and are the focal point for work being taken forward by my Department to effect improved services for our families and communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has had discussions with other Ministers regarding potential revenue-raising streams, including instances where fees are charged for services at rates that do not cover costs, and therefore result in losses to the public purse.
answer_text: My officials have been engaging with all other departments on a Strategic Income Review, which includes an outline of income generation opportunities and full cost recovery potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps he (i) has taken to date; and (ii) plans to take in the coming months, to increase the use of Financial Transactions Capital.
answer_text: Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) can only be used to provide loans to or equity investment in the private sector.  This funding is ring-fenced for this purpose and cannot be used for anything else.My own department has limited policy areas in which FTC funding can be deployed. One area in which my department has successfully utilised FTC has been through the establishment of the £150 million Investment Fund. I have consistently engaged with and encouraged Ministerial colleagues to consider how their departments can best utilise the available FTC funding and I remain committed to working with Executive colleagues to further explore options to deploy the remaining FTC. My department is always available, to provide this support where needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the current projected collection percentage of 2025-2026 rates; and (ii) the target collection percentage of 2026-2027 rates.
answer_text: Billing and collection of rates for 2025-2026 is still ongoing.(i) Land & Property Services (LPS) is projecting a collection performance of 93% for the 2025-2026 rating year. This includes rates billed in 2025-2026 and rates carried forward from previous years.(ii) Collection performance targets for 2026-2027 have not yet been set.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on disposals under the Civil Service Estate Strategy, including estimated receipts from sales in the 2025-2026 financial year.
answer_text: In the 2025-26 financial year, my Department disposed of Victoria Hall, Belfast, on 5 December 2025, Clarence Court, Belfast, on 16 February 2026, and exited the lease of Bankmore House, Belfast, on 31 January 2026.The capital receipts from sales in the 2025-2026 financial year are £7,950,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has included provision for any revenue-raising schemes in his Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: My draft budget proposals include annual increases to the regional rate of 5% for domestic properties and 3% for nondomestic properties over the three-year resource budget period.  These increases are forecast to generate some £250 million in additional funding across the budget period to support essential public services, including healthcare, education and policing.The different approach to domestic and nondomestic rates recognises the ongoing pressures facing businesses and aims to strike a careful balance between generating necessary revenue whilst avoiding further hardship for families and businesses.In addition to this, the Budget Sustainability Plan commits the Executive to the regular review of income generation measures.  My officials are currently working on this review which will provide an overview of the income generation opportunities available to the Executive, including each department's assessment of the potential for additional fees and charges. The review is in its final stages and will help inform decision-making as we move into a multi-year budget.It is the responsibility of all ministers to examine their income generation measures. My draft Budget includes a proposal that any department that increases its income, may retain the additional funds generated to help manage its budget pressures. My proposals also aim to encourage departments and their Arm’sLength Bodies to dispose of unused or underutilised land and property by permitting them to retain up to £20 million per year in capital receipts from asset sales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of non-teaching staff currently employed within the education system.
answer_text: In 2024/25 there were 28,636.1 full-time equivalent (FTE) non-teaching staff employed within the education system.Data for voluntary grammar (VG) and grant-maintained integrated (GMI) schools was taken from the Annual Survey of Non-teaching Staff in VG and GMI schools. In 2024/25 two VG schools and two GMI schools did not complete the survey and have not been included in the figures.Data for all other schools was taken from the Education Authority annual accounts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any policy which in place for paediatricians to be on duty in Emergency Departments; and (ii) how parents can check which Emergency Departments have paediatricians on shift at the time of their emergency.
answer_text: Whilst there are a number of policies and strategies in place regarding Emergency and Urgent Care in relation to Paediatrics, most notably “A Strategy for Paediatric Healthcare Services Provided in Hospitals and in the Community (2016 - 2026)” that sets out guidance in relation to this area there is no specific policy that sets in place that paediatricians are on duty in Emergency Departments.Trusts’ Emergency Departments can ask for paediatric input when required, as they can for any speciality.  However, it must be noted that as part of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine syllabus a Consultant in Emergency Medicine must have competence in treating children and young people at all stages of their development.Through various regional networks and partnerships my department seeks means to improve the provision of care for children and young people when they require emergency care or treatment.  It is hoped that this will ensure children and young people can access high quality care across the province.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his rational for proposing a 5 per cent increase to domestic rates, and a 3 per cent increase for non-domestic rates for each of the next three years, in the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: Many households continue to struggle with the cost of living, while businesses are experiencing higher costs of doing business.In my draft Budget proposals for regional rate increases for domestic and non-domestic properties, I have sought to strike a careful balance between generating the additional income needed to fund essential public services and avoiding undue hardship for families and businesses.My decision to propose a smaller increase in non-domestic rates reflects recognition of the significant pressures facing local businesses and acknowledges their vital role in creating jobs that benefit our entire community. This approach and the increases proposed are consistent with the policy agreed by the Executive and Assembly for 2025-26 and would see over £250 million in additional rates being generated over the Budget period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the roll-out of gritting services, during the recent period of adverse weather.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements are finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and our salt barns are filled to capacity.My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Where possible, once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is secured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. The full policy can be viewed on the Department’s website below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022	It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey. During this recent cold spell, staff across all our roads divisions have been working tirelessly day and night in some very difficult conditions, to keep traffic moving safely. We owe a huge debt of gratitude to our gritter drivers and snow blowers, who are often the first traffic on many roads, leaving their own families behind to ensure we can travel safely. Their hard work and persistence ensured tangible results for our road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of teaching staff currently employed within the education system.
answer_text: In 2024/25 there were 20,123.6 full-time equivalent (FTE) teaching staff employed within the education system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she has taken to assess the impact of the fishing quota cuts to the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: I note the recent concerns raised by the NI Fish Producers’ Organisation in relation to quotas and I am aware that officials in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs continue to engage closely with industry representatives and processors to monitor and understand emerging pressures. Fishing opportunities must be set in line with scientific advice to ensure the long-term sustainable management of fish stocks, this should include an examination as to why certain species of fish are in decline and the factors that have caused this.  Some year-to-year variation in quota levels is unavoidable based on that scientific advice and factors mentioned above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current position of the containment of bluetongue.
answer_text: Since bluetongue virus (BTV) was first detected, and subsequently confirmed in Northern Ireland, my officials have carried out active surveillance and testing within the temporary control zone (TCZ) to ensure that where possible the further risk of disease spread is mitigated.  Tracings have been completed as part of the investigation for any animals which moved onto or off the four infected premises. As surveillance testing in the TCZ has now ended, and noting that we are now within the vector inactive period, my Department announced that all animals, except animals that have tested positive for BTV, are free to move under general licence within and out of the TCZ unless they are in certain high-risk categories.  Further details on this can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/changes-bluetongue-movement-restrictions Furthermore, on 8 January 2026, I announced financial support for pre-movement testing in the TCZ.  A press release on this announcement can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/daera-announces-financial-support-pre-movement-testing-bluetongue-temporary-control-zone My officials are continuing to work closely with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland and across the UK with respect to this BTV incursion and to determine next steps. Stakeholders have been kept updated through a range of communication channels including meetings, press releases, letters, farmgate interviews, web and social media updates.Updates, when appropriate, will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he has taken to address the cuts to the fishing quota and their impact on the fishing industry, including any engagement he has had with the Minister for the Economy.
answer_text: I am keenly aware of the challenges that the 2026 fishing opportunities present for the fishing industry.  The fishing opportunities for 2026 have been determined by the Secretary of State following international negotiation and support the long-term sustainable use of fish stocks while maintaining economically viable fishing fleets. 		Financial assistance is currently being provided through my Department’s Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund and subject to future funding availability, will continue to be offered in subsequent years.	This fund provides opportunities for vessel owners and processors to seek support towards investments that will enhance their economic resilience to annual fluctuations in fishing opportunities. My department will be engaging with stakeholders to determine what, if any, assistance can be provided to support the industry. I will engage with the Economy Minister as and when required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the need for support to the fishing industry, fish processing factories and the associated businesses, following the cuts to fishing quotas.
answer_text: I am aware of the concerns of our local fishing industry following the quota allocation. I recognise the importance of the agri-food sector to the local economy, and the role that the fish and aquaculture sectors play in supporting rural and coastal communities here.My Department, through Invest NI, will continue to work with local fish processing businesses, supporting them across a range of business development activities including export sales development, research & development, skills support, and energy efficiency advice and support.Support for primary production, including on-board fishing, rests with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA). I am aware that DAERA offers financial assistance for vessel owners and processors to deliver investments that will enhance their economic resilience to annual fluctuations in fishing opportunities. My officials and I will continue to work alongside Minister Muir on this and other matters relating to the sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the level of vaccination for bluetongue that was made available; and (ii) the level of vaccination uptake, in the last two months.
answer_text: (i) the level of vaccination for bluetongue that was made available.Deployment of Bluetongue vaccination is a decision for a farmer in consultation with their Private Veterinary Practices (PVP). In July last year I approved the voluntary use of BTV-3 (Bluetongue serotype 3) vaccines in Northern Ireland for use under specific licence.(ii) the level of vaccination uptake, in the last two months.Between 1 November 2025 to 7 January 2026, 45,400 doses of BTV-3 vaccine were applied for, on the specific licence applications received by DAERA from herd/flock keepers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) nature; and (ii) estimated value, of expenditure made by his Department for services described as Cash in Transit in the Construction and Procurement Delivery Collaborative Procurement Pipeline.
answer_text: The Cash in Transit is a collaborative contract which provides a secure collection and delivery service for cash and cheques or postal orders from a number of locations and at varying frequencies. The collaborative contract is used by six Departments; four Arm’s Length Bodies; one local Council and six Regional Colleges. Within my Department, the service is used by Land and Property Services and the NISRA’s General Register Office at a cost of approximately £5,500 per annum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the level of shortage of veterinary staff; and (ii) any plans to address this.
answer_text: Within DAERA’s Veterinary Service and Animal Health Group, there are currently 45 veterinary posts that are not substantively filled.  These posts are split across functions for which I carry ministerial responsibility and functions which are under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Defra and her ministerial team.Officials are engaging with colleagues in the Department of Finance and plans are underway to launch a veterinary recruitment competition for substantive Veterinary Officers early this year.  In the meantime, the Department is making use of agency staff to cover vacant posts to ensure that relevant statutory obligations can be delivered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 36548/22-27, to detail (i) an update on the Local Growth Fund; and (ii) any steps he has taken to support the voluntary and community sector.
answer_text: Since an answer was provided to AQW 36548/22-27 on 8 December 2025, cross-departmental and cross-jurisdictional work on the Local Growth Fund has continued. This has led to the identification of suitable options for the Local Growth Fund’s 2025/26 £3.75m Capital allocation, which were agreed by the Executive for submission to the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) and NIO. Minister Fahnbulleh from MHCLG confirmed on 10 December that Capital funding would be made available to the Executive through a Budget Cover Transfer.MHCLG will directly deliver the Resource element of the Local Growth Fund in 2026/27. This direct delivery will allow space and time for further collaborative working between NICS, MHCLG, NIO and the sector on the development of a programme for the Resource element from April 2027. I have been pushing for this collaboration since my appointment as Finance Minister. Whitehall delays, indecision and lack of clarity have created this difficult situation. £9.276 will be made available for Economic Inactivity in 2026/27. This is significantly less than in previous years. I have been repeatedly raising these issues with both MHCLG and Treasury.  Most recently, on 23 December, I wrote to MHCLG pressing for a more sensible Capital/ Resource split for the Local Growth Fund - one that, at least, reflects the more favourable Resource split given to English Mayoral Authorities and which would provide more flexibility to support our Community and Voluntary Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 35321/22-27 and AQW 35322/22-27, to detail (i) how the deficits in schools' budgets are funded; and (ii) the impact of this on his departmental budget.
answer_text: The in-year net increase in schools’ deficits is a charge to and thereby funded by the Education Authority’s (EA) block grant.The EA has forecast that accumulated deficits will increase by c£79m in 2025-26.  This represents a significant proportion of the EA’s forecast overspend, which in turn feeds into my Department’s forecast overspend of c£267m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) his role; and (ii) any actions taken, to address fees for public services which are set at a level lower than cost recovery, against the guidance of Managing Public Money NI.
answer_text: It is important that departments pursue full cost recovery where appropriate. It is the responsibility of individual Ministers and Accounting Officers to ensure their departments are adhering to relevant public expenditure guidance including Managing Public Money NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the number of new social homes able to be built in the 2025-26 financial year (a) pre-December Monitoring Round 2025 capital funding award; and (b) following the December Monitoring Round capital funding award; and (ii) whether an assessment has been made as to the number of new social homes that would be able to be built in the 2025-26 financial year had he been awarded the December Monitoring capital funding in July 2025.
answer_text: (i) Prior to December Monitoring, the Housing Executive assessment was that the available budget would deliver around 1,250 new social home starts. Following confirmation of the December Monitoring allocation the Housing Executive is profiling an increase to 1,750 units.(ii)There are several factors which can influence this assessment – for example, the profile of deliverable scheme proposals may have changed if greater funding certainty was confirmed earlier in the year. The timing of confirmation of Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) Starts also impacts on the amount of ‘in-year’ budget spend. It is therefore not possible to be definitive about how the SHDP Starts profile for the 2025/26 year would have changed if a higher budget allocation was confirmed earlier in the programme year.
