To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 35635/22-27, whether sufficient infrastructure is in place to provide free parking at hospitals once the Automatic Number Plate Recognition system is operational.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 36132/22-27, to detail the annual running cost of Kilkeel Medical Practice when it is under the management of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust compared to the annual running cost when the practice was being managed by GPs.
answer_text: In the 2023/24 financial year, the partners of Kilkeel Medical Practice handed their GMS Contract back to the SPPG.  The Southern Health and Social Care Trust managed the Contract from 1st November 2023.The SHSCT commenced managing the Kilkeel Medical practice on the 1st November 2023, on a rolling contract basis with SPPG.During the period from 1 April 2020 to 1 November 2023 the GMS contract(s) for patients registered with Kilkeel Medical Practice was managed by GPs.  Funding was allocated to the GPs, via their Global Sum, to cover all of the running costs of the Practice.Expenses directly relating to the management of Kilkeel Medical Practice incurred by SHSCT are:Financial YearPayroll*Goods and Services£k£k24/255272023/24**15312*The SHSCT payroll costs do not include any medical expenditure as this is managed directly by SPPG.**The Financial year 23/24 is for 5 months from 1st November 2023 until the 31st March 2024.Kilkeel Medical Practice is also located within a Trust owned building and the direct maintenance costs of operating this building are as follows:YearOperating Costs20212022202320242025Maintenance£15,945£35,760£31,332£24,488£17,714These Service charges are for operating the building for the 12 months of the financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33703/22-27 and AQW 18969/22-27, given there is no commissioned service for adult ADHD diagnoses and patients obtain their ADHD diagnosis through privately-paid healthcare professionals, for his assessment of whether the patient should be able to complete their prescription for medication through the NHS.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he has taken to improve financial governance in response to the December 2025 Northern Ireland Audit Office report Comptroller and Auditor General's Report on Financial Audit Findings 2025, which highlights £329 million was awarded via Direct Award Contracts in Health and Social Care Trusts and that in a significant number of cases spending had commenced prior to approval or full award of any contract.
answer_text: The grounds on which Direct Award Contracts (DACs) can be awarded are restricted by tightly drawn legal parameters as set out in the Procurement Act 2023. The application of NI public procurement policy is informed by policy notes issued by DoF’s Construction Procurement & Delivery after consultation with Departments.  These are in turn disseminated by my Department to Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and other Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs).  My department has procedures in place to ensure that proper governance and control over the award of DACs is maintained. These include the need to obtain expert procurement advice and appropriate approvals in advance of any award. Details of all DACs for goods and services with a value over £30k are published monthly by the Business Services Organisation on its website.  DACs for construction related contracts are published by Health Estates on the departmental website.In addition, ALBs regularly report on all DACs to their Audit and Risk Assurance Committees (ARAC) and the Department of Health ARAC receives a biannual report on the number and value of all DACs awarded by the Department and its ALBs. The December 2025 Northern Ireland Audit Office report Comptroller and Auditor General's Report on Financial Audit Findings 2025 will be reviewed by the Department and consideration given to any changes and improvements in Governance or other processes which may be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education in relation to the letter sent by the Education Authority to taxi drivers on 5 December, to detail  (i) whether the appropriate procurement procedure was followed in the award of the current contracts with these taxi drivers; (ii) whether it is within the Education Authority's legal power to change or terminate contracts in this way; and (iii) the reason why the Education Authority has not asked all drivers to move to the Department for Infrastructure's published maximum fare rate per mile, to ensure equity, rather than taxi drivers on varying rates being asked
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, which includes the procurement and management of contracts held with private taxi operators. Due to the scale of the anticipated shortfall in the EA’s overall budget this year, it has had to make challenging decisions aimed at reducing costs while maintaining essential services.  The EA’s request of taxi operators to voluntarily reduce their invoiced rates by 10% followed an in-depth analysis of current contracts, benchmarking cost per mile against the maximum fare rates for metered taxi transport, which are set out in legislation and were updated in December 2023 to reflect rising costs in the taxi industry. This analysis identified significant variations in fare rates between operators, with many charging much more than the referenced rates.  (i) The EA informs me that all taxi contracts awarded were competitively tendered in compliance with procurement legislation. (ii) The EA has confirmed that it has not breached any contract in making this request and no contracts have been terminated to date. The terms and conditions of contracts under the Dynamic Purchasing System allow the Authority to terminate contracts if necessary.   (iii) While DfI maximum fare rates informed the EA’s benchmarking, they do not apply to school transport, which is governed by procurement agreements and contracts, with fares agreed in advance. School transport is different in nature to standard metered passenger services and in determining a fare, taxi operators often need to account for factors such as multiple stops, rural routes, non-continuous journeys and significant repositioning “dead miles” with no passengers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 29987/22-27, 28858/22-27, 28857/22-27 and 27017/22-27, given the acknowledgement that Northern Ireland does not have sufficient capacity to use all the refuse derived fuel that is produced from recycling operations and the increasing reliance on export of waste, to detail any proposals included in the upcoming Waste Management Strategy to ensure that Northern Ireland’s waste is dealt with locally in accordance with the circular economy and reducing negative impacts of transporting waste, including transportation
answer_text: Waste infrastructure is a major factor in managing waste locally and the WMS highlights the necessity for Northern Ireland to increase waste treatment capacity.  My Department has commissioned a study from the Strategic Investment Board to assess the infrastructure needs of NI until 2050. This report should be available in 2026.The upcoming Waste Management Strategy (WMS) will include proposals and planned actions aimed at reducing consumption and waste prevention, as the primary means of addressing waste should be to stop it being generated in the first instance.  It is also important to recognise that waste exports are not just RDF and waste infrastructure is more than simply landfill and energy recovery facilities.Improving the quality as well as the quantity of materials collected for reuse/recycling is a key aim of both the WMS and the Departmental Response on waste and recycling reforms in Northern Ireland, for which I am seeking Executive approval. Increasing the availability of high quality recyclate locally will benefit businesses currently reliant on imports for this feedstock. This circular economy approach, aimed at keeping materials in use for as long as possible and establishing resilient supply chains, provides opportunities for expansion of reprocessing facilities, further enabling our production and manufacturing industry to create new products with higher levels of recycled material sourced locally, limiting the use of virgin materials. Our proposals will not only have benefits in the reduction of greenhouse gases from the waste sector and mitigate the risks of waste movements across borders, but will also provide significant economic opportunities for NI, helping drive a transition to a low carbon, Circular Economy, creating more local green jobs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 29987/22-27, 28858/22-27, 28857/22-27 and 27017/22-27, given the acknowledgement that Northern Ireland does not have sufficient capacity to use all the refuse derived fuel that is produced from recycling operations and the increasing reliance on export of waste, to detail any coordination there has been between his Department and the Department for Infrastructure in respect of the preparation of the sectoral plan for waste under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 to address the carbon impacts of expo
answer_text: The development of the Waste Sectoral Plan, required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, is in the very early stages. As such, there has been no engagement to date with the Department for Infrastructure.However, I recognise that the waste sector has a responsibility in addressing climate change, emissions and overall environmental improvement, with a key role to play in enhancing our circular economy.  Proposals and planned actions to tackle these challenges form the basis of our upcoming Waste Management Strategy and were included in the Departmental Response on waste and recycling reforms in Northern Ireland, for which I am seeking Executive approval.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department is promoting the reduction of waste exports through planning policy, pursuant to (i) AQW 29987/22-27; (ii) AQW 28858/22-27; (iii) AQW 28857/22-27; and AQW 27017/22-27, in which the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs acknowledged that Northern Ireland does not have sufficient capacity to use all the refuse derived fuel that is produced from recycling operations and the increasing reliance on export of waste, in line with the principles of the circular economy.
answer_text: My department is responsible for regional strategic planning policy, as contained in the Strategic Panning Policy Statement (the SPPS). The aim of the SPPS in relation to waste management is to support wider government policy focused on the sustainable management of waste, and a move towards resource efficiency. My department is also responsible for planning legislation, and I can advise that my officials are currently working to provide new permitted development rights for reverse vending machines. Officials are drafting amendments to the Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (NI) 2015 to introduce permitted development rights for the installation of reverse vending machines. The proposed changes are aimed at protecting the environment by promoting the recycling of drinks containers and will help facilitate the introduction of the Deposit Return Scheme by DAERA and contribute to a reduction in waste.You will be aware council planning authorities are responsible for bringing forward policies and proposals in their local development plans (LDPs) that support the aims and objectives of my department’s Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035 and the SPPS in relation to waste management, tailored to local circumstances. Councils must also assess the likely extent of future waste management facilities for the plan area and identify specific sites for the development of such facilities. The SPPS recognises that LDP preparation affords the opportunity to engage with relevant government departments and agencies with responsibility for various aspects of waste management, and with neighbouring councils where appropriate, thereby fostering a necessary joined up approach. My department performs an important oversight role in seeking to ensure that LDPs take account of regional policy and guidance and has been encouraging of approaches that support the sustainable management of waste. Nine out of the eleven councils here have published Plan Strategy documents and six of these have been adopted. All plan strategies to date contain waste management policies for decision making.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether planning permission was (i) sought; or (ii) granted, for the memorial structure at Kelly’s Road, Killean, South Armagh.
answer_text: The issues you raise are not a matter for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether planning permission was (i) sought; or (ii) granted, for the memorial structure in the Square, Castlewellan, South Down.
answer_text: The issues you raise are not a matter for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the level of abortions, per the Northern Ireland Abortion Statistics Publication 2024-25.
answer_text: I have noted the recent publication showing that the number of abortions increased by 3.7% compared with 2023/24 as services continue to be embedded and women become informed about the choices available to them.  Although it is not possible to fully assess long-term demand based on only two full years’ data since these services were commissioned in December 2022, I can confirm that the level of abortions remains within the planning assumption made at that time, based upon the average demand in England and Wales for 2017 – 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the repair works on the Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, which have been under temporary traffic lights for over two years, with the traffic lights recently having been extended in distance.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closures of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, following the land/verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.I can confirm that site investigation works were completed in recent days and a contractor has been appointed to complete the works. It is hoped that work will commence early in the new year and will take eight to ten weeks to complete once started. My Department will continue to prioritise the reopening of the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown to two-way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Civil Service staff at each Senior Civil Service Grade, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The number of Civil Service staff at each senior Civil Service grade, in each of the last three years is presented in the table below.Grade202320242025Head of NICS111Permanent Secretary and analogous (Grade 2)1089Deputy Secretary and analogous (Grade 3)434747Assistant Secretary and analogous (Grade 5)217216210Total271272267These figures relate to numbers of staff in substantive grade and do not include numbers of staff temporarily promoted to these grades.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions taken within her Department to support businesses during prolonged periods of mobile phone outages, especially in rural areas.
answer_text: Mobile infrastructure is delivered on a fully commercial basis here and the maintenance of equipment is the responsibility of the private sector operators.In the event of an outage therefore enquiries about the restoration of service and any appropriate compensation should be made to the relevant supplier directly. I recognise the importance that good connectivity plays in supporting businesses in rural areas and my officials are involved in several initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity. These include the Mobile Action Plan and the Barrier Busting Taskforce which has identified  the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and provided solutions to remove barriers that prevent the fast and efficient deployment of mobile connectivity throughout the North. My officials will continue to engage with and support relevant stakeholders with a view to improving mobile coverage here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any process by which Civil Service Departments determine the number of employment posts required in their Department; (ii) who reviews this number of posts as accurate or reasonable; and (iii) any process used for (a) challenging long term recurring vacancies; and (b) reviewing the continued need for these posts.
answer_text: Each Department is headed up by a Minister and Permanent Secretary, they are responsible for determining their Departmental workforce needs and establishing the number of employment posts required in their Department.  They are also responsible for keeping the number of posts under review in order to ensure affordability and efficient delivery of Departmental business.Workforce planning is a core element of the People Strategy. The Civil Service Board approved the vision for Strategic Workforce Planning (SWP) in Spring 2025. To strengthen workforce planning, my Department has issued an SWP Guide and workbook and supports a Community of Practice for sharing best practice and addressing challenges. My Department also leads an SWP Working Group to develop an integrated approach, including an SWP module within the new Finance and HR system, Integr8. As capability grows and Integr8 tools become available, Departments will use their plans to determine required posts.When a Department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR in my Department to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. Each Department’s Minister and Permanent Secretary is responsible for managing its resources, both financial and staff and ensuring that any additional staffing and current staffing resources are affordable.The referral process involves confirmation of affordability by the relevant Department. Any review of the continued need for vacancies which have been declared to be filled, is the responsibility of the Minister/Permanent Secretary of the Department in question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) his assessment of; and (ii) whether he plans to meet with the UK Treasury to discuss, the recent Autumn Budget 2025 announcement.
answer_text: Ahead of the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget I consistently called on the Westminster Government for a Budget that provided support for families, workers and small and medium size businesses.  Therefore, the removal of the two-child benefit limit and the decision to increase both the Minimum and Living Wage are welcome.  These measures will help lift thousands of children out of poverty and support our lowest-paid workers.However, the reality is that the Chancellor’s decision to freeze income tax thresholds will hit working families who will have less disposable income to spend in our local businesses. The Chancellor had an opportunity to ensure those with the broadest shoulders carried more of the burden – but she didn’t go far enough.  While I welcome the additional £370 million in Barnett funding announced in the Autumn Budget, I believe the Chancellor could and should have gone further. This funding is insufficient to reverse the damage caused by years of underfunding in our public services due to austerity, and it falls short of what is required to address current and future funding gaps.We need a tax system that is fair and progressive, one which supports the delivery of high-quality public services and allows our businesses to compete on a level playing field in the all-Ireland economy. I will continue advocate for this in my ongoing discussions with the Treasury around the Executive’s finances and when I meet the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the upcoming Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee meeting early next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) costs to run; and (ii) income received, from parking charges, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Details of expenditure and income associated with the delivery of all car parking services, including on-street Pay and Display, from 2022-23 to 2024-25 are shown in table below.Financial YearExpenditure £mIncome £m2022-2314.012.42023-2414.913.02024-2514.211.8

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when there will be clarity on the detail of the Local Growth Fund and what it means for the Voluntary and community sectors.
answer_text: In October I met the Secretary of State, Hilary Benn, and Steve Reed, Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government to discuss the Local Growth Fund. We agreed to an intensive period of trilateral working, at official level, to urgently develop a way forward on the Local Growth Fund. This work is underway.I recognise and share the concerns and frustrations of the Community and Voluntary Sector at the delay in progress with the Local Growth Fund, and the impact of the reduced funding and heavily skewed Capital profile. This is a challenging situation, particularly for the Community and Voluntary Sector, and I am committed to continued, transparent engagement with the sector on this issue. I will share further information as soon as we receive clarity and agreement from Westminster.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any conditions attached to the £370 million additional funding announced for Northern Ireland in the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: The additional £370 million announced in Autumn Budget 2025 is provided as additional Barnett consequentials and is therefore not subject to any specific Treasury conditions. As Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, it is for the Executive to determine how this funding is allocated, in line with local priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why parking restrictions, in all towns and villages, were not reduced for the Christmas period.
answer_text: Recently, for the festive period I announced a pilot initiative for Newry and Lisburn Controlled Parking Zones (CPZs). This extends the 10-minute grace period to one hour after a paid parking session ends for people parking on-street. The pilot was introduced in Lisburn and Newry because these towns have CPZs, where on-street paid parking is actively managed. This makes them suitable for testing changes to grace periods in a controlled environment. Belfast also has a CPZ, but due to its size and complexity as a major city, a different approach is required, so it was not included in this initial pilot. Other towns and villages do not have CPZs, which means the same measures would not apply there.This pilot approach will allow my Department to gather feedback, learnings and evidence-based PCN data during and following this initiative. This will help guide my future intentions for on-street paid parking in CPZs, ensuring they continue to meet the needs of local communities and businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of (i) the changes announced in the Autumn Budget 2025, to the Motability scheme; and (ii) the impact they will have on people in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the changes announced by the Chancellor to the Motability Scheme. While the Scheme provides a lifeline for many disabled people and families, supporting their independence by enabling them to lease a car, wheelchair accessible vehicle, scooter or powered wheelchair, it benefits from generous tax breaks which unfairly subsidise provision beyond its core objectives. The changes announced deliver a package of reforms which will ensure fairness for the taxpayer, while continuing to support disabled people. These reforms will not affect eligibility for the Motability scheme.  The changes will not apply to existing leases or wheelchair adapted vehicles, and the Scheme will continue to offer vehicles which require no advance payment, meaning that people will still be able to access a suitable vehicle.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) number of taxpayers; and (ii) the number of higher rate taxpayers, (a) today; and (b) in 2023.
answer_text: HMRC’s latest Income Tax liabilities statistics, published in June 2025, estimates that there are 960,000 income taxpayers in the North in 2025-26, with 131,000 of those being higher (40%) rate taxpayers. It is estimated that in 2023-24, there were 892,000 income taxpayers, with 103,000 of those being higher rate taxpayers.  It should be noted that these figures are estimates, with the most recent outturn data only available up to 2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of additional payments that will be provided to families in Northern Ireland following the removal of the two-child limit.
answer_text: It is estimated that an additional £65.1 million will be paid in 2026/27 to Universal Credit households following the removal of the two-child limit, made up of £57.3m of AME spend and £7.8m of DEL spend related to mitigation of the benefit cap, which will require Executive funding. In addition, an estimated £1.6m in 2026/27 is required for the administration and IT relating to the mitigation element.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any additional cost to the public sector salary bill, following the announced increase in minimum wage in the Autumn Budget 2025.
answer_text: My department does not hold that information. It is for individual public sector organisations to assess and manage any budget implications from this announcement.However, regarding Civil Service employees, the Autumn Budget announcement has no impact on the Civil Service salary bill here. This is because under the commitment in New Decade, New Approach, the Civil Service pays the Living Wage Foundation rate, which reflects the real cost of living and is higher than the new National Minimum Wage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when the outworkings of the Autumn Budget 2025 will be aligned to Northern Ireland’s (i) monitoring round; and (ii) budget process.
answer_text: monitoring roundChanges in the level of Barnett consequentials for 2025-26 confirmed in the Autumn Budget have informed the level of funding available in the December Monitoring round.  The Executive agreed the December Monitoring round on the 9th of December 2025.(ii) budget processHaving considered the outcome of the Autumn Budget, I will bring forward a multi-year budget to the Executive later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) topics covered; (ii) agreements reached; and (iii) actions agreed for her Department, following her recent meeting with the Chief Executive and Director of Economy, Regeneration and Tourism from Newry Mourne and Down District Council, the Minister for the Economy and South Down Sinn Fein MLAs. [R]
answer_text: In response to an invitation from Sinead Ennis MLA, I recently met Sinn Fein representatives and officials from the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council to discuss the Narrow Water Bridge Project and associated council led tourism projects; the branding and development of Kilbroney as a geological UNESCO attraction; the provision of Active Travel and further economic opportunities in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) topics covered; (ii) agreements reached; and (iii) actions agreed for her Department, following her recent meeting with the Chief Executive and Director of Economy, Regeneration and Tourism from Newry Mourne and Down District Council, the Minister for Infrastructure and South Down Sinn Fein MLAs. [R]
answer_text: In response to an invitation from Sinead Ennis MLA, Minister Kimmins and I recently met Sinn Fein representatives and officials from the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council to discuss the Narrow Water Bridge Project and associated council led tourism projects; potential development at Kilbroney Forest Park; the provision of Active Travel and further economic development opportunities in the area. It was agreed that my Departmental and Council officials will continue to collaborate to progress the opportunities discussed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work ongoing in the Civil Service to (i) highlight awareness of; (ii) reduce; and (iii) prevent, online scams involving people posing as Government Departments in their interactions with individuals.
answer_text: My department is responsible for NI Direct, the official government website for citizens. NI Direct brings together information from government departments and agencies, and general advice about scams is published in the newsroom page, pointing to the Scamwise section of the site: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/news/avoid-getting-caught-out-scamsThere are useful tips within the NI Direct website to help people avoid getting scammed on the phone, online, by mail or on their doorstep. There is a prominent landing page https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/scamwiseni which provides advice about scams, how to spot a scam, how to report a scam, specific types of scams, and how a user can report a scam to Action Fraud, PSNI or Consumerline.Guidance is clearly laid out detailing that should an individual get a suspicious email claiming to be from NI Direct, or if people are unsure about any email or web item related to NI Direct, they should report it or send feedback to NI Direct. Some scams involve websites designed to look like other official government websites, so prevention, through awareness, is a vital tool in combating scammers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the number of people presenting to the health service requiring medical attention following cosmetic surgery they have obtained outside of Northern Ireland, in the last three years.
answer_text: Trust information systems do not have coding to identify information in respect of presentations potentially linked, directly or indirectly, to a requirement for medical attention following cosmetic surgery outside of Northern Ireland, assessed in either secondary or primary care Securing this information would likely require a specific search of all patient records in each of the 3 year period requested where there is the potential for attendance to be linked to a cosmetic procedure carried outside of Northern Ireland.Whilst I am unable to provide an estimate of the timeframe for this, I understand the scale of search required would involve redirection of staff time with a likely impact on direct patient care or the identification of funding to support a specific initiative over and above core hours.Whilst appreciating the concerns in respect of this issue, I would caution against asking the service to direct resource to undertake this given the current pressures on the service, the scale of the ask and the potential for erroneous results as outlined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the anticipated timeline for delivering the practical elements of the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I plan to have the necessary legislation in place before the end of the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the most recent value of rates in arrears.
answer_text: As at the 30 November 2025, unpaid rates carried forward from previous years is £121.9 million. This is a £59.0 million (32.6%) reduction from the position as at 31 March 2025.	Of the £121.9 million;	a.	£9.4 million is in agreed payment arrangements;	b.	£56.9 million has been through a court process and is subject to further legal actions; and	c.	£55.6 million, not yet through a court process, is being pursued for payment and where appropriate legal action through the Magistrates Courts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she will take to ensure the Warrenpoint Harbour Authority Board is adequately constituted, following the resignation of a number of elected representatives in recent months.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing a recruitment competition for two Councillor board members for Warrenpoint Harbour Authority. The board remains quorate, however it is intended that the board will reach its full complement as outlined in the legislation following the current recruitment competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the need to increase the limit on advances from the Consolidated Fund from 2 per cent to 4 per cent.
answer_text: Clause 18 of the Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill proposes that the limit on advances from the Consolidated Fund be increased from 2 per cent to 4 per cent. This proposed change reflects my Department’s assessment of the risk that exists in relation to the time available to achieve Executive agreement for a Budget, gain passage for a Budget Bill in the Assembly and then obtain Royal Assent.It has been clear for some time that it would only take a relatively short delay in the passing of a Budget Bill or in achieving Royal Assent for the current 2% limit to be reached. The Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill is viewed as a timely opportunity for the Department to take pro-active action and increase this limit in order to mitigate this key risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the management arrangements of Kilkeel Medical Practice.
answer_text: Kilkeel Medical Practice is still under the management of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust and these arrangements remain in place for the foreseeable future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the risk to financial governance within her Department, given her Department's accounts having been disclaimed by the Northern Ireland Audit Office, for a second financial year.
answer_text: It was expected that the Comptroller and Auditor General would disclaim her audit opinion on the 2024-25 accounts due to inherent limitations carried forward from the disclaimer opinion on the 2023-24 accounts.  These impact opening balances at 1 April 2024 and the prior year comparatives within the 2024-25 accounts.  Nevertheless, the Department recognises the seriousness of the issues identified and is committed to addressing them. The Department continues to implement a range of improvements to its accounts preparation process, including further quality assurance process, moving to producing interim accounts, and increasing in-year preparation work where possible.  A new model for valuing student loans, aligned with best practice in England, is currently undergoing user acceptance testing and it is anticipated that it will be used to value student loans for 2025-26.Regarding Northern Regional College (NRC) and the quality and reliability of financial information provided by it, my department has taken significant steps to strengthen governance at NRC through my appointment of an interim Chair with governance expertise and increased financial and governance oversight led by the Permanent Secretary.  NRC itself has engaged additional resources and increased direct engagement with senior management and the Governing Body. Importantly, the issues highlighted by the Northern Ireland Audit Office do not call into question the approach of the Department’s or its Arm’s Length Bodies to value for money, propriety, or the management of its budget.  The Department remains committed to transparency, accountability, and continuous improvement in financial reporting and governance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) number; and (ii) names of all public sector bodies who are in the process of appealing the valuation of their commercial properties, as detailed in the Land and Property Services' 2026 revaluation.
answer_text: Reval 2026 is a statutory exercise that has been undertaken to revalue all non-domestic properties here for rating purposes. All properties will be assessed to an antecedent valuation date of 1 April 2024, and the new valuation list will take effect for rating purposes from 1 April 2026. Under the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order, it is not possible to challenge or appeal a Reval 2026 valuation until after the new list comes into effect. Therefore, no public sector bodies are in the process of appealing their valuation as part of Reval 2026 at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he plans to publish the guidelines, in support of the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill.
answer_text: Work continues to prepare the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme Regulations that I intend to bring to the Assembly following Royal Assent being given to the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill.  These Regulations will set out the details of the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme and allow my officials to put in place policies and procedures to make the scheme operational.  As part of this process my officials will ensure that full information and guidance documentation will be available to applicants and the staff implementing the scheme.  It is my intention that the scheme will become operational by the end of this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount that was bid to fund the health pay award to health and social care workers.
answer_text: When seeking Executive approval for the Direction issued to his Accounting Officer for the implementation of the 2025-26 pay awards to the health and social care workforce, the Health Minister identified the cost as being some £209 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of private sector bodies who are in the process of appealing the valuation of their commercial properties, as detailed in the Land and Property Services' 2026 revaluation.
answer_text: Reval 2026 is a statutory exercise that has been undertaken to revalue all non-domestic properties here for rating purposes. All properties will be assessed to an antecedent valuation date of 1 April 2024, and the new valuation list will take effect for rating purposes from 1 April 2026. Under the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order, it is not possible to challenge or appeal a Reval 2026 valuation until after the new list comes into effect. Therefore, no private sector bodies are in the process of appealing their valuation as part of Reval 2026 at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether he approved the Minister of Health's decision to move £73.5 million away from waiting list initiatives, as was allocated.
answer_text: The Executive decided, as part of its consideration of the final Budget for 2025-26, to allow the Health Minister flexibility to allocate a portion of the £80 million funding to general healthcare, should the planned elective care capacity not be fully realised. This was in recognition that achieving the targeted level of expenditure on elective care may take time to implement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has increased the targets on rates collection to increase available finance and achieve the objectives, as outlined in his Oral Statement on 18 November 2025 on rating policy.
answer_text: The targets and objectives for rate collection are a key priority for my Department in terms of securing revenue. Targets were set at the start of the rating year, increasing these now will not affect actual performance outcomes.Any revenue secured through rates collection is already anticipated in budgetary planning to meet existing expenditure requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for a breakdown of the additional £20 million, detailed as unlocked, in his Oral Statement on 18 November 2025; and (ii) to detail the amount that will be unlocked for (a) central government; and (b) local government.
answer_text: (i) The £20million referred to in the Oral Statement, represents the value associated with the empty property relief of 50% provided to vacant commercial properties. This would be the amount of revenue generated following full removal of the relief.(ii) The current £20M amount for this relief is currently distributed evenly between central and local government elements of the taxbase. It is too early to determine the precise distribution of any future revenue savings between central and local government, as this will be dependent upon both future rate setting decisions (by both District Councils and the Executive) and the redistribution effect of the Reval 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any of the detail in his Oral Statement on 18 November 2025 on rating policy, will result in changes to the rates bills to be issued to businesses in April 2026, for the 2026-27 financial year.
answer_text: I plan to launch a short consultation exercise on small business rate relief at the start of December with an aim of securing Executive approval in the New Year. My aim continues to be to make changes for April 2026. Executive agreement will be required before change can be implemented. As I stated in my statement, I am committed to delivering positive and progressive change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following his Oral Statement on rating policy on 18 November 2025, to detail the timeline for the completion of reviews of rates for pubs and hospitality in Scotland and England.
answer_text: Last month the Finance Minister in Scottish Government appointed Advocate Depute BJ Gill KC, as Chair to lead an independent review of the way licensed hospitality premises are valued for non-domestic rates.This is due to report by the end of 2026 in order that any recommendations can be considered before the 2029 revaluation cycle in Scotland.In England, in their interim response to the ‘Transforming Business Rates’ paper on 12 September, Treasury advised that, ‘they will explore concerns in relation to the Receipts and Expenditure valuation methodology’ and ‘will consider options to address these for the next revaluation.’  It should be noted that the scope of the English considerations will include all properties subject to that valuation methodology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of properties that have benefited from the Non-Domestic Vacant Rating, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The table below sets out the numbers of Properties, within each of the last three Rating Years, that benefitted from Non-Domestic Vacant Rating (NDVR), Empty Property Relief at 50%.Rating YearNumber of Properties with NDVR 50% Relief during the Rating Year2024-255,8262023-245,6702022-235,810

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the additional cost to bring social care staff in line with the pay offer being introduced.
answer_text: Based on the Living Wage Foundation rates for 2024/25 the estimated cost of implementing the Real Living Wage (RLW) for social care staff in the independent sector for the 2025/26 financial year would have been in the region of £50 million; to implement from September 2025 would have cost approximately £25million in-year. I remain fully committed to resolving pay issues across the sector and to funding the RLW at the earliest affordable opportunity. Unfortunately, I do not currently have the capacity within my budget to deliver this commitment in the current financial year. It will, however, be a priority for me in planning expenditure for 2026–27. When this uplift becomes possible, I intend to ensure that funding is allocated transparently, with clear timing and ringfencing for the pay uplift.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the cuts to quotas in catch advice announcements made for the fishing industry in 2026; and (ii) to detail any steps he plans to take to support the fishing industry in relation to this.
answer_text: I am deeply concerned by the scientific advice for stocks in the Irish Sea and the impact this will have for fisheries of key importance to Northern Ireland.  I have already written to the Secretary of State, Dame Eagle, to raise my concerns with the implications of this advice for the Northern Ireland fishing industry.My officials are engaged in the negotiations between the UK and the EU in the setting of fishing opportunities on these shared stocks and I am monitoring progress closely. While the advice presents significant challenges, I will be seeking to mitigate the impact on the respective fisheries as far as possible, while seeking to still maintain an approach that supports the long-term goal of sustainable and viable fisheries.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of vacant posts in domiciliary care roles, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the (i) value; and (ii) timeline of the crystallisation of the McCloud judgement.
answer_text: The estimated costs of remedy-related pension payments for the Civil Service Pension Scheme, where my Department has direct responsibility,  is approximately £150 million. These will crystallise in the form of increased pension payments over the coming decades to eligible members who have retired, or will retire in the future. Remedy payments are accounted as AME. All eligible active scheme members will make their remedy choice at the point they retire. This will continue until all such members retire.Eligible members who had already retired, or taken partial retirement, before the remedy was in place will receive their remedy choice by 31 March 2027. The current position is that 60% of these have already issued.  These members have 12 months to make their choice and these choices must be actioned. The estimated timeline to have this exercise completed is 30 September 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department has taken to address the issue of hospital free parking, since it was raised in 2024, which prevented legislation being implemented.
answer_text: The Hospital Parking Charges Act (NI) 2022, which prohibits the imposition of charges for parking vehicles in hospital car parks for staff, patients, and visitors, was originally planned to come into operation on the 12 May 2024. In May 2024 the NI Assembly agreed to the Hospital Parking Charges Act (NI) 2024 which postponed the operational date of the 2022 Act to 12 May 2026. The deferral period is being used to put in place the required infrastructure to control parking preserve blue light routes and protect designated spaces in the absence of charging. Each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust is responsible for the operational implementation of the required infrastructure. The Department of Health established the Hospital Parking Charges Working Group in October 2022, and it continues to oversee the operational implementation of the Act. The working group is made up of representatives from the Department, operational and financial HSC Trust representatives, Trade Unions representatives and is chaired by a Departmental official.The Department made a capital allocation of £5 million to HSC Trusts to allow preparatory work on upgrading barriers on current car parks to commence and facilitate Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) equipment procurement. Phase 1 of the implementation programme, installation of ANPR cameras and signage, was completed at the end of September, except for the South West Acute Hospital as it operates under a PFI contract. Phase 2 of the programme, testing, monitoring and reviewing of the ANPR technology has begun and will last for several months. The ANPR system is on track to be operational by May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) financial; and (ii) systematic, support available to an 18 year old, in full time education who becomes homeless, to enable them to stay in education, rather than requiring them to leave education to access benefits.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, which includes the provision of homelessness accommodation. A range of initiatives are available to support young people including:Housing First for YouthYouth programmes funded through the Homelessness Prevention FundFloating Support Services Additional support, such as vacation accommodation, may be available to qualifying relevant young people who are engaged in education via Health Trusts.  		Work Coaches in Jobs and Benefits Offices also provide tailored support to claimants, including young people, and will signpost them to benefits to which they may have entitlement. The following link https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/information-and-services/benefits-and-money provides details of benefits that are available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30567/22-27, to detail when his Department's review of university fee funding will be completed.
answer_text: My Department has commenced engagement with key stakeholders to ensure the scope and remit of the proposed public consultation are comprehensive and well-informed. This collaborative work will continue into the new year, after which officials will finalise the review and develop options for public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of payments made through the Civil Service Inefficiency Compensation award, in each of the last 3 years.
answer_text: The total amount of compensation paid to NICS employees in the past 3 full financial years is outlined in the table below. Figures relate to the date payments were made.  Financial YearCompensation paid22/23£1,474,256.1623/24£1,782,162.4324/25£2,542,924.55

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 33895/22-27 and AQW 34475/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 33895/22-27 on 21 November 2025 and AQW 34475/22-27 on 17 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will reassess how the £360 million Fishing and Coastal Growth Fund is distributed, to take account of the number of Northern Ireland-registered vessels and the value of their landings.
answer_text: I do not have the authority to reassess the distribution of the £360 million Fishing and Coastal Growth Fund, as this is a matter for the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA).		However, I have previously written to Dame Angela Eagle MP, Minister of State at DEFRA, to underline the importance of Northern Ireland’s fishing industry. At a recent meeting with Dame Eagle, I also pressed for reconsideration of the currently proposed means of distribution that takes into consideration the number of Northern Ireland-registered vessels and the value of their landings rather than the application of Barnett.		My Department will continue to emphasise the need for Northern Ireland to receive an equitable and proportionate share of the overall UK fund and will ensure that any funding allocated is directed to deliver maximum benefit for our fishing communities and coastal areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the reason why NISRA publications on sickness absence in the Civil Service details the longest duration of absence as more than 20 days, rather than longer than six months.
answer_text: According to guidance issued by UKG, employees absent due to sickness for more than four weeks (i.e., 20 consecutive working days) may be classified as experiencing long-term sick leave. NISRA defines long-term sick leave as any period exceeding 20 days and reports sickness absence by both short-term (up to 20 consecutive working days) and long-term (more than 20 consecutive working days) as shown in Figure 9 of the Sickness Absence in the NICS 2024/25 report.  More detail on the duration of short-term absences is shown in Figure 10 and similar information providing detail on duration of long-term absences is shown in Figure 16 of that report (including absence for the financial year).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of instances in the Civil Service in which the review point on frequent short-term absences, as defined in the NICS Staff Handbook, Chapter 6.06, has been triggered, in each of the last 3 years, broken down by Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of instances of sickness absence in the Civil Service lasting (i) up to 6 months; (ii) between 6 and 12 months; and (iii) over 12 months, in each of the last 3 years.
answer_text: The number of instances of sickness absence in the Civil Service lasting (i) up to 6 months; (ii) between 6 and 12 months; and (iii) over 12 months, in each of the last 3 years is presented in Table 1 below. Table 1. The number of instances of sickness absence in the Civil Service by duration in each of the last three years. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current powers regarding the capturing and distributing of images of car accidents and their victims; and (ii) any plans she has to reform the law regarding this.
answer_text: (i) In Northern Ireland, there is currently no specific law relating to the the capturing and distributing of images of car accidents and their victims.  However, the Communications Act 2003 contains offences relating to offensive, malicious and nuisance communications, which may be of relevance in incidents of this nature. (ii) This is an important issue that cuts across the responsibilities of many Departments and organisations, including my own, and I am committed to ensuring that my Department engages to consider how best we might provide support.  Therefore, I have agreed to participate in a proposed roundtable discussion convened by Road Victim Support Northern Ireland – Donegal, to consider appropriate measures to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rational as to why NISRA Sickness Absence In The Northern Ireland Civil Service reports continue to report Covid-19 as a separate line in the report, when testing is no longer widespread or required.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the importance of highlighting which elements of all Departmental budgets are statutory to that which is discretionary.
answer_text: When setting a budget, the Executive collectively agrees on a funding envelope for each department, typically comprising baseline funding, earmarked funding and a general allocation.  Each Minister is responsible for managing and operating within their allocated budget.Earmarked funding must be utilised for specific purposes, which may include statutory functions.  Baseline and general allocations may be spent at the discretion of respective Ministers in line with their departmental priorities. It is for each Minister, to ensure that their department’s statutory obligations are met within their overall funding envelope.  However, it is important to note that there is often a degree of flexibility and scalability with statutory functions, and it is for each department to determine the suitable level.  Similarly, departments should also consider whether improved outcomes could be delivered through legislative changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools reporting annual financial results to the Education Authority, that are declaring (i) surplus position; (ii) breakeven position; and (iii) deficit position; in each of the last 2 financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the net value of budget deficit positions from the Education Authority report's on school budgets, in each of the last 2 financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has conducted an assessment of the impact of Forest Service work on the recent floods in Newcastle, Co. Down.
answer_text: Forest Service’s management of the forest estate is independently audited annually to verify its compliance with UK Forestry Standards. This includes an assessment of full range of forestry activities planned and undertaken, including in Donard forest.  Donard forest and the surrounding area suffered extreme rainfall on the 19th October. This led to extremely large volumes of water running off the hillside, and for time, exceeding the capacity of the drainage system, in particular the drainage infrastructure beyond the forest boundary.Following the flooding event, Forest Service has commissioned works to repair drainage channels damaged by the flood waters.  Forest Service has taken advice from officials in DfI Rivers in respect of the works and has agreed to work with other agencies to explore any additional actions which could be taken to reduce the risk of future flooding in the area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any process followed by the Education Authority in response to schools reporting budget deficit positions.
answer_text: Under Local Management of Schools arrangements the responsibility for managing school budgets is delegated to the Board of Governors (BoG) of each school.Under guidance on Financial and Management Arrangements for Controlled and Maintained Schools BoGs are annually required to submit a three-year rolling financial plan to the Education Authority (EA) for approval which includes an annual budget for the forthcoming financial year. This guidance highlights that expenditure should be contained within the approved budget unless otherwise agreed by the EA. A historic deficit cannot be increased without advance approval from the EA and no BoG can plan to do so or commit to any actions causing the deficit to increase until approval of the financial plan has been received. Schools identifying a forecast in-year deficit are supported at a financial planning meeting to identify areas where expenditure can be reduced to operate within budget. In certain circumstances this can include integrated curriculum financial planning to ensure the efficient use of resources in educational delivery.BoGs are required to sign an assurance statement that confirms all possible actions to minimise a deficit have been considered.In instances where a school in deficit reports a proposed change in staffing to EA Human Resources or their School Improvement Officer, a cross-directorate meeting may be held. All schools submitting a signed financial plan receive conditional approval that reflects the financial position of the school. For schools in deficit those conditions require the school to control expenditure to minimise any overspend.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of his entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: My Department's 2025-26 Opening Budget provides £940.9m Resource DEL, (£756.8m Non-Ring-Fenced and £184.1m Earmarked), £270.0m Capital DEL (£120m Non-Ring-Fenced and £150m Earmarked), £48.1m FT Capital, and £10.5bn Annually Managed Expenditure (AME). Resource DELIn setting my Department's initial Budget allocations for 2025-26, over 85% (£643.3m) of my Department’s Non-Ring-Fenced Resource DEL requirement related to areas of contractual, inescapable, and statutory spend, with less than 15% (£113.5m) classified as discretionary.  A significant proportion of the discretionary requirement related to other statutory and contractual obligations related to programmes, Councils, Arm’s Length Bodies, and voluntary bodies.  However, at this late stage in the year, because of budgetary constraints, pressures faced, and the commitments already provided, my Department's Non-Ring-Fenced Resource DEL Budget is fully committed and, as such, is not deemed discretionary.My Department's Earmarked Resource DEL Budget (£184.1m) provides for Executive and Treasury priorities. These priorities are not deemed discretionary, with any easements in Earmarked Resource DEL Budgets required to be returned to the Executive and Treasury respectively. Capital DELMy Department received a Capital DEL allocation of £270m, of which £150m was Earmarked Budget for inescapable Social Housing Development Programme costs, Flagship and City Deals projects which are not discretionary. As with Earmarked Resource, any easements on Earmarked Capital must be returned to the Executive.The £120m general net Capital DEL allocation met inescapable bids of £43m (net). I allocated a further £63m to the Social Housing Development Programme, £62m short of what was required, to allow for 1,000 new build homes to be started against a target of 2,000. The remaining £14m (5% of my total Capital budget) was allocated to Priority programmes across the Department.  Annually Managed Expenditure (AME)In addition to the budget provided by the Executive, my Department is also responsible for AME on behalf of HM Treasury. The Department’s AME allocation for 2025-26 totals £10.5bn. This primarily covers expenditure for social security and housing benefits which are demand-led services, which are generally less predictable than DEL expenditure. The AME benefit expenditure reflects need and, as such, is not classed as discretionary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the grounds under which Civil Service Inefficiency Compensation award criteria and decision making are applied.
answer_text: Compensation payments are provided for by Section 11 of the Rules of the Civil Service Compensation Scheme (Northern Ireland) and apply to dismissals for unsatisfactory attendance or performance.Decisions on the amount of compensation to be paid to civil servants are considered on a case-by-case basis against criteria outlined in the Civil Service Inefficiency Sickness Absence and Performance Management policies, and associated guidance.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount of their entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: The Executive Office’s budget is overwhelmingly non-discretionary, reflecting expenditure on statutory duties including payments to Victims, and Executive commitments. Discretionary spend is minimal and primarily relates to operational areas such as training and travel. Discretionary spend in 2025-26 is approx. £1.7million, which is 0.7% of TEO’s consolidated budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of his entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: My Department’s net DEL budget following June Monitoring is non-earmarked Resource £162.1m, earmarked Resource £79.5m and Capital £33.5m. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of his entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: The 2025-26 Budget settlement for my Department included allocations of £599.5m Resource DEL, £31.8m Ringfenced Resource DEL and £119.5m Capital DEL giving a total DEL budget of £750.8m.  The Resource DEL allocation included £332.5m (44.3% of total budget) of funding to support Agriculture, Agri-Environment, Fisheries and Rural Development and £4.1m (0.6% of total budget) for PEACEPLUS.  These Earmarked allocations are for Executive priorities and are therefore not deemed discretionary. The remaining £262.9m Resource DEL (35.0% of total budget) relates to the delivery of frontline services, statutory obligations and funding for Arm’s Length Bodies and is therefore not deemed discretionary. All £31.8m of Ringfenced Resource DEL (4.2% of total budget) was committed to fund the non-cash deprecation of departmental assets and is therefore not deemed discretionary.  The Capital DEL allocation of £119.5m (15.9% of total budget) excludes an opening overcommitment which is being managed down throughout the year to ensure that DAERA lives within its budget.  Therefore none of this funding is deemed discretionary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of his entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: The finance system within Health does not distinguish between discretionary and other types of spend therefore there has been an element of subjectivity applied in the calculating the discretionary spend.  It would not be feasible to look at individual transactions, and we have categorised some transactions as discretionary that may be subject to a contractual commitment and vice versa.  However, this represents our best assessment of the level of discretionary spending in 2024-25.  It should also be noted that it would not be possible for the organisation to function effectively should all discretionary spending be eliminated.  However, given the financial context the Department continues to seek to minimise such expenditure.The department and each of its ALBs have assessed their total discretionary spend in 2024-25 as £70 million, which is 0.84% of the total Resource budget for Health.The remaining spend relates to expenditure on staff costs and other statutory or contractual commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of her entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: It is estimated that around 95% of my Department’s resource budget is considered to be front-line service delivery, with the vast majority regulated, statutory, fixed or contractually obliged. In relation to the 2025-26 opening resource budget, this equates to approximately £605m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of her entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: In 2024-25, £6.2 million of my Department’s £1,337 million non-ringfenced Resource DEL budget was discretionary expenditure. This represented less than 0.5% of the total 2024-25 non-ringfenced Resource budget. The remaining 99.5% related to expenditure for staff costs and other statutory or contractual commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of his entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: To ensure consistency of responses I have adopted the same approach as I have used in my response to AQW 34785/22-27 in respect of what the member described as my Department’s discretionary spend.  In that response discretionary spend was understood, following clarification, to specifically refer to my Department’s budget, excluding any funding allocated to the Education Authority, schools or any of my Department’s other Non-Departmental Public Bodies.Please note that my Department does not operate a discretionary budget.  Instead, my Department draws a distinction between statutory and non-statutory expenditure.  While the costs included within the figures below are not statutory, they are considered either contractual, necessary to support statutory educational duties or to deliver Ministerial or Executive priorities.2025-26£000Total non-Statutory Budgets92,747Total Resource Budget     3,338,815 Total as a percentage of overall Resource Budget2.78%In addition to the above, my Department receives the following ring-fenced funding from the Executive, over which my Department has an element of discretion over how it is utilised. If this funding is not used for the intended ring-fenced purpose, the funding must be returned to the Executive via the Department of Finance.2025/26£000Early Learning and Childcare Strategy             34,645 SEN Transformation Funding               8,070

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of her entire consolidated budget that is discretionary spend, expressed as (i) monetary value; and (ii) percentage of total budget.
answer_text: My Department’s consolidated budget comprises of Resource DEL (RDEL), Capital DEL (CDEL), Financial Transactions Capital, Ringfenced RDEL and Annually Managed Expenditure.  Except for RDEL and CDEL, the other budget categories are designated for specific types of expenditure; therefore, this response focuses exclusively on RDEL and CDEL.The Department’s current RDEL allocation stands at £840 million and its CDEL allocation is £202 million, both fully committed to advancing the priorities outlined in my Economic Vision.Excluding Executive Earmarked funding, of the remaining £795 million RDEL funding is allocated as follows:approximately 70% flows to Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs) and Higher Education Institutions;over 20% supports student support and Education and Skills Programmes; and10% funds other contractual and statutory obligations including the Industrial and Fair Employment Tribunal, Trading Standards, Careers Service, Departmental Salaries, Public Service Obligation for City of Derry Airport, decarbonisation initiatives and the Good Jobs BillExcluding Executive Earmarked funding, of the remaining £153 million CDEL funding circa 87% flows to ALBs and Higher Education Institutions with the remainder supporting the delivery of capital programmes within my core Department.This breakdown demonstrates that all resources are directed toward statutory obligations and strategic priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Education Authority sets a uniform charge rate for school meals, or if individual schools have a role in setting these charges.
answer_text: There have been no increases in the price charged by the Education Authority (EA) for a school meal in the last three years. In all schools where the EA is responsible for the catering, it applies the same  price for a school meal, based on whether it is a nursery, primary or post-primary school. Boards of governors in voluntary grammar and grant maintained integrated schools are responsible for setting the meal price in their schools.
