answer_text: The table below shows the number of departmental fossil fuel and hybrid vehicles for each of the last three calendar years;      Calendar Year 2023 2024 2025Fossil Fuel Vehicles14109Hybrid111In addition, the Department owned four off road non-fossil fuel vehicles for use on the Stormont Estate in each of the last 3 years. Two of these vehicles were sold in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the social value delivered through the collaborative contracts for (a) Travel Management Services; and (b) End Point Security; and (ii) whether it has met the 10 per cent target, set in the Social Value Strategy 2025-2027.
answer_text: The procurement of the new Travel Management Services contract is underway and will be advertised by the end of January 2026. The tender will include 10% of the award criteria allocated to social value, 5% to deliver secure employment and skills and 5% to deliver climate action; meeting the target as set out in the Social Value Strategy 2025-2027.The End Point Security contract is due to be tendered via the Cabinet Office’s Crown Commercial Services Agreement, G-Cloud 14 in the next few weeks.  The 10% social value target to deliver climate action will be incorporated in accordance with the G-Cloud 14 Protocol.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the value of social value delivered through contract awards through the collaborative contracts for the Disposal of Hazardous and Clinical Waste and Active Directory Recovery and Management Tools; and (ii) whether it has met the 10 per cent target set in the Social Value Strategy 2025-2027
answer_text: My Department has commenced the procurement for the collaborative contract for the Collection and Disposal of Hazardous and Clinical Waste which is scheduled to be advertised in the coming weeks. The tender will include 10% of the award criteria allocated to social value to deliver climate action to meet the target set out in my Department’s Social Value Strategy 2025-2027. The Active Directory Recovery and Management Tools will be tendered in 2027 and will include 10% of the weighting allocated to the social value to deliver climate action to meet the target set out in the Social Value Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the role of Social Value Champion is a full time role or will be held alongside other duties of the Deputy Secretary of Contact Construction & Procurement Delivery.
answer_text: The Social Value Champion role is not a full time role and is held alongside the other duties of the Deputy Secretary of Construction and Procurement Delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will consider acquiring or occupying any new premises as part of his review of the Civil Service Office Estate.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the management and strategic planning of the NICS Office Estate, this includes ensuring the Office Estate meets the changing needs of the NICS and delivers on its energy efficiency targets. As part of its ambitious Office Estate Strategy, my Department is moving at pace to consolidate the office portfolio and reduce the estate footprint by 40% by 2028 which, to date, has included the divestment of 16 properties and will include the lease of one new location in the next financial year. The second stage of the Estate Strategy (2028-2035) will focus on the delivery of energy efficient, modern and welcoming working environments which may include further divestment and the acquiring or leasing of new premises as appropriate. All options will be assessed through the formal Business Case process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether medical staff and practices receive (i) targets; and (ii) payments, based upon the number of Covid-19 booster vaccinations administered.
answer_text: The COVID-19 vaccination programme is delivered by a number of providers which includes General Practitioners (GPs), Community Pharmacies and Trust clinics.  There are no set targets, but the public messaging encourages everyone eligible to take up the offer of vaccination when invited to do so.During the autumn/winter 2025/26 COVID-19 campaign GPs and community pharmacists are paid an ‘item of service’ fee of £7.54 for each COVID-19 vaccine they administer.  Trusts receive funding based on their cost requirements.There are additional financial arrangements in place to cover the costs of community pharmacies who visit a care home to offer vaccination to residents as well as costs associated with community pharmacies vaccinating in their own premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the need to review planning rules, to reflect the increase in Airbnb rentals and holiday homes.
answer_text: A cross-departmental working group (DfC, DfI and DfE) has recently been established to look at this issue.  The working group met in early October to finalise Terms of Reference to enable the Department for Communities to commission research. The purpose of the research is to provide a more informed understanding of the relative impact of short-term/holiday lets on housing supply. It is the current intention that a final research report will be available later next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will undertake a full (i) review; (ii) clearance; and (iii) investigation, into gullies and drains along (a) the A2 Annalong to Newcastle Road; (b) the Tullybrannigan Road, Newcastle; and (c) the Bryansford Road, Newcastle, following three instances of flooding in recent weeks.
answer_text: Flooding in relation to the incident on 19th October 2025 is suspected to be as a result of run off from the mountain. However, further to this flooding event, my Department has completed checks and undertaken cleaning of the road drainage systems in the Tullybrannigan area. The road drainage systems along all other locations mentioned above, including gullies and drainage lines, are scheduled to be cleaned shortly as part of the routine schedule. Should any issues arise during this action, they will be investigated and repaired subject to available resources.  My officials will also notify NI Water of any issues pertaining to the road gullies discharging into their systems if identified as part of the investigation process.The Department aims to inspect and clean if necessary, all gullies once annually with other known areas which are prone to flooding being attended to more often subject to available resources. In recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people not registered with an NHS dentist, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Business Services Organisation (BSO) publishes annual General Dental Services Statistics for Northern Ireland, available at https://bso.hscni.net/directorates/operations/family-practitioner-services/directorates-operations-family-practitioner-services-information-unit/general-dental-services-statistics/. This data includes the number of individuals currently registered with a Health Service dentist. While the number of people not registered with a dentist is not directly recorded, these statistics, used in conjunction with population data from the Northern Ireland Census, could be used to provide an estimate of the number of individuals not registered with a Health Service dentist. This figure however is heavily caveated due to various factors, including individuals accessing private dental care and population change since the last census. People can also access community and hospital dentistry via referral rather than registration, and they can also access emergency dental clinics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the higher proportion of public sector employees in Northern Ireland, compared with the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The latest figures from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) estimate that 26.4% of those in employment in the North, were employed by the public sector in June 2025. This compares to 24.1% in Wales, 22.3% in Scotland, and 17.2% in England.However, there are a number of issues which mean that such comparisons need to be treated with caution. The North’s level of relative need is higher, while some services in the public sector here are delivered by the private sector in Britain, for example, water utilities and some transport services. In addition, our size and rurality can make it more difficult to benefit from economies of scale in some areas of delivery. While our private sector is relatively smaller here when compared to England, Scotland and Wales.It is encouraging that private sector employment locally has grown by 10.3% over the past decade. This has exceeded the growth of local public sector employment (8.4%); outpaced the rates of private sector growth in Scotland (1.9%) and Wales (-1.5%); and on par with overall private sector growth levels in Britain overall (10.5%). Sustained growth in private sector jobs is a trajectory I hope will continue as the Executive takes forward its commitments in the delivery of our Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when improved signage will be provided at the junction at Ballyardle Crossroads, Kilkeel.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.I am pleased to confirm that the provision of new signage for the Ballyardle Crossroads in Kilkeel is included in the planned programme of work for 2025/26. Details of the planned work programme are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council As the precise timing of these works is subject to the availability of contractor resources, it is not possible to provide a definitive timeframe for the completion of this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance To ask the Finance Minister for his assessment of the impact of two pieces of correspondence relating to his Department being leaked to the media this week.
answer_text: ANSWERIt is unacceptable that documents that were shared with the Executive have been leaked. The Executive should have space to discuss issues in confidence. I understand the need for transparency and public interest in Executive business however it is essential we uphold the integrity of our processes and commentary on leaked Executive papers risks undermining due process and public trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the process of communication with local councils when roads are adopted, to ensure services commence appropriately; and (ii) for her assessment of how this has been applied in relation to (a) Granite Mews; and (b) Sawhill Park, Annalong, which were adopted in 2024.
answer_text: My department processes road adoptions for new development roads through powers granted under Article 8 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980. On completion of an adoption, officials write to their counterparts in the relevant local council, advising of the adoption and providing a map of the adopted area.  A list of new road adoptions is also included in my Department’s annual report to councils.The roads in Granite Mews, Annalong were adopted on 10 April 2024 and Newry, Mourne and Down District Council were duly notified.The roads in Sawhill Park, Annalong are not yet adopted as they are still within their one-year maintenance period.  The roads will become eligible for an adoption inspection on 17 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of people who paid their rates bill by (i) cash; and (ii) cheque, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: ANSWERLand & Property Services (LPS) does not accept direct cash payments. Ratepayers who wish to pay by cash can do so at Post Office counters or PayPoint outlets, where payments are processed on behalf of LPS.As these transactions are handled externally, LPS does not receive information on the specific payment method used by the ratepayer.The number of ratepayers who made payments by cheque over the past three years is shown in the table below. When interpreting these figures, it should be noted that ratepayers may use multiple payment methods within a rating year in addition to a cheque payment. The data reflects the number of ratepayers who made at least one cheque payment during the year, and a single cheque may cover rates for multiple properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current provision of the Travel Management Services Framework for (i) the Civil Service; (ii) Government Agencies; (ii) Non-departmental Public Bodies; and (iii) other participating bodies, including the total amount spent, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The Travel Management Services Framework is a single supplier collaborative arrangement currently delivered by Selective Travel Management. The Agreement provides a fully managed service for all travel within Britain and international travel, accommodation and any other associated services. The range of travel services cover as a minimum Air, Rail, Ferry, Car Hire, Taxi / onward road travel, Car parking, Accommodation and Hotel Conferencing. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of (i) voluntary; and (ii) community sector groups, involved in the delivery of his Department's Go ON NI programme.
answer_text: The Department’s Go ON NI programme is delivered in collaboration with Libraries NI and Supporting Communities. Libraries NI offers individual and group training sessions across its network of 96 libraries, providing access to free Wi-Fi and devices to participants. Supporting Communities provides basic digital skills courses, primarily targeting individuals and community groups, with a particular focus on older people and those in rural areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the breakdown of the 1,400 helped in this financial year, under his Department's Go ON NI programme, broken down by constituency area.
answer_text: Statistical information for the Go ON NI Programme is not available per constituency area, but it is available per local council area. The number of individuals assisted per local council area in this financial year is shown below.Council AreaNumber of IndividualsAntrim and Newtownabbey165Ards and North Down110Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon102Belfast City188Causeway Coast and Glens87Derry City and Strabane115Fermanagh and Omagh65Lisburn and Castlereagh City267Mid and East Antrim139Mid Ulster135Newry Mourne and Down39Total1,412

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how those in need are identified and targeted, under his Department's Go ON NI programme.
answer_text: The Go ON NI programme focuses outreach and promotional efforts on digitally excluded groups, including:People aged over 50;Individuals with disabilities;Socio-economically disadvantaged communities;Rural populations; andThe long-term unemployed.To reach these audiences, the Digital Inclusion Unit coordinates a range of activities and projects, forming strategic partnerships with organisations such as Libraries NI, Supporting Communities, and other government departments.The Unit also collaborates across the public, private, voluntary and community sectors through the Digital Assist Steering Group, which fosters joined up working and shared learning. This group brings together key stakeholders to improve digital skills across all levels, encouraging a more collaborative, coordinated, and innovative approach to addressing digital exclusion.Key engagement activities include:Attendance at local outreach events, as well as major events such as Belfast Mela and the Balmoral Show;Collaboration with Age Friendly Coordinators in each council area;Use of the Department’s social media channels to promote opportunities and share success stories; andCross-departmental working to align efforts and maximise impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to identify the cause of the recent severe flooding in Newcastle, as a key part of the process to progress a permanent solution to prevent further flooding in this area.
answer_text: Investigations to determine the cause of the flooding are ongoing, preliminary findings suggest the flooding may have been caused by surface water run-off from the Mourne Mountains after heavy overnight rain.My officials have inspected the forest area immediately behind Sunningdale and identified a number of drainage related maintenance issues.Forest Service has agreed to a site meeting with my officials in early November to discuss its ongoing repair works following the storm and how the water flow exiting the forest can best be accommodated by the drainage infrastructure during exceptional rainfall events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of confirmed cases of Asian hornets to date, broken down by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of reported cases of bovine TB, in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any apprenticeship schemes that are operating within his Department.
answer_text: The NICS ICT Apprenticeship Scheme is currently operating within my department. Additionally, the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) is running several other Apprenticeship Schemes. These include: Level 2 in Meat & Poultry Industry Skills;Level 2 & 3 in Agriculture, the Equine Industry, Horticulture (Pathways – Sports turf or Parks, Gardens and Green Spaces) and Food Industry Skills; Level 3 in Floristry; Level 5 Higher Level Apprenticeship in Food and Drink Manufacture; andLevel 6 Degree Apprenticeship in Food and Drink Manufacture.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any sectors and skillsets within his Departmental remit where there have been recruitment challenges.
answer_text: Following the decision to centralise all NICS Human Resource functions within the Department of Finance (DoF) in April 2017, DAERA no longer conducts its own staff recruitment competitions. The department is experiencing significant recruitment challenges in disciplines such as Veterinary, Forestry, Scientific Grades, and ICT.  The higher salaries offered by private practices or in other jurisdictions are making it difficult to attract candidates, especially for positions that require shift work. This issue is further compounded by the fact that the NICS is competing with private and public sector organisations to recruit/retain staff during a period of low unemployment.  To address these vacancies, the department is allocating staff to priority work areas, sometimes through temporary promotions, to ensure that its resources are concentrating on meeting our strategic objectives. It is also working closely with NICS HR to fill priority posts.It is important to note that the vacancies spread across responsibilities that fall under my purview and those that rest with the Defra Secretary of State since the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024.Given that DAERA is a significant employer of veterinary professionals, the department is collaborating closely with DoF to tackle the challenges related to recruiting and retaining veterinarians into DAERA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with dementia, by five year age brackets, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts do not routinely report on the number of people diagnosed with dementia. NISRA data reports that in 2024 there were 13,900 people diagnosed with dementia.The Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system, Encompass. As the Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, the data generated within the context of service delivery is subject to a process of extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data that Trusts are able to submit for reporting purposes.My Departmental officials have convened a regional Dementia Encompass Group that meet fortnightly. The key objective of the group will include a focus upon how to standardise and streamline data reporting across the region and how to make best use of data reported in respect of dementia to shape service improvements. The work of this group will also what dementia data is required across the region to ensure that dementia data can be collated and retrieved from Encompass and employed to assist in future commissioning activity.

Consumer Safety is a reserved matter sitting with the Department for Business and Trade and the Office for Product Safety and Standards. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the need to support community transport services and home to hospital schemes for access to healthcare in rural Northern Ireland
answer_text: The provision of community transport services and their funding is largely a matter for the Department for Infrastructure.Where my Department does have a statutory responsibility, however, is under Article 10 of the Health and Personal Social Services (Northern Ireland) Order 1972, where transport may be provided for patients with a medical need as identified by a clinician.As a general principle, patients are expected to make their own way to healthcare facilities, either using their own transport, or that of friends and relatives, or by using schemes that exist to help people who have transport difficulties. Where this is not possible, my Department can make arrangements for transport to be provided, as outlined in my Department’s Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland. These services are primarily delivered by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service through their Patient Care Service; however, there are a range of options open to Trusts, including the use of volunteer car drivers, private ambulance services and the use of taxis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Public Health Bill Consultation.
answer_text: An extensive public consultation on policy proposals to inform the development of a new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland took place last year. The Department received over 8,000 responses to the consultation. An extensive analysis of consultation responses has been carried out and feedback has been reviewed and considered. A summary report has now been prepared and will be published on the Department’s website in the coming weeks. MLA’s will be made aware of the publication when this becomes available. Taking into account the obvious level of public interest and the wide variety of issues highlighted in the public consultation response,  as well as the timescales required to address these adequately, I have taken the decision to instruct my officials to reengage with key stakeholders to refine and agree any policy proposals that will ensure any future legislation is fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any targets in place for early intervention measures for children with special educational needs.
answer_text: The recently refreshed Healthy Child, Healthy Future framework introduces several enhancements to the existing universal and targeted health visiting contacts regime.This includes new developmental assessments at 8–9 months, extended reviews at 15–18 and 27–30 months, and a structured joint pre-school and health contact at age 3+ which is intended to strengthen early identification of children with additional needs, including those with special educational needs (SEN), through enhanced developmental reviews and structured health visitor contacts.The Department of Education will also likely have its own targets given its wider responsibility regarding SEN provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his Department's latest estimate of (i) the total cost of the PSNI data breach; and (ii) the amount of this that will be paid in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: ANSWERThe business case submitted by the Department of Justice for the PSNI data breach business case detailed  the cost of the data breach incident could reach an estimated £119 million. The final quantum and timing of any payment will be dependent on the outcome of the ongoing mediation and legal processes relating to the compensation claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of planning applications referred to her Department, in the last three years.
answer_text: Section 29(1) of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 allows my Department to direct that an individual application or applications be referred to my Department instead of being dealt with by a council.  A similar referral power, in relation to applications for conservation area consent, exists under Section 88(1) of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 by virtue of 105(6) of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.In the period from 10 October 2022 to 10 October 2025, two applications for planning permission and one planning application for conservation area consent were referred (‘called in’) to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any viable alternative project to the Mourne Mountain Gateway Project within the Belfast Region and City Deal, has been identified and progressed to date.
answer_text: Officials in my Department are supporting Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in identifying an alternative project. Any replacement must first be considered and agreed by the Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD) partners before submission to the Department. While no formal deadline has been set, the BRCD partners expect any alternative to be deliverable within the 10-year timeframe of the Deal, signed in December 2021. While it is for BRCD partners to propose a new project, I am supportive of retaining the funding within the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area.  I am committed to supporting the Council and the wider BRCD partners to develop a project that will deliver sustained and inclusive economic growth for the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the need to upgrade the junction between Dromore Street and Newry Street, Rathfriland, following regular traffic accidents, including recent serious accidents.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.My Department receives information from PSNI on collisions involving personal injury.  Officials are currently in the process of reviewing the latest Road Traffic Collision data from the PSNI for accident cluster sites. If the Dromore Street and Newry Street junction is highlighted and a common causation factor can be identified, then effective road safety measures may be introduced, if appropriate. In the interim, officials will review this junction in relation to road safety to ensure that the existing signs and road markings are appropriate and comply with the Department’s current policy guidelines.I am also pleased to confirm the Department plans to undertake a resurfacing scheme on Newry Street further improving the condition and safety of the road. This work will be completed after the Council’s planned public realm works. I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year.Concerns regarding driver behaviour should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the risk of failure to deliver, under the Belfast Region City Deal, the project to develop Newry including the civic centre.
answer_text: The Newry City Deal project consists of four elements: the Civic and Regional Hub; the Theatre/Conference facility; public realm improvements; and an Office Development Grant. There has been significant progress on the scheme with planning approval granted; the Full Business Case approved; a preferred contractor identified; and the Office Development Grant launched. I understand that a Pre-Action Protocol Letter in relation to an application for Judicial Review has been received by Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in respect of the Civic and Regional Hub, which is not being funded through the Executive City Deal funding being administered by my Department. Council is currently seeking legal advice and considering its response. Therefore, it would not be appropriate to comment further at this stage, but my officials will continue to engage with Council and the Belfast Regional City Deal in the coming weeks to determine the impact on the Newry City Deal programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the need for a Trafficwatch NI camera to be installed on the busy Kilkeel to Hilltown Road, near Spelga Dam.
answer_text: My Department installs traffic cameras to monitor traffic flows, manage congestion and deal with incidents on the network. We currently have over 200 traffic cameras installed across the road network in the North. They are located in main urban areas such as Belfast, Newry and Lisburn. Traffic cameras are also located on motorways and main interurban routes and are used to provide driver messaging on the roadside variable message signs and set signals to control traffic speed and provide lane information.Regarding the B27 Moyad Road/Kilkeel Road running between Kilkeel and Hilltown, this is a rural B Class Road with no history of congestion and so the Spelga Dam location is not considered viable for the implementation of a traffic camera.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the operation of private parking enforcement firms in Northern Ireland, including any need for reform.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement, it has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether it would be equitable to members of the public to apply numerical limits to local councils, regarding the number of planning applications that may be called in.
answer_text: Section 31 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, requires a council to produce a scheme of delegation for operation within its area. This allows decision-making for local applications to be delegated to an appointed officer rather than the council. Each council can tailor its scheme to suit their local circumstances and administrative procedures.  In accordance with section 31(5) of the Act, a council may, if it thinks fit, decide to determine an application itself which would otherwise fall, under its scheme, to be determined by a person so appointed. Any such decision must include a statement of the reasons for which it has been taken. Any proposal to limit the number of planning applications that may be called in to a committee is a matter for each council to consider through its scheme of delegation. As part of my Department’s ongoing Planning Improvement Programme, my officials are working with planning authorities to help identify challenges, blockages and delays in the operational aspects of the planning process. There has been constructive and ongoing engagement with the local planning authorities, including observational visits to planning committee meetings to help inform opportunities for support, shared learning and best practice, with the aim of improving performance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether planning regulations and climate change requirements, in their current form, can be applied harmoniously or whether conflicts within them, highlight the need for reform.
answer_text: I recognise that the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (CCA) represents a decisive change in the North of Ireland’s approach to the climate emergency, requiring a step change across all sectors to achieve the ambitious targets within it. As Minister for Infrastructure I am committed to ensuring that the two-tier planning system plays a positive role in supporting the delivery of those targets. I recognise that the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (CCA) represents a decisive change in the North of Ireland’s approach to the climate emergency, requiring a step change across all sectors to achieve the ambitious targets within it. As Minister for Infrastructure I am committed to ensuring that the two-tier planning system plays a positive role in supporting the delivery of those targets. The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (2011 Act) is the primary planning legislation for the North of Ireland which provides the basis for the development planning, development management and enforcement systems as well as other planning functions. Importantly, under the 2011 Act, the Department must, in formulating policy do so with the objective of furthering sustainable development and promoting and improving well-being. In doing so it must also take account of policies and guidance issued by other Departments, and any other matter which appears to it to be relevant. Within this context I am satisfied that there is no conflict between planning legislation and climate change requirements. My Department will continue to ensure that planning policy aligns with climate change requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the works her Department has (ii) carried out; and (ii) has planned for the future, to link active travel targets with schools as they work to increase schemes like walk to school and cycle to school.
answer_text: My department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan which will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan to provide a firm basis for prioritising delivery of improved active travel infrastructure within our towns and cities.  Routes to key destinations, including schools, have been prioritised within the Plan. Officials are also developing a toolkit of measures for areas around schools, as part of updated design guidance, to encourage more people to consider walking, wheeling, and cycling for school journeys.Public consultation on the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan closed on 28 February 2025.  Officials are now considering responses to the consultation, and I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan by the end of the year.My department recognises behavioural change work as a key element to maximising the benefits of increased investment in active travel infrastructure. To this end my department works in partnership with schools on the delivery of a number of road safety and active travel programmes, such as Child Pedestrian Road Safety Training and the Cycling Proficiency Scheme.Additionally, my department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) jointly fund the Active School Travel programme which is delivered by the Walk Wheel Cycle Trust (formerly Sustrans). Schools apply to join the programme for an initial three-year period with the aim of encouraging children to walk, wheel or cycle to school.  Sixty schools joined the latest programme, which commenced in September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Domestic Abuse Incidents and Crimes Recorded by the PSNI bulletin, published in August 2025.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the cost of duplication of administrative tasks in arm's-length bodies across the public sector.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance (DoF) is not in a position to assess the cost of duplication of administrative tasks in Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs). However, DoF is currently engaging with other departments to gather information on the current ALB landscape, including the status of departmental ALB reviews, and any priority areas for reform.In parallel, the Department of Finance continues to offer a range of shared services, such as professional services and business support systems, to departments and ALBs, helping to reduce duplication and improve efficiency in public service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Civil Service staff who commenced employment in full-time office-based roles, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The number of Civil Service staff who commenced employment during each of the past three years, including those assigned to the Department for Communities' contract for the Department of Work and Pensions, is detailed below: 2024/251,8322023/247422022/231,414The Department of Finance does not hold information centrally on whether these are full-time office-based roles. Job roles in the Civil Service are either hybrid, office-based or mobile and it is up to each department to determine this, informed by the nature of the job role and business need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30566/22-27; and AQW 30567/22-27, to detail the number of people whose fees are paid for worked within the NI healthcare system, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: This information requested is not available. Universities do not have mechanisms in place which allow them to track employment destinations of graduates from healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department. In addition, when Health and Social Care Trusts make offers of employment they do not actively seek information on whether the individuals concerned have had their university tuition fees paid.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether he has a target or aim for reducing the cost of consultants and agency staff in the Civil Service.
answer_text: ANSWERAll departments are responsible for ensuring the engagement of external consultants is limited strictly to situations where their expertise is genuinely required. Individual departments must ensure that such appointments deliver demonstrable value for money and that the consultants’ knowledge and skills are applied effectively and appropriately.  The use of agency workers is a necessary way to manage some time bound aspects of temporary work and to provide maximum flexibility to support the business needs of the Civil Service and its departments. 										Whilst the utilisation and deployment of agency workers is the responsibility of individual Departments and is important to maintain the delivery of public service, the focus of Department of Finance remains on building a resilient and permanent workforce through strategic planning and ongoing recruitment efforts to reduce the necessity to engage temporary workforce solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail number of vacant posts in the Northern Ireland Civil Service, broken down by Department.
answer_text: ANSWERAs at the end of September 2025, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 5,451 vacancies across the Civil Service. The breakdown by Department is shown in the table below. Vacancies at Month End September 2025DepartmentNumber of vacanciesDAERA927DE137DfC2415DfE207DfI616DoF500DoH73DOJ449PPS52TEO75Grand Total5451Source: NICSHRWhen a department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. Therefore, details of the total number of vacant posts in each department is not held centrally by NICSHR or the wider Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the level of uptake of the Back in Business Rate Relief scheme, in the past year.
answer_text: ANSWERFigures on the Back in Business rate support scheme are published online on a quarterly basis on the Open Data portal. The figures include the number of applications and awards by District Council, with the latest figures at 30 September 2025. Between 30 September 2024 and 30 September 2025, the number of Back in Business awards increased from 18 to 60 awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the need to advertise Northern Ireland Civil Service staff vacancies, based outside of Belfast, in local advertising networks outside of HR Connect.
answer_text: The decision to advertise Civil Service staff vacancies, including those based outside of Belfast, in local advertising networks is the responsibility of the lead department for each competition. The relevant department is best placed to determine the most appropriate advertising channels based on the location and nature of the vacancy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Northern Ireland Civil Service staff currently working 40 per cent of their time in the office, under the new hybrid working guidelines.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the response provided to AQW 30815/22-27.The revised Hybrid Working policy came into effect on 1st September and will be fully operational across all departments, by 31st October 2025.Management information on the operation of hybrid working across the Civil Service will be available later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current number of temporary promotions within the Northern Ireland Civil Service, broken down by Department.
answer_text: Temporary promotions are designed primarily to meet business and management needs and can be for several different reasons, examples of which include covering sick absence, maternity leave, special exercises (time bound projects), and to fill vacancies.As at the end of September, a total of 3,488 NICS employees were on temporary promotion. NICS Temporary Promotions by Department at month end September 2025.NICS DEPARTMENTTP Headcount @ Sept-25DAERA517DE136DfC1,225DfE183DfI340DoF385DoH124DOJ446PPS54TEO78NICS Total3,488Source:  HRConnect & COMPASS. ME September 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment on whether the £4.06 billion value of student loans held in her Department’s Accounts is accurate, given the significant concerns and disclaimed audit opinion on the balance.
answer_text: The valuation of the student loan book recorded in the Department's 2023-24 financial statements was derived using a financial model that incorporates long-term assumptions in respect of borrowers’ future earnings, inflation, and interest rates. This represented the best estimate available at the time of reporting. Due to the complexities associated with modelling of this nature, and in light of the disclaimer issued, the Department is actively working with the Audit Office to improve assurance in this area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide details of any funding for the Belfast night bus as part of the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: Ending Violence Against Women and Girls requires a collaborative response from all of Government and society.  Everyone has a role to play in ensuring that women and girls not only feel safe, but are safe, wherever they live, work, learn and socialise.We are pleased to confirm that the night-time services pilot is funded through a collaborative funding model, of which £92.3k is being provided by TEO, emphasising the role of safe transport in sustainable EVAWG interventions.As we are all aware, Ending Violence Against Women and Girls is a Programme for Government priority. This cross-Executive funding to support this service, reflects our determination to work together across all departments to do everything we can to deal with this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30110/22-27, to detail the timescale of when road users will be given details of the solutions she has outlined.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closures of one lane on the Barnmeen Road, Rathfriland following the verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.  The temporary closure is in place to ensure the safety of the travelling public whilst officials consider a viable long term solution.  As soon as resources are available, the works will be completed and the road reopened to two lanes. Road users will be advised of road closures and reopenings through my Department’s TrafficWatch website and via signage in the vicinity of the works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any steps he is taking to ensure a balanced budget is delivered for 2025-26.
answer_text: The Executive is facing significant financial challenges to ensure a balanced budget is delivered for 2025-26.These financial pressures are a symptom of the continued underfunding of public services by the British Government.Nonetheless, each department has a responsibility to live within the budget agreed for it by the Executive.In my view, it is also vital that all our public sector workers are appropriately rewarded.Given the well-publicised pressures on the British Government’s finances, we can’t rely on significant additional funding being made available by Treasury this year. The Executive along with its partners must work together to find a solution – Ministers are committed to doing this. As the Executive works towards a multi-year budget which will set Resource spending for 2026-2029 and Capital spending for 2026-2030, it is important we all understand the conditions in which we operate and how these impact the options we have before us and the choices we might make.My Department has already started this conversation with other Executive departments and Arm’s Length Bodies, and more recently with MLA’s, by hosting a fiscal outlook briefing in the Long Gallery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the 2024-25 Tourism NI annual accounts will be completed, certified and published.
answer_text: Tourism NI’s current timetable with the Northern Ireland Audit Office allows for the certification of the annual accounts to take place in December 2025 with the plan of publishing the annual accounts on the Tourism NI website in January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what is meant by compromise agreements in the Tourism NI 2023-24 Annual Accounts, which are set out as £21,130 special payments in the Losses Statement.
answer_text: ‘Compromise Agreements’ in the Tourism NI 2023-24 Annual Accounts refers to an agreement reached and payments made to resolve an internal dispute.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the date on which the (a) Northern Ireland Hotel Federation for the delivery of the Co-Operative Marketing Fund; (b) Quality Grading Scheme; and (c) expenditure relating to the Certification Scheme, were paused by Tourism NI; and (ii) whether these schemes will be closed or reopened in future.
answer_text: (i) (a) Tourism NI paused the delivery of the Co-Operative marketing programme in September 2023. (i) (b) The voluntary Quality Grading Scheme for visitor accommodation was paused temporarily in November 2022.(i) (c) Fees were not levied for accommodation certification re-inspections conducted by Tourism NI between 19th March 2020 and 31st March 2022 in recognition of the impact of the Covid19-pandemic on the tourism sector. The Certification Fees waived for 2020/2021 totalled £13,884, and for 2021/2022 totalled £67,987 (combined total £81,871).(ii) (a) Tourism NI has currently no plans to reopen the fund. However, this may be reviewed in the future. (ii) (b) The visitor accommodation quality grading scheme resumed in August 2023 and continues to operate. (ii) (c) The visitor accommodation certification scheme continues to operate normally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the £60,184 of irregular expenditure, incurred by Tourism NI during 2023-24.
answer_text: The irregular expenditure of £60,184 in 2023-24 comprises three items:Deficit on the visitor accommodation grading scheme - £5,644 Deficit on the visitor accommodation certification scheme - £40,271The Co-operative Marketing Fund - £14,269 Visitor Accommodation Certification and Grading SchemesThe deficits on the two visitor accommodation schemes arose due to costs exceeding income generated. These deficits were met within Tourism NI’s 2023-24 budget allocation.My Department and Tourism NI are currently undertaking a comprehensive review of the costs of operating the two visitor accommodation schemes and the associated fees to be charged. Once revised fee structures are approved, the associated fee regulations will be updated. Co-Operative Marketing FundThe Co-operative Marketing Fund ensured that funds went into helping the tourism industry grow by enabling hotels and accommodation providers here to market their offer to tourists in the South and at home. It played a vital role as part of the Tourism Recovery Action Plan in helping businesses to rebuild after the pandemic. Expenditure on the Fund was classified as irregular as it was deemed that Tourism NI did not have the power to delegate the delivery of it to the NI Hotels Federation, a third party. All of the funding went towards supporting tourism businesses and this technical issue should not take away from the successful outcomes of the Fund. The Fund operated successfully until paused in 2023.  Over the period, where the Fund operated alongside Tourism NI’s marketing campaigns, visitor spend from the South rose from £60.9m in 2015 to an estimated £233m in 2022, a growth of 283%. Spend by domestic visitors rose from £219m in 2015 to £267m over the same period. The scheme also met its objective in driving up the return on Tourism NI’s marketing investment.  An independent post project evaluation of the campaigns in the Summer of 2022 showed a return of £148.76 per pound spent in the North and £217.04 per pound spent in the South.The value of irregular expenditure in 2023-24 was not deemed to be material in the context of Tourism NI’s financial statements as a whole. As a result, the audit opinion on Tourism NI’s 2023-24 accounts was not qualified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how she ensures regional balance in terms of tourism investment
answer_text: Our tourism industry supports over 70,000 jobs and nearly 6,000 businesses, with 70% of both being located outside of Belfast, demonstrating its ability as a sector to drive regional balance.My department’s 10 Year Tourism Vision and Action Plan is based on my four economic priorities including regional balance. Tourism NI’s draft Corporate Plan 2025-28 includes a destination-led approach through the development of local action plans based on community strengths and potential. These Destination Stewardship Plans will form the bedrock of its ‘regional balance’ approach and deliver inclusive, balanced sub-regional growth and help to address economic inactivity in all regions. Furthermore, as part of the Tourism Vision & Action Plan, another key action is the delivery of major multi-million-pound tourism projects under the City and Growth Deals Programme to create new tourism offerings across the North. This includes the Derry on the North Atlantic (DNA) Maritime Museum at Ebrington and significant enhancements to the Cuilcagh Lakelands Geopark in County Fermanagh. One of the KPIs for integrated marketing activity is “to ensure that 80% of the content featured is outside of Belfast”.  Seasonal marketing campaigns are planned accordingly to ensure regional balance and deliver on this target.  Tourism NI’s Experience & Industry Development programmes are also focused on supporting tourism businesses in the regions. The Embrace a Giant Spirit Portfolio comprises of our most innovative experiences and attractions who are engaged on a targeted development programme; 85% of the members are from outside the Belfast City Council area. Tourism NI also works with a range of strategic partners across International Travel Trade and Business Tourism to promote the North as a destination of choice for international visitors. As part of this work, they recruit and support businesses from across all regions to engage with international buyers. A programme of Familiarisation trips is delivered annually across the region. In 2024-25, 92% of these trips included regions outside of Belfast on the itinerary.  In the same year, 65% of our tourism businesses outside of Belfast were awarded places at events and platforms in international markets, providing them with the opportunity to engage directly with international operators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a breakdown of the £180 million savings achieved by the Health and Social Trusts reported between June and July 2025 reducing overcommitments of his Department in the outturn reports.
answer_text: You will be aware that following the agreed 2025-26 Final Budget, my department was facing a funding gap of £614.7 million.  In response my Department has established the Systems Financial Management Group (SFMG) to drive a series of efficiencies and savings to reduce as far as possible the financial deficit in this financial year and, in tandem, work towards reducing the cost of our system overall, making it affordable and sustainable going forward.  By July some £155.6 million had been realised from savings and cost containment with a further £25 million funding being received as an in-year allocation in June Monitoring.  A breakdown of this is provided in the table below:Movement of Funding Gap 2025-26£m£mOpening Funding Gap(614.7)Savings/Cost ContainmentTrusts Savings (low and medium impact)49.0Departmental and other ALB savings33.1Elective Care spend reduction73.5155.6In- Year FundingJune Monitoring25.025.0Funding Gap at 31st July (434.1)Trusts had delivered some £49 million savings through a combination of reduction in agency and locum costs, managed vacancies, a reduction in corporate and estates costs, management of growth and cost containment.The Departmental and other ALB savings have primarily resulted from review of central budgets within the core Department and Strategic Planning and Performance Group. Again this has been achieved through managed vacancies, reductions in demand led services, cost containment and reduced costs of new drug treatments.Within the 2025-26 Final Budget the Executive ringfenced £80 million funding to increase elective care capacity, in line with the finalised Programme for Government. They also provided some flexibility around this in that it could be redirected to meet other pressures within the HSC system.  While £6.5 million had already been committed, my officials advised me that along with the additional activity being driven forward with the ringfenced £85 million red flag/time critical waiting list funding and the new activity planned from the investment of the £50 million waiting list funding, this will exceed the 70,000 interventions promised to meet the 2025-26 PfG target. Based on this advice I give approval that this £73.5 million balance of the waiting list funding could be redirected to help address the significant funding gap.On top of this my Department received £25 million from June Monitoring.  This reduced the overall funding gap to at the end of July to some £434.1 million.Since July the Department received £87.6 million for pay pressures and a further £185.4 million through the Reserve Claim. Alongside these allocations, additional savings across the Department have reduced the remaining funding gap to £26.2 million. While it will remain considerably challenging to breakeven this year I will continue to make every effort to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20721/22-27, to ask the Minister to detail the amount spent on agency staff costs to date under the 2021 framework.
answer_text: ANSWERThe total expenditure for all Departments on the 2021 framework agreement for agency workers at 8 September 2025 was £418,341,444.60. Individual Departments are responsible for pay bill costs associated with agency workers deployment. Just over half that cost is associated with agency workers, contracted to the Department of Communities to deliver a contract for the Department of Work and Pensions in Whitehall.As at month ending August 2025, 2514 agency workers contracted to Department for Communities were assigned to roles to deliver the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) contract. The cost for this DWP agency worker recruitment since 2021 is £221m.  As the Department for Communities have a concordat with the Department for Work and Pensions to recover these costs this expense is  cost neutral and therefore are not recovered from the block grant.  The use of agency workers is a necessary way to manage some time bound aspects of temporary work and to provide maximum flexibility to support the business needs of the Civil Service and its departments. 										While agency workers help maintain service delivery, our focus remains on building a resilient and permanent workforce through strategic planning and ongoing recruitment efforts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Justice Minister's public statements that the removal of Education Authority transgender guidance could lead to open season for teachers to bully pupils.
answer_text: I wrote to the Minister of Justice (9 September) advising that I found the accusation that the removal of the Education Authority guidance ‘could lead to open season for teachers to bully pupils’, wholly unwarranted and deeply offensive to a profession that is committed to safeguarding and supporting all pupils. This remains the case. I will take this opportunity to reiterate my request that the Minister of Justice  withdraw the remarks and publicly apologise to teachers in Northern Ireland.
