question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) his target spend on agency staff costs for the current financial year; and (ii) how that aligns with the NICS People Strategy.
answer_text: Since each department manages its own temporary recruitment and agency worker costs, I do not set a target for agency staff costs. The NICS People Strategy remains central to our approach; while agency workers help maintain essential services, our primary commitment is to strengthen a resilient and permanent workforce through strategic planning and continuous recruitment aligned with the objectives of the NICS People Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether the £90 million in capital funding earmarked for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme may be reallocated to other 2025-26 capital projects which are capable of delivery during the financial year.
answer_text: It is a matter for DfI to determine the level of any reduced requirements from the £90 million in capital funding earmarked for the A5 Western Transport Corridor.  Any Executive-earmarked funding allocated in 2025-26 which is not required for the A5 should be returned to the centre for reallocation at the earliest opportunity. The reallocation of any reduced requirements will be a matter for the Executive to consider and agree upon as part of the in-year monitoring process. It is for individual departments to determine, when making bids as part of the in-year monitoring process, whether any resources allocated can be used within the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the timetable; and (ii) his expectations for the 2025 October monitoring round.
answer_text: The timing and scope of October Monitoring round is currently under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total value of the Immigration Health Surcharge Scheme, in each of the last five financial years.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the value of unpaid rates written off in the 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) the current value of rates arrears owed to his Department.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the responses provided for AQW 30220/22-27 & AQW 30378/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the process in place to monitor and report on the amount of time Civil Service staff are working in the office going forward, under the new requirements of at least two days per week.
answer_text: ANSWERThe revised Civil Service Hybrid Working Policy requires staff operating hybrid working to attend the workplace for a minimum of 40 per cent of their contracted working hours each week, which equates to at least two days for most full-time staff.Line managers are primarily responsible for ensuring that staff adhere to the workplace attendance requirement, taking account of individual circumstances, including any agreed alternate working patterns, or reasonable adjustments.To support the revised policy, a new case management system has been developed, which post policy implementation, will enable management information to be produced, including the number of staff operating hybrid working across departments, the number of in-office days and office location. The revised Hybrid Working Policy will be implemented across all departments from 1 September to 31 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail sick leave in the Civil Service broken down by (i) total cost; and (ii) average days absent, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The most recently published figures on sick leave in the Civil Service broken down by (i) total cost; and (ii) average days absent, can be found in the Key Facts table within The 2023/24 report. This report provides figures from 2019/20 to 2023/24. 2024/25 figures will be published in the Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail for how long he has extended the Civil Service’s membership of the Stonewall Diversity Champions Programme.
answer_text: Civil Service membership to the Stonewall Diversity Champions Programme has been renewed for one year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her plans to support and enhance the economic growth of the aerospace and defence sector.
answer_text: The British Government has an industrial strategy and the Department for the Economy has a sectoral plan for advanced manufacturing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the East Border Region, since she took office, in terms of potential future investments in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area.
answer_text: East Border Region has not approached me to meet since I took office. However, there has been extensive engagement between the Department, Invest NI and three northern council areas covered by East Border Region: Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon, Ards and North Down and Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in relation to the establishment of Local Economic Partnerships and how to grow the local economy more broadly. The Department, through Local Economic Partnerships will encourage collaboration between all council areas including those with a long history of collaboration through initiatives such as East Border Region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the expectations of the Belfast Region and City Deal Board with regards to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council's presentation of an alternative to the Mourne Gateway Project, including how developed the proposal presented needs to be to be deemed capable of being delivered within the City Deal period.
answer_text: The Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD) Board has agreed to give Newry Mourne and Down District Council (NMDDC) time and space to develop an alternative proposal to the Mourne Mountain Gateway project. An alternative gondola project in Kilbroney, which was being considered, will now not proceed. I have been advised that NMDDC is continuing to consider an alternative project and that significant engagement with key stakeholders is ongoing and that an alternative will be presented to the BRCD Board in the coming months.The Deal, which was signed in December 2021, aims to deliver a 10-year programme of investment. It is the expectation of the BRCD partners that any alternative option can demonstrate deliverability within that timeframe.The BRCD Board will have to agree the alternative use of the funding in the first instance. An Outline Business Case will then need to be developed and submitted to the Accountable Department, in this case, the Department for the Economy. Once agreed as representing value for money and meeting City Deals policy requirements, the proposal will be brought for agreement to the Delivery Board and the Executive, in line with the agreed City and Growth Deals Governance and Funding Arrangements.I will continue to keep the Executive updated on progress on the alternative use of the funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail a breakdown of the £806,000 awarded to gender identity services in July 2025; and (ii) whether it will be an annual recurring cost.
answer_text: The funding of £806k allows a Regional Lifespan Gender Service, alongside an appropriately resourced endocrine service, to be established within Belfast Trust, bringing together the existing CAMHS Knowing Our Identity (KOI) Team and the Adult Gender Service into one model of care, which would encompass both a child and adult service, under a single management structure. The enhanced service will adopt a holistic, multi-disciplinary integrated approach to assessing and responding to an individual’s needs in view of any co-presentations, and the range of complexities relating to gender identity development. The most appropriate clinical pathway in the best interests of the patient will be determined through an integrated multidisciplinary team (MDT) approach, fully involving the patient and their family if a child or young person is involved.The funding includes the creation of two senior Psychologist posts complements the multidisciplinary team, as well as a dedicated Service Manager for the Lifespan model. This role will include setting strategic direction, developing governance and policy underpinnings for the newly established service, implementing learning from the Cass Report, co-ordinating the final approach to the research element of the Cass recommendations, outlining the resource required, and working nationally to develop procedures to facilitate data collection. A breakdown of the staffing complement for the Lifespan and Endocrine services is shown below:Lifespan ServicePostBandWTEPsychologist8C1.0Service Manager8B1.0Practitioner8A1.0Psychologist8A1.0PractitionerB70.4Admin SupportB41.0Endocrine Service        PostBandWTEConsultant EndocrinologistConsultant1.0Medical SecretaryB40.5NurseB72.0Pharmacy technicianB50.6The business case submitted by SPPG, on 6 March 2025, which I approved in April, sought recurrent funding of £806,625 for 2025/26 onwards to establish the Regional Lifespan Gender Service alongside an appropriately resourced endocrine service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) support she has provided for the childcare sector, since taking office; and (ii) plans to provide more in future.
answer_text: I remain committed to supporting the childcare sector and ensuring my Department supports the Executive’s strategic priority to deliver affordable, accessible, high-quality early learning and childcare opportunities.i) Through my Department, I invest in skills development for those working within the childcare and childminding sectors. We currently facilitate a range of training and qualification pathways through Further and Higher Education institutions, as well as vocational routes including apprenticeships and traineeships. I have also allocated over £350,000 from my Skills Fund to support FE colleges in developing online accredited micro-courses focused on supporting individuals working with children with additional needs.  Further funding has also been provided for research into barriers to managerial recruitment within the sector. My Department also signposts existing business support services to the sector through the NI Business Info website. Looking ahead, a second Assured Skills Academy in Early Learning and Childcare has recently launched to support participants with the training needed to gain entry-level roles in the childcare sector. (ii)  The Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Research Report, which examines how best to support childcare providers from a business perspective, was published on my department’s website on 24th September and can be accessed at Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research. I have written to the Minister for Education, who has lead responsibility for the Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Action Plan 2025–26 along with the longer term ELC Strategy, to outline my response to the report’s recommendations. Recommendation 1 of the Research Report provides for the creation of an on-going childcare business advisor role or service. I have agreed with the Education Minister that my Department will take the operational lead on the design and delivery of this service. Its longer-term strategic ambitions will be overseen by the Early Learning & Childcare Task and Finish Group on which my Department is represented.  My officials are working to develop a costed proposal for this important service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail plans within his Department to increase training and awareness of ovarian cancer detection.
answer_text: To support early detection and improve awareness of red flag symptoms, the Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) has delivered monthly primary care cancer and palliative care education sessions since 2021. These sessions target GPs, nurses, pharmacists, and allied health professionals, and are delivered in partnership with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and Macmillan GP facilitators.A dedicated session on ovarian cancer was held in November 2022. All sessions are recorded and accessible via the NICaN website: https://nican.hscni.net/info-for-professionals/primary-care/primary-care-education-sessions/The NICaN Gynaecology Clinical Reference Group is currently reviewing the NICaN primary care guidance for suspected gynaecological cancers, which includes red flag referral criteria for ovarian cancer. Once the updated guidance is regionally agreed, it will be disseminated to all GPs and Trusts and made publicly available on the NICaN website.NICaN will schedule a further education session to ensure primary care professionals are informed of the latest updates and best practices for identifying red flag symptoms, including those associated with ovarian cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health,pursuant to AQW 27253/22-27, whether he considering alternative options to create provision for those under 50 to avail of screening, including provision of funding to charitable organisations like Action Cancer to support screening.
answer_text: As outlined in AQW 27253/22-27, the current age thresholds for population screening programmes are based on recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific advisory committee which advises the four nations, on all aspects of screening. The UK NSC regularly reviews its recommendations on screening as new research and evidence becomes available. When considering who to screen, the benefits of offering a screening programme are weighed up against the possible harms. The UK NSC only recommends screening when it has concluded that the evidence shows that the benefits of screening outweigh the potential harms, for each age cohort.  There is ongoing research to examine the benefits and harms of extending breast cancer screening to women aged 71-73 years of age and also 47-49 years of age (the age X trial). The first report from this trial is expected in 2026. My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC recommendations in relation to screening matters.Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any conditions attached to courses funded by (i) his Department; and (ii) its associated arm's-length bodies, requiring those funded to work within the Northern Ireland healthcare system for a period of time following qualification.
answer_text: There are currently no conditions attached to courses funded by my Department or its Arms-Length Bodies, requiring graduates to work within the Northern Ireland healthcare system following qualification. However, my officials are currently reviewing the existing funding arrangements for both commissioned, and non-commissioned, tertiary level courses. This review will support the development of proposals for a public consultation, which I aim to launch later this year. As part of this work, several options are under consideration, including models that incorporate a return on service obligation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how much (i) his Department; and (ii) associated arm's-length-bodies, have funded university tuition fees in each of the last three years, broken down by course funded.
answer_text: The information requested is set out in the table below.Academic Year Course2022/232023/242024/25Nursing & Midwifery£30,439,648.21£26,711,418.88£27,735,607.00Dietetics£625,968.00£608,359.97£596,550.00Occupational Therapy £1,281,062.06£1,273,298.17£1,369,043.32Physiotherapy £1,846,058.00£1,846,094.09£1,959,100.00Podiatry £493,772.00£479,484.00£479,408.00Radiotherapy£2,368,370.00£2,410,453.10£2,614,970.64Speech & Language Therapy£742,258.00£771,054.51£836,096.00Paramedicine£925,000.00£1,140,833.36£1,295,000.04Post-graduate Allied Health Professions (AHP) £45,000.16£45,277.21£45,140.04Medical & Dental£3,719,277.43£3,169,491.00£2,687,009.07Clinical Psychology£1,028,400.00£1,182,761.00£1,177,521.00Physician Assistants£523,919.37£667,310.15£700,561.36None of my Department’s Arm's-length Bodies fund university tuition fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to bring bowel cancer screening in line with the rest of the United Kingdom.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current success rates for women receiving breast cancer treatment, broken down by age category.
answer_text: Data held by the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) reports that there were 7,566 cases of female breast cancer diagnosed during 2018-2022 in Northern Ireland. On average this was 1,513 cases per year. The average number of cases of female breast cancer in 2018 -2022 is 1513. The average number of deaths per year in 2018-2022 is 310. The number of survivors at end of 2022 (25-year prevalence) is 18, 207.Data held on survival following a diagnosis of breast cancer (diagnosed 2013-2017) at one-year is 96% and at five-years is 83.1%.The survival characteristics at five-years (diagnosed between 2013-2017) is 91% for patients aged between 15-54 years and 69% for patients aged 75-99 years.  Survival for those diagnosed at Stage 1 (early) is 96% and Stage IV (late) is 19%. The full report on Female Breast Cancer 1993-2022 published by the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry in 2024 is available to read here; www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 28065/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the roll-out of encompass in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Encompass completed the programme’s core ‘go lives’ of the Epic system in the last two Trusts, Southern and Western, on 8 May 2025. Both Trusts have stabilised well, building on the lessons learned through earlier implementations and support from colleagues throughout HSC. The Trusts and the encompass programme continue to work to build on the excellent ‘personalisation’ levels across the Trusts, aiding efficient clinician working in the new system, on resolution of any issues arising and on the next steps to maximise the benefits of the transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for a routine breast cancer referral, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: It is important to note that the Department does not routinely collect data specifically categorised as “routine breast cancer referral.” Accordingly, we have interpreted your query to refer to breast assessments in cases of suspected breast cancer, and our response is framed on that basis.Following the implementation of a single regional waiting list for breast assessment services, waiting times are now reported at a regional level rather than by individual Health and Social Care Trusts. The most recent official statistics on median waiting times for urgent breast cancer referrals, broken down by Trust, were published in response to AQW 30046-22-27.Due to the necessary validation processes required to ensure the accuracy and completeness of the data, there is an inherent time lag in reporting. As such, data beyond March 2025 is not yet available. The next Cancer Waiting Time Statistics bulletin, covering the period from 1 April to 30 June 2025, is scheduled for publication on 2 October 2025.  My Department is working on plans to reduce waiting times in the short term with additional waiting list initiatives clinics and utilising Independent Sector capacity and in the long term with an additional £5m investment in core capacity of the HSC Trusts. I acknowledge that the current waiting times for red flag breast cancer are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. I do expect the position to improve in the coming weeks however.  Nevertheless, the current situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery.  I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of planned capital works in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In my response to your previous request for an update on the capital plans for Daisy Hill Hospital (DHH) and Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH) in June 2025 (AQW 28159/22-27 refers), I advised that the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) had been allocated £2 million of capital funding in 2025/26 for the Low Voltage electrical project at DHH to progress enhancements to electrical infrastructure.In addition, the Trust has been allocated £4.5 million for general capital projects, £2.8 million for backlog maintenance, £0.9 million for two GP improvement projects, £0.6 million for digital health, £0.5 million for imaging and diagnostic replacement equipment and £0.4 million for research and development. With regards future plans, following the announcement of the outcome of the 2025 UK wide Spending Review in June, the Department of Finance has commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30. My ability to commit funding for capital projects going forward, will depend on the Department of Health (DoH) capital allocation from the Budget process. The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the DoH capital investment plan which will set out the capital projects and programmes to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 29964/22-27, for an update on the progress of the sales of Northern Ireland Civil Service properties valued at £20.1 million and classed as assets held for sale at financial year end.
answer_text: ANSWERAt present, four of the five surplus office properties are on the market for sale: Clarence Court, Victoria Hall, 34 College Street, and Fermanagh House, all of which are in Belfast. With regard to 86 Main Street, Limavady, final preparations are being worked through before it is advertised for sale on the open market.Work is ongoing by officials in Properties Division and Land & Property Services and by the Department’s appointed Estate Agents with prospective bidders interested in the properties.Sales of buildings generates additional capital funding for reallocation to departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 29965/22-27, to detail the total amount (i) paid out under the Local Restrictions Support Scheme (LRSS) to date; (ii) paid out under LRSS deemed to be overpayments; (iii) paid out under LRSS deemed to be ineligible claims; and (iv) of overpayments or ineligible claims paid out under LRSS recovered to date.
answer_text: I can advise:The total amount paid out under the Local Restrictions Support Scheme (LRSS) to date - £319,995,109	The total amount deemed to be overpayments - £3,857,084	The total amount deemed to be ineligible claims - £5,383,188	£4,954,732 has been recovered. This is inclusive of £2,429,565 repaid by the recipient and £2,525,167 offset against entitlement to other grant schemes the recipient was eligible for but did not receive payment from.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 29965/22-27, for assessment of how much of the £4.444m owed to his Department in relation to the Localised Restrictions Support Scheme for over 361 days will be recovered.
answer_text: The current balance of overpayments stands at £4.15 million. The Department has provided recipients with a final opportunity to repay, including offers to repay over a period of time. The Department is assessing the feasibility of legal actions to recover these funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the value of rating debt written off by Land and Property Services in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has robust processes and procedures in place to reduce rating debt this includes progressing legal actions for recovery of unpaid rates for persistent non-payment.Debt is written off for a number of reasons including if companies have dissolved or are in administrative receivership. Writing off irrecoverable debts is only initiated after all appropriate LPS actions to recover the debt have been utilised and exhausted. In the 2024–25 financial year, a total of £1.9 billion in rates was collectable through the rating system. Of this, Land & Property Services (LPS) successfully collected over £1.8 billion from ratepayers and through the administration of rate relief. Less than 1%, approximately £18 million, was deemed uncollectable and written off this relates to both current and prior years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 29963/22-27, for an update on the status of, and plans for, Dundonald House.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department is progressing work in relation to Dundonald House and future plans will be announced once all the relevant information has been collected and an appropriate business case developed to allow due assessment of the viable options for the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on whether any parts of the A2 Kilkeel to Newcastle Road will be resurfaced or upgraded during 2025-26.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons for the continuation of long-term temporary traffic lights at Barnmeen Road, Rathfriland.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closures of one lane on the Barnmeen Road, Rathfriland following the verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.  The temporary closure is in place to ensure the safety of the travelling public whilst officials consider a viable long term solution.  As soon as the solution is identified and resources available, the works will be completed and the road reopened to two lanes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on works to repair and fully reopen the A2 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, following almost two years of the road being reduced to single lane traffic.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closures of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road Hilltown following the land/verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.Engineers are continuing to work towards a permanent and lasting solution for this location; however, the design must go through an rigorous approval process prior to any works commencing to ensure the proposal is fit for purpose.  In the interim, my officials are considering if a viable temporary solution could be implemented to allow the road to reopen to two way traffic.  As you will appreciate, the safety of the travelling public must be prioritised, and therefore any temporary solution must also be thoroughly assessed to ensure it is safe, cost effective and complies with the required standards. Due to the topography of the location, both a temporary and permanent solution have complex engineering difficulties which will take time to address.My Department is continuing to prioritise the reopening of the B27 Kilkeel Road to two-way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the management of the road collapse on the A2 Killowen Road at Cassey Water Bridge, Kilkeel, including details of (i) how the collapse was not detected during inspection; (ii) the management of the closure; and (iii) the communications between DfI Roads and Translink on the closure and associated bus routes.
answer_text: In accordance with National Highways Standards, bridges in my department are subject to an inspection regime, requiring cyclical inspections to be completed every 2 years and detailed Principal assessments every 6 years. In line with this regime, a cyclical inspection of the Cassey Water Bridge, Kilkeel was completed on 24 May 2024 and was deemed to be in good condition. However, a period of unprecedented rainfall in July 2025 caused erosive damage to the main piers of the bridge and the road was closed in the interests of public safety.Following the closure of the road, a diversionary route was put in place to maintain access for the local community and Translink were informed through my Department’s well established protocols to ensure continuity of local bus services in the area.  The closure was also publicised via social media and my Department’s TrafficWatchNI website.The essential repair work was prioritised that started on 2 August 2025 was completed on 13 August allowing the road to be reopened to traffic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail who approved the pay increase to Senior Civil Servants for 2024-25.
answer_text: ANSWERPay negotiations with the recognised Civil Service trade unions (i.e. NIPSA, FDA, POA, GMB and Unite) commenced in November 2024.Following negotiations, a 20-month Civil Service 2024-25 pay offer which applied to all civil servants, comprising progression plus 3% revalorisation of pay scales from 1 August 2024 and progression plus 6% revalorisation from 1 August 2025 was approved by the former Finance Minister in January 2025. .  The Civil Service 2024-25 pay offer was accepted by all trade unions and implementation of the pay award for all civil servants agreed on 26 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any evaluations completed on funding provided through the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Local Change Fund.
answer_text: The £1.4million Local Change Fund for 2025/26 supports grassroots organisations tackling violence against women and girls. It is delivered by 11 Councils and supports over 100 projects, benefitting over 48,000 participants in youth groups, sports clubs, schools, businesses, and the arts sector. The £1.2million Regional Change Fund is used to provide EVAWG programmes in schools, communities and workplaces led by eight expert organisations.The Momentum Fund supported 225 Council-led events, impacting over 7,300 people. Evaluation focussed on the need for community awareness, capacity building and collaboration. Early results showed an: 88% increased knowledge of VAWG;81% improved understanding of healthy relationships; and85% felt more confidence in helping EVAWG. This early evaluation shows a positive picture but is only a start. Although we are moving in the right direction, there is much more work to be done, and we remain committed to doing everything we can to create a society where women and girls are free from all forms of gender-based violence, abuse and harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) his assessment of the current status of Dundonald House; and (ii) the plans for Dundonald House.
answer_text: Dundonald House has been vacant of staff since June 2023 when it was closed to normal day to day operations due to a serious Health and Safety issue. The building has since been emptied of all furniture and IT equipment and will be secured until future options have been assessed. The Department of Finance is currently seeking a rates exemption. Dundonald House was given Category B+ Listed status on 26 March 2021.Surveys are being progressed to test and determine the condition of the structure and component materials of the building, which has passed its design lifespan. These will help inform future options which will have to take into account a range of factors including the listed status and location of the building, on the Stormont Estate, as well as projected costs. Once surveys have been completed, options will be developed and assessed through the appropriate business case process and a decision made on the future for Dundonald House.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the properties and value held in the Accounts disclosed as Assets Held for Sale at 31 March 2025 totalling £24.8 million, classed as expected to be sold within the next 12 months.
answer_text: The properties and value held in the Accounts disclosed as Assets Held for Sale at 31 March 2025 totalled £20.1 million. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail why there is over £5 million owed to the Department of Finance at 31 March 2025 aged at over 361 days and increasing from the prior year position.
answer_text: This figure includes a range of debts, the majority of which relates to overpayment or ineligible claims under the Localised Restrictions Support Scheme (LRSS) which provided financial support during the pandemic equating to approximately £4.444m. The remaining £0.569m of debt represents amounts due from customers (mainly other departments or public bodies) for services provided by the Department. The Department continues to take all appropriate steps to recover outstanding debt.The variance on the previous year is mainly due to timing differences of payments around year end.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the nature and reason for the Department of Finance writing off £460,000 of monies owed to them during 2024/25 as uncollectable, bringing the total to over £1 million written off in the last two financial years.
answer_text: The table below summarises the amounts written off in the 2023-24 and 2024-25 financial years by my Department.CategoryNature2023-242024-25Cumulative Cost1Losses arising from overpayments of social security benefits, grants, subsidies.167,92715,800183,7272Individual waived or abandoned claims (Rates)432,190442,626874,8163Individual waived or abandoned claims01,7361,736600,117460,1621,060,279The reason for each of the category of write off is as follows - Write-off of unrecoverable Grants.Write-offs relating to legal fees and costs written-off (waived) in respect of ratepayers, in particular process stamp fees court costs, decree costs, notice of intention costs and enforcement of judgements court costs.Write-off of bad debt in relation to Professional Fees.

answer_text: A breakdown of the (i) number and (ii) value of legal settlements made by this Department for (a) legal settlements for staff complaints cases and (c) other, can only be provided at disproportionate costs.In relation to Health and Social Care Trusts, (a) legal settlements for staff complaints - information is set out in the table below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current value of Donated Assets held on the Department’s Asset register and disclosed in the 2024-25 Departmental Accounts.
answer_text: The value of Donated Assets held on the Department’s Asset Register and disclosed in the 2024-25 Departmental Accounts at 31 March 2025 is £1.402m.
