question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of internal control in the Department of Finance following Internal Audit reporting four final limited opinion reports during 2024/25 in relation to (i) Grants Advisory; (ii) Travel and Subsistence; (iii) Inefficiency Compensation Payments; and (iv) McCloud Implementation, in addition to a follow-up audit of a previous limited opinion which remained limited, Departmental Solicitors – Litigation, and the remaining two limited opinion reports, Occupational Health Services and Information Assurance within Digital Shared Services, from previous yea
answer_text: ANSWERMy assessment of the internal control in my Department is aligned to that of the Head of Internal Audit, who based on all the audit work performed, provided an opinion that the Department has established and maintained “satisfactory” governance, risk management and control processes during 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the compliance with summer recess deadlines for financial reporting across the public sector.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) is responsible for laying the Annual Report and Accounts (ARA) of the nine ministerial departments, four non-ministerial departments, three departmental pension schemes and one Trust Statement.  Of the seventeen sets of ARA to be laid for 2024-25, eleven have been laid prior to the Assembly’s summer recess with the remaining six expected to be laid by the legislative deadline.  In respect of five of these remaining entities, DoF had not expected to receive and lay their 2024-25 ARA before summer recess. This was due to the timing of the audits, which were not due to commence until July at the earliest, and their ARA not expected to be laid until the autumn. For the other entity, it is anticipated that their ARA will be laid during the summer. DoF officials remain in regular contact with each of these entities.Whilst the DoF is not responsible for laying the ARA of executive agencies, it notes that all nine agencies have laid their 2024-25 ARA by the summer recess deadline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the status of Kilkeel Medical Practice GP management.
answer_text: Kilkeel Medical Practice is still under the management of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust. This is in place until 31 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of where there is potential to increase use of Financial Transaction Capital in the public sector.
answer_text: It is for each individual Minister to bring forward suitable proposals that make use of the available Financial Transactions Capital and to make bids for funding at the appropriate point. However, I would encourage all Ministers to consider whether there are further opportunities for the use of Financial Transactions Capital within their respective departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the potential to increase the use of Financial Transaction Capital within the work of his Department.
answer_text: The nature of the Health & Social Care sector means that there is limited scope to deploy Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) funding.Previously, a GP Loan scheme using FTC funding had been administered by the Health and Social Care Board over the period 2015 to2017. Although £20 million of FTC funding had been available, due to insufficient demand and the timescales required to secure planning permission, only 3 loans totalling £1.25 million were issued over the period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which services are operating fluidly across the Southern and South Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts' boundary.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts have arrangements in place to ensure optimum cross boundary community and acute care. If a patient receives acute care for example in a Trust other than where they are resident, community care if required will be arranged with their home Trust prior to discharge. Some patients may receive community care outside of their home Trust and arrangements are agreed between Trusts for these patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the reporting requirements of the Independent Autism Reviewer; and (ii) where these outputs are published.
answer_text: As detailed in section 3C of the Autism Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, the Reviewer must issue a report annually on the exercise of their functions and this report will be laid before the Assembly by my Department. The Reviewer may also issue additional reports.I understand that work is also being finalised on a website for the Reviewer and the intention will be to publish relevant material outlining the work and activity of the Reviewer on an ongoing basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the potential of funding through October Monitoring in 2025.
answer_text: A monitoring round in the Autumn is likely but will only proceed if funding is available or if there are emerging issues that need to be considered. In terms of potential funding, any changes to Whitehall department budgets result in Barnett consequentials for the Executive, and it is anticipated that these would be provided as part of the Autumn Budget. Additional resources may also become available from departmental reduced requirements, but these will not be confirmed until monitoring returns have been submitted.Given this uncertainty, it is imperative that departments take action now to live within their existing budget allocations, as set out in the June monitoring round.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the fact that in 2024-25, two NISRA publications were revised due to major corrections.
answer_text: ANSWERThe NISRA Business Plan 2024/25 contains a measure “Number of official statistics releases with corrections (including major corrections and minor errors)”. In this context, NISRA defines a major correction as:“one which affects the utility of key statistics in the publication e.g. changes the direction of travel of a trend, results in a non-trivial change to key figures, or changes a substantial proportion of the statistical output or publication (due to the extent of the impact on users).”This definition applies even if the publication is relatively low profile as was the case in one of the revisions or if the error is corrected quickly as was the case in the second revision. For statistics to support decision making, they must be trustworthy, of high quality and valuable. This means correcting errors as soon as possible after they are discovered, and being open about the scale, nature, cause and impact. These requirements are embedded within the Code of Practice for Statistics, which NISRA is rigorous about implementing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the total value; and (ii) number, of incidences of financial losses reported by NISRA in 2024-25
answer_text: During the 2024-25 financial year, NISRA reported two financial losses to the Department of Finance, with a combined total value of £1,554. These related to foreign exchange rate differentials incurred during the purchase of software licences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the three breaches of the Code of Practice related to NISRA statistics reported to the Office for Statistics Regulation in 2024-25.
answer_text: ANSWERNISRA takes a rigorous approach to adherence to the Code of Practice for Statistics. This includes being open and transparent when it falls short of the high standards of the Code.For statistics to support decision making, they must be trustworthy, of high quality and valuable. This means being open about issues when they occur and ensuring the appropriate learning is put into practice. These requirements are embedded within the Code of Practice for Statistics, which NISRA is rigorous about implementing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the crumbling portions of Newry Street, Rathfriland, will be resurfaced or substantially repaired.
answer_text: My officials have advised that they are aware of the condition of Newry Street, Rathfriland and have planned to undertake a resurfacing scheme. However, in order to ensure best use of public expenditure and to increase the effectiveness of the scheme, they are awaiting the completion of Armagh Banbridge and Craigavon Council’s public realm works in the vicinity. Officials are advised that these works are expected to start September 2025. In the meantime, my department plans to carry out large scale patching on Newry Street, until we can progress with the full resurfacing scheme. The patching works are to be undertaken during the summer months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on works to reopen Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, to two lanes, which have now been closed for some 20 months.
answer_text: I recognise the continued inconvenience and frustration that has been caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the Kilkeel Road, however, it is important to stress that this restriction has been kept in place to ensure driver safety by keeping traffic away from the unstable road edge.My Department has appointed specialist structural and geotechnical engineers who have completed ground investigations of the embankment and are using this information to develop a permanent solution. Once a design solution has been developed it will go through an approval process before works can begin.I can confirm that officials are also in discussions with the specialist geotechnical engineer to consider if a viable temporary solution could be introduced to facilitate the reopening of two lanes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when the plan on how and when the £200 million funding to the PSNI will be allocated will be made available.
answer_text: Earlier this year Department of Justice (DoJ) submitted the PSNI Workforce Recovery business case to DoF for consideration. The preferred option identified in the business case is to increase PSNI officer numbers to 7,000 FTEs and that police staff be increased to 2,572 FTEs. It is estimated that this will cost £206 million. To fund the first year of the Workforce Recovery programme, I along with my Executive colleagues have provided DoJ a commitment, that the PSNI will be given the first call for an additional £7 million of funding in future monitoring rounds. I will be recommending to my Executive colleagues that the PSNI Workforce Recovery Plan is funded in full as part of the next three-year budget cycle. The delivery of the PSNI Workforce Recovery Plan for future years will be considered by the Executive, as part of the Budget 2026-29 process.  An information gathering exercise for this was commissioned on 30 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the need to introduce an equivalent of the Tell Us Once service to Northern Ireland to help reduce administration for those bereaved.
answer_text: I recognise the difficult circumstances faced by bereaved families and the important role that a scheme similar to the Tell Us Once service could play in helping to reduce the administration on them following a death.  To investigate how a similar scheme could be taken forward here my officials are engaging with Department for Communities to understand the scope of any potential service and the range of issues such as legislation, technical and resource matters that would need to be addressed for this work to be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the expenditure under the current contract for the Provision of Independent Assurance Reviewer for GovAssure, including the (i) term of the contract; (ii) the initial value of the contract at the time of award; and (iii) total expenditure to date on the contract, including the base contract value and the total of the call-off costs.
answer_text: My department provides a range of shared services, one of which is the provision of cyber security services to departments. GovAssure is a cyber security assurance scheme, launched in April 2023 by the Cabinet Office Government Security Group. It is designed to assess the cyber resilience of critical systems across government departments and public sector organisations. The Department of Finance put in place a contract commencing on 19 May 2025 for an initial period of 2 years, with the option to extend for a further 2 years. The value of the contract at the time of award is £1,200,000. Total expenditure to date has been £25,292.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how much of the £45 million Regional Balance fund has been used to date.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the consistency of the death and stillbirth registration process across local councils.
answer_text: ANSWERProcesses for the registration of deaths and still-births are set out in legislation, specifically the Births and Deaths Registration (Northern Ireland) Order 1976 and the Civil Registration Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 and registration office staff carry out their duties in line with these processes.	Registration staff are employees of the local councils and work under council line management to provide the registration service, on the authority of Registrar General, ensuring there is a consistent service across all council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) amount; and (ii) total value, of rates bills paid in 2025 via the Early Payment Discount allowance.
answer_text: The (i) amount; and (ii) total value, of rates bills paid in 2024/25 via the Early Payment Discount allowance by domestic ratepayers is detailed below:Rating Year2024/25(i) Amount of Early Payment Discount£8,677,141(ii) Value of rate bills paid£209,489,331It should be noted that, in some cases, the amount collected may include payments for rates assessed from previous rating years. These figures relate to Domestic properties only (mixed use properties have been excluded).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he expects to receive clarity on the £360 million announcement for a Fishing and Coastal Growth Fund.
answer_text: The Fishing and Coastal Growth Fund was announced by the UK Government without any prior discussions with the devolved governments.  Other than securing confirmation that the scheme covers the United Kingdom, rather than Great Britain as indicated in the press release on 19 May, details regarding the shape, structure and delivery of the £360m Fishing and Coastal Growth Fund have not been provided to date. I have written to the Defra Minister, Daniel Zeichner, to emphasise the need for Northern Ireland to secure an equitable share of the UK Fund and to seek an assurance that my officials will be fully and meaningfully involved in all aspects of its design, delivery and promotion. This is to ensure the needs and circumstances of our stakeholders are known to Defra and help in maximising the benefits from the available support for Northern Ireland.My officials, along with those from the other devolved governments, are meeting regularly with DEFRA officials to be informed of any progress made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he plans to bring forward the first muti-year budget plan.
answer_text: My officials are currently supporting departments to develop their five-year budget plans.  Following the Spending Review announcement on 11 June, a multi-year budget information gathering exercise will be commissioning shortly.This information gathering exercise, along with departments 5-year budget plans, will inform recommendations that I will bring to the Executive in the Autumn on a multi-year budget covering a three-year period for Resource and four-years for Capital in line with the Spending Review.Subject to Executive agreement, I intend to publish a draft multi-year budget, and following a full 12-week consultation, seek Executive agreement for a final multi-year budget in advance of the new financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the app showing the profile of coastal erosion in Northern Ireland will be made available to the public.
answer_text: My Department has commissioned research projects with Ulster University to address evidence gaps on how the Northern Ireland coastline is changing.  These projects have produced a Historic Shoreline Analysis tool and a Coastal Change Information tool, which are publicly available on the Northern Ireland Coastal Observatory website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide details of the Financial Transactions Capital arrangements for the £50 million UK Government funding announced for the Casement Park project.
answer_text: The announcement around Casement Park, as part of the Spending Review on 11 June, marked a significant step forward in the redevelopment of Casement Park, with the British Government joining the Executive, the Irish Government and the GAA in the commitment of funding. As part of the Spending Review a commitment was made by the British Government that the Executive will receive £50 million Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) for Casement Park.  FTC can only be used as a loan to, or equity investment in, a private sector entity. This additional £50 million FTC does not need to be repaid to the Treasury. I have written to the Communities Minister seeking a meeting with him and, advising him that my officials will engage with Department for Communities officials, along with any other officials as necessary, to work through the use of FTC to fund this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the change in funding for agriculture from the Comprehensive Spending Review.
answer_text: Although there is a reduction in the Barnett Consequential relating to Environment, Food and Rural Affairs of £1.3m / £2.4m / £5.1m over the three years to 2028-29, this is netted off the other increased and decreased Barnett Consequentials to provide an overall total amount of funding available to the Executive to allocate over this period.  There is no direct change in the funding for agriculture, agri environment and rural affairs from the Spending Review which remains the same as in 2025-26 at £329.4m Resource DEL.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will be changing policy on cervical screening from July 2025 so that younger women aged 25 to 49 who test negative for HPV will be invited for screening every five years rather than every three years.
answer_text: In line with a UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) recommendation and the Northern Ireland Screening Committee’s endorsement, the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme will extend the screening intervals from 3 to 5 years for women aged 25 to 49 years old, who test HPV negative as part of their routine screen, once the supporting IT infrastructure is in place.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers, to outline any discussions it has had in relation to a review of the use of toilet facilities in Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the importance of notifying local residents if a child sex offender is living in their area.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what is included in the Terms of Reference in the current review of arms'-length bodies across all Departments.
answer_text: ANSWERIt is for sponsoring Departments to take forward assessments of their relationships with Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs), with the preparation in each Department of an Action Plan for the review and reform of their ALBs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the commitment made in the 2025 Public Procurement Policy Statement Principle 2: Efficiency and Effectiveness, that regular progress reports on Major Capital Projects will be provided to the Northern Ireland Civil Service Board, whether these reports will include details of the scale of the overspends and time overruns for such projects.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Civil Service Board agreed the format and process for reporting on major capital projects at its meeting in May 2025. The agreed six monthly reporting will provide the Civil Service Board with visibility of time and cost overruns for these projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the process of how the requirements of the 2025 Public Procurement Policy Statement Principle 3: Social Value will be monitored, to ensure that what is committed to during the initial bid is delivered during the contract delivery.
answer_text: There are six social value objectives outlined in the Public Procurement Policy Statement. The Social Value Principle: Objective 1 (SVP1) is linked to where social value is included as a scored criteria or as a condition of contract. The implementation of this objective is monitored through a central social value monitoring system which tracks the social value commitments outlined in the supplier’s tender. Suppliers are required to update the monitoring system at intervals set out in their contract.  Reports are issued to contract managers at regular intervals to ensure the supplier is delivering what they committed to in their tender.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the 2025 Public Procurement Policy Statement Principle 1: Accessibility, whether there will be increased accessibility for local small businesses on local contracts where possible, rather than bids being set out on large regional basis.
answer_text: The Accessibility Principle Objective 2: (AP2) within the Public Procurement Policy Statement requires that barriers for SMEs, Micro Businesses and Social Enterprises are reduced. The action required to achieve this Objective is that ‘Departments, or their respective CoPEs, must consider potential barriers for SMEs when structuring their procurement strategies’. This should include consideration of market participation and the benefits of structuring larger contracts into smaller lots to encourage small business participation.	The thresholds contained within Procurement Policy Note 04/21 – Procurement Control Limits have also been updated from 6 March 2025 to allow Departments more flexibility when seeking to award contracts to small businesses and third sector organisations. 	The threshold at which Departments should conduct a tender exercise has been revised from £30,000 to £50,000 (£65,000 for Utilities) allowing Departments to seek quotations from local small businesses below the £50,000 threshold.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the 2025 Public Procurement Policy Statement Principle 1: Accessibility, whether there will be increased provision of up-front funding to suppliers, rather than small businesses and charities being required to be significantly out of pocket and then recoup the funding at a later stage.
answer_text: The Accessibility Principle Objective 2: (AP2) within Public Procurement Policy Statement requires that barriers for SMEs, Micro Businesses and Social Enterprises are reduced. The action required to achieve this Objective is that ‘Departments, or their respective CoPEs, must consider potential barriers for SMEs when structuring their procurement strategies. Cashflow is also a key consideration for SMEs. Contracts must be structured to ensure appropriate cash flow in return for tangible benefits’. This focus on cashflow should avoid suppliers being out of pocket for significant periods of time. The Accessibility Principle Objective 3 (AP3) also requires suppliers to be paid on time and within a maximum period of 30 days from receipt of an invoice. The process of up-front or advance payments is not included within the Public Procurement Policy Statement, however the process for this is outlined in Managing Public Money Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of: (i) the need for a review of the level of uptake of the hybrid working policy in the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS); and (ii) the need to monitor it against feedback from public service users, given the absence of detail on this in the NICS People Strategy’s plan.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Civil Service Hybrid Working Policy is currently under review and includes consideration of a minimum workplace attendance requirement and the provision of improved management information on the uptake of hybrid working through enhanced IT functionality.The Civil Service People Strategy contains a key commitment regarding the completion of a HR policy renewal programme, which aims to modernise Civil Service people policies to ensure they are user-centric and have a positive impact on employee experience. The People Strategy’s two-year road map includes the Hybrid Working policy as one of the key policies to be reviewed as part of the HR policy renewal programme.The Civil Service monitors the delivery of public services through Departmental Business plans including delivery targets, key outputs and evaluation of service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his plans to set out a fair total reward approach in a new Pay and Reward Strategy, as included in the new Northern Ireland Civil Service People Strategy.
answer_text: As part of the People Strategy 2025–2030, the Department of Finance is shaping a new Pay and Reward Strategy designed to deliver a fair and forward-looking total reward approach. This Strategy will support our ability to attract and retain staff with valuable skills, champion fairness and equity, and ensure long-term financial sustainability. Following feedback from the Civil Service Board, the draft Strategy is being refined ahead of my review and subsequent consultation with our recognised civil service unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, as part of the plan in the new Northern Ireland Civil Service People Strategy to simplify and streamline the recruitment process, to detail whether this will include an assessment of current barriers to accessibility experienced by applicants in certain demographics.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Civil Service People Strategy 2025–2030 sets out the vision for building a modern, inclusive, and high-performing Civil Service.  Key commitments in the Strategy include: 	improving, simplifying and speeding up the recruitment process ensuring a modernised and streamlined approach; and	completing a HR policy renewal programme to modernise people policies to ensure they are user-centric and have a positive impact on the employee experience.As part of this work, my officials are engaging with a range of stakeholders, including applicants and staff, to identify barriers that may be experienced by applicants in the recruitment process. This engagement will inform improvements to our recruitment approach including the development of new recruitment and selection methods. Key areas of policy work being progressed as part of the HR policy renewal programme which have the potential to address barriers experienced by applicants in certain demographics in the recruitment process, include, reviews of the Civil Service Recruitment policy, the Competency Framework (which will include consideration of the introduction of success profiles focusing on identifying a candidate’s strengths, talents, and abilities) and the Reasonable Adjustment Policy; the implementation of a new Ring-Fencing Policy for disabled applicants; and participation in employability initiatives such JobStart to expand the entry routes into the Civil Service and support the diversification of our workforce.Our goal is to ensure that the Civil Service workforce is representative of the society it serves and that everyone has a fair and equitable opportunity to join and thrive in the Civil Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, as part of the plan in the Northern Ireland Civil Service People Strategy to complete a review of Civil Service professions, to detail whether such a review will include an audit of all skills, experiences and talents of the existing NICS workforce to identify those currently employed in roles which are not the most suited to their skills.
answer_text: The Civil Service is introducing a new corporate approach to Strategic Workforce Planning (which includes skills gap analysis) supported by workforce planning toolkits. As part of the review of the Civil Service Professions, a new Framework for Professions will, over time, expand the role of Heads of Profession in relation to Strategic Workforce Planning.  A new Workforce Model is being developed which will help align roles with employees’ skills and experience. The new Model will define clear job families, categories, and career frameworks which will support more effective skills matching and help civil servants thrive in roles and careers that reflect their potential. A staff skills audit is not currently planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, as part of the plan in the new Northern Ireland Civil Service People Strategy to improve digital skills, to detail what plans are in place to roll out IT hubs or interfaces to assist members of the public with using the predominantly electronic and online public service systems.
answer_text: ANSWERThe implementation of the Civil Service People Strategy seeks to build a resilient and agile Civil Service that can consistently deliver outstanding public services and will support colleagues to adjust and adapt to the changing needs of the public.The strategy recognises technological innovation in transforming public, private and third sector delivery and the need to leverage these advancements to enhance efficiency, effectiveness and employee engagement. It includes initiatives to enhance digital skills among the civil service workforce, supporting colleagues to best utilise the tools and platforms available to them as well as emerging technologies.The Digital Inclusion Unit, within my Department, promotes Digital Inclusion in response to the increased use of online channels, including those utilised to access public services. The Unit’s current Go ON NI programme assists citizens to attain the necessary digital skills to safely and confidently use online services, and continues to grow by targeting events, digital skills training sessions and promotional activities at the harder to reach digitally excluded sections of the population.The GO ON NI programme provides free digital training sessions in libraries and community centres/hubs. These digital skills sessions introduce digital technology to people who are not familiar with going online and support people who want to improve their online skills. Sessions being delivered currently include:‘Organise Your Life Online’ – which helps people find out about online banking, paying bills and websites offering money advice and benefits information.‘Using the NI Direct website’ – advice and guidance on what NI Direct offers and how to navigate the website.‘Privacy and Security’ – which gives tips on keeping online personal information private and secure.‘Internet Scams’ – this provides tips on how to stay safe online and how to avoid online scams.In the last financial year, the programme helped over 3,500 people with digital training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women admitted to mental health units following childbirth in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that, although they hold data e.g. on the length of stay for all admissions, unfortunately, they do not hold data specific to postnatal women.The Directorate of Hospital Care within my Department have also informed me they do not hold data relating to admissions into mental health units, following childbirth either. It is anticipated that in the future that Encompass will be able to provide the relevant data. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have reported that there have been no admissions after childbirth, as in directly from the Maternity unit.The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have detailed their response in the table below:Year of Admission(i) expectant mothers(ii) six months postpartum mothers2022<5020230<520240<5It must be noted that for cells within the table above showing <5, the Trust are unable to provide an exact figure as the small number involved could make patients personally identifiable.  Disclosure would constitute a breach of the principles of the General Data Protection Regulation, 2018.The Western Health and Social Care Trust have stated that they do not hold information centrally on the number of women admitted to mental health units following child birth. I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of the increased employers' National Insurance contributions on healthcare providers, including community pharmacies, following April 2025.
answer_text: My Department estimates the increase in cost to the Health and Social Care Trusts and the arms-length bodies for directly employed staff to be in the region of some £100 million.  I am aware other providers such as those within Family Health Services (FHS) (including community pharmacies) and the independent sector also have increased costs which my Department has assessed to be some £40 million.Following the 2025-26 final budget settlement my Department is facing an extremely challenging financial position as we have been provided with additional funding of some £200 million compared to our 2024-25 position, of which £165 million has been ringfenced to tackle waiting lists. The final budget also committed £33.8 million of funding for Employer National Insurance contributions (NIC) increases for directly employed staff and £3.5 million for FHS Employer NIC increases.  It is clear there is a significant gap between the funding I have been allocated and the increased costs.Despite the extremely challenging financial position and recognising the valuable role played by GPs, community pharmacies, dentists, optometrists, care homes and hospices I have agreed that funding of some £40 million is made available to support these providers in meeting their additional National Insurance bills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average length of stay for women admitted to mental health units following childbirth.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they do not hold the information pertaining to this matter.The Directorate of Hospital Care have responded by highlighting that, unfortunately, they do not hold data relating to admissions into mental health units following childbirth, however, they hope encompass will be able to provide the relevant data in the future.The Northern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that, unfortunately, due to the information recording systems, they are unable to extract data that is this specific to answer this question.   The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have reported that there have been no admissions after childbirth, as in directly from the Maternity unit. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have illustrated the average length of stay for women admitted to mental health units following childbirth in 2023 and 2024 within the table below:Year of AdmissionAverage Length of Stay Postpartum2022N/A202329 Days202426 DaysThe Western Health and Social Care Trust have advised that, unfortunately, due to the information recording systems, they are unable to extract data that is this specific to answer this question.   I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of occasions that Community First Responder organisations have attended to patients experiencing emergencies before health service organisations have reached them in each of the last three years, broken down by Heath and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to the current nature of how the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) collects and stores Community First Responders (CFR) call data, it is not possible to answer the specific query within the AQW due to costs involved.  Staff would be required to spend additional time quality assuring data and collating into a workable format, making the cost disproportionate. While CFR data is currently collected and processed manually, NIAS are in the process of developing a new digital smartphone app for community first responders, which will run in conjunction with NIAS’s computer aided despatch system (CAD).  This will significantly enhance NIAS’s ability to audit all aspects of CFR calls, in real time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on capital plans for (i) Daisy Hill Hospital; and (ii) Craigavon Area Hospital.
answer_text: Following my response to your previous request for an update on the capital plans for Daisy Hill Hospital (DHH) and Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH) in December 2024 (AQW 19217 /22-27 refers), the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) has been allocated £2 million of capital funding in 2025/26 for the Low Voltage electrical project at DHH to progress enhancement to electrical infrastructure.The Trust continues to develop the long-term plans for the site redevelopment programme at CAH.  Due to the scale of the programme the project will be broken down into phases, and the Trust is currently refining the preferred phased redevelopment approach at CAH.The substantial projected cost of the programme means that affordability will be a key issue, in the context of the challenging financial environment and the scale of demand for other capital investment projects in the Health and Social Care sector.The Trust will be invited to submit business cases for these proposals once budget affordability is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of complaints of staff bullying reported in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of complaints of staff bullying reported in each of the last three years is shown in the table below;Complaints of bullyingYearBHSCTNHSCTNIASSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTTotal2022/23774711301022023/246014821762024/2558322422*91Total19581321923269* Following the launch of the Western Trust’s Conflict, Bullying and Harassment in the Workplace Policy in April 2019 (since superseded by the current Conflict, Bullying and Harassment Policy October 2023), only cases requiring formal investigation of Bullying/Harassment were recorded by the Trust. Effective 1 April 2024 the Trust began recording all reports of Conflict, Bullying and Harassment

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of bereavement support service provision, including the level of provision for children.
answer_text: The NI Bereavement Network was established by the Department of Health in April 2021, comprising members from key statutory, community and voluntary organisations.In March 2024 the Network launched a dedicated website - Bereaved NI.  The website signposts users to a range of resources to assist them as they step through their bereavement journey, including information and support for children and young people who have been bereaved and for adults after the death of a child.  The resource includes information relating to the Coroner’s Service and funeral arrangements.The website has attracted over 15,000 users to date.The Network continues to work with children and young people to help map out the bereavement support that would be helpful for them in recognition of the lifelong impact bereavement can have on development and wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost to his Department for each mammogram completed.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process of data sharing across health provision services on the occasion of a person’s death.
answer_text: Business Services Organisation (BSO) process death notifications weekly upon the receipt of an electronic file provided by General Register Office (GRO) containing death information. This information is uploaded to the patient registration platform - National Health Application and Infrastructure Services (NHAIS) - and patients identified as deceased are deducted.When a patient is deducted from NHAIS an electronic notification is sent to the relevant GP practice to advise of this so that the patient may be removed from the practice clinical system.In addition, a Death Data Report is issued by email to multiple Trust departments and BSO units (Data Quality Team, ITS, Finance, NIDIS, BSO Information Unit, Screening programmes etc). This report is a notification of the weekly list of death, reinstatement and amendment data in Northern Ireland.GP practices request from BSO registrations that the patient is deducted from the practice list, citing the reason as death, and these numbers are included as above.Within HSC Trusts when a person is marked as deceased on Encompass, this updates the patient status across all Trusts. Any appointments, referrals, waiting lists and orders for that patient will be automatically closed or cancelled.When a death is registered with the General Registry Office (GRO), this is shared with the Northern Ireland Digital Identity Service (NIDIS), who have responsibility for communicating patient demographics to downstream system who then update encompass accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the level of controls in place to prevent repeat prescriptions being collected after a person has died.
answer_text: It is normal and accepted practice for GP practices to operate a repeat prescribing system underpinned by a robust repeat prescribing policy to ensure repeat prescription requests are dealt with efficiently and safely. This would include arrangements for collection of all prescriptions, but especially controlled drugs and drugs liable to misuse.  Each practice should have their own repeat prescribing system and policy which should be reviewed and audited on a regular basis by the practice.Practices should have internal processes including stopping prescribing when they are notified that a patient dies.  There are processes in place between Business Services Organisation and practices to notify deaths and remove deceased patients from the practice’s registered list.Additionally, as part of the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework (NICAF) process, my Department requires submission of assurance and evidence that the practice adheres to the requirements of the Medicines Act for the storage, prescribing, dispensing, recording and disposal of drugs; this also provides assurance to my Department about the importance of robust repeat prescribing systems at GP practice level.Furthermore, community pharmacist contractors are expected to review their procedures for prescription collection from GP practices and the onward supply of medicines to patients or their representatives to ensure

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the anticipated social and economic benefits for Northern Ireland from hosting The 153rd Open, particularly in terms of tourism and international profile.
answer_text: We are incredibly proud to be hosting the 153rd Open Championship at Royal Portrush this July. This is a huge moment for Northern Ireland, one that brings real benefits for our economy, our tourism sector, and our international profile.Building on the estimated £100 million impact of the last Open in 2019, the 153rd Championship is predicted to generate more than £213 million in total economic benefit for Northern Ireland, according to the Sport Industry Research Centre (SIRC) at Sheffield Hallam University. The Open also puts Northern Ireland on a global stage, with estimates for TV coverage reaching over 100 million homes and attracting almost 280,000 attendees to the event showing the world everything we have to offer. Local businesses, jobs and communities right across the region will feel the benefit.It is of course more than just a sporting event. It inspires pride, brings people together, and leaves a lasting legacy.To mark this milestone, the First Minister and I hosted a special event on Tuesday 27 May, celebrating the collective effort that brought The Championship back to Portrush. The Open is highlighting Northern Ireland for all that it has to offer as a place to live, work, visit and invest.We look forward to welcoming the world this summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the main pressures on her 2025-26 budget position.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 10 - AQO 2073/22-27			ANSWER My Department received £137.7m of additional Executive funding in the 2025-26 Final Opening Budget which, together with the indicative June Monitoring allocation of £10.4m, brings my Department’s budget to £1,425.7m.As a result, my Department started the current financial year with stabilisation pressures in excess of £40m which consisted mainly of pressures for policing, legal aid and prisons.  My officials are currently undertaking a full review of the latest financial position for DoJ business areas as part of June Monitoring.In addition, my Department continues to face exceptional pressures in excess of £225m in relation to legal claims for Holiday Pay, PSNI Data Breach and McCloud. These pressures are unaffordable for my Department within current budget allocations and I continue to engage with the Finance Minister and Executive colleagues to secure the necessary funding for these costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the estimated dates and plans for the implementation of the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department consulted on proposals for regulations to operationalise the Act last year.  Work is ongoing to finalise the consultation analysis and I hope to publish my response in the Autumn.  I wish to have further meaningful engagement with stakeholders as my Department designs and develops safe leave guidance for employers and workers, in conjunction with the development of regulations.  The views of stakeholders will inform the timeline for implementation but I hope to be in a position to make the necessary regulations in early 2026, with a view to them coming into operation later that year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the need to lower the age at which women are invited for breast screening.
answer_text: The current age thresholds for population screening programmes are based on recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific advisory committee which advises the four nations, on all aspects of screening. The UK NSC regularly reviews its recommendations on screening as new research and evidence becomes available. When considering who to screen, the benefits of offering a screening programme are weighed up against the possible harms. The UK NSC only recommends screening when it has concluded that the evidence shows that the benefits of screening outweigh the potential harms, for each age cohort.  There is ongoing research to examine the benefits and harms of extending breast cancer screening to women aged 71-73 years of age and also 47-49 years of age (the age X trial). The first report from this trial is expected in 2026. Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.
