question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the communication channels available for public transport users to contact Translink outside of normal office working hours, for example when the Dublin Airport to Belfast bus service does not arrive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink communication channels  Digital channels Customers can self-serve information through the Translink website and journey planning app 24 hours a day. These channels provide planning, timetables and real time updates for services including disruptions and cancellations. This information is also provided on in-station digital display boards, and in the case of rail services, through long line PA systems. There are currently gaps in real time information provision. We are in the process of rolling out a real time passenger information project across Ulsterbus that will enable bus tracking and Real Time Information to all scheduled coach and bus services. This will also be supported by a 24-hour Bus Operations Control function that will manage passenger information and any service disruption in real time. This project will also provide a significant increase in real time information displays at shelters across the Ulsterbus network (up to 1000 extra displays). Contact Centre Translink Contact Centre Business Hours: Mon-Sat 8am to 6pm & Sun 9am to 5pm. Callers receive the following message if they call outside of these hours: “Thank you for calling Translink, our phone lines are now closed.   Our opening hours are Monday- Saturday 8am to 6pm and Sunday 9am to 5pm. For timetable information and travel updates, please visit our website www.translink.co.uk or use the Translink app.   Social Media and Messaging platforms are available Monday to Friday from 7am to 8pm and on Saturday and Sunday from 8am to 6pm.   You can message us on WhatsApp 07583122646  Or get in touch on X @Translink_NI” Translink Social Media Team Business Hours are Mon-Friday 07:00 to 20:00 and  Sat/Sun 08:00 to 18:00. Additionally, the Social Media Team are available on a call-out basis up to 00:00 hours to support incidents and events. Where required, this is activated by operational colleagues who employ the Social Media/Messaging team to push out posts on X to advise followers. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has (i) completed any research on; and (ii) raised awareness of , the difference between gluten-free and coeliac-safe classifications on food and the impact on health outcomes for those with associated conditions.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the Food Standards Agency (FSA) is responsible for allergen labelling and providing guidance to consumers with food hypersensitivities which includes food allergy, intolerance and coeliac disease. Any voluntary food information must comply with the requirements of Regulation (EU) No. 1169/2011 on Food Information to Consumers. Voluntary information must be accurate, not mislead consumers or be ambiguous or confusing, and where appropriate, be based on the relevant scientific data. Where foods have been voluntarily labelled as ‘gluten-free’ or ‘very-low gluten’ they must meet the requirements set out in Regulation (EU) No. 828/2014. This legislation sets out the conditions under which foods may be labelled as ‘gluten-free’ or ‘very-low gluten’. The rules surrounding the use of the terms ‘gluten-free’ and ‘very-low gluten’ apply to all foods including non-prepacked foods such as those served in restaurants. No other statements to describe the absence or reduced presence of gluten are permitted under Regulation (EU) No. 828/2014.Use of terms, such as ‘coeliac-safe,’ would be deemed voluntary information, and therefore must comply with the requirements of voluntary food information. I am not aware of any specific research or campaign on the differences between the regulated ‘gluten-free’ voluntary information and any other voluntary terms used on food, such as coeliac-safe. The FSA regularly runs campaigns to raise public awareness around food hypersensitivity and consumers living with food allergies, including the mandatory requirement around gluten.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the need for the current number of arm’s-length bodies and non-departmental public bodies that fall within the remit of their Department.
To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to include provisions in hospitals for patients with coeliac disease to receive appropriate food.
answer_text: Coeliac disease is a condition where your immune system attacks your own tissues when you eat gluten. This damages your gut (small intestine) so your body cannot properly take in nutrients.The symptoms of coeliac disease vary but can include severe or occasional diarrhoea, excessive wind and/or constipation, persistent or unexplained nausea and vomiting, recurrent stomach pain, cramping or bloating, iron and/or vitamin deficiency etc.Coeliac disease cannot be cured but patients manage their condition through adherence to a strict gluten-free diet with regular review outpatient appointments for monitoring. Reviews may be with consultants or dietitians.All HSC Trusts have confirmed that inpatient menus provide gluten-free options for patients with coeliac disease.  Appropriate arrangements are in place to ensure dietetic advice is available and procedures are in place to meet the needs of all patients with special dietary requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time to get an endoscopy, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with coeliac disease, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The majority of the HSC Trusts do not capture this information.  The Western HSC Trust, however, has reported that there are 2,686 known patients in its area diagnosed with Coeliac Disease.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of blood tests completed for coeliac disease in each of the past three years.
answer_text: The level of blood tests undertaken for suspected coeliac disease is not recorded by the HSC.  Blood tests are provided for a number of conditions and are not specific to patients with suspected coeliac disease.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of coeliac awareness training provided to medical practitioners.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have confirmed there is no specific coeliac awareness training provided to medical practitioners.   All medical practitioners will, however, be aware of the symptoms and approaches to treatment as part of their initial medical training.   Postgraduate training is also undertaken by all practitioners in those areas relevant to their role consistent with continued professional development requirements to maintain accreditation.The diagnosis and subsequent treatment of this condition is fully supported by recognised pathways within general practice.  On presentation of symptoms, GPs will make an assessment and programme a number of tests to identify possible causes.  The test results will help guide the GP to a conclusion on potential causes which in the case of coeliac will prompt the referral for blood test and subsequent scope referral if appropriate. The Department is not aware of any particular concerns with the current awareness and management of this condition.  Symptoms for coeliac are common across a number of gastrointestinal conditions and the approach to confirmation of diagnosis of any of these conditions will be through a combination of patient history, advice and a selected order of tests as the means of accurately identifying the underlying cause.On this basis, it has not been deemed necessary to secure any further awareness training for coeliac.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s plans to reduce waiting times for people to receive a diagnosis of coeliac disease.
answer_text: Patients referred to secondary care for Coeliac Disease are cared for by specialist dietitians and gastroenterologists in multidisciplinary teams who manage a wide range of conditions.  Diagnosis is through a blood test at primary care level followed, in some cases, by an endoscopic procedure to obtain a biopsy of the patient’s intestine for testing.  Much work has already been done under service transformation to fully utilise the endoscopic capacity across the HSC.  The potential to further streamline the process is being explored whereby a diagnosis can be made routinely without a biopsy. Some years ago, the Trusts piloted dietetic led coeliac review clinics as an alternative to consultant outpatient clinics.  This service model proved effective in prioritising coeliac patients within gastroenterology.  Where it was possible to have specialist clinics, patients benefited greatly from high-quality cost-effective care and more timely reviews.  Consultant capacity was released for other gastroenterology patients as well.  Unfortunately, however, some Trusts were unable to recruit dietitians and recurrent funding support was not available in each area.  Although this service is available in some Trusts, any future development of dietetic led clinics across the HSC would require a dedicated resource funded recurrently which is not possible at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on progress with the UK Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: You will be aware the Ministry for Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) has confirmed a one-year extension of the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) for the 2025/26 financial year, with an allocation for here of £45.5m. This is a reduced funding allocation, despite sustained engagement and repeated representations calling for the maintenance of previous funding levels.The one-year extension is intended to serve as a transitional period, allowing work to commence on a successor programme. Nevertheless, the precise details and scale of any future funding will remain uncertain until the formal announcement is made as part of the British Government’s Spending Review, scheduled for June 2026. The lack of detail on any successor to SPF is unacceptable. I have written to the MHCLG Minister, Alex Norris MP, and deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner MP, on this issue with a copy of the agreed Executive position paper on how successor funding programmes should be developed and delivered here.Any delay has the potential to negatively impact the Voluntary and Community sector in particular. Therefore, a successor to the Economic Inactivity intervention under SPF must be a priority. To this end, my officials are working closely and collaboratively with other Executive departments, and with the community and voluntary sector, to gather evidence to start to inform the development of any potential successor. This will help us in moving as quickly and effectively as possible, once we have the clarity we need from Whitehall.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury ahead of the forthcoming Spending Review.
answer_text: ANSWERIn February I, along with my other devolved government counterparts, discussed the upcoming Spending Review with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC).In March I met with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) in advance of the Spring Statement where he outlined some of the key points in Spring Statement.Throughout April I wrote to the CST on a range of Executive specific issues to be considered in advance of the Spending Review, due to be announced on 11 June 2025. A joint letter issued from the devolved Finance Ministers to the CST covering a number of common issues across all devolved administrations. Together with my counterparts in Wales and Scotland, I met the CST to discuss the issues raised in our joint letter.In May, I, along with my other devolved government counterparts, issued a further joint letter to the CST pressing for a F:ISC meeting and associated bilateral meetings in advance of the Spending Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when he will provide further details on (i) the Strategic Review of Rates; and (ii) the policy reviews into the Small Business Rate Relief and Non Domestic Vacant Rating.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the written response provided to AQO 2018/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the level of investment in hockey compared to other sports in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Since 2019 my Department has invested £773,702 in hockey in Northern Ireland through Sport NI.Around 3% of all people engaging in sport play hockey and this is a little less than 3% of Sport NI’s investment in sport over the last six years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department’s participation in and engagement with Deaf Awareness Week to raise awareness and celebrate the language and culture of the Deaf community.
answer_text: My Department currently funds the Centre for Translation and Interpreting at Queens University Belfast to provide a British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language Interpreting MA to boost future interpreter capacity. In addition, it supports the QUB Sign Language Network to provide public events open to all, to promote sign languages and Deaf culture across the wider community and, to provide a networking space for members and associates of the deaf community.During Deaf Awareness Week, the QUB Sign Language Network held an event for the MA interpreter students to examine the importance of emotional intelligence for interpreters working with deaf people who may be experiencing social isolation and its potential impact on their interpersonal relationships and communication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment on the progression of the Sign Language Bill (NIA Bill 10/22-27).
answer_text: Following my Second stage introduction of the Sign Language Bill on 18th February 2025, the Sign Language Bill has been referred to the Committee for Communities for its scrutiny role which has been extended until 13 February 2026.The Committee launched a consultation to seek views from stakeholders which closed on Friday 9 May 2025 and it will now consider the responses. As part of its role to scrutinise the Bill, the Committee has received a briefing from my officials and requested further information on the Sign Language Bill. The Sign Language Bill remains a priority for me, therefore, I have instructed my officials to continue to support the Committee in its scrutiny role and, to support the progression of the Bill through the various Assembly stages until enactment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25982/22-27, whether this measurement of childhood obesity is in line with how it is measured in other UK jurisdictions.
answer_text: The figures provided in response to question AQW 25982/22-27 are not directly comparable with figures reported in other UK jurisdictions. The official figures provided for Northern Ireland are based on actual measurements taken in school for Primary 1 and Year 8 aged children and apply the International Obesity Task Force (IOTF) classification of obesity for Body Mass Index (BMI) categories which is in line with many international countries. This provides consistent trend data spanning years and links the data which supports my extant obesity prevention strategic framework with its predecessor strategy.While similar child measurement programmes are conducted in other UK jurisdictions, they apply the UK90, or British 1990, growth reference, to classify BMI rather than IOTF.In addition, while child measurement programmes in other UK jurisdictions measure children in reception year (aged 4-5 years) as with Northern Ireland, they do not measure children in year 8 (aged 11 to 12 years).It is possible however to produce analysis according to the UK90/British 1990 method, which have been provided in the table below, although such breakdowns for NI are not regularly published.Table 1. Percentage of Primary 1 and Year 8 pupils living with obesity 2019/20 – 2023/24% Pupils Living with obesity (according to UK90 / British 1990 Growth Reference)School Year2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24*Primary 1UnavailableUnavailable11.9%10.7%11.3%Year 819.8%UnavailableUnavailable19.0%19.5%Source: Department of Health. Height and Weight data source: Child Health System. *Figures for 2023/24 are marked provisional and may be subject to change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she plans to review the current model for student loans in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The financial model relating to the valuation of the student loan asset is delivered by the Department for Education in England under a service level agreement. The latest iteration of the student loan repayment model is expected to be received later in the 2025/26 financial year. The model will be subject to rigorous quality assurance testing to ensure that it is fit for purpose. My officials are also currently developing plans for a proposed review of higher education funding, including financial support to students.  Officials are due to brief me in coming weeks regarding the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of how much of the £4.06 billion student loan financial asset will change under the new valuation method for student loans in 2025-26.
answer_text: The financial model relating to the valuation of the student loan asset is delivered by the Department for Education in England under a service level agreement. The latest iteration of the student loan repayment model is expected to be received later in the 2025/26 financial year. Until the model has been received and quality assured, any potential impact on the valuation of the student loan asset cannot be estimated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) status of; and (ii) options available for, the Belfast Region City Deal funding which was allocated to the Mournes Gondola project, now that the National Trust has confirmed they are denying a lease and therefore stopping the project in its current development form.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Mourne Mountain Gateway Project was to receive £29.8 million of the Executive’s contribution to the Belfast Region City Deal. Belfast Region City Deal had secured approval for funding from the Department for the Economy to take forward development, design and survey work. This kind of work is a normal part of how major public projects are developed. Expenditure on the project to date is around £500k. City and Growth Deal funding is ring-fenced by the Executive. If a project does not proceed, a Deal must bring forward an alternative project. The Belfast Region City Deal partners will now consider alternative proposals for the remaining £29.3 million, which had been earmarked for the Mournes project. Relevant Departments, the City and Growth Deals Delivery Board and the Executive will consider these proposals in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) status of; and (ii) options available for, the Belfast Region City Deal monies which were allocated to the Mourne Gondola project, now that the National Trust has confirmed they are denying a lease and therefore stopping the project in its current development form.
answer_text: (i) Belfast Region City Deal funding is ring fenced by the Executive and not lost to the Deal following the National Trust’s decision.(ii) In the first instance, examining alternative options is a decision for the Belfast Region City Deal Executive Board. Any proposal will also require the agreement of the Department for the Economy and the NI Executive.  The Board has not yet presented my Department nor Tourism Northern Ireland with any alternative proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the potential economic and tourism benefits from the return of the Festival of Flight to Newcastle.
answer_text: According to the event organisers the most recent Festival of Flight in 2019 attracted over 90,000 visitors, and generated approximately £1 million for the local economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the increase in losses incurred by Invest NI from waived and written off items from £1,469,000 in 2022-23 to £2,668,000.
answer_text: The losses written off in the Invest NI accounts increased from £1,469,000 in 2022-23 to £2,668,000 in 2023-24. The 2023-24 year included write off of three cases materially over £350k, while the 2022-23 year included one case just over the £350k threshold requiring disclosure. The three cases over £350k in 2022-23 related to Invest NI assistance provided in the period from 2012 to 2020. One of the three companies suffered a decline in its financial position and entered administration in 2018, following the sudden death of the managing director / major shareholder in 2017. The second company was forced to cease trading in 2018, as a result of being unable to gain and retain market traction despite several pivots and an attempted restructuring. The third company entered into liquidation in 2020/21 as a direct result of the COVID pandemic. Following the completion of the wind up of the three companies, the grant clawback recovery due and the amounts invested through Invest NI’s loan and equity funds were written off in 2023-24.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 24458/22-27, to detail (i) when the Tourism NI (a) 2022-23; and (b) 2023-24 annual accounts will be published; and (ii) the reason for the delays.
answer_text: It is expected that both sets of accounts will be published by the end of May 2025.The delay in the 2022-23 and 2023-24 annual accounts was caused by a combination of the challenges associated with the introduction of Review of Financial Processes and the specific issues flagged regarding the regularity of a number of Tourism NI Schemes. In respect of the regularity issues, delays arose due to the Department and Tourism NI seeking legal advice on the specific issues identified.  The Tourism NI Board also appointed an external consultant to advise on the conformance of other areas of financial support provided by Tourism NI to third parties with the Tourism Order 1992.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the ramifications of Tourism NI paying out £6,021,760 of public funds through the Co-Operative Marketing Fund, which legal advice has confirmed that they did not have legislative authority to deliver between 2016-17 and 2023-24.
answer_text: The Co-operative Marketing Fund enabled hotels and accommodation providers here to market their offer to tourists in the South, as part of the Tourism Recovery Action Plan to help businesses rebuild after the pandemic. All £6,021,760 went towards supporting tourism businesses and despite this technical issue, the outcomes of the scheme were successful for the local industry and it played a significant role in protecting jobs in the hotel and accommodation sector. Over the period, where the fund operated alongside Tourism NI’s marketing campaigns, visitor spend from the South rose from £60.9m in 2015 to an estimated £233m in 2022, a growth of 283%. Spend by visitors from within the north rose from £219m in 2015 to £267m over the same period. The scheme also met its objective in driving up the return on Tourism NI’s marketing investment. An independent post project evaluation of the campaigns in the Summer of 2022 showed a return of £148.76 per pound spent in the North and £217.04 per pound spent in the South.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 25513/22-27, to detail the process and criteria by which loans are waived or written off by Invest NI to end up in the category of losses.
answer_text: The Invest NI Access to Finance funds provide debt and equity financing to SMEs whose growth is constrained by a lack of traditional finance. Invest NI normally provides loans through the Access to Finance funds, managed by independent NI based FCA approved fund managers who retain responsibility for all investment decisions. At the conclusion of the fund period, the fund manager will return the proceeds from the loan investments to the investing parties. If there is an overall loss to Invest NI on the fund, a write-off will be prepared. The criteria applied for the loss to be approved must include an explanation of the reasons for the loss, a detailed description on the recovery actions that have been taken, and a recommendation for any lessons that can be learned for future investments. The fund manager will pursue all debts as per normal debt recovery processes, including legal proceedings if appropriate. In most instances of losses on investment the company will be under an insolvency arrangement.   In the circumstances where Invest NI has issued direct loans to companies, these are actively managed by Client Executives and overseen by a Credit Committee comprising senior Invest NI managers. The Credit Committee approves all loan investments, provides direction on loan conditions, and advises on underperforming loans. If all options for debt recovery on an underperforming loan are exhausted the loss will be written off. All waivers and write offs are subject to delegated approval limits and any waiver in excess of £50k or write off in excess of £100k must be approved by the Department of Finance. Those that are under these limits and approved within Invest NI must be approved by the Finance Director or Executive Director of Finance, depending on the total quantum.In some rare circumstances Invest NI may waive its right to specific conditions related to grant clawback or other investments. A waiver will be approved if it is considered to be the best option to maximise the return on investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when the Women’s Health Strategy will be progressed.
answer_text: In view of my constrained budget, I intend to focus during the remainder of this mandate on the actions that can be delivered within current resources to stabilise our Health and Social Care system and address the health inequalities that contribute to poor health outcomes, rather than commit to any new long-term strategies which cannot be funded at this time. Work to develop a Women’s Health Action Plan is ongoing, which will include the wide range of policy and service development initiatives currently in progress and outline any actions that can be delivered within current resources over the next three years to improve health services and deliver better outcomes for women.  My Department recently hosted a number of online engagement events with women's representative groups and healthcare professionals and will be reviewing the input received over the coming weeks to help inform the priorities in the action plan.  My Department also provided funding to Derry Well Women, who in partnership with Queen’s University Belfast, developed a Northern Ireland Women’s Health Survey as Phase 1 of a large-scale public listening exercise with women.  I launched this survey in November 2024 with the aim of hearing directly from women about their health priorities and barriers to accessing services.  Early findings from this survey are due at the end of May 2025 and will be considered as work develops on the Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the uptake rate of breast screening for those invited.
answer_text: The uptake rate for routine breast screening in Northern Ireland, for those invited, meets the national acceptable standard of greater than, or equal to 70.0%, as set out in the NHS Breast Screening Programme screening standards. Uptake rates for Northern Ireland since 2020, are as follows:Year2020/212021/22 2022/232023/24Uptake Standard*72.2%74.8%74.3%73.9%*Acceptable level: greater than or equal to 70.0%; Achievable level: greater than or equal to 80.0%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people living with a diagnosis of (i) Alzheimer’s disease; and (ii) dementia in each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not currently hold data on the number of people diagnosed with Alzheimer’s disease.In May 2024, my Department established a Regional Dementia Project Board to refocus on dementia care. One of the priority workstreams formed as a result of the Project Board is the Data and Diagnosis worksteam. This workstream will focus on developing the capability of encompass to assist with the collation and publication of dementia data, including sub type, across Northern Ireland.The total number of people with a diagnosis of dementia in Northern Ireland over the last three years is as follows:2022 - 13,2832023 - 13,6252024 - 13,900.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action taken by Departments on the Pooling of Budgets, as detailed in guidance from his Department in April 2025.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Guidance on Pooling Budgets issued to departments on 15 April 2025 as part of the department’s ongoing work to improve the strategic nature of our budget process and in light of the Public Accounts Committee Report on Child Poverty (recommendation 9).  Departments will consider the guidance as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for a red flag breast cancer patient to be seen, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that voluntary and community organisations received £25.75 million in funding for 2025-26 from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to deliver programmes addressing economic activity programmes, equating to an eight per cent reduction on the previous year's funding, and given the cross-cutting impact of this work on Programme for Government outcomes, to detail any steps her Department is taking to mitigate the impact of this funding cut to service delivery.
answer_text: The Department of Finance are leading the engagement with the British Government on this, with the support of my and other local Departments It is disappointing that the British Government took the decision to reduce the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) allocations in 2025/26. The extension of the SPF in 2025/26 is intended to be a transition year to provide time for a successor programme to be developed.  My Department provided input to the agreed Executive position on successor funding, which the Finance Minister has sent to the Deputy Prime Minister, Angela Raynor MP, and the Minister for Local Growth, Alex Norris MP.  Although the loss of EU funding has significantly reduced the support to people who face barriers to employment, my Department delivers a range of support, including Step Up, Skill Up and Skills for Life and Work, NI Apprenticeship Programme and an all-age careers service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that voluntary and community organisations received £25.75 million in funding for 2025-26 from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to deliver programmes addressing economic activity programmes, equating to an eight per cent reduction on the previous year's funding, and given the cross-cutting impact of this work on Programme for Government outcomes, to detail any steps his Department is taking to mitigate the impact of this funding cut to service delivery.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) is delivered directly here by the Ministry for Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) and it is disappointing that the UK Government took the decision to reduce the allocations in 2025/26. The extension of the SPF in 2025/26 is intended to be a transition year to provide time for a successor programme to be developed. The Department of Finance is leading the engagement with the UK Government on this, with the support of DAERA and other local departments. My Department provided input to the agreed Executive position on successor funding, which the Minister of Finance has sent to the Deputy Prime Minister, Angela Raynor MP, and the Minister for Local Growth, Alex Norris MP. That Executive position on successor funding calls for: a genuine partnership approach in the development of a new programme, and for this to be put in place as soon as possible. funding at an appropriate level to meet our needs; the UK Government to deliver on its manifesto commitment to return local decision-making over the use of this funding; and there to be no repeat of the mistakes of the SPF in the design of a successor programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that voluntary and community organisations received £25.75 million in funding for 2025-26 from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to deliver programmes addressing economic activity programmes, equating to an eight per cent reduction on the previous year's funding, and given the cross-cutting impact of this work on Programme for Government outcomes, to detail any steps his Department is taking to mitigate the impact of this funding cut to service delivery.
answer_text: I acknowledge the concerns raised regarding the reduction in funding for the UK Shared Prosperity Fund for the voluntary and community sector, which plays a crucial role in delivering programmes that significantly impact economic activity and align with the Programme for Government outcomes.Details regarding the breakdown of the £25.75 million funding for 2025-26 and its eight per cent reduction, specific to programmes of care, are not available.My Department continues to work with HSC Trusts to develop and refine contingency arrangements for those that are not supported by the Shared Prosperity Fund. This includes mapping alternative community placements and identifying capacity in statutory services. Trusts are committed to ensuring that services are provided to meet assessed needs of vulnerable adults. My Department is committed to supporting the community and voluntary sector as much as possible, recognising its vital role in promoting public health and social wellbeing. My Department has a Core Grant Funding Scheme that provides financial support to community and voluntary sector organizations to help them meet core operating costs. For the fiscal year 2025/26, we have launched a new scheme that has been co-designed with community and voluntary sector organizations and with the assistance of the Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action (NICVA). Subject to budget availability, there is an intention to increase the Scheme budget in future years to further support these organisations.While these measures do not directly compensate for the specific reduction in funding from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund, they represent a strategic approach to support and enhance the capacity of the community and voluntary sector to continue their valuable work in the face of financial challenges. In addition, my Department is finalising the Learning Disability Service Model, which aims to provide a consistent and early-stage support framework for individuals with Learning Disabilities across the region.My officials continue to participate in cross-departmental discussions on the successor to the Shared Prosperity Fund which is led by the Department of Finance who are leading the engagement with the British Government on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether the inclusion of an incorrect weblink in letters in relation to rates bills for the upcoming financial year was as a result of a human typographical error on the letter or as a result of Land and Property Services system suffering a hack and this weblink being added outside the control of the Department of Finance or its staff.
answer_text: ANSWERThis matter is still being investigated by Land & Property Services (LPS). Without prejudice to the ongoing review, the evidence to date indicates the incorrect weblink contained in some of the bills that issued in April 2025 was a result of human typographical error. Land & Property Services will be conducting a learning review and will be strengthening existing checks to prevent this issue re-occurring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, given that voluntary and community organisations received £25.75 million in funding for 2025-26 from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to deliver programmes addressing economic activity programmes, equating to an eight per cent reduction on the previous year's funding, and given the cross-cutting impact of this work on Programme for Government outcomes, to detail any steps his Department is taking to mitigate the impact of this funding cut to service delivery.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) is delivered directly by the Ministry for Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG). Despite repeated calls and pressure for the level of SPF funding to be at least maintained, it is disappointing that the British Government took the decision to reduce the allocations in 2025/26. I met with NICVA last month to discuss the impact of this, and my officials continue to engage with other Executive departments in this regard.The extension of the SPF in 2025/26 is intended to be a transition year to provide time for a successor programme to be developed. My focus during this transition period is to press for: a genuine partnership approach in the development of a new programme, and for this to be put in place as soon as possible. funding at an appropriate level to meet our needs; the British Government to deliver on its manifesto commitment to return local decision-making over the use of this funding; and there to be no repeat of the mistakes of the SPF in the design of a successor programme.I secured the agreement of my Ministerial Colleagues to an Executive position on successor funding in March 2025 and have written to the Deputy Prime Minister, Angela Raynor MP, and the Minister for Local Growth, Alex Norris MP, providing them with this position paper and calling for a meeting to discuss how an appropriate funding programme can be put in place. Separately my officials are working on a cross-departmental basis, and in partnership with the community and voluntary sector, to gather evidence to inform the development of any potential successor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the level of childhood obesity in Northern Ireland in the last three years.
answer_text: The latest available statistics, show that there has been no notable change in the proportion of Primary 1 or Year 8 pupils classified as living with obesity during the latest three years of available data.% Pupils Living with obesity School Year2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24*Primary 1UnavailableUnavailable6.1%5.1%5.7%Year 86.0%UnavailableUnavailable5.6%5.8%Source: Child Health System. *Figures for 2023/24 are marked provisional and may be subject to change.Height and weight information for Primary 1 and Year 8 schoolchildren is provided to the Department of Health by the Child Health System (CHS) which is collected from measurements of all available Primary 1 and Year 8 pupils within schools each year as part of the NI schools measurement programme. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, disruption in schools resulted in some children not being measured during this time. As a result, data coverage was not sufficient to provide information for 2019/20 and 2020/21 for Primary 1 children, and for 2020/21 and 2021/22 for Year 8 children. Although data for 2021/22 for Primary 1 children, and 2019/20 for year 8 children is provided, the coverage is lower than in normal years and so caution is advised when interpreting this data.The current obesity prevention strategic framework ‘A Fitter Future For All’ has 17 outcomes aimed specifically at tackling obesity in children and young people, to help achieve long term outcomes such as:Early identification of children who are living with obesity or are at risk of living with obesity;Children and Young People make healthy food and physical activity choices;Children and young people are making healthy food choices; andA greater proportion of children and young people are achieving recommended levels of physical activity.Work is ongoing to develop a new obesity strategic framework ‘Healthy Futures’. Since the close of the ‘Healthy Futures’, my Department has been working through the detail of the responses and looking at the wider research and evidence base. In addition, my Department has been engaging with colleagues across the UK and Ireland to consider emerging developments for those living with overweight and obesity.Work on finalising the consultation response report is almost complete. Once agreed, the consultation report will be published, and we will work to finalise the Healthy Futures strategic framework, with an aim to publish this in 2025, subject to agreement and the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the role of sports in delivering health outcomes in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department’s ‘A Fitter Future for All’, obesity prevention strategy for Northern Ireland, seeks to promote greater participation in all forms of sport and physical activity. Physical activity plays a key role in improving health and well-being. There is a clear link between physical inactivity and chronic disease and premature death. Therefore, everyone should try to take part in appropriate levels of physical activity. It helps in preventing and managing many health conditions including coronary heart disease, stroke, diabetes, obesity and cancer. The current advice from the UK Chief Medical Officers is for adults to be physically active 150 mins per week and for children and young people to be physically active for 60 minutes per day, to help them stay active and healthy. These guidelines are statements of physical activity levels based on epidemiological thresholds where optimal behaviour is associated with a significantly reduced risk of a range of health conditions.The Wellbeing in Sport Action Plan 2019-2025 recognises that sport can play a key role in the health and wellbeing of our communities. Being active is good for both our physical and mental health, and being involved in team activities can create social bonds and friendships.  Sport can also provide an opportunity for people to support each other and identify issues that may be affecting someone’s wellbeing.  The PHA works with Sport NI and other partners to raise awareness and improve understanding of mental health through sport. Finally, the ‘Active Living’, Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland, was launched in March 2022 by the Department for Communities. ‘Active Living’ is a cross-departmental strategy developed through co-design with Government Departments, Councils, Communities and the wider sports sector. The main aim of the Strategy is to get more people, more active, more of the time with a focus on underrepresented groups.Regular participation in sport and physical activity has been shown to significantly enhance physical and mental wellbeing and reduce health inequalities. ‘Active Living’ aims to ensure that sport and physical activity is inclusive, safe, diverse, shared, fun and offers equality of opportunity to participate for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the value of performance bonuses publicly-funded Housing Associations paid to their Board Members in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold or request this information. This is an operational matter for Registered Housing Associations. As Housing Associations are registered charities their board members would not receive performance bonuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the funding model for Housing Associations; and (ii) whether there is a requirement for Housing Associations generating a profit to return excess funds to the Housing Executive.
answer_text: Many social housing providers in Northern Ireland are housing associations that have charitable status. Housing Associations are independent, not-for-profit organisations that provide homes for people in housing need. The Charity Commission monitors Housing Associations to ensure they adhere to the law and the conditions of their charitable registration.Housing Associations receive funding for new build social housing through the Social Housing Development Programme. The Total Cost Indicator (TCI) system is currently used to determine the cost of building new social housing and the rates of Housing Association Grant (HAG). The TCI and HAG methodology was most recently reviewed in 2022 and found the methodology to be reasonable and proportionate as a means to calculate grant funding. Both TCI and HAG rates are kept under review. The Supporting People Programme is grant funded by the Department via the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) who work with over 80 service providers, including Housing Associations. In the Social Housing Development Programme, any trading surplus is used to maintain existing homes and help finance new homes. Any Supporting People grant (reserves) that has not been spent on the purposes intended during the relevant financial year must be recouped by the NIHE in line with Department of Finance guidelines on public funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the new cross-border health scheme will be open to patients in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.The Scheme will commence in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the progress of her Department's move to the new model of student loan asset in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: The financial model relating to the valuation of the student loan asset is delivered by the Department for Education in England under a service level agreement. The updated model is expected to be received later in the 2025/26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the value of the write-off of student loan balances where the amount cannot be recovered in each of the last three financial years, broken down by (i) university; and (ii) further and higher education college.
answer_text: Under the terms and conditions of student loans, outstanding balances are forgiven if: 25 years have passed since the statutory repayment due date, the borrower reaches age 65, the borrower dies, the borrower becomes disabled or the borrower is declared bankrupt. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he is considering funding proposals for drones with the ability to carry water up mountains for Sky Watch NI to assist in fighting fires.
answer_text: Wildfire response is under the command and control of the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service and as such these matters should be directed to them.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has a process in place to engage helicopter support to fight mountain fires.
answer_text: (i) NIFRS has established protocols for aerial support to include:Air ambulancePSNI helicoptersMaritime and Coastguard AgencyAerial support for wildfire attackSky Watch Civil Air PatrolIn the case of mountain fires these relate to either aerial reconnaissance, transportation of Firefighters/equipment which can be supported by PSNI, Maritime & Coastguard Agency or Sky Watch, or in the case of firefighting support through the Aeronautical Rescue Coordination Centre (ARCC). (ii) Neither the Department of Health nor NIFRS are aware of proposals to enhance equipment provision for Sky Watch NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the current powers and sentencing for environmental crimes.
answer_text: My Department takes all reports of environmental crime very seriously and applies the DAERA Enforcement Policy (DAERA Enforcement Policy - 2023) where it is appropriate and proportionate to do so. NIEA prioritises resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health and utilises powers contained within the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to conduct investigations and pursue enforcement and prosecutions. Prosecutions are often coupled with confiscation proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 aimed at depriving those convicted of the proceeds generated from their crimes whilst asserting the ‘Polluter Pays Principle’.My Department’s Enforcement Liaison Group has engaged with the Fisheries Bill team who are reviewing enforcement across a range of policies proposals to inform the draft Fisheries Bill. This will include the introduction of fixed and variable monetary penalties for non-compliance with certain offences and the increase in some maximum penalties including for water pollution. My Department is engaged with officials from the Department of Justice on this issue and it is hoped that the policy consultation will take place in late June. I would encourage anyone with information relating such crime environment to contact the NIEA by email at environmentalcrime@daera-ni.gov.uk or by telephone on 0800 80 70 60.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of proposals to enhance equipment provision to Sky Watch NI to assist support to the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service.
answer_text: (i) NIFRS has established protocols for aerial support to include:Air ambulancePSNI helicoptersMaritime and Coastguard AgencyAerial support for wildfire attackSky Watch Civil Air PatrolIn the case of mountain fires these relate to either aerial reconnaissance, transportation of Firefighters/equipment which can be supported by PSNI, Maritime & Coastguard Agency or Sky Watch, or in the case of firefighting support through the Aeronautical Rescue Coordination Centre (ARCC). (ii) Neither the Department of Health nor NIFRS are aware of proposals to enhance equipment provision for Sky Watch NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 24526/22-27 and AQW 24527/22-27, to detail the total amount paid in Recruitment and Retention Allowance for these posts to date.
answer_text: ANSWERThe total amount paid in Recruitment and Retention Allowance for the seven posts in the Executive Office, from June 2013 to March 2025, is £527,717.31.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on progress of the Workforce Plan for the Northern Ireland Civil Service and the Capacity and Capability review.
answer_text: ANSWERWorkforce planning is currently taken forward at departmental level with cross-departmental groups established both to inform the Integr8 system build for workforce planning and to ensure greater consistency of approach between departments. Strategic Workforce Planning typically looks three to five years into the future and is an incremental, iterative and long-term process.  As noted in my department’s updates on progress against the Capacity and Capability Report, Strategic Workforce Planning will be improved by further initiatives including improved data and reporting linked to Integr8 project delivery, an improved Workforce Model, a path to increased commonality of approach between departments through provision of guidance as well as a strategic approach to the future of work.  Each of these building blocks is being managed at pace within my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when there was last a review of the finance functionality across the Northern Ireland Civil Service and whether it is staffed sufficiently at the correct grades.
answer_text: ANSWEREach department has responsibility to maintain and review its own structure, including that of the finance function and to determine the sufficiency of staffing levels. Work is being taken forward within the NICS Finance Profession to strengthen the capacity and capability of finance teams across departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the impact of the Chancellor's Spring Statement 2025 on Northern Ireland’s 2025-26 budget.
answer_text: The Spring Statement was disappointing as it failed to deliver on what we need the most which is investment in public services. The Spring Statement provided the Executive with £14 million of additional Barnett consequentials, £12.5 million Resource and £1.6 million Capital DEL. Whilst welcome, these additional funds will not address the significant pressures facing our public services. The Chancellor also announced the creation of a £3.25 billion Transformation Fund to support the reform of services in England and Wales. The Barnett consequentials for allocations from this fund in 2025-26 will be notified at Westminster Main Estimates in June, with future years likely to form part of the Spending Review. It will be for the Executive to decide how any additional funding is used.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the level of vacancies and temporary promotions across the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: ANSWERAs at the end of March 2025, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 5,486 vacancies across the Civil Service. These figures reflect the challenges faced in managing and filling vacancies within the constraints of allocated financial and staffing resources.When a department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. Therefore, details of the total number of vacant posts in each department is not held centrally by NICSHR or the wider Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. As at the end March 2025, there were a total of 3,192 temporary promotion arrangements in place in the NICS.  Temporary promotions can be used for several different business reasons, such as to manage short-term needs or gaps within the workforce, cover sick absence, maternity leave, time bound projects. The NICS is actively working to reduce the number of temporary promotions however in instances such as special exercises or short-term projects filling these posts substantively would create a permanent budget pressure which would remain after the project has completed. The management and recording of temporary promotions are the responsibility of individual business areas in Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline who has responsibility for covering the cost of medical treatment received at the Dreadnought Medical Service by seafarers from Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Dreadnought is a medical service for seafarers (and, in some cases, their wives, husbands and children) based at St Thomas’ Hospital in London. It is not a separate unit in the hospital, patients are treated in whatever ward or facility is best suited to their needs.Dreadnought patients are treated according to clinical need, and are assigned to the in-patient or out-patient area most suitable for their medical condition. However, there is a designated administrative office, dealing exclusively with Dreadnought patients, headed by the Dreadnought administrator.The route of referral to access the Dreadnought services for NI residents falls in line with the tertiary referral process, managed by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health. This is called the Extra Contractual referral (ECR) process.Historically, central government has paid the cost of running the Dreadnought, and all medical treatment for seafarers eligible to use it, in the form of specially earmarked NHS funding. However, the Dreadnought admin team have confirmed that, it is now each devolved administration’s responsibility to review the appropriate applications and fund the requested treatment at the Dreadnought unit.For seafarers from Northern Ireland, the Dreadnought unit now requires a supporting letter from the NI seafarer’s local commissioning body, which is the Strategic Planning and Performance Group within my Department. This is to ensure treatment has been reviewed and approved for funding, falling in line with the established Extra Contractual Referral process, which has strict funding criteria in place to award funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's consideration of the need for rates exemptions for further healthcare premises, such as community pharmacies.
answer_text: Land and Property Services within the Department of Finance are responsible for non-domestic rating policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the NHS and healthcare employees that will be exempt from the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions from April 2025; and (ii) how that will apply in practice.
answer_text: There will be no actual exemption for NHS and healthcare employees, but funding will be provided to the NI Executive through the Barnett consequentials for the National Insurance increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail which type of healthcare premises are exempt from paying rates.
answer_text: The statutory framework setting out the criteria for Non-Domestic Rates Exemption is detailed in Article 41 Rates NI Order 1977. The District Valuer considers all applications for exemption against these provisions. The table below indicates the number of exempt and non-exempt properties in the primary classification “Hospitals, Clinics, Surgeries, Homes” at 31 March 2025.Property Sub-Class Non ExemptExemptTotalCentres and Surgeries707Hospital79180Residential Care Establishments other than Hospital5247771301Total6107781388Exemption is applicable where the property is occupied by a registered charity, for which the statutory requirements set out in Article 41(2)(c) have been fulfilled and to which the District Valuer has granted exemption. In addition to exemption which is determined by the District Valuer, Articles 31B of the Rates NI Order 1977 includes provision for 100% rate relief for qualifying hereditaments used by institutions for the provision of residential accommodation, training facilities or personal social services for persons with an illness or disability.  These types of premises are not distinguished as Exempt in the Valuation List, but rates relief may be awarded following an application process, if the statutory criteria is satisfied. 	At 31 March 2025, there are 480 properties in receipt of rate relief under Article 31B of the Rates NI Order 1977.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how clean and safe beaches are in South Down, given that the busy Easter holiday period is approaching.
answer_text: South Down contains seven bathing water locations, six identified sites and a further candidate site.  The most recent classifications after the 2024 bathing season are: Ballyhornan – Excellent; Cranfield – Excellent; Kilclief – Excellent; Murlough – Excellent; Newcastle – Sufficient; Tyrella – Excellent; and Warrenpoint – Sufficient.  The classification at Warrenpoint is preliminary in that it is based on two years of data, rather than the recommended four years.Current and historic findings for all bathing water sites are provided on the DAERA and NI Direct websites.I am also keen to emphasise the importance of water safety on our beaches and the need to be proactive in this area.  To this end, my Department has agreed to support Water Safety NI in order to assist with amplifying their message.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of (i) demand; and (ii) award for the Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund which closed on 31 December 2024.
answer_text: There were 59 applications received in total across the Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund 2024/25. 36 awards utilised the £3.1m EU replacement funding and 4 awards utilised the £350k Fisheries Green Growth funding. An additional 4 awards of £428k were made from the £600k Green Growth Blue Carbon funding budget. The anticipated £172k shortfall in spend against the available budget is a result of the late withdrawal of an application towards the end of December 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the current badger population in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how this number has changed in recent years.
answer_text: The last survey of badgers in Northern Ireland was in 2007/8, estimating a total population of 34,100 (7,600 social groups) More recent survey data is not available for comparison.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department's plans to reinstate trees lost though storm damage in forest parks in South Down.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn caused significant damage to our forests including in South Down. Forests particularly affected include Bohill, Castlewellan Forest Park, Tollymore Forest Park, Drumkerragh, Mourne and Rostrevor forests.  I understand from officials in Forest Service that plans for the required harvesting interventions following the storm are well advanced. However, due the extent of storm damage, these clearance interventions are expected to continue over the forthcoming year.  Re-establishing trees in the storm damaged areas will follow the clearance operations. This will include tree planting of complete areas worst affected and supplementary planting in areas where sufficient trees have withstood the effects of the storm.  Opportunities to regenerate trees naturally from local seed sources available will also form part of the re-establishment of the storm damaged woodlands. The re-establishment of trees provides for an increased diversity of species and improved forest resilience in the areas affected by the storm.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the areas in Northern Ireland that have seen rapid progression of coastal erosion.
answer_text: Under the existing policy and legislative framework, my Department is not responsible for assessing the risks of coastal erosion in Northern Ireland. The lack of coastal erosion risk management policy was recognised by previous Ministers who created the Coastal Forum to address this policy and legislative gap.As part of the work of the Coastal Forum, my Department has made good progress in addressing the shortfall of scientifically robust baseline data on how the Northern Ireland coastline is changing, by identifying and contextualising historical coastal change from the early 1800s to present day. Findings identify areas in Northern Ireland that have seen rapid progression of coastal erosion.  Significant change at certain coastal locations may be attributed to factors such as the local geology and the impact of wave hydrodynamics. Alternatively, other areas of significant erosion may be attributed to anthropogenic factors, such as the building of promenades or sea defences. This evidence and research is publicly accessible on the Northern Ireland Coastal Observatory website.While my Department has no remit to carry out assessments of coastal erosion, it is hoped that the provision of these data and tools will help inform those who do have to make decisions at the coast, as evidence is provided on how the coastline is changing at a localised scale. These key resources will also help to inform future policy and legislation in coastal erosion risk management.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the need to commission a review of the composition and structure of the Northern Ireland Civil Service as part of the objective to deliver reform.
answer_text: ANSWER	The Civil Service has planned a review of its Workforce Model to deliver in two phases: phase 1 by March 2026 and phase 2 by March 2027.  The Workforce Model is the framework that defines the structure and composition of the Civil Service, in terms of how jobs are defined and organised based on the types of work performed through professions and occupational groups. 		The review of the Workforce Model supports many of the transformation initiatives set out in the Civil Service People Strategy 2025 – 2030 which will be published soon, such as initiatives on future skills, recruitment and retention and is also integral to the digital transformation project, Integr8, for Civil HR and Finance services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the need to advise all Departments of the needs to plan for efficiencies and savings in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of individual Executive Ministers and Permanent Secretaries as Principal Accounting Officers to manage their departmental budgets including the consideration of efficiencies and savings measures.My department is leading on work to develop longer-term financial sustainability plans which will ask departments to set out their future plans, associated efficiencies and transformation opportunities. Departments will be asked to complete these plans by Autumn and once in place, these longer-term plans will be updated on a rolling basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the proportion of public sector staff in Northern Ireland compared to other jurisdictions across the United Kingdom.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 24377/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he expects the legislation bringing baby loss certificates to Northern Ireland to be enacted.
answer_text: ANSWERI am keen to see this initiative continue to be taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill, which will provide a power to make regulations to form a scheme, completed its First Stage in the Assembly on 25 March 2025.Work will follow – involving officials from the Department of Health – to consult with the stakeholders on the detail of the scheme, to draft the required secondary legislation and to operationalise these processes.If these matters progress smoothly, I anticipate that the scheme may be operational by the end of the year.

answer_text: ANSWERThe most up to date number of civil servants employed by the Civil Service here, broken down by (i) Department; and (ii) grade can be found in Table 3a of Employment in the Northern Ireland Civil Service - Quarterly Report 1st January 2025 - https://www.nisra.gov.uk/publications/employment-northern-ireland-civil-service-1-january-2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions that have taken place in relation to opportunities for Northern Ireland to avail of the UK National Wealth Fund to finance new infrastructure.
answer_text: Department of Finance officials have had a number of engagements with both Treasury officials and directly with representatives of the National Wealth Fund, prior to the launch of the Fund and since its formation. The aim of these engagements has been to discuss the remit of the Fund and to explore the potential for the Fund to finance investments here.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what skills shortages and market pressures were experienced in recruiting the seven posts in the Executive Office for which the rationale has been applied to award the Recruitment and Retention Allowance costing £9,750 per month, as detailed in the response to FOI DOF/2025-0078.
answer_text: The skills shortage experienced by The Executive Office (TEO) relates to specialist skills to draft primary legislation i.e. Executive Bills.  There is a very small external market for experienced drafters, and this gives rise to the known challenges in recruiting and retaining such drafters for the Office of the Legislative Counsel (which is part of TEO). question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why the Tourism NI Quality Grading Scheme, which operated from 1 April 2012 to 14 August 2023, set fees at a deficit where the income was £553,404 and expenditure was £1,122,217, giving a loss of £568,813 to public funds.
answer_text: Following establishment of the Quality Grading Scheme (QGS), Tourism NI subsidised the costs of the scheme by setting fees at a deficit in recognition of the value of the scheme to the tourism sector and to encourage industry participation in it.A planned review of fees was delayed by the Covid-19 pandemic. However, my Department and Tourism NI are currently undertaking a comprehensive review of the costs of operating the QGS scheme and the associated fees to be charged. Department of Finance approval is in place to operate the QGS at a deficit until this review is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any of the £553,404, charged by Tourism NI since June 2011 under the voluntary Quality Assurance Grading Scheme for the serviced accommodation sector and under Article 21 of The Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992, which was paused in November 2022 as it was found Tourism NI did not have the authority to charge such fees, was returned to the payees.
answer_text: The Quality Grading Scheme for visitor accommodation operates on a voluntary membership basis. A membership fee is charged annually by Tourism NI, in advance of the annual assessment visit.The Scheme was paused temporarily in November 2022. At the time, there was fee income with an aggregate value of £20,985.60 received by Tourism NI where an assessment visit had not yet been undertaken. Tourism NI informed the payees of these fees of their eligibility for a refund. No refunds were requested, and payees received an assessment once the Scheme resumed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the status of Tourism NI’s Annual Report and Accounts for (a) 2022-23; and (b) 2023-24; and (ii) where they are published.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office’s (NIAO) audit of Tourism NI’s 2022-23 Annual Report and Accounts is complete. The accounts will be presented to the Tourism NI Board for approval on 20 March 2025 and, subject to approval, will be published on the Tourism NI website when NIAO certification is obtained. The Northern Ireland Audit Office’s (NIAO) audit of Tourism NI’s 2023-24 Annual Report and Accounts is in the final completion stages. The accounts will be published on the Tourism NI website when NIAO certification is obtained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 23769/22-27, to assess the opportunity to increase the return on investment of public funds by investing the £193 million collected up front for rates to earn interest income for the Northern Ireland Consolidated Fund.
answer_text: Rates income must be paid into the NI Consolidated Fund (the Exchequer). The Rates (NI) Order 1977 section 9(2) states “For all purposes, including the purposes of section 4 of the Crown Debts (Ireland) Act 1781, the whole of the rate comprised of the items mentioned in paragraph (1) shall be a debt due to the Crown.”Section 4 of the Crown Debts (Ireland) Act 1781 states that “monies levied by virtue of such a process shall be duly paid into the receipt of the Exchequer.”Once the funds have been paid into the NI Consolidated Fund, the Statement of Funding Policy states that the interest received goes to the UK Consolidated Fund. See extract below:Managing the Consolidated Funds8.1 Each of the devolved administrations manages its own Consolidated Fund. These funds are the vehicle into which the cash associated with the block grant and other allocations from the UK Government is paid, alongside much of the funding raised by the administrations themselves.8.2 The devolved administrations are responsible for managing their Consolidated Funds according to the following principles:balances should be placed on overnight deposit and the interest received should be returned to the UK Consolidated Fund, where appropriate, through the usual channelsThe processes currently in place adhere to these requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has set a target to report on how many additional local small businesses will be able to access public sector contracts under his new simplified procurement practices.
answer_text: The revised Procurement Policy Note 04/21 has increased the Procurement Control Limits to reflect increases in inflation over the last five years. Additional flexibility has also been introduced to help public bodies target spend below £50,000 (£65,000 for Utilities) to micro businesses and the Voluntary, Community and Social Enterprise Sectors. For these contracts, public bodies can seek price checks or quotations by email. This simplification of the process will mean that the businesses will not be required to use the eTendersNI system which can be seen as a barrier by these groups. As these contracts will not be procured through the tendering system, there will be no mechanism to report on the spend. Therefore, establishing a target would create a disproportionate burden as manual records would be required to be collated and analysed. For contracts above £50,000 (£65,000 for Utilities), I plan to bring forward a revised Public Procurement Policy Statement which will increase the transparency in terms of the value and volume of public contracts awarded to SMEs, Micro Businesses and Social Enterprises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the regularity with which the PSNI are unable to apprehend offenders during a pursuit as a result of those offenders crossing the border.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of why Northern Ireland has a higher level of public sector workers than in the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The latest figures from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) estimate that 26% of those in employment in the North, were employed by the public sector in December 2024. This compares to 24% in Wales, 23% in Scotland, and 17% in England.However, there are a number of issues which mean that such comparisons need to be treated with caution. Some services in the public sector here, are delivered by the private sector in Britain, for example, water utilities. While our private sector is relatively smaller in the North when compared to Britain. In addition, ONS indicates that the use of workplace-based Labour Force Survey estimates of total employment may overestimate the proportion of public sector employment locally.It is encouraging that private sector employment locally has grown by 13% over the past decade, outpacing that of public sector employment (6%) and above growth in Britain (10%) over this time. A continued growth in private sector jobs is a trajectory I hope will continue as the Executive takes forward its commitments in the delivery of our Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the accuracy of the opening balances in the Department for the Economy Group Accounts 2024-25.
answer_text: The Department implemented a new model to produce the DfE Group Annual Report and Accounts (the Accounts) for the year ended 31 March 2024.  In doing so it re-processed the corresponding information for the year ended 31 March 2023 through this new model and identified a number of differences to the certified 2022-23 Accounts. Under international accounting standards, the Department was required to correct material errors in comparative figures within its 2023-24 Accounts. The Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) in her report on the Accounts acknowledged that the Department made these adjustments as a result of its efforts to improve its consolidation process and the quality of its accounting information.  The audit of the 2023-24 closing balances did not raise any material issues with Group figures. The Department has already carried out a verification exercise with its component bodies to agree those closing balances being carried forward.
