question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of whether there are sufficient qualified accountants employed within her Department and its group bodies.
answer_text: The number of qualified Accountants within the Department and its Arm’s-length bodies (ALBs) is kept under frequent review. In her recent report on the 2023-24 Accounts, the Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) highlighted the complexity of the Departmental Group from an accounting perspective with 18 component bodies in addition to the core Department itself.  The C&AG’s report noted particular issues which arose during the audit in respect of Northern Regional College.  The Department continues to engage with the College about the capacity of its finance team and how the Department can provide direct support in the short to medium term.The Department has already implemented several improvements in preparation for the 2024-25 Accounts including further increasing the capacity of its Finance team and continues to engage with its ALBs to ensure their finance teams are sufficiently resourced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of (i) the £137.4 million which the Northern Ireland Audit Office reported that her Department was not able to provide sufficient explanations and documentation to conclude on the appropriateness of 19 adjustments her Department made; and (ii) whether this amounts to monies lost to the Northern Ireland pubic purse.
answer_text: (i) The Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) disclaimed her audit opinion on the basis of Prior Period Adjustments (PPAs) that the Department for Economy (DfE) made to the 31 March 2023 comparative figures in the 31 March 2024 Accounts.  These adjustments were identified by DfE and arose as a result of the Department implementing a new, more robust model to produce the Group Accounts for the year ended 31 March 2024. The £137.4 million absolute value of unexplained PPAs is made up of a total of 19 adjustments to the Statement of Comprehensive Net Expenditure (SoCNE) and the Statement of Financial Position (SoFP) over the two prior financial years.  The net impact on the Net Assets in the SOFP for 2021-22 is a reduction of £15.29 million and an increase for £1.59 million for 2022-23.  This is offset by an equivalent movement in reserves therefore the net amount of the PPAs for both 2021-22 and 2022-23 is £nil.  The net impact of the reclassification of expenses in the SoCNE is £nil.  The individual areas impacted are outlined in Annex A and are set out in the published Accounts. The Department invested significant resource and time in investigating the origin of the errors in prior years, but several balances remain unexplained. The Department made the decision that it had exhausted all reasonable avenues to try to identify the prior year errors and that its resources would be better allocated to ensuring that adequate processes were in place to deliver robust figures for 31 March 2024 and beyond.  This approach was discussed with the Northern Ireland Audit Office and endorsed by the Department’s Audit, Risk and Assurance Committee. (ii) The PPAs do not amount to monies lost from the public purse.  Each DfE component body consolidated within the DfE Group Annual Report and Accounts are subject to their own individual independent audit.  For those components with a year end that does not align with the Department’s i.e.  a non-31 March year end, they are required to complete an accounting pack containing Financial Statements and appropriate disclosures with supporting documentation as at the 31 March for consolidation, which is also independently audited.  The issues that arose with the PPAs, as described by the C&AG in her report, do not relate to any individual component accounts, but rather the consolidation process and its complexity. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions taken to address the accounting errors made by staff which resulted in a disclaimed audit opinion on her Department's Annual Report and Accounts 2023-24.
answer_text: The Comptroller and Auditor General disclaimed her audit opinion on the basis of Prior Period Adjustments that the Department for Economy (DfE) made to the 31 March 2023 comparative figures in the 31 March 2024 Accounts.  While this is disappointing, the adjustments were identified by DfE and arose as a result of the Department implementing a new, more robust model to produce the Group Accounts for the year ended 31 March 2024.The Department has already implemented several improvements in preparation for the 2024-25 Accounts including: further increasing the capacity and capability of the Finance team; increasing support and communications with component bodies; maximising the use of IT solutions to improve communication channels with all key stakeholders throughout the audit process; and enhancing overall review procedures for the consolidation process. A further programme of work, involving working with Partner Organisations and other stakeholders, is already underway. This involves: finalising a granular level project plan for production of the Accounts; agreeing a detailed plan for the audit with NIAO to work towards laying the 2024/25 Accounts by the statutory date; and carrying out a further review of structures within Finance Division to identify resources to re-allocate to this priority area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 23323/22-27, to detail (i) the number of Northern Ireland Civil Service staff that have taken up the Translink annual travel cards scheme in each of the last three financial years; and (ii) whether there is any cost to the public purse of this scheme.
answer_text: ANSWERIn the last three financial years, in total 57 different civil servants availed of the Translink annual travel card scheme. The table below shows the number of salary advancements, by financial year, processed to recoup the cost of the annual travel cards purchased by these officers. These figures do not total 57 as many of the civil servants purchased a card in more than one year therefore had more than one salary advancement over the period.  Number of Salary Advancements to Civil Servants to Purchase a Translink Annual Travel Card2021/222022/232023/24443534Salary advancements for the purchase of these annual travel cards are recouped in full and deducted from the cardholder’s salary each month. There is no cost to the public purse for this scheme other than a small administrative resource to process the salary advancements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 23325/22-27, how long after a change of location will a staff member receive these payments.
answer_text: Excess Fares Allowance (EFA) is generally provided for a period of three years from the date of transfer and is not extended for days when it is not payable. Departments are authorised to pay EFA for up to five years for transfers where a move of home would otherwise be necessary. This discretion is typically exercised only in exceptional circumstances and when there is a saving on removal expenses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 23325/22-27, whether this allowance has been reviewed to assess if the staff members travel to their workplace every day or takes hybrid working into account.
answer_text: ANSWERExcess Fares Allowance (EFA) is reimbursed only for days the staff member travels to work. The Authorising Officer (usually the line manager) is responsible for verifying claims are accurate and not claimed when EFA is not payable, such as during periods of sick leave, annual leave, detached duty or on days when the member of staff might be working from home under the hybrid working policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline recent engagements that the Executive Office attended in Washington DC.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister travelled to Washington DC for St Patrick’s Day from 11th to 14th March. The programme included meetings and engagements with the President of the United States; the White House Reception; the  Speaker's Luncheon; members of Congress and President Trump's nominees for UK and Ireland Ambassadors; the British Ambassador and his Reception with the Secretary of State for NI; the Irish Ambassador's Reception, including discussion with the Taoiseach, the Irish Ambassador and other stakeholders; an 'in conversation' with Georgetown University students; the British Embassy Reception to recognise Ulster University's status as UK and Ireland University of the Year; the Queen's University event with the  Smithsonian; the US Department of State; a fireside chat with the State Department at the Washington Forum; a business roundtable with the US Chamber of Commerce; the Ireland Funds Gala Dinner; and hosting NI's networking Breakfast for 250 guests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any commitment he received from the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the recent Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee on progress on Agriculture Property Relief.
answer_text: I raised significant concerns around Agricultural Property Relief with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee on 27 February 2025.While acknowledging the concerns raised the Chief Secretary to the Treasury gave no indication that the policy would be reconsidered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 22827/22-27, to detail (i) how his Department manages the significant income received within the first month of the financial year to ensure its maximum potential return on investment for the public purse; and (ii) the return on the investment of the (a) £196 million early payment in the 2023-24 financial year; (b) £181 million early payment in the 2022-23 financial year; and (c) £176 million early payment in the 2021-22 financial year.
answer_text: ANSWERCash received by Land & Property Services in relation to rates payments is paid into the NI Consolidated Fund on a daily basis. This is taken into account when determining the daily cash grant that is drawn from the NIO, which therefore reduces the cost to the public purse as a whole.As we operate under resource accounting and budgeting rules, the timing of cash receipts is irrelevant in terms of impact on the Executive's budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any commitment he received from the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the recent Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee on an amount which will be contributed towards the increased employers' National Insurance contributions in Northern Ireland public services, including any timeline for this.
answer_text: The matter of increased employer National Insurance Contributions was discussed in detail at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee meeting on 27 February.The Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) again stated that the devolved governments would receive Barnett consequentials on additional funding provided to Whitehall department budgets for increased employers National Insurance Contributions. The quantum will be confirmed at Westminster Main Estimates expected in June. The devolved governments are facing a shortfall as result of this decision taken by Westminster. In my view this is not an acceptable position. I made my view clear to the CST.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any commitment he received from the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the recent Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee on progress on Hospitality VAT in Northern Ireland and across the United Kingdom.
answer_text: ANSWERI raised the matter of Hospitality VAT with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at our bilateral meeting on 27 February.I will be following this up with the Exchequer Secretary to the Treasury following the completion of the Cost of Doing Business research. This independent evidence which my Department has commissioned will enable me to provide as strong as possible a case for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any commitment he received from the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the recent Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee on potential increased investment in childcare in Northern Ireland and across the United Kingdom.
answer_text: I raised the matter of tax-free childcare at my bilateral meeting with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 27 February.The Chief Secretary to the Treasury advised that matters such as this should be considered as part of Phase 2 of the Spending Review, which is due to conclude in June 2025. I will be raising the issue again in my input to the Spending Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 22347/22-27, whether a woman whose result is presenting as positive or indicative of a need for further action is expedited in communication.
answer_text: All cervical screening samples (‘slides’) with an HPV positive result also require cytology reporting. This is a multi-step process with all samples reviewed by at least two people. The regional laboratory has advised that if a significant finding is noted at the first stage of the cytology process, the slide is then prioritised to a consultant for a final screen so that the patient can be referred directly to colposcopy if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 22347/22-27, to detail (i) the average length of time taken for women to receive the result of their cervical screening test; (ii) the longest length of time a woman has had to wait for the result of her cervical screening test; and (iii) the target turnaround time on returning results of cervical screening tests to women.
answer_text: An average waiting time for all cervical screening samples received in the laboratory cannot be provided as cervical samples are managed in different pathways through the laboratory and is dependent on the result of the HPV test and whether additional cytology reporting is required.  The waiting time for most laboratory results to issue to a woman’s GPs is currently up to 12 weeks. There have been delays in the authorisation process for the completed test results to issue to GPs, as the process is currently manual. An electronic auto-authorisation system is expected to ‘go live’ from 12 March 2025, which will speed the process up.As of 3 March 2025, the cervical sample which is currently waiting the longest for the laboratory result to be issued to the GP is 110 days. The NHS England standards for cervical screening can be found at  https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/cervical-screening-programme-standards. These revised standards were endorsed by the  Northern Ireland Screening Committee and my Department in July 2019, as the standards to be adopted by the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme. These state that greater than, or equal to, 98% of women should expect to receive their screening results in writing within 14 days from the date of the sample being taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any financial incentives in place to encourage Northern Ireland Civil Service staff to use public transport to travel to work.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Civil Service offers all staff the opportunity to purchase Translink annual travel cards by an advance of salary, which is recouped monthly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of Northern Ireland Civil Service staff receiving travel allowances due to being required to move location of their base employment; and (ii) the value of these travel allowances, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: ANSWERCivil servants required to relocate their primary workplace may qualify for Excess Fares Allowance (EFA). EFA compensates for additional travel expenses resulting from a permanent, compulsory transfer. The Civil Service HR Handbook details the eligibility criteria for EFA. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of car parking spaces; and (ii) the value of car parking spaces paid for Northern Ireland Civil Service staff, broken down by (a) Department; and (ii) grade.
answer_text: The Department of Finance does not separately lease car parking spaces.At present, 52 buildings across the Civil Service Office Estate have car parking spaces attached to them. There is a total of 5,532 parking spaces across the 52 buildings, including 1,494 available on the Stormont Estate. The parking spaces are used and managed by the departments and public bodies located at each building and usage is not broken down by department or grade.The annual running costs of the car parking spaces are included in the total costs for the buildings and cannot be disaggregated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether, within the Northern Ireland Civil Service Office Estate Strategy stage on A Leaner NICS Office Estate, there will be a quantification of the number of days staff now work from home under the 2021 hybrid working policy.
answer_text: The Office Estate Strategy has been developed on an average 50% occupancy ratio across the office estate. Some business areas may require staff to be in the office more often than others and these specific occupancy levels are considered when designing individual consolidation projects.The NICS Hybrid Working Policy provides the framework for hybrid working arrangements. The Policy is currently being reviewed and includes consideration of the Office Estate Strategy and minimum workplace attendance requirements. Any changes to the Policy will be subject to consultation with Trade Unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how the performance is being assessed of Health and Social Care Trusts where data has not been provided to NISRA statistical reports due to the roll-out of encompass; and (ii) why those Health and Social Care Trusts cannot produce this data in another format for the purposes of transparency.
answer_text: While validated data is not always readily available during HSC Trusts’ transition to the new Encompass system, my Department does have access to unvalidated data which is used to assess Trust performance.  In addition, other management information is available via Trust manual returns which is used to measure progress. This data includes HSC Service Delivery Plan returns and theatre productivity data which are discussed at regular Trust level performance meetings chaired by Departmental officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16082/22-27, to detail his Department’s responsibility over the management and maintenance of the Aughrim River, Kilkeel.
answer_text: DfI leads on the maintenance of the Aughrim River. Under the terms of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973, the Aughrim River is considered to be an undesignated watercourse. Therefore, the responsibility for the maintenance of the Aughrim River lies with the riparian landowners. If necessary, the Department for Infrastructure has powers under the Drainage Order to enforce riparian landowners to undertake maintenance works.		My Department has a lead role in the management of waters under the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017. This is effected through an integrated catchment approach as outlined in the draft 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan for Northern Ireland which involves a range of public bodies and organisations. The Plan includes the Programme of Measures to protect and improve our waterways through regulation, education and policy initiatives like the innovative Soil Nutrient Health Scheme. Such an approach applies to the Aughrim River in Kilkeel. 	The draft River Basin Management Plan has been prepared, consulted on and reviewed in light of emerging information on water quality and recent reports from the Office of Environmental Protection, the Northern Ireland Audit Office and the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan. I have approved the third cycle River Basin Management Plan and subject to Executive approval, I will publish the plan in the near future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the scale of offshore wind farms planned for Northern Ireland; and (ii) the impact they will have on the fishing industry.
answer_text: Potential impacts of offshore renewable energy development on the fishing industry are outlined in a public consultation on the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) Environmental Report and Report to Inform Appropriate Assessment (RIAA). This consultation is being undertaken by the Department for Economy and is informed by evidence provided by my department and the fishing industry. The consultation outlines potential renewable energy zones and gives stakeholders an opportunity to provide input into offshore renewable energy policy development. My department will continue to work with the Department for Economy to ensure full consideration is given to the potential impacts of offshore renewable energy development on the fishing industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current length of time taken to return results of smear tests to women following testing, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All laboratory services for cervical screening in Northern Ireland moved to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trusts from 1 November 2024. As outlined in AQW 22347-22-27, this service is now provided by a single regional laboratory and as such a breakdown of the current waiting times by Health and Social Care Trust is not applicable. The current wait time for the laboratory results of cervical smear tests to issue to an individual’s GPs is up to 12 weeks. The time to receive a result is dependent on the result of the HPV test and whether additional cytology reporting is required. However, there are ongoing delays in the authorisation of completed test results to GPs as the process is currently manual. Testing of an electronic auto-authorisation system is underway and this process is expected to be completed in the next few weeks, which will speed the process up. Of the samples authorised during the week commencing 10th February 2025, 11.3% were authorised in less than 8 weeks and the remainder were longer than 8 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) why the target for rate collection is not 100 per cent; and (ii) how the current target of 93 per cent was arrived at.
answer_text: ANSWERWhen target setting for Rate Collection, there is a strong aspiration to achieve 100% collection of all rates that are due for payment. However, this aspiration is balanced with the reality that it is not possible to collect all rates within a single rating year.There are many reasons that prevent full collection, these include:Individual ratepayers’ bankruptcy and company insolvency events that arise outside the control of Land & Property Services (LPS) that occur throughout the rating year,Enforcement of legal actions to obtain payment of unpaid rates,Challenges to property Valuation,Liability assessments raised and billed throughout the rating year (through property conveyance, change in ownership/occupation or new property being added to the Valuation List),Challenging financial circumstances faced by ratepayers that inhibit payment in full within 12 months,Individual decisions made by Ratepayers not to pay rates.LPS will exercise a range of legal recovery procedures through the courts system, however this does not necessarily result in payment. To maximise rate collection and reduce loss, LPS engages with ratepayers that have rate arrears to pay rates owing through agreed payment arrangements over an extended period of time.Taking account of previous year rate collection performance and recognising a wide range of external factors that impact on collection activities, LPS set collection targets that stretch performance and increase rate collection. The 2023/24 target of 92.5% Gross Rate Collection was achieved. In achieving this target, LPS raised the target for 2024/25 to 93%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the move to encompass in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) whether there has been a review of lessons learnt which will assist in the move to encompass in other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: (i) encompass is a system that has been designed by bringing clinicians and other care professionals together to agree a ‘once for Northern Ireland’ way to support care pathways thus creating a more consistent way for care to be delivered across the province.South Eastern Trust were pathfinders in the process and while they implemented the regional solution, as the first HSC Trust to Go-Live with encompass, they provided significant insight into the system and identified and worked through early issues and challenges.With the support of all staff across the organisation and in partnership with encompass, Epic and other Trusts, implementation has been successful, the system is well embedded in the Trust and measurable benefits are already being realised.  This includes improved outpatient clinic utilisation, saved clinical time as charts can be viewed instantly, more timely interventions, increased safety in the administration of medications and better utilisation of resources and appointments with a reduction in ‘Did Not Attend’ rates.Alongside these quantitative benefits, there are also an array of qualitative measures, where staff highlight the huge benefits of a fully integrated platform for delivering joined up health and social care, including safety, efficiencies and other upsides.(ii) The lessons learned through the South Eastern Trust implementation and stabilisation periods were shared with the other Trusts as they prepared for, and completed their own Go-Lives.  Through each implementation, the experience in the system and the processes involved in its implementation are strengthened and significant learning is carried forward into each subsequent Go-Live to support planning and to minimise impact on patients and clients.This has materialised in three successful implementations to date and HSCNI is in a strong position for the joint Go-Live in Southern and Western Trusts in May this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has instructed Health and Social Care Trusts to impose time restriction guidance on the amount of time Domiciliary Care workers spend in each patient’s home.
answer_text: I can confirm that my department has not issued such instruction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the targets set out in the Office Estate Review across (i) Short Term (2022 – 2025): Greater Belfast; (ii) Medium Term (2022 – 2030): Regions; and (iii) Long Term (2026 – 2035): Belfast.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department plans to publish a report outlining progress on the Civil Service Office Estate Strategy up to 31 March 2025, which incorporates the targets in the Office Estate Review. The progress report will be published by 31 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the additional cost for the Northern Ireland public sector in relation to increased employers' contributions; (ii) the additional funding that will be provided towards this cost; and (iii) whether he has advised Departments of the expected shortfall value to include the extra costs in their 2025-26 draft budget plans.
answer_text: ANSWERIn December 2024, departments provided the Department of Finance with estimated costs in relation to increased employer’s National Insurance Contributions of some £200 million. This relates to departments and Non-Departmental Public Bodies only.  In addition, correspondence from councils has indicated they are facing additional costs in the region of £12 million. I am continuing to press the Treasury to cover these additional costs in full and to provide support for other sectors. However, the Treasury has advised that the devolved governments will receive Barnett consequentials on additional funding provided to Whitehall apartments. Based on discussions with the Treasury, our internal assumption is that the Barnett consequentials are likely to be in the region of £140/145 million. This is not a confirmed position and amounts ultimately provided may be different.The additional funding will not be confirmed until the conclusion of the Westminster Main Estimate process, this is usually in June. This means that the additional funding will be allocated through the in-year monitoring process. I intend to invite bids for this purpose in the June Monitoring Round.My Executive colleagues have been informed of the potential shortfall in the support provided by the Treasury. It is for departments to consider their draft Budget positions, in this context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for her assessment of the £2.0 million cost to her Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action she is taking to reduce this cost to her Department, including any targets put in place by her Department’s Human Resources business partners.
answer_text: Department for Economy implements sickness absence policies set out by Northern Ireland Civil Services Human Resources (NICS HR) which is part of Department of Finance. Analysis of data is held by the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his latest assessment of progress on the recommendations of the Report on Capacity and Capability in the Northern Ireland Civil Service by the Public Accounts Committee.
answer_text: ANSWERA substantial body of work has been undertaken on Civil Service Capacity and Capability. The Department of Finance Memorandum on the Fifth Report from the Public Accounts Committee Mandate 2017-2022 - ‘Capacity and Capability in the Northern Ireland Civil Service’ presented to the NI Assembly in August 2021 contained 12 commitments. Of the 12 commitments five are deemed achieved; three are on track for achievement; and four are likely to be achieved with some delay. Progress against the commitments continues to be monitored as part of the Department of Finance departmental accountability processes and reviewed regularly by the Civil Service Board. My further assessment of progress will be informed by the current review of Civil Service Capacity and Capability being undertaken by the NI Audit Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the £1 million cost to his Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce this cost to his Department, including any targets put in place by his Department’s Human Resources business partners.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the £1.1 million cost to his Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce this cost to his Department, including any targets put in place by his Department’s Human Resources business partners.
answer_text: (i) NICS sickness absence policy is a matter for the Minister of Finance.(ii) The NICS People Plan 2024-25 developed by NICSHR, in conjunction with departments, detailed the NICS commitment to provide the tools and interventions required to support the health and wellbeing of all staff and assist line managers to effectively manage sickness absence.  One of the key outworkings of this is the NICS Health and Wellbeing Strategy and Action Plan, launched in January 2025, which will enable us to develop targeted interventions at both a NICS-wide and departmental level.  In all departments line managers have responsibility for managing sickness absence in line with the relevant HR policies and procedures. NICSHR also has a key role, supporting line managers and ensuring cases are progressed in a timely and supportive manner.  Recent measures taken forward by NICSHR to strengthen line manager support include:The development of a Sickness Absence Toolkit to build line manager skills and knowledge when managing sicknessUpdated, mandatory sickness absence training for all new line managersThe development of a new Line Manager Brochure to assist line managers in a range of functions, including the management of staff health and wellbeingThese measures will help line managers to support their teams before, during and after a period of sickness absence, leading to improved outcomes both for the individual and the organisation.In addition, in October 2024 NICSHR established the NICS Mental Health Forum as a mechanism for departments to identify and share good practice and develop collaborative approaches to support staff.  Work is also underway to strengthen and expand the services provided by the NICS Occupational Health Service to support a more proactive approach to sickness absence management by providing early intervention, holistic care, and tailored support to employees. Officials have also taken the lead in establishing a public sector Occupational Health (OH) Forum, to develop a system-wide collaborative approach to the transformation of OH services across the public sector here, to deliver value and outcomes that would be difficult or impossible to achieve individually. NICSHR has recently developed Management Information Dashboards for analysing sickness related data. Data is extracted from live HRConnect Records, enabling earlier access to information to identify any emerging trends of rising sickness, including sickness reasons. This will allow a more proactive approach to addressing issues and employing support to business areas and staff as necessary. There are no agreed departmental targets to reduce sickness absence levels, at this time, and it is not within the remit of HR Business Partners to manage sickness absence or set such targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the £6 million cost to his Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce this cost to his Department, including any targets put in place by his Department’s Human Resources business partners.
answer_text: My department recognises the need for an understanding and supportive approach towards managing sick absence to reduce the financial impact of ill health on the department and the public purse. In addressing sick absence levels my department takes its lead from the Department of Finance (DoF) which is responsible for the NICS Sick Absence policy, and has developed a multifacted range of proactive, preventative, and sustained interventions for the complex issue.HR Business Partners do not set targets relating to sickness absence. In all departments line managers manage sickness absence in accordance with NICS HR policies and procedures with all new managers required to undertake mandatory sickness absence training. NICS HR also has a key role, supporting line managers and ensuring cases are progressed in a timely and supportive manner.  The NICS People Plan 2024-25, developed by NICS HR in conjunction with other NICS departments including my own, details the commitment to support the health and wellbeing of staff and line managers to effectively manage sickness absence. Initiatives arising from the People Plan include a Health and Wellbeing Strategy; a Line Manager Brochure and associated Sickness Absence Toolkit; and the establishment of the NICS Mental Health Forum, all of which enable good practice to be developed and shared through collaborative engagement across the NICS.In addition, my department is represented on, and actively engages with, three Department for Communities (DfC) Social Inclusion Strategy working groups namely those on Mental Health, Autism, and Disability. DAERA has also appointed ten departmental WELL Champions. The Champions support the NICS WELL programme which incorporates support, education and information on a wide range of health and wellbeing issues which is available to all Northern Ireland Civil Service staff online.The above measures are all focused on informing and supporting staff and line managers before, during and after a period of sickness absence, with the objective of improved outcomes both for the individual, the organisation and the impact of sick absences on the public purse.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the scoping exercise on a business support scheme for the childcare sector.

To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the £5.2 million cost to his Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce this cost to his Department, including any targets put in place by his Department’s Human Resources business partners.
answer_text: I recognise that the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS), like other sectors, has been experiencing an upward trend in sickness absence levels, underscoring the urgent need for investment in proactive strategies to support employee health and well-being.The NICS People Plan 2024-25 developed by NICSHR, in conjunction with departments, detailed the NICS commitment to provide the tools and interventions required to support the health and wellbeing of all staff and assist line managers to effectively manage sickness absence. In all departments line managers are responsible for managing sickness absence in line with the relevant HR policies and procedures. Work is also underway to strengthen and expand the services provided by the NICS Occupational Health Service to support a more proactive approach to sickness absence management by providing early intervention, holistic care, and tailored support to employees. Officials have also taken the lead in establishing a public sector Occupational Health (OH) Forum, to develop a system-wide collaborative approach to the transformation of OH services across the public sector here, to deliver value and outcomes that would be difficult or impossible to achieve individually. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for her assessment of the £5.1 million cost to her Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action she is taking to reduce this cost to her Department, including any targets put in place by her Department’s Human Resources business partners.
answer_text: (i) NICSHR, established in 2017, consolidated services from Corporate HR Business Partners, the Centre for Applied Learning (CAL), and departmental HR functions. It oversees the standardisation and implementation of the NICS Managing Attendance Policy, with the Minister of Finance responsible for the NICS Sickness Absence Policy. Due to budgetary pressures, reducing this cost is a priority for my department. (ii) Currently, NICS HR has no departmental targets for reducing sickness absence, and HR Business Partners do not manage or set such targets. NICSHR has recently developed Management Information Dashboards for analysing sickness related data. Data is extracted from live HRConnect Records, enabling earlier access to information to identify any emerging trends of rising sickness, including sickness reasons. This will allow a more proactive approach to addre

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the £12.2 million cost to his Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to reduce this cost to his Department, including any targets put in place by his Department’s Human Resources business partners.
answer_text: With a workforce of over 11,000 people (almost 40% of the total NICS workforce) it may be expected that we will have a higher sickness level than smaller departments. However, the cost of sickness absence is not something I or my department takes lightly.  Whilst sickness levels in my department are not the highest in the NICS, and are still short of pre-pandemic levels, my officials will continue to work with their managers and NICSHR to explore interventions to reduce sickness absence.My department is proactive in its efforts to reduce sickness levels.  It has developed its own People Strategy and Health & Well-being Framework and we are making steady progress having committed to a range of initiatives, including the provision of Mental Health First Aiders, Well Champions, a Bereavement Toolkit, a Carers Forum and the offer of health checks.  These initiatives are available to all staff, but we know it will take time for them to have lasting impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 14829/22-27, (i) for her assessment of the £9.3 million cost to her Department in relation to Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence in 2023-24; and (ii) to detail any action she is taking to reduce this cost to her Department, including any targets put in place by her Department’s Human Resources business partners.
answer_text: Reducing the level of sickness absence is a priority for my Department, as well as the Northern Ireland Civil Service, to minimise cost and the impact on service delivery.  All sickness absences within my Department are managed in line with the relevant NICS sickness absence policies.  The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on the development of those policies which aim to promote regular attendance at work, minimise the level of absence, and to provide a framework within which sickness absence can be effectively managed.   There is ongoing engagement between DoJ staff and NICSHR colleagues within the DoF, including HR Business Partners, to review and consider levels of sickness absence across the Department and potential management actions.   While there are no specific targets developed by HR Business Partners for reducing the level of sickness absence, work has been ongoing to seek to understand the underlying causes of sickness absence.  Within the Department there is emphasis on the management of absences through HR processes as well as promoting health and well-being initiatives, in the context of an understanding and supportive approach.  For example, given the prevalence of mental health issues, both in the workplace and wider society, the NICS has taken a proactive approach to promoting and supporting colleagues, offering a comprehensive range of interventions. This includes a welfare support service, access to a free 24-hour counselling service, alongside wellbeing, mental health awareness and sickness absence training interventions for staff and managers.  It is also important to note that while staff within the Department experience the same types of illnesses as the wider population, due to the nature of the Department’s business, a high number of staff working in front-line operational roles, such as the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS), deal with unique challenges not faced by other parts of the NICS, which can impact on levels of sickness absence. Given the nature of those roles, the NIPS provides a range of interventions to assist staff who have reported a sickness absence including access to an embedded HR Strategic Business Partnering Team who offer confidential stress intervention interviews as well as providing a referral service to Welfare Support Services and the Occupational Health Service.  NIPS has also put in place additional support for Prison Officers through its arrangement with the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) who are suffering with mental illness or who have sustained a physical injury that they can be referred to them for counselling and\or physiotherapy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 21519/22-27, to detail (i) why there are 909 classroom assistants in Northern Ireland awaiting outcomes of Access NI checks as at 29 January 2025; (ii) when these outstanding checks will be completed; and (iii) whether she has any plans to work with the Education Authority to ensure these checks are completed as quickly as possible.
answer_text: As of 12 February 2025, there are 256 Enhanced disclosure applications from the Education Authority with AccessNI (50 of which are for the role of Classroom Assistant).  Of these cases, 78 were received within the previous 24 hours of the date of reporting, with the remaining 178 are currently at various stages of processing, including under-consideration by police in accordance with established procedures.This would suggest that the majority of the 909 Classroom Assistant applications referenced are currently at a preliminary application stage, either with the applicant or the employer (for checking identity documentation, etc), and have not yet been submitted to AccessNI. AccessNI is currently processing Enhanced disclosure applications within the previous 24 hours of receipt.  c75% of these will be completed, with disclosure certificates issued, within 1-2 days; the remaining c25% will have to be referred to police to consider the relevance of any non-conviction information they might hold.  PSNI are currently returning 92% of such referrals within 14 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 16293/22-27, whether she is reviewing the procedure by which the PSNI book their travel to prevent a repeat of the £128,000 loss, described as fruitless payments in her Department’s 2023-24 Annual Report and Accounts, where expenditure was not able to be cancelled in time to avoid a charge or cost.
answer_text: My Department has no role with respect to PSNI internal administrative procedures, and it would, therefore, be inappropriate for me to comment.  You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable or the Policing Board, to which PSNI are accountable for such operational and administrative matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18415/22-27, to detail his plans for his Department’s estate, in particular the 30 vacant sites owned by his Department and Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Department’s strategy for vacant estate aligns with the objectives of the Executive’s Asset Management Strategy.  Health ALBs are required to record vacant assets, rationalisation plans and provide progress updates against agreed rationalisation targets, in annual Property Asset Management Plans. Only vacant assets which have a clear foreseeable health need should be retained; the remainder are to be declared surplus.Of the 30 vacant assets, 14 are in the disposal process, 9 are identified for re-use subject to capital investment and 7 are being assessed to determine the best value for money option.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for results of cervical screening tests, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All laboratory services for cervical screening in Northern Ireland moved to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trusts from 1 November 2024. As this service is now provided by a single regional laboratory, a breakdown of current waiting times by Health and Social Care Trust is not applicable. The time to receive a result is dependent on the result of the HPV test and whether additional cytology reporting is required. However, there are ongoing delays in the authorisation of completed test results to GPs as the process is currently manual. Testing of an electronic auto-authorisation system is underway and this process is expected to be completed in the next few weeks, which will speed the process up. Of the samples authorised during the week commencing 10th February 2025, 11.3% were authorised in less than 8weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20262/22-27, how Health and Social Care Trusts are held accountable for reported theft or unaccounted loss of controlled drugs if there is no estimation of the value which is going missing.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts are held accountable for reported theft or unaccounted loss of controlled drugs (CDs) through the The Controlled Drugs (Supervision of Management and Use) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 The Controlled Drugs (Supervision of Management and Use) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 The regulations require my Department’s Controlled Drug Accountable Officer (CDAO) to facilitate a Local Intelligence Network (LIN) to share information and intelligence about the misuse and safe use of CDs.The CDAO’s statutory functions are set out in The Controlled Drugs (Supervision of Management and Use) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 as amended. Each Trust provides a quarterly Occurrence Report to the LIN and a report is produced annually. These reports are not formally launched but are noted by the DoH Senior Leadership Team and the Departmental Board.  Occurrence Reports may include details of any concerns that the designated body has regarding its management or use of CDs in their setting; or confirmation by the designated body that it has no concerns to report regarding its management or use of CDs. CDAOs submit their quarterly Occurrence Report to the Chair of the LIN, and concerns arising from these reports are shared within the confines of the confidential LIN meeting. Organisations share well-founded concerns and, wherever possible, learn from these concerns. Designated Bodies and Responsible Bodies are also encouraged to share best practice standards within this arena. While the report provides an overview of the Northern Ireland situation, it also recognises the position of CDAOs responsibility for CDs in their own organisation and does not detract from their accountability to their own Designated Bodies or indeed the totality of assurances that they would be expected to give over the extent of the legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether NI Water will be paying compensation to customers who were left without water in the aftermath of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that as this was a weather event and beyond its control, it does not pay compensation as a consequence.  I can confirm that my Department submitted a bid for funding in respect of the impact of Storm Éowyn to cover repairs, remedial action, lost income and an alternative water supply. If a customer is unhappy with the service it has received from NI Water, the customer can raise a complaint through the NI Water complaints process. Furthermore, if a customer remains unhappy with NI Water’s response to the issue the Consumer Council NI (CCNI) is there to provide the customer with independent advice and support in respect of the complaint. For future queries relating to NI Water’s customer service issues, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the aspects of her bid for £27.2 million for support in response to Storm Éowyn she will apply the awarded £8 million to.
answer_text: The table below sets out how the £8 million allocation received will be applied to support the response to Storm Éowyn.Spending Area£mNI Water (overtime, repairs, alternative water supply)2.9Translink (lost income, repairs and remedial action)1.7Driver and Vehicle Agency (repairs and vehicle/driving test cancellations)0.2Waterways Ireland (repairs and remedial action)0.1Department (overtime, repairs, remedial action, lost income)3.1Total8

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why she did not bid for funds to provide compensation to NI Water customers who were left without water in the aftermath of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that as this was a weather event and beyond its control, it does not pay compensation as a consequence.  I can confirm that my Department submitted a bid for funding in respect of the impact of Storm Éowyn to cover repairs, remedial action, lost income and an alternative water supply. If a customer is unhappy with the service it has received from NI Water, the customer can raise a complaint through the NI Water complaints process. Furthermore, if a customer remains unhappy with NI Water’s response to the issue the Consumer Council NI (CCNI) is there to provide the customer with independent advice and support in respect of the complaint. For future queries relating to NI Water’s customer service issues, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) their assessment of the storm response plans following Storm Éowyn; and (ii) any plans they have to review these for future incidents.
answer_text: The Executive Office co-ordinates civil contingencies planning across the Executive. It is underpinned by operational plans, owned by each Department which are specific to each potential emergency. Following Storm Éowyn Civil Contingencies Division, along with stakeholders, will assess the lessons learned and review current plans for any future incidents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on works planning and timelines for the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown; (ii) whether has there been an assessment of further deterioration of the road in its current condition; and (iii) when the road will fully reopen, following the landslide in 2023.
answer_text: I recognise the continued inconvenience caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, resulting from the land and verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.The road closure on the B27 Kilkeel Road from Bryansford Road junction to Castlewellan Road junction to facilitate NIW works started on 6 January 2025 and will remain in place until Friday 11 April 2025. Unfortunately, due to the scale and complexity of land and verge slip it will take longer to identify and implement a design solution than the current 12 week closure road closure for the NIW works.  My Department has taken forward preliminary geotechnical investigations and are planning further investigation works during the period of the closure.  Work has also started on the development of design options to be taken forward to allow site works to begin.  My Department is continuing to prioritise the reopening of the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown to two way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how much of the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme which was being administered by Newry, Mourne and Down District Council has not been distributed.
answer_text: Newry, Mourne and Down District Council are responsible for the administration of the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme. Any queries relating to the value of payments made to businesses should be directed to the council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) what her Department plans to do with the funding not yet distributed from the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme; and (ii) whether she will revisit the criteria by which some businesses’ costs were omitted from claims processed.
answer_text: When coming into office, Conor Murphy agreed to provide up to £10 million to fund three schemes that provided additional support to businesses impacted by the severe flooding in November 2004, i.e. the Enhanced flood Support Scheme (EFSS) and two hardship funds. There was no specific amount of funding set aside for the Enhanced Flood Support Scheme (EFSS).  Letters of Offer to the value of approximately £4.5 million have been issued to applicants in relation to the scheme. Eligible costs for the EFSS include replacement/repair of damaged or destroyed equipment, refitting costs and replacement of damaged stock.  There are no plans to revisit this criteria.  Any queries on omitted costs relating to specific business claims should be directed to Newry Mourne Down District Council who are responsible for administering the scheme. Whilst it was estimated that the flood schemes would cost up to £10 million, the final cost of delivering the schemes was less than anticipated.  Conor had, therefore, asked officials to explore options for utilising any unused funding, including options to transfer funding to other Departments, but no realistic deliverable options were identified given the time frame available. However, before leaving office, Conor asked officials to explore options for implementing a more permanent flood support scheme for businesses similar to schemes operating in Britain.  In recognition of the increased number of flood incidents over the past few years, he also felt it would be an opportune time to include a review of support for everyone impacted by flooding, including residents, sports clubs etc.  He, therefore, instructed officials to establish a cross departmental group to explore options for future flood schemes to support recovery and resilience for all those impacted by flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the allocations for Outdoor Learning Projects, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Last week I launched the Outdoor Learning Project which represents a £4m investment for pre-schools, nursery schools, primary, schools and special schools to buy the outdoor furniture and equipment they need most to enhance high-quality outdoor learning.Details of the allocation provided to each setting, including the constituency in which they are based, are available on the Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how much the UK Treasury has offered to cover the increase in employers National Insurance costs to public service in Northern Ireland; and (ii) which public services specifically will this funding be applied to.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Chancellor has said that support will be provided to departments and public sector employers for the additional cost due to increases to employers' National Insurance Contributions. The Executive will receive Barnett consequentials on any additional funding provided to Whitehall departments.As yet the Treasury has not confirmed the amount the Executive will receive. However, it is anticipated that the funding provided may fall well short of what is required. I raised this issue with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 5 February.Once funding is confirmed it would be a matter for the Executive to decide how it is allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost of the temporary traffic control measures in place at the Shore Road, Rostrevor, from November 2023 to 2 February 2025 when it was reopened to two lane traffic.
answer_text: The information requested is commercially sensitive and disclosure may allow the public, in particular economic competitors, to identify commercial rates/costs for carrying out these activities which may damage the Department’s and contractor’s legitimate economic interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the breakdown of additional funding received by non-statutory settings in 2024-25 as a result of an uplift in funding rate.
answer_text: Since taking up post, I have approved significant increases in the funding for non-statutory pre-school settings participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP).  This has resulted in the rate increasing from £2,150 to £2,550 per pupil per year. In addition, I also approved a one-off allocation of £100 per pupil to help stabilise provision.The most recent uplift to the funding rate, which I approved in December 2024, was an increase of £350 per pupil, which is the largest single increase in the PSEP funding rate my Department has ever made.  As a result of these increases and the one-off payment, non-statutory pre-school settings will receive approximately £3.2m additional funding in the 2024-25 academic year.  A breakdown of the estimated total additional funding these settings will receive is broken down by parliamentary constituency in the table below.Constituency£Belfast East 84,500Belfast North 107,000Belfast South and Mid Down 105,000Belfast West46,000East Antrim 104,500East Londonderry201,000Fermanagh and South Tyrone343,500Foyle56,000Lagan Valley184,500Mid Ulster402,000Newry and Armagh 282,500North Antrim 243,500North Down 107,500South Antrim 138,000South Down 272,500Strangford170,500Upper Bann128,000West Tyrone217,000Total3,193,500

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in advance of increases to employers’ National Insurance contributions in April 2025, whether he has any plans to consider a reduction in contractual targets with the voluntary and community sector.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the requirements of the composition of the Local Economic Partnerships £45 million Regional Balance Fund.
answer_text: The Department issued guidance on membership to councils in November 2024, this included a list of mandatory and proposed members for each Local Economic Partnership (LEP).	Membership of the LEP is for to councils to determine. Proposed membership will be reviewed and approved by the Department prior to funding being released from April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there will be a publicly available register of interests of the members of the Local Area Partnerships responsible for distributing the £45 million Regional Balance Fund.
answer_text: Councils are responsible for the operational aspects of the Local Economic Partnership (LEP) and will manage conflicts of interest. This will be outlined in Terms of Reference for the LEP or be in line with current council procedure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will maintain an oversight review of how each local council plans to distribute funds from the £45 million Regional Balance Fund.
answer_text: My Department, in designing the governance arrangements and operating the Regional Balance Fund, will maintain an oversight of the funded projects in each local council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any work ongoing in his Department to support those who are leaving the education system; and (ii) any plans to progress support, for post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: The Department of Health co-chairs a cross-departmental Transitions working group with the Department of Education to review and improve the SEN Transitions process and to update the existing Code of Practice.  The working group includes representatives from PHA, Education Authority, DfC and DfE.  To date, the group has conducted a survey, and the outputs will be used to improve the transitions process which will inform further work to provide children with much greater choice and control on their lives after school, while recognising that Health has a pivotal role to ensure care and health needs are supported. Health and Social Care will support a cohort of children with Special Educational Needs throughout their life in school and transition to adult services.  Supports provided are dependent on assessed need, although it is likely that HSC services will have a continued presence in the lives of children with disabilities and their families. This may include day activity, transport, short breaks, accommodation-based support, and self-directed support. My officials have worked closely with Trusts to develop a regional protocol for Transitions to ensure that the supports we provide are regionally consistent. The next phase of this work is to commence a localised pilot ahead of regional scale up. There is currently no legislation in NI to provide special educational needs pupils with an Education and Health Care plan (EHC) after the age of 19.  My officials continue to engage with cross-departmental partners to explore potential legislative change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any work ongoing in his Department to support those who are leaving the education system; and (ii) any plans to progress support for post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: There are a range of supports for young people with special educational needs attending provision at the Further Education Colleges, Universities and across our vocational training programmes. The support varies depending on individual needs. It can include, for example, financial assistance through the Disabled Student’s Allowance, technical assistance such as specialist IT and software or personal support.  It can also involve extended age eligibility and extended duration on programmes such as Skills for Life and Work, or tailored courses in Further Education for those who are unable to access the mainstream provision.  In addition, the Careers Service plays a key role in helping young people make informed decisions about their education and training opportunities. However, we also know there are challenges to be addressed and that is why my predecessor tasked officials with reviewing the education and training provision for school leavers with special educational needs, and the relevant legislative protections, in order to provide recommendations for improvements.  Officials are currently working through these issues, including participating in extensive stakeholder engagement to inform their work.  I expect to receive their advice in the Spring and I will say more at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he plans to take to support community pharmacies in relation to the estimated £10 million increase to their existing staff costs following the increase in employers National Insurance contributions commencing in April 2025.
answer_text: I recognise that the increase in employer’s National Insurance contributions announced in the Autumn Budget will significantly increase the cost-base for Family Health Service and Social Care providers at a time when these services are fragile. GPs, community pharmacies, dentists, optometrists, care homes and hospices have all raised concerns about the impact this will have on them. While my department does not hold the detailed information to make absolute estimates, we have assessed that the projected increased costs will be some £36.5 million. Currently I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these additional costs, however I have written to the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, to urge him to work to ensure the treatment of these providers faced with these cost pressures is equal to our other providers of health and social care. I have also written to the Finance Minister asking that he also bring his influence to bear on HM Treasury in this regard and I am aware that the previous Finance Minister has written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in similar terms. Following the announcement of the Draft Budget allocation my department has assessed that we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/2026 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand. In recognition of the valuable role played by those providers affected, I will be considering these funding pressures alongside other priorities before I make any final funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20721/22-27, to detail why there is such high spending on agency staff when the Northern Ireland Civil Service has 3,500 vacancies and 2,800 people on temporary promotions.
answer_text: Over the last two years, due to significant budgetary challenges, there have been a number of pauses to large scale general service recruitment, agreed with Permanent Secretaries, resulting in a decrease in supply at general service grades.  Priority recruitment over this period has been for professional and technical vacancies, such as, scientific, agriculture, engineering, ICT, and Courts Service roles.  Department of Finance are managing a range of recruitment competitions (100+) that will create a supply of permanent staff to assist Departments to reduce agency spend, including for Administrative Officer, as well as launching a number of high-volume competitions before the end of the financial year.  Over 2000 vacancies have been filled between April 2024 and December 2024.A significant number of agency staff have been deployed to support delivery of critical Department for Communities services and the Department for Works and Pensions (DWP) contract as well as implementation of the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20062/22-27, when he will answer AQW 19396/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20722/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the £323,495,745.31 spent on agency workers by (i) Department; and (ii) financial year of expenditure.
answer_text: Information on the annual cost of hiring recruitment agency workers across the Service is not held centrally by DoF. This information may be found through published Departmental Annual Report and Accounts or by contacting individual Departments. The table below provides DoF expenditure in relation to temporary workers, contract staff and recruitment agency workers. Although the majority of the expenditure will relate to recruitment agency workers, the total figure may also include costs for temporary workers and fixed term contract staff across DoF.YearCost (£'000) 2019/20£6,8752020/21£6,7152021/22£7,1552022/23£6,5432023/24£6,017Total £33,305Individual Departments are responsible for ensuring that the pay bill costs associated with agency workers are affordable.It is for each Minister to record and report the spend in their respective departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has issued guidance or directions to other Departments on the need to review their contracts for services with the voluntary and community sector and consider a reduction in contractual targets from April 2025 as they will incur a reduction in available staff resources as the increases to employers National Insurance contributions will be commencing.
answer_text: I am aware of the impact the Increase in National Insurance Contributions will have on the third sector and have written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury appealing for support to be considered for those outside central government who provide vital public services.Each department is responsible for taking decisions on necessary measures to manage its individual contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of enforcement cases investigated by each local council in 2024; and (ii) the number of these cases that resulted in enforcement action being taken.
answer_text: The Councils are independent organisations responsible for their own functions and processes.  The Department does not hold the requested information and the Member may wish to approach the relevant Councils directly for the relevant figures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has secured a commitment from the UK Government (i) to provide additional funding to cover the repairs needed in response to Storm Éowyn; and (ii) that storm-related overspend can be ring-fenced and will not affect the budget returns to meet the demands of the financial restoration package.
answer_text: I have spoken with the Secretary of State and also written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in respect of the impact of Storm Éowyn.Departments are currently assessing the costs as a result of Storm Éowyn. Once these costs have been quantified, I will assess the financial position and engage further with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how his Department oversees the enforcement actions of each local council to ensure they are fair and reasonable.
answer_text: District councils are independent organisations responsible for their own statutory processes. My department has issued several Enforcement Practice Notes, and also a Development Management Practice Note specifically in relation to unauthorised EIA development. Going forward, I want to build upon the momentum created through the Planning Improvement agenda, and through continued collaboration between my Department and local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that presented as homeless as a result of Storm Éowyn, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As of 30 January 2025, there was one household which presented as homeless due to fire / flood / other emergency where the presentation was as a result of Storm Éowyn.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensurethat you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the length of time from when a planning application is submitted until a case number and officer is allocated, in the last reporting year, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: In terms of timelines for a case number and officer allocated, my department does not hold information on this and I would refer you to each of the individual councils in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the importance of the Carlingford ferry service for local infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department does not have any operational responsibility for Carlingford Ferry Service, therefore I cannot make an assessment on its importance to the local infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail when an announcement will be made on the level of increase to Translink bus and rail charges for 2025-26; and (ii) for his assessment of how much the increase will be.
answer_text: My officials are currently working with Translink to review future fare options.  This review needs to ensure that we continue to maintain and improve public transport; to encourage modal shift and to recognise the challenges facing many people in the current financial climate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail when an announcement will be made on the level of increase to non-domestic water charges for 2025-26; and (ii) for his assessment of how much the increase will be.
answer_text: NI Water will make an announcement on non-domestic tariff levels for 2025/26 in mid-March 2025. The level of non-domestic tariffs is a regulatory matter.  Any proposed tariff increase must comply with price limits set by the Utility Regulator. These price limits, which were revised and published in the PC21 Mid-Term Review Final Determination (MTR FD), limit the extent to which tariffs can be increased. Approval by the Utility Regulator is required before revised changes can be introduced on 1st April.

answer_text: There are a number of different sources of income received by the Health and Social Care Service in Northern Ireland.
