question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the value of client contributions to nursing and residential care in each of the last three years.
answer_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the value of client contributions to nursing and residential care in each of the last three years.ANSWERThe total value of client contributions to nursing and residential care in each of the last three years is set out in the table below: Year£m2021/22166.02022/23181.52023/24209.9*Note: figures above have been rounded to the nearest £0.1m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the (a) staff headcount; and (b) budget that will transfer from Invest NI to Tourism NI to implement the recommendation from The Independent Review of Invest NI that capital support for development of new tourist accommodation should transfer from Invest NI to Tourism NI; and (ii) when this will occur.
answer_text: A Working Group comprising officials from my Department, Invest NI and Tourism NI has been set up to manage the implementation of Recommendation 6 of the Independent Review of Invest NI that capital support for development of new tourist accommodation should transfer from Invest NI to Tourism NI.This work also involves an independent assessment to consider the future provision of visitor accommodation across the North and to inform policy on future capital support and the scope of any new scheme which Tourism NI may put in place.The existing scheme operated by Invest NI does not have a ring-fenced capital budget. Consideration will be given to the availability of future capital budget for any new scheme as part of the standard budget bidding processes within my Department, recognising the current challenging budget environment faced by the Executive. In parallel, Tourism NI is examining staffing and associated requirements for the new scheme. The target date for transfer of the functions to Tourism NI is 31 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will review the membership of the Tourism Partnership Board to consider including a representative from Visit Mourne Mountains and Warrenpoint Harbour Authority, who are performing well as a cruise ship destination, to demonstrate their important roles in the Northern Ireland Tourism plan for the next 10 years.
answer_text: The Mourne Mountains are an incredible asset to our tourism offering and I recognise the growing importance of our cruise industry. In establishing the Tourism Partnership Board, the Chair stated that membership would be kept under review. With the recent launch of the Tourism Action Plan, we now move towards its implementation and engagement with appropriate stakeholders will be key to our success. The Chair is currently considering the formation of sub-groups and the appointment of additional members to form those groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when the cross-border healthcare directive will return.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme opened on Monday 2 June 2025.  Subject to prior approval, the Scheme is available to patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, and have been waiting two years or more for hospital treatment on a HSC waiting list, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to HSC in Northern Ireland.The new Scheme is being initiated on a phased approach.  The first phase will allow reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland. The second phase will be introduced in the autumn and will see the geographical scope of the Scheme expanded to all countries in the European Union (EU). By opening up the Scheme to the EU, patients will have a greater range of providers to choose from.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail why, on the Tourism Northern Ireland website under its Marketing Campaign and in its Spring Marketing Campaign toolkit, the Republic of Ireland and its components is listed above, before and more prominently than Northern Ireland throughout.
answer_text: Tourism NI’s mission is to support the development of a productive, regionally balanced and sustainable tourism industry, by promoting the North as a must-see destination to markets which offer the greatest potential for growth.As Tourism NI’s marketing remit is the whole of Ireland, the market with the greatest potential for growth is the South.  The economic value of visitors from the South is higher than those from the North, as they bring additional revenue, rather than recirculating existing money within the North’s economy. Tourism NI’s spring 2024 marketing campaign generated £68.35m in attributable visitor revenue from the South compared to £28.39m in attributable visitor revenue from the North, demonstrating the relative potential of the market in the South.Around four-fifths of our  tourism providers reported similar or higher volumes of business from the South during between January and September compared to the same period in 2023.Cardholder spend between January and September 2024 indicates a 7% year on year increase in tourism spend from the South.The Tourism NI Spring campaign targets potential visitors across the island and encourages them to take a short break here. To reflect the additional growth potential, approximately 75% of the total campaign budget is targeted at the South.  This is why the campaign toolkit lists the Southern market first, as the market representing the greatest potential for long term growth and economic benefit to the North in terms of visitor numbers and associated spend.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail who will be given responsibility for the development and promotion of Peace Tourism, as outlined in his Tourism Vision and Action Plan 10 Year Plan, including the writing of what he describes as our unique story to tell, to ensure it is unbiased.
answer_text: Our tourism industry has become one of the greatest success stories of the wider peace process, fostering co-operation and understanding between communities.  Our peace process is known across the world and heralded as a success story. Tourism NI’s visitor research shows that over 50% of people in both the North and the South want to learn more about our recent political history. The international market is also keenly interested in our rich culture and heritage.This offers an opportunity to grow peace tourism and maximise its contribution to the 10-year plan’s objective to substantially increase the value of tourism.The primary responsibility for the development and promotion of peace tourism lies with Tourism NI, working closely with Tourism Ireland and other partners. It is recognised that our past can be perceived and remembered in many different ways. There are many examples of tourism businesses that are already telling our story in an inclusive way, both acknowledging and valuing differing interpretations that will guide future development.An audit exercise has been conducted to establish a baseline of associated tourism products and help identify gaps. This work will help to define priority markets and audiences. Engagement with key industry stakeholders is ongoing to ensure a fully inclusive process.  Collectively, these activities will feed into a co-ordinated, informed and shared approach to the promotion and development of peace tourism.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the (i) tourism industry; and (ii) profile of Northern Ireland will be enhanced and protected in his plans to extend Failte Ireland’s regional experience brands into Northern Ireland and build upon the Embrace a Giant Spirit brand.
answer_text: Incorporating areas of the North into Fáilte Ireland’s regional brands is an objective within my Department’s Business Plan to deliver regional economic growth, as well as a key Action as part of my Department’s new Tourism Vision & Action Plan: 10 Year Plan. Broadening the reach of these brands should encourage greater numbers of people to visit, whether visiting from the South or for international tourists visiting Ireland.The extension of these brands will deliver increased connectivity and an improved visitor experience across Ireland. Improving the connectivity of various routes and access to iconic experiences will create a more compelling proposition, encouraging visitors to explore more widely, stay longer, and spend more. Tourism NI are working collaboratively with councils to surface the opportunities and deliver improvements to strengthen the routes with the support of the Shared Island Fund.	Embrace a Giant Spirit remains the lead experience brand for the North and Failte Ireland’s regional experience branding will represent an additional opportunity for our tourism industry to build on the success of Embrace a Giant Spirit campaigns, with different brands targetting different market segments. The values that underpin the tourism experience remain unchanged. We know from extensive research that visitors seek authentic, immersive experiences that connect them to local places and communities and these traits will continue to shape and lead efforts to establish this region as an internationally compelling destination.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of interest earned on the Northern Ireland Investment Fund in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The interest earned by the Investment Fund for the last 3 years is presented in the following table: 2022£2023£2024£Interest Receivable (loans advanced)1,238,8672,896,7505,408,282Bank Interest Receivable 73,000864,4832,723,358Total1,311,8673,761,2338,131,640

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will review the potential to purchase the site at 2 Railway Street, Newcastle, the former Victorian Belfast and County Down Railway Station and Clock Tower, with a view to preserving and enhancing this historic site and its place in Northern Ireland connectivity over the years.
answer_text: My Department does not normally purchase historic buildings with a view to preserving and enhancing them. The Department’s approach to listed historic buildings is to work with owners to seek a sustainable long-term future. In this case, the Clock Tower at the former railway station is a listed building.  My Department’s Historic Environment Division are happy to engage with owners regarding proposals to their listed building.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will review the potential to purchase the site at 2 Railway Street, Newcastle, the former Victorian Belfast and County Down Railway Station and Clock Tower, with a view to including South Down in plans for future rail connections across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The recent All-Island Strategic Rail Review provided an evidence-based framework to inform future investment in the railways across the island. Rail services to locations such as South Down would function as commuter links to Belfast and thus fell outside the scope of the Review.  As a result, rail connections to South Down are not actively being considered by the Department at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the assessment of the Embrace A Giant Spirit brand was measured in order to deem it a success, as referred to at Page 11 of his Tourism Vision and Action Plan 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: The ‘Embrace a Giant Spirit’ experience brand was launched in 2019 following extensive research with visitors in six priority target markets (North America, Great Britain, France, Germany and the island of Ireland), alongside local industry and trade partners engagement. This research showed that the brand generated a 30% increase in the likelihood to visit the North in future years. The brand is built on key objectives such as increased visitor spend and length of stay alongside enhanced destination reputation. This continuing success is evident in the most recent NISRA statistics for 2023, showing visitors making 5.4 million overnight trips and spending a record £1.2 billion, of which 78% came from outside of the North.		Tourism NI consumer sentiment research in November 2024 shows increased awareness of “Embrace a Giant Spirit” amongst audiences in the North and South, with 66% and 38% respectively having awareness of the brand. 		Tourism NI continues to deliver award-winning “Embrace a Giant Spirit” marketing campaigns, with the total value of attributable visits from 2024 campaigns reaching £182.18 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number agency workers engaged on the framework agreement for agency workers in the Northern Ireland Civil Service; (ii) the current spend on this contract; and (iii) her assessment of how this compares to numbers and costs in previous periods.
answer_text: At month ending December 2024 there were 4,367 workers engaged on the 2021 framework agreement for agency workers.The total expenditure on the 2021 framework agreement for agency workers at 8 January 2025 was £323,495,745.31.The use of agency workers is a necessary way to manage some time bound aspects of temporary work and to provide maximum flexibility to support the business needs of the Civil Service and its departments.A combination of pauses to recruitment in the NICS, a number of reprioritisation exercises given the budgetary situation, staffing the Department for Work and Pensions contract as well as pressures around EU Exit preparation and Windsor Framework requirements, has increased the number of vacancies across NICS and the need for temporary agency workers in recent years.Individual Departments are responsible for ensuring that the pay bill costs associated with agency workers are affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the contract period for the current framework agreement for agency workers in the Northern Ireland Civil Service; and (ii) the value of this contract.
answer_text: The period of the current NICS Agency Worker Contract is 5 years from January 2021 to January 2026.The value of this contract is £425 million with a contract modification agreed on 13th January 2025 to increase the value by £127.5 million to £552.5 million.  This will support the delivery of vital services across the NICS for the remainder of the contract. It is important to note the contract is demand driven. The increase in the contract value does not guarantee this level of spend under the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the total increase in costs to departments, their agencies, non-departmental bodies and Arm’s Length Bodies arising from the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions from 1 April 2025.
answer_text: The planned increase to Employers’ National Insurance Contributions will prove incredibly challenging for our public sector employers.Departments estimate the additional cost to departments, their agencies, Non-Departmental Public Bodies and Arm’s Length Bodies arising from the increase in National Insurance Contributions to be around £200 million for 2025/26.Furthermore, this doesn’t include the costs facing healthcare providers; local councils; or for the community and voluntary sectors. For example, the Department of Health has estimated an additional annual cost of some £36.5m for Family Health Service providers and independent providers.I also understand the Department for Communities has received information from local government which estimates an additional cost to Councils of around £12m.While the Chancellor has said that support will be provided for departments and public sector employers, and we are waiting on confirmation on the funding the Executive will receive, our view is that this is likely to fall far short, possibly by some £100 million pounds. This was a decision taken in Westminster on a reserved taxation measure and the Executive does not have the ability to mitigate against that.I have written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury pressing for urgent clarity on the quantum of funding we will receive and asking for these costs to be met in full. I have also appealed for consideration to be given to support for those outside central government who provide vital public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether procedures on ectopic pregnancies are reported as medical abortions.
answer_text: Procedures on ectopic pregnancies are not reported as medical abortions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of abortions carried out in each of the last three years, broken down by Heath and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department publishes abortion data annually.  This details the number of abortions carried out each year and includes a breakdown by Health and Social Care Trust.  The publication released in June 2024 includes data from 2020/21 to 2022/23:2020/212021/222022/23Belfast HSC Trust528566667Northern HSC Trust293419572South Eastern HSC Trust259383438Southern Trust283359415Western Trust 2062876Further details are available on the Department of Health website.  Data for 2023/24 will be released on 30 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions, from 1 April 2025, will have on GPs, dentists, community pharmacists and independent providers.
answer_text: I recognise that the increase in employer’s National Insurance contributions announced in the Autumn Budget will significantly increase the cost-base for Family Health Service and Social Care providers at a time when these services are fragile.  GPs, community pharmacies, dentists, optometrists, care homes and hospices have all raised concerns about the impact this will have on them.While my department does not hold the detailed information to make absolute estimates, we have assessed that the projected increased costs will be some £36.5 million. Currently I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these additional costs, however I have written to the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, to urge him to work to ensure the treatment of these providers faced with these cost pressures is equal to our other providers of health and social care.I have also written to the Finance Minister asking that she also bring her influence to bear on HM Treasury in this regard and I am aware that she has written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in similar terms.Following the announcement of the Draft Budget allocation my department has assessed that we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/2026 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.In recognition of the valuable role played by those providers affected, I will be considering these funding pressures alongside other priorities before I make any final funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of compensation claims; and (ii) the total value of compensation claims, made against her Department and arm's-length bodies by (a) prisoners held in prisons; and (b) accused held in PSNI custody suites, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: In relation to parts (i) and (ii) (a) of the question, information on the number and value of claims made by prisoners against the Department in the period from April 2021 onwards has been provided in the table below.Financial Year Number of Prisoner Claims Received Number of Prisoner Claims SettledValue of Claims Settled (£)Apr 24 - Present8519227,700Apr 23 - Mar 2412019206,333Apr 22 - Mar 2318832100,026Apr 21 - Mar 221983475,700Note: Figures in this table relate to claims made by prisoners against the NI Prison Service and the Youth Justice Agency.The Department does not hold information on compensation claims made against arm's-length bodies.  That information may be available directly from them.In relation to part (ii) (b) of the question, the recording of information in relation to the value of compensation claims by persons held in PSNI custody suites is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in part (ii) (b) of the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the number of people called to jury who were excused in each of the last three financial years; and (ii) how this equates as a percentage of the total number of people called to jury.
answer_text: Each year jury notices are issued to approximately 40,000 people randomly selected from the Electoral Register.  Jury service runs from July to June each year.It is important that juries are representative of the population.  However, persons may be excluded from jury service for a variety of reasons including some criminal convictions or employment in the administration of justice.  Others are excusable as of right because of their age or their profession; this includes teachers, nurses and doctors.  Persons summoned to jury service but who are unable to attend can make a request for excusal or deferral from jury service. The table below provides details on the number and percentage of persons who are not qualified, disqualified, ineligible, or who have had their jury service excused or deferred.  2021-20222022-20232023-2024Total Notices issued40,30041,00040,800Persons Not Qualified, Disqualified, Ineligible & Excusable as of right14,09413,29413,191% - Persons Not Qualified, Disqualified, Ineligible & Excusable as of right35.0%32.4%32.3%Persons fully excused or deferred 19,10610,45211,455% - Persons fully excused, deferred 22.4%25.5%28.1%Source – Integrated Court Operations System (ICOS)1 – May include some persons who failed to attend. Persons who fail to attend for jury service may be fined up to £1,000.Jurors who are not available for the full period of jury service may be granted a partial excusal.  Figures for the number of persons granted a partial excusal are not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any work completed by her Department to quantify the level of unreported cases of domestic abuse in the last three years.
answer_text: Quantifying the level of unreported domestic abuse is a complex challenge: however, I recognise the importance of improving our understanding of the nature and scale of domestic abuse and its impact. My department has made progress towards this, and in particular included additional input to the Northern Ireland Safe Community Survey, which now includes a self-completion module on experiences of domestic abuse. This module is currently in the field for the 2024/25 survey year.However, I should highlight that the inclusion of this module was not possible over recent years due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. During this time, the survey was conducted via telephone, and given the sensitive nature of the topic, it was not considered appropriate to include the domestic abuse module in that format.Looking forward, my department plans to take forward additional work in partnership with the community and voluntary sector, and with officials in the Executive Office, to explore how we can better utilise the data held across various sectors. This collaborative work will be taken forward under the multi-year action plan stemming from the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy. By taking this joined up approach, we aim to enhance our understanding of the nature and scale of domestic abuse in Northern Ireland, including cases that go unreported. This will ensure that our policy and operational responses are informed by robust and comprehensive evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how many of those (i) accused; and (ii) convicted in the cases of the 25 women murdered in Northern Ireland since 2020 were (a) on bail at the time of the murder; (b) subject to Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conference; (c) referred for Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conference; (d) in the Public Protection Arrangements in Northern Ireland process; and (e) had previous criminal records for abuse or violent offences against women.
answer_text: The information that has been requested is not held by my department. I therefore, recommend redirecting your query to the Chief Constable of Police Service of Northern Ireland, who is best placed to assist with your request

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the value of reported theft or unaccounted loss of controlled drugs in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Controlled Drug 2009 Regulations stipulate that controlled drugs (CD) incidents or concerns must be reported to the relevant Controlled Drugs Accountable Officer (CDAO) in each Trust.  However, the actual costs are not quantified, due to the fluctuating prices of medications, which sometimes occurs on a daily basis making it difficult to compare, and also the cost of medications is not related to the harm associated with the incident or concern. Therefore, we are unable to detail the value of reported theft or unaccounted loss of controlled drugs in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the steps he is taking in advance of admissions to further and higher education organisations in September 2025 to address skills shortages.

To ask the Minister of Finance how much of the £119 million previous years' outstanding rates balances, reported in December 2024, is (i) expected to be collected in 2024-25 financial year; and (ii) anticipated to be written off.
answer_text: As rate collection is ongoing, it is not possible at this point in the rating year to predict with certainty how much will be collected or written off by 31st March 2025. I can advise that the reported £119m carried forward rating debt as at 1st December 2024, has further reduced to £115m as at 31st December 2024 through ongoing collection and recovery actions taken by Land & Property Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of schools that are in locations outside the DfI Roads salting network, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My department does not hold the information requested but does carry out secondary salting to 80 Rural Schools when required, after the primary salted network has been treated, broken down by Division:•	7 Northern•	30 Southern•	2 Eastern•	41 Western

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the target time to repair reported streetlight outages; and (ii) the average time taken to repair reported streetlight outages in the last 12 months, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: I am pleased to inform the member that, for the current financial year, I have been able to allocate funding to enable my Department to provide a full street lighting maintenance service.My Department does not hold the requested information by local council area, however I can confirm that street lighting outage repairs are generally being carried out within the established target of 5 working days from the receipt of an initial report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the steps he has taken since coming into office to protect the Northern Ireland tourism industry from any adverse effects of the electronic travel authorisation (ETA); and (ii) the steps he will be taking to urgently address the risks posed to the tourism industry following the introduction of the ETA to ensure tourism is maximised.
answer_text: (i) Since taking up office, I have consistently made clear my concerns about the impact of the introduction of the Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme which threatens the growth of our tourism industry and, with that, our wider economy.  From my engagement with the sector, including with the Northern Ireland Tourism Alliance (NITA), visitor attractions and tour operators, I know it is a major concern for local tourism and hospitality businesses here in the north. Our all-island economy is vital, and seamless all-island travel is key to building on the growth we have seen in our tourism sector. I share the strong concerns expressed by the tourism industry.Following early meetings with tourism industry representatives, I wrote in April to the then Minister for Legal Migration and the Border at the Home Office seeking an urgent meeting. Subsequently, following the British General Election, I wrote in July to Seema Malhotra MP, the new Minister for Migration and Citizenship at the Home Office to again raise my concerns and seek an urgent meeting. I met with Minister Malhotra in November and pressed for solutions to protect the north’s tourism industry.I have since issued a further letter to Minister Malhotra in December with more information on our concerns and to provide additional evidence of the anticipated economic impacts including the issues for tour operators and our visitor attractions.This week I have undertaken a series of media interviews to again highlight our concern about the impact of the ETA scheme on tourism as the scheme went live for non-European visitors. During these, I again pressed for the Home Office to reconsider its implementation of the ETA and to find a pragmatic solution to protect our tourism sector.(ii) I will be launching the Tourism Vision and Action Plan on Wednesday 15 January to set out my vision for growth of the tourism sector over the next ten years and this will include a specific action to continue to press for a solution to the impact of ETA.I believe the Home Office could take a pragmatic and flexible approach. Ideally, I would wish to see a complete exemption introduced for tourists crossing the land border into the north but not travelling on to Britain. At the very least, however, I would want to see a time-limited exemption, for people visiting for up to 5 to 7 days, which would cover most of our international visitors. In addition, my officials continue to engage with the Home Office on its proposal to bring forward a clear communications package on the ETA Scheme, bespoke to all of Ireland travel. While this engagement on communications is welcome, I do not believe it will be enough to completely address the risks posed by the introduction of the ETA scheme. As a result, I will continue to press for an exemption that would more fully address the threat to our tourism sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 19396/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to progress the work to repair the damage to the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, following adverse weather in 2023 during the current 13 week road closure for NI Water works.
answer_text: I recognise the continued inconvenience caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, resulting from the land and verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.The road closure on the B27 Kilkeel Road from Bryansford Road junction to Castlewellan Road junction to facilitate NIW works started on 6 January 2025 and will remain in place until Friday 11 April 2025. Unfortunately, due to the scale and complexity of land and verge slip it will take longer to identify and implement a design solution than the current 12 week closure road closure for the NIW works.  My Department has taken forward preliminary geotechnical investigations and are planning further investigation works during the period of the closure.  Work has also started on the development of design options to be taken forward to allow site works to begin.  My Department is continuing to prioritise the reopening of the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown to two way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19738/22-27, when the Council data published in the impact of local government reform on service delivery and cost effectiveness report will be audited and reported upon.
answer_text: The financial metrics in Section 7 of the report were collated from the Northern Ireland Audit Office Local Government Auditor’s Reports. The direct or indirect benefits and efficiencies realised by councils, that are included in the report, were provided by councils and there is no plan to carry out an audit of this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18897/22-27, when the audit on missed appointment reasons will be complete, along with published outcomes and recommendations.
answer_text: My Department are currently working with the Governance Department within SPPG to sign off the Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and the Data Access Agreement (DAA), which are essential to allow access to the required patient level data. The initial focus of the DNA audit will be on the Lagan Valley Day Procedure (DPC) and this audit should be completed in February 2025. The LVH audit will form part of a wider regional programme, with further audits to be scheduled over the coming months.The findings of the DNA audit will help identify those actions which may be required to tackle the  underlying causes  of non-attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19218/22-27, for his assessment of the reasons for the increase in home births in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust and not in other areas.
answer_text: The Southern Trust is the only Trust so far in Northern Ireland to have a dedicated team called Birth @ Home - that service is used as an example of excellent practice in Professor Mary Renfrew’s report entitled 'Enabling Safe Quality Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland' which was published on 22nd October 2024.It should be noted however that this service is currently suspended due to staffing challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when he will progress the reopening of two lanes of traffic on Shore Road, Rostrevor, following commitments made in December 2024.
answer_text: I recognise the continued inconvenience caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, resulting from the land and verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.My Department has completed the complex design and environmental approval process required to progress a temporary solution to facilitate the reopening of the A2 Shore Road to two way traffic.  I can confirm that a contractor has been appointed and my officials are currently engaged to agree a start date with the aim of proceeding with the work at the end of January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of government contracts procured through Construction and Procurement Delivery that have been extended beyond the originally advertised term of contract; and (ii) the value of contract spend on these extended contracts, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for providing procurement services to departments and a number of arms-length bodies (ALBs) that do not have a Centre of Procurement Expertise of their own. While Construction and Procurement Delivery supports the management of some construction related contracts on behalf of departments and ALBs, it is the Department or ALB funding each contract that has overall responsibility for its management including any contract extensions and associated spend. Full details of contracts extended and the spend on those contracts are therefore not held by my Department.I have provided details of those contracts funded by my Department that have been extended beyond their original contract date and the value of those extensions over the last 3 years below:24-25 (to date)23-2422-23NumberTotal Value of Modifications NumberTotal Value of ModificationsNumberTotal Value of Modifications11£8,094,83218£7,055,10421£8,649,420

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the need to introduce new rules on minimum sentencing and bail conditions for those charged or convicted of offences of domestic abuse and violence against women and girls.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 13 - AQO 1329/22-27			ANSWERI recognise the devastating impact of domestic abuse and violence against women and girls on individuals, families, and society as a whole.Addressing these crimes is a priority, and we need to ensure a zero-tolerance approach across all parts of the justice system, including in relation to sentencing and bail.   However, members will be aware that sentencing and bail decisions are guided by existing legislation and determined by the independent judiciary based on the circumstances for each case, therefore it is not in the remit of my department to comment on bail or sentences imposed on individual cases.  That being said, I recognise the concerns being raised, particularly when repeat offenders are involved.  Minimum sentences are exceptionally rare and carry risks of fettering judicial discretion. They would therefore require very careful consideration and public consultation.  My preference would be to focus on ensuring appropriate maximum sentences are available to the judiciary for these crimes. I should also highlight that under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceeding Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, the maximum sentences which I legislated for are amongst the highest available for any offence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when pensioner households should expect to start receiving their £100 winter fuel payments.
answer_text: I announced on 19th November 2024 that a one-off payment of £100 would be provided to pensioner households in Northern Ireland no longer eligible for the Winter Fuel Payment. The department is working at pace to put the necessary arrangements in place for making this payment before the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the ten year review of councils’ performance since the Review of Public Administration will be published.
answer_text: The report: Impact of local government reform on service delivery and cost effectiveness, was published on the departmental website on 7 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what happened to the £11 million of at risk stock without expiry dates referenced in the Business Services Organisation Annual Report and Accounts for year ended 31 March 2024.
answer_text: The £11 million non-expiring stock is housed in the Business Service Organisation’s warehouse. Whilst it is considered slow moving it has an indefinite useful life and remains in saleable condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any progress made since 31 March 2024 on the settlement of the Northern Ireland Co-operation Overseas’ £2.1 million liability, as disclosed in the Invest NI Annual Report and Accounts 2023-24.
answer_text: At the end of August 2024, the NI-CO Board placed the company into a Members Voluntary Liquidation, with Invest NI approval as sole shareholder, and a liquidator was appointed to wind up the company. The NI-CO Accounts for 2023-24 contained a provision of £2.1m for the potential clawback of monies by the EU on contracts completed previously by NI-CO. Contracts between NI-CO and the EU include rights for the EU to undertake financial audits on each project for up to five years following final payment, with any expenditure deemed ineligible being repaid. This provision was an estimation based on a percentage of the total contract value of all projects that were still within the five-year audit period. To date the £2.1 million provision has not crystallised, nor been settled, as no clawback of EU payments have been requested from completed audits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19392/22-27, whether he will (i) review the current economic position of the fishing industry; and (ii) more regularly review the economic impact of the fishing industry going forward.
answer_text: The economic position of the fishing industry is reviewed on a regular basis. This takes place through stakeholder engagement, briefings and review of publications such as the annual Seafish UK Fishing Fleet Survey.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the value of medical stock written off or disposed of due to reaching expiry dates in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: An audit of the previous screening pathway(s) in patients diagnosed with invasive cervical cancer in Northern Ireland was established by the NI Cervical Screening Programme for the purpose of learning and improvement. The process for undertaking this audit is set out in the 2014 “NI Protocol for the audit of Invasive cervical Cancers,” guidance document. When a case of invasive cervical cancer is diagnosed the audit process commences, with the diagnosing Trust leading a review of all elements of the women’s screening history, where one exists. The Public Health Agency (PHA) introduced the ‘Framework for the Audit of Invasive Cervical Cancers and Disclosure of Findings,’ in December 2019.  The purpose of this framework was to standardise approaches in Northern Ireland in terms of reporting and reviewing invasive cervical cancers; categorisation of audit outcomes and informing women of audit activities and audit findings. Where an audit of a case of invasive cervical cancer is assigned a category 3 outcome (unsatisfactory) following discussion by the Trust’s Multidisciplinary Team, a process to review and identify potential learning should be taken forward by the respective Trust under the Trust’s Serious Adverse Incident (SAI) review process. It is the responsibility of the diagnosing Trust’s Multidisciplinary Team to determine the most appropriate outcome for each case of invasive cervical cancer that is audited. Between 2019-2023, the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) reported an audit outcome in 12 cases as a category 3 (unsatisfactory) outcome. Review and assessment of these cases was then taken forward under the Trust’s SAI process. This number is higher than the number of category 3 audit outcomes identified by other HSC Trusts over the similar time period. The WHSCT have advised that at that time the regional guidance did not include guidance on the standard to be applied in the review process. The WHSCT sought advice from their legal team in September 2020 which concluded that the WHSCT could use the “reasonableness method”.  As a result, a different threshold was applied in WHSCT than in the other HSC Trusts which resulted in a higher number of category 3 outcomes identified in WHSCT. The impact was that a higher proportion of patients with cervical cancer in WHSCT had their screening pathway notified as a serious adverse incident, compared to other Trusts.Additional guidance was then issued by the PHA, in July 2024, using a more recent toolkit published in England, to promote a more standardised approach by all Trusts in Northern Ireland and WHSCT have confirmed this has since been implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what her proposal to increase the cap of Maximum Capital Value on domestic properties from £400,000 to £485,000 means for the increased spending power for the Executive.
answer_text: The measure is estimated to bring in just over an additional £2M for the Executive and will also raise a similar amount of additional revenue at District Council level.As I stated in the Assembly following my statement of 9 December, this particular proposal is not primarily focused on revenue generation but is related to my broader objective of making the tax system fairer and more progressive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the regional distribution of the core health grant.
answer_text: The Department does not routinely collect information at a District Council/Local Authority level and therefore details of funding provided to organisations is only available at a regional level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of when the weight loss injection Wegovy will be rolled out in Northern Ireland following the announcement in September 2024 that it will be rolled out in England.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.NICE has published TA875 which advises that semaglutide (Wegovy®) is recommended as an option for weight management, including weight loss and weight maintenance, alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity, however it can only be prescribed in secondary care by a specialist multidisciplinary tier 3 weight management service.Unfortunately, there is currently no specialist multidisciplinary tier 3 weight management service available in Northern Ireland. In November last year, my Department launched a public consultation on the proposed introduction of a Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS), which may provide a means for people living with obesity in NI to access GLP-1 medications.The consultation closed on 1 March 2024, and I am currently considering the analysis of the responses received.  At this time, I am unable to give any commitments as to the potential future establishment of this service.  Any decisions regarding future development of this service will be based on consideration of the evidence presented, availability of resources and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to assess and address the challenges facing the fishing industry across both Departments’ objectives.
answer_text: My officials continue to work alongside the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) colleagues in relation to access to labour. We are analysing all available data, determining any potential impacts and/or changes on the wider economy, and engaging with Home Office and the Migration Advisory Committee on matters relating to immigration. Recent engagement has focused heavily on the labour difficulties in our agri-food sector, and I am aware Minister Muir has written to the British Government regarding this. I have written to the Home Secretary regarding the new immigration rules as they do not serve the needs of the economy here and will continue to engage with British Government Ministers on on a range immigration issues, including the level at which the minimum salary threshold is set, and the levels that required skills are set to enable access to migrant workers in the agri-food and drink sector. Invest NI works with local fish processing businesses, assisting with a range of business development activities including export sales development, research & development, skills, and energy efficiency. In doing so, Invest NI engages with DAERA’s Marine and Fisheries Team, and formally meets with DAERA on a six-monthly basis to discuss the export of agri-products.In relation to renewable energy, my Department established the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group in April 2022, bringing together key delivery partners to develop and deliver the action plan for offshore renewable energy in the north. DAERA is represented on the Steering Group by Marine and Fisheries policy and legislation representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the (i) economic output; and (ii) benefits of the Northern Ireland fishing industry.
answer_text: According to the Northern Ireland Fishing & Seafood Development Programme Final Report (2021) the estimated economic output of the local fishing and seafood processing sectors comprised turnover of £135m, with Gross Value Added of £55.5m. It also supported 1,550 full time equivalent jobs. In addition, the latest full year Regional Trade Statistics show local fishing related exports totalled £53.8m in 2023.There are also many other businesses operating in support of the sector, which provide additional jobs and produce additional economic output. 	In terms of benefits, the industry sits within the wider “blue” economy of other maritime economic sectors, many of which have the potential to align with the priorities outlined in my economic plan, such as regional balance and promotion of a circular economy - these include tourism, aquaculture, marine biotechnology and other maritime training and R&D opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) who is responsible for paying the cost of medical treatment for Northern Ireland-based seafarers eligible to use the Dreadnought Medical Service; and (ii) any engagement he has had in relation to Northern Ireland-based seafarers accessing this service.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount paid to Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) staff for NICS on-call allowance in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The amount paid to Civil Service staff* for on-call allowances, for the last three financial years is provided below.2021/20222022/20232023/2024Total On-Call Allowance (£)1,763,7021,751,1571,815,333Source:  figures taken from HRConnect*Civil Service staff includes Non-Industrial staff and Industrial staff i.e. staff on Civil Service Pay Scales. The figures given do not include any payments for Prison Service uniformed grades.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) training; and (ii) apprenticeship measures in place across his Department and its arm's-length bodies to support people progressing to work in the Northern Ireland fishing industry.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI programme provides funded apprenticeship training at Levels 2 and 3 across a wide range of sectors, including the food processing sector.  Under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme, Level 2 and level 3 Food and Drink apprenticeship frameworks include a pathway for the fish and shellfish sector. Whilst these can currently be delivered by five contracted private training providers, as well as Southern Regional College and the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), there has been no demand to date from employers to deliver this training.The Agri-Food Sectoral Partnership is available to work with the fishing industry as required to refresh the current apprenticeship frameworks, identify and introduce any new frameworks and source relevant qualifications to meet the needs of the sector.	A range of vocational training is available through the Traineeship and Skills for Life and Work programmes that can support transition into further education or employment in many fields.	In addition, my Department’s Careers Service can provide impartial and personalised advice and guidance for those wishing to pursue a career in the fishing industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for capital investment in (i) Daisy Hill Hospital; and (ii) Craigavon Area Hospital.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have submitted proposals in relation to Craigavon Area Hospital to increase the bed capacity, commence Phase 1 and 2 of the Redevelopment of the Hospital, provide additional dementia beds at Bluestone Mental Health Unit, construct a new modular decontamination unit, the installation of a MRI and CT scanner and to repurpose a Day Surgery Unit.  At Daisy Hill Hospital, the Trust submitted proposals to commence the redevelopment of the Hospital and enhance the current bed evacuation provision. The Trust plan to install an MRI refurbishment solution in 2025/26 in advance of a twin MRI and CT diagnostic suite due to commence once the current low voltage works have completed.The Trust will be invited to submit business cases for these proposals once budget affordability is confirmed.Low voltage works are continuing at Craigavon Area Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital. In addition, Encompass will go live across the Trust in 2025/26.Annually, the Department issues the Trust with a general capital allocation for capital works, including estate maintenance, medical equipment, ICT and transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to maximise Northern Ireland’s participation and success in the 2026 Commonwealth Games.
answer_text: The 2026 Commonwealth Games provides an excellent opportunity to build on the success of Northern Ireland athletes at the Paris Games this year. As part of that legacy, I provided £100,000 for the Olympic Medallists’ Fund so that our winning athletes could nominate grassroots sports to benefit.  Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, also intends to invest a modest amount of exchequer funding into equipment at the Sports Institute for Northern Ireland to help our high performing athletes. I have also bid to secure funding in the next budget cycle to continue to build on the Paris legacy help those athletes who are preparing for the 2026 Commonwealth Games as part of their competitive sporting pathway. Sport NI also invests Lottery funding in Commonwealth Games sports, and I have arranged for a summary of this investment to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number people, resident to Northern Ireland, that are estimated to have spent their holidays in other parts of Northern Ireland in each of the last four years.
answer_text: NISRA has published data on domestic tourism (NI residents taking overnight trips within NI) in Tables 1 and 2 at this link. Whilst this doesn’t show the data you have requested the tables do show the number of overnight trips taken by NI residents by reason for trip (including for example holiday, visiting friends and relatives, business).As a result of the Covid-19 pandemic, all face-to-face interviewing on NISRA surveys was suspended in March 2020, with NISRA resuming its survey activity during 2021. However, it was not until January 2023 that the full suite of data required to produce tourism statistics became available again. In domestic tourism reporting terms, this impacted 2020 and 2021 calendar years of domestic tourism data. Data for 2022 and 2023 are detailed below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the preschool settings in South Down that will be moving from 12.5 hours provision to 22.5 hours in September 2025 as part of the delivery of his Early Education measures to expand preschool provision.
answer_text: I have approved both Bronte Primary School Nursery Unit and Drumadonnell Primary School Nursery Unit to transition to deliver the standardised pre-school education session length from September 2025.  Details of all settings approved thus far to transition have been published on the Department of Education website at Pre-School Education Programme - Standardisation | Department of Education.It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to the standardised session length. The speed of transition will largely be dependent on the resources available from the Executive at each stage, as well as the circumstances of individual pre-school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the progress of the return to in-person appointments after the Covid-19 restrictions by (i) GPs across all Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) health visitors across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to is important to us all. I would like to reassure you that GP practices are open and are continuing to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as appropriate. Our latest statistics, of week ending Friday 29 November 2024, show that of the 205,293 total GMS consultations across the region, 56% (114,733) of these consultations where in fact face-to-face appointments.GP services have been experiencing sustained, high levels of demand since before the COVID-19 pandemic. Practices have therefore continued to adapt how services are delivered in order to make best use of available resources for everyone who is seeking to access the care that they need. It is important that primary care continues to transform to meet growing patient demand.Under the general practice telephone first consultations model, it allows patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems. The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise. Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged. Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services. Health visitorsThe Southern, South Eastern and Northern Health and Social Care Trusts continued to offer face-to-face visits during the COVID-19 pandemic.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust undertook risk assessments prior to face to face appointments with health visitors using appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). Virtual contacts were used for those who declined face to face contact.The majority of the Western Health and Social Care Trust’s contacts were carried out face to face once the correct PPE was established and supplied to staff.  There was some reduction in home visiting due to parental concerns in the Southern area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the private health institutions that are recognised by his Department for diagnoses of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder to enable prescriptions to be issued through the health service.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process for capturing the reasons why patients miss hospital appointments.
answer_text: To ascertain the reason for non-attendance would require each Trust to undertake retrospective audits of those patients who failed to attend their appointment. Given the resources required to undertake this work, only small scale audits have been undertaken by Trusts to date.To help better understand the reason for non-attendance the Department have agreed to undertake a retrospective audit to ascertain the reasons why patients did not attend their appointment or treatment. The initial audit will focus on the South Eastern Trust. My Department is currently working with the Trust to agree the Data Access Agreement which will give staff access to the relevant patient level data. Pending the outcome of the audit, changes may be made to the current processes for engaging with patients, including booking letters and text reminders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how long patients were recorded as waiting for an attention deficit hyperactivity disorder assessment in 2023-24, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of GP service provision in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) is currently managing two GP practices:Kilkeel Medical Practice Mullaghbawn SurgeryThis current arrangement will remain in place until 31st March 2025 and working is ongoing to secure a permanent contractor for both practices from 1st April 2025. My Department continue to work with the Trust in the management of the practices. The SHSCT are striving to increase access to the practices for patients. In relation to those Practices in the SHSCT area, where a contract hand back fell in the current financial year my Department have worked with the 5 Practices in order to identify a new contractor. In respect of these Practices, 3 were taken over by another GP contractor, and the other 2 were taken over by the GP Federation Contract Management CIC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the estimated increase in the cost of employers' National Insurance contributions following the Autumn Budget 2024 on (i) his Department's arm's-length bodies; (ii) organisations delivering departmentally funded projects; and (iii) the overall voluntary and community sector.
answer_text: The estimated cost for my Department’s arm's-length bodies, following the Chancellors announced increase in Employers NIC is £2.0m.  As my department is not the employer for organisations delivering departmentally funded projects or Voluntary and Community organisations, no detail is held on the estimated costs increases for these respective groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated increase in the cost of employers' National Insurance contributions following the Autumn Budget 2024 on (i) his Department's arm's-length bodies; and (ii) organisations delivering departmentally funded projects.
answer_text: (i) The estimated increase in the cost of employers’ National Insurance contributions following the Autumn Budget 2024 on my Department’s arm’s-length bodies is anticipated to be in the region of £9m. This will be subject to change, depending on future pay awards and employee numbers. This includes NI Water, Translink, Driver and Vehicle Agency and Waterways Ireland.(ii) My department does not hold the information for the estimated increase in the cost of employers National Insurance contributions following the Autumn Budget 2024 on organisations delivering departmentally funded projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the projected overspend in each Health and Social Care Trust for 2024-25, including the projected savings as detailed in his Written Ministerial Statement of 21 November 2024.
answer_text: The latest reported financial forecasts from each Health and Social Care Trust for 2024-25 indicate a breakeven position for 2024-25 as plans to reduce expenditure are implemented, in line with the savings targets outlined in my Written Ministerial Statement of 21 November 2024.As outlined in the Written Ministerial Statement, the allocation of £350m from the latest Executive monitoring means that we are in a position where we expect to achieve financial balance without resorting to those measures that would cause immediate or serious harm. It does not, at this point, allow for full 2024/25 pay settlements in line with those in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement his Department has had on the issue of assisted dying.
answer_text: My Department has had no engagement on the issue of assisted dying.In Northern Ireland, encouraging or assisting the suicide or attempted suicide of another person or killing someone on compassionate grounds (euthanasia) remains a criminal offence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to progress data sharing within her Department, between the General Register Office and the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) Pension Scheme, in relation to death registration in order to reduce the occurrences of overpayments of a NICS pension after a person’s death.
answer_text: There is currently a data sharing arrangement in place between the General Register Office (GRO) and the Civil Service Pension Scheme for the provision of death certificates on a weekly basis.When a death registration is being completed by staff in a District Registration Office and the occupation is entered as civil servant the registrar checks a tick box to indicate that the pension organisation should be notified.  A weekly report is produced from this information with GRO staff issuing the death certificates to NICS Pensions at the beginning of each week.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the progress of the administration of the Change Fund included in the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls delivery plan.
answer_text: The Executive Office is investing £3.2m over the next 15 months in community-based action to help end violence against women and girls. The £2 million Local Change Fund was formally launched on the 8 January 2025. It will be open for applications across all 11 Councils between January and March 2025. Delivery will begin from April 2025. £1.2m investment in the Regional Change Fund was announced on the 21st of January 2025. This funding will strengthen the impact and reach of EVAWG expertise and programmes offered by key regional community and voluntary sector organisations. Eight organisations will be supported to increase delivery and coordination of EVAWG programmes and initiatives, making resources and expertise readily available to grassroot groups, schools and workplace settings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the progress on supporting technological and other innovations by the private sector included in the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls delivery plan.
answer_text: The Executive Office has utilised ring-fenced funding under the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) to launch two complementary and reinforcing Phase 1 SBRI challenges, namely:  Addressing Violence Against Women and Girls Through Data and Tech Innovations; andMaking Spaces Safer for Women and Girls Through Design and Technology.Following an open competition, 6 suppliers have been selected to develop diverse Phase 1 proofs of concept. The Phase 1 challenges will run from November 2024 to the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the total amount of money distributed through the Dormant Accounts Fund to date; and (ii) her plans on how the remainder will be distributed.
answer_text: ANSWERAs of November 2024, over £25 million has been awarded via Dormant Assets NI (formally Dormant Accounts Fund NI) including £19.8 million for the Phase One grant programme, £1.7m for strategic grants and £3.6 million for Access to Resilience. Under the policy directions set in 2022, dormant funding is made available to the voluntary, community and social enterprise (VCSE) sector to increase capacity, grow resilience and encourage sustainability. The National Lottery Community Fund (TNLCF) distributes the fund on behalf of the Department under the policy directions. An independent evaluation of the Phase One Grant Programme is due to complete in summer 2025 and an interim evaluation report is available to review. My officials along with TNLCF will review the findings from this evaluation to inform the design of the next dormant grant programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average time taken to pay legal aid bills by her Department in each of the last three years.
answer_text: In response to this request, Table 1 presents a breakdown of the average time taken by the Legal Services Agency (LSA) to process legal aid bills submitted by practitioners for the last three financial years, 2021/22 – 2023/24.  In addition, the current year to date position as at 31st October 2024 has also been provided.  To provide additional context, the number of payments that were authorised and completed for each year have been provided along with a breakdown of case and payment type.  Civil case payments (Advice and Assistance, Representation Lower and Representation Higher courts, Exceptional Funding) have been broken down into:Standard Fee – Cases that are largely based around standard fees for costs incurred;Taxed Case – Will largely consist of Payments where the legal aid bill of costs is taxed by the Taxing Master.  LSA staff then ensure that submitted payments align with the remuneration determined by the Taxing Master.Time and Line – Typically payments in Representation Lower and Exceptional Funding cases involving detailed assessment of expense lines which generally take more time and resource to process.Criminal case payments are largely based around Standard Fees for services provided.The underlying data has been sourced from the LSA’s case management system and constitutes management information that may be subject to revision.Table 1: Average Legal Aid Payment Processing Time and Number of Completed Payment Requests by Case and Payment TypeCase/Payment Type2021/20222022/20232023/202401/04/2024-31/10/2024Payments Completed1Average Weeks to Process2Payments Completed1Average Weeks to Process2Payments Completed1Average Weeks to Process2Payments Completed1Average Weeks to Process2CivilStandard Fee38,524 734,425 839,350 919,358 7Taxed Case1,597 41,427 61,838 5624 5Time and Line7,845 149,107 158,099 144,644 11Overall47,966 844,959 949,287 924,626 8Criminal42,738 940,640 1041,661 1222,487 11All Cases90,704 985,599 1090,948 1147,113 9Source: Legal Services AgencyNotes: Payments completed include payments that have been assessed within the referred financial year by LSA and subsequently authorised or rejected.Average weeks to process are based upon the length of time taken for properly presented bills to be assessed from date of submission to completion date.  In some cases where suppliers have not submitted enough information to proceed with payment, a query or message is raised by LSA staff to the supplier requesting further information or clarification which can lead to longer processing times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how the composition of the Tourism Partnership Board was determined; and (ii) why there is no representative of Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, Visit Mourne or Mourne Heritage Trust, given that the Mourne Mountains are one of the most significant tourist attractions in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i) The Tourism Partnership Board (TPB) brings together 27 key representatives from across the tourism and hospitality sectors as well as representatives from key Executive Departments, tourism agencies and local government. Members were selected in consultation with my departmental officials and Tourism NI colleagues and are experts in their field.  The broad range of representation on the TPB ensures a collaborative co-design approach to developing a set of actions to grow our tourism offering over the next 10 years. (ii) SOLACE NI, the representative body covering all the Council areas within the North, is a member of the TPB. The current SOLACE representative is the CEO of Newry, Mourne and Down District Council. My department alongside our delivery partner Tourism NI carried out a public consultation at the beginning of the year on a draft Tourism Strategy. Both the Mourne Heritage Trust and the Newry Mourne and Down District Council were respondents to that consultation. Feedback received from this exercise has been used to inform the draft Tourism Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a change in NI Water’s policy to accept contributions from developers towards wastewater works will be recognised as revenue raising which can be applied against the budget sustainability plan.
answer_text: The impact of developer contributions and how they could be classified as additional income, and the corresponding budgetary implications, will be fully assessed through stakeholder engagement and consultation.   It should be noted, however, that there is no guarantee that developers will choose to proceed with developments that may require a developer contribution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the timeline for the steps of the upcoming January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The upcoming January Monitoring Round was commissioned to departments on 26 November 2024. It will be split into a two-stage approach which is necessary to facilitate the tight timetable for the Spring Supplementary Estimates process. The first stage to include technical adjustments is due from departments by 06 December 2024, and the second stage to include transactions requiring Executive approval is due from departments by 03 January 2025. I will bring proposals to the Executive for agreement shortly after that.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of (i) reports; and (ii) convictions of (a) modern slavery; and (b) human trafficking in the last 12 months, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of crimes reported to police.  The recording of information in relation to crimes reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in part (i) of the question to the PSNI. The Department holds information on cases dealt with at courts in Northern Ireland.  In 2023, the most recent year for which statistics are available, there were fewer than three convictions at courts for a human trafficking  or modern slavery offence, prosecuted under the Human Trafficking and Exploitation (Criminal Justice and Support for Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.  Note:Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and at magistrates’ courts. Figures for council area have not been provided under rules of disclosure, due to the small number of cases involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the decision-making process in the Northern Ireland Civil Service to engage the Stonewall 2024 Workplace Equality Index survey and grading of employers; (ii) whether other organisations or providers were considered for this piece of work; and (iii) all costs associated with this work.
answer_text: The decision to engage the Stonewall 2024 Workplace Equality Index (WEI) survey was taken in accordance with the provisions of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 (‘the Act’) and the associated Secretary of State Guidance on decision-making for Departments.The WEI is only offered by Stonewall.  There is no cost to make a submission to the WEI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the tourism budget in each of the last three financial years (i) within Tourism NI spent directly on tourism in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area; and (ii) provided to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council to distribute on tourism themselves within the area.
answer_text: within Tourism NI spent directly on tourism in the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council area; and The Newry, Mourne & Down District Council (NMDDC) area benefits from a significant amount of promotional activity that takes place across a number of council areas, for which specific council area figures cannot be disaggregated. These include activities such as familiarisation trips by journalists and tour operators, training events and fully integrated marketing campaigns that take place twice a year in Spring and Autumn.  As a result, the figures provided below relate to direct spend solely in the NMDDC area, excluding broader regional activity that the NMDD region benefits from:2023/24 - £1.1m 2022/23 - £80k 2021/22 - £116k  provided to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council to distribute on tourism themselves within the area.The amounts provided directly to NMDDC to support tourism in their area over the past three years is:2023/24 - £Nil2022/23 - £50,6272021/22 - £308,505

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why Northern Ireland Tourism Statistics published by NISRA and detailing a variety of tourism statistics, omit four full financial years from 2019-20 to March 2023 citing Covid.
answer_text: ANSWERNISRA’s tourism statistics, which measure the number of visitors to the North and their value to the economy, are compiled using the four data sources detailed below. The first two of these are under NISRA’s direct control whereas the latter two fall to Central Statistics Office (CSO) Ireland. the Continuous Household Survey, carried out by NISRA via face-to-face interviews; the Passenger Survey, carried out by NISRA in face-to-face interviews;the Inbound Tourism Statistics, carried out by CSO in face-to-face interviews; andthe Household Travel Survey, carried out by CSO via paper surveys and moving to electronic data capture in 2023.As a result of the Covid-19 pandemic, all face-to-face interviewing on NISRA surveys was suspended in March 2020, with NISRA resuming its survey activity during 2021. However, it was not until January 2023 that the full suite of data required to produce tourism statistics (including those collected and processed by CSO) became available again. This affected information for the 34-month period March 2020 through to December 2022, impacting the production of the NISRA tourism statistics for Q1 2020 through to Q4 2022.In reporting terms, while this impacted three calendar years 2020, 2021 and 2022, it impacted the production of statistics for the four financial years 2019/20, 2020/21, 2021/22 and 2022/23. This is attributable to the 34 months of data impacted by Covid straddling these 4 rolling 12 month reporting periods. The latest release of the full suite of tourism statistics covered the 12-month rolling period up to March 2024 and can be accessed on NISRA’s NI Quarterly Tourism Statistics webpage.  The 2023 Annual Tourism Statistics publication (the first following a three year omission) was published on 29th August 2024 for the reporting period January 2023- December 2023. This publication can be accessed on NISRA’s NI Tourism Statistics Annual webpage.

answer_text: Civil Service employees that were employed on a full-time equivalent (FTE) salary point of over £124,500 and £90,500 in 2023-24 (March 2024), broken down by Department, is provided below.
