question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions she has had with UK Treasury since the Labour Government has taken power.
answer_text: Following the election, I had introductory meetings with both the Chief Secretary to Treasury and the Chancellor of the Exchequer in mid-July. I also met with the Chief Secretary to Treasury on 29h July. Then, along with the First and deputy First Minister I met with the Chancellor of the Exchequer on 12 September, to discuss the 2024-25 financial position, including a request for early clarification of any additional Barnett consequentials for 2024-25. On the 16 of September I met with the Chief Secretary to Treasury and Secretary of State I to discuss the pausing of funding for City and Growth Deals.Together with the Devolved Administration Finance Ministers I met with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance: Interministerial Finance Committee on 3 October, where I took the opportunity to highlight the need to invest in public services, infrastructure and public sector workers following years of Tory austerity and I also raised the fiscal rules, particularly in respect of investment in infrastructure. On the day of the Autumn Budget, 30 October, I also met the Chief Secretary to the Treasury.Following the Autumn Budget, the First Minister, Deputy First Minister, Minister for Agriculture and I, jointly wrote to the Chancellor regarding our concerns around the changes to Agricultural Property Relief and Business Property Relief and the impact of this. In addition, my officials are in regular contact with their counterparts in the Treasury to discuss any emerging issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the estimated increase in cost to the Education Authority employers contributions following the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: The initial estimate of the increase in cost to the Education Authority’s employers’ National Insurance Contributions (NIC) from 6 April 2025 is approximately £51m.  This figure includes NIC costs relating to Education Authority (EA) staff funded via the EA block grant and earmarked funding as well as schools-based staff funded via the Aggregated Schools’ Budget (ASB), which funds controlled, maintained, voluntary grammar and grant maintained integrated schools.  Schools-based costs are included on the basis that the ASB forms part of the EA’s budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the findings of the National Fraud Initiative in Northern Ireland 2024 Report, to detail the value of (i) the overpayments in the 46 cases where pension remained in payment after the date of death of the pensioner; and (ii) the amount recovered since detection.
answer_text: Civil Service Pensions have confirmed that they have 32 cases in relation to the findings of the National Fraud Initiative 2024 Report, where pension remained in payment after the date of death of the pensioner relating to the NICS Pension Scheme. The value of the overpayments in the 32 cases equates to £54,924.55, of which £39,749.00 has been recovered to date. These overpayments occur due to the notification of a death to Civil Service Pensions after payroll cut-off or the monthly pension has been paid. None of the overpayments noted have been raised as suspected fraud at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the £2.8 million provided in the October Monitoring Round for costs incurred by the PSNI due to disturbances over the summer is to reimburse monies already spent or to build resilience for the future.
answer_text: The £2.8 million provided in the October Monitoring Round for costs incurred by the PSNI due to disturbances over the summer is to reimburse monies already spent. The PSNI has advised the additional unfunded costs incurred include elements for pay, overtime, mutual aid and other support services in excess of normal policing operations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) consequences; and (i) action taken by her Department in relation to the 103 cases of rates avoidance detected in The National Fraud Initiative in Northern Ireland 2024.
answer_text: Of the cases identified, all liable parties have been billed for the appropriate amount of rates. A total of 50 have paid their full liability. A further 33 are making payments. The remaining cases are subject to normal recovery processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated increase in cost to the Health and Social Care Trusts and associated health arms-length bodies employers contributions following the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: My Department estimates the increase in cost to the Health and Social Care Trusts and the arms-length bodies to be in the region of some £100 million. It is my expectation that my Department will be provided with funding for these costs.Other providers such as those within Family Health Services and the Independent Sector will also have increased costs. I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these costs and therefore they will add to the many demands I will need to consider and prioritise once a budget settlement is received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the estimated increase in cost to the Northern Ireland Civil Service employers contributions following the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: In the Autumn Budget 2024, the Government announced an increase to the Employer Class 1 National Insurance contribution rates from 13.8% to 15.0%, effective from 6 April 2025. In addition, the threshold where employer contributions become payable will fall from £9,100 to £5,000.The increase in cost to Civil Service employers’ contributions as a result of the change is estimated to be £20.8m.  The Chancellor announced that there would be support for departments and other public sector employers in respect of these additional costs. Discussions are ongoing with the Treasury in respect of this funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16997/22-27 and AQW 16998/22-27, to detail the number of the cases that resulted in (i) clamping; and (ii) referrals to court.
answer_text: During 2023/24, 131 vehicles were clamped relating to 782 outstanding PCNs.During 2023/24, the Department referred 120 cases relating to 456 PCNs to the Enforcement of Judgements Office (EJO)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the NIEA has approved works to allow DfI Roads to commence repairs to reopen the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, following a landslide in 2023.
answer_text: NIEA officials have confirmed to DfI Roads that the installation of proposed temporary measures along a section of the A2 are permitted within the NIEA managed Rostrevor Forest Nature Reserve.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when the (i) A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor; and (ii) B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, will fully reopen following last year's landslides.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closures of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road Hilltown and A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor due to the land/verge slips caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023.Unfortunately, the scale and complexity of the work required at B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, together with many competing demands placed on my Department’s limited resources means that it will take time to deliver a safe and cost effective scheme.  My Department will however continue to prioritise this project to have the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown opened to two way traffic. Officials from my Department have concluded the complex design and environmental application/approval process on the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor landslip, which will allow a temporary solution to be constructed, and the road reopened to two-way traffic. At present I am unable to confirm when work will commence or when the road will reopen, although rest assured this will be as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of land slippages on either side of the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor.
answer_text: DAERA is not responsible for assessing the risk to roads and therefore does not have a site-specific assessment of land slippage risks in proximity to the A2 road.  DAERA has commissioned research projects to assess how the Northern Ireland coastal environment is changing. The Historical Shoreline Analysis project, undertaken by Ulster University for the Department, has identified this section of coastline at Rostrevor as relatively stable over the historical period, with only minor rates of erosion or accretion, largely attributed to the low energy conditions of the area. The Coastal Change Information Tool, also completed by Ulster University, does indicate that the long-term stability of this area may change in the future to a more erosive condition in light of sea level rise and an increase of extreme storm events.  This evidence and research are publicly accessible on the Northern Ireland Coastal Observatory website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much of the £1.5 billion increase to the Northern Ireland budget in 2025-26, announced in the Budget Statement, will be additional money to spend freely on new items rather than absorbed by inflation, pay increases and other already committed items.
answer_text: The funding announced by the Chancellor on 30 October for 2025-26, included the baselining of the £609.3 million Resource DEL consequential received for 2024-25 as well as the additional Barnett consequentials of £600.9 million Resource DEL and £270.8 million Capital DEL for 2025-26.Also included in the Executive’s Resource DEL baseline was the £332.9 million Resource DEL which was previously provided on earmarked basis for farm and fisheries support.Excluding Treasury earmarked funding the Executive’s 2025-26 Resource DEL is some £933 million higher than the Autumn Budget outcome for 2024-25. However, this includes the farm and fisheries support. Providing this at the same level in 2025-26 would reduce the increase to £600 million. I am currently developing proposals for a draft Budget for 2025-26 which will take account of committed items, pay increases, inflation and any new policies. Even with an increase on this year, it will be a very challenging budget which inevitably will not be able to fund everything we would wish to do.Subject to Executive agreement, I intend to publish a draft Budget for consultation in December.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has commissioned any research into the impacts of the recent budget announcement on specific sectors, either directly within her Department or in partnership with other bodies such as NICVA for the voluntary and community sector impact.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage with the Treasury to better understand the impact of the tax measures announced by the British Government at the Autumn Budget. Officials are also engaging other Executive departments to consider their analysis in this regard, and the feedback they have received from their stakeholders on the impact of the budget on individual sectors. I will then consider what further analysis or research might be useful to strengthen the evidence base. I will also be engaging directly with stakeholders, including NICVA, to discuss the impact of the Budget on them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 15525/22-27,  for an update on the delivery of the targeted business support scheme for childcare providers, including how much of the £2 million has been committed to childcare providers to date.
answer_text: As I outlined in my response to AQO 940/22-27, the Department for Education (DE) has responsibility for the targeted business support scheme for childcare providers. My officials are assisting DE with this work by leading on a market assessment of the childcare sector.  This research, which will identify the key issues impacting the financial sustainability of childcare providers and develop options for government intervention, has now commenced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current policy and practice on the value of overpayments, made by the Department, that are automatically written off.
answer_text: Officials in Civil Service Pensions (CSP) have advised that current policy effective from July 2019 is to automatically write off any pension overpayments under £200.A decision not to seek recovery on an overpayment over the automatically written off limit would be taken in line with Managing Public Money NI and the evidence provided.  Any decision not to pursue recovery, or not to pursue recovery in full, should be defensible in the public interest and must be approved at the appropriate level before the recipient(s) are notified of any write off. Such waivers should be exceptional. Pension overpayments which CSP decide not to pursue are also subject to departmental Guidance on Losses and Special Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he plans to take to assess and react to the impact of the Autumn Budget 2024 on the childcare sector in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Budget will affect local businesses in a variety of ways, though the nature of that will depend upon the characteristics of each individual business, including its size, which sector it operates in and how it chooses to manage new arrangements. There are welcome benefits for workers as a result of the increases in the National Living Wage and National Minimum Wage. However, rather than offsetting this increase in pay with a decrease in taxes, the Budget increases employer National Insurance contributions. At a time of high costs on businesses this will put many businesses under more strain.The full impacts of the Autumn Budget on individual sectors, including the childcare sector, will take time to become clear. In support of colleagues in the Department of Education, my department has recently commissioned research to determine the key issues impacting the financial sustainability of providers within the childcare sector in the North of Ireland.My officials and I will continue to engage with local business stakeholders and consider the full detail of the announcements, as well as the possible implications for the North’s economy, in the context of my economic plan.

answer_text: Civil Service Pensions overpayment figures for past three financial years. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he plans to take to assess and react to the impact of the Autumn Budget 2024 on the (i) retail; (ii) hospitality; and (iii) leisure sectors in Northern Ireland which the Chancellor has identified additional support for in England.
answer_text: The Budget will affect local businesses in a variety of ways, including those in the retail, hospitality and leisure sectors. The nature of that will depend upon the characteristics of each individual business, including its size, which sector it operates in and how it chooses to manage new arrangements. There are welcome benefits for workers as a result of the increases in the National Living Wage and National Minimum Wage. However, rather than offsetting this increase in pay with a decrease in taxes, the Budget increases employer National Insurance contributions. At a time of high costs on businesses, this will put many businesses under more strain.The Budget also reduced business rate relief in England for Retail, Hospitality and Leisure from 75% to 40%, and the Department of Finance has outlined that the Executive has received a reduction of £58.2 million in 2025-26 in relation to business rates. The Department of Finance is assessing the details and implications of the Transforming Business Rates paper published by Treasury following the Autumn Statement and further work can be progressed in this area by the Finance Minister, once the Executive finishes considering her proposals in relation to the strategic short, medium and long-term steps for the rating system.My officials and I will continue to engage with local business stakeholders and consider the full detail of the Budget announcements, as well as the possible implications for the North’s economy, in the context of my economic plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of staff that are employed in his Department in roles related to (i) human resources; (ii) employee relations; (iii) staff development; and (iv) staff well-being, broken down by grade.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) Human Resource functions were centralised within the Department of Finance in April 2017.   Accordingly my department does not recruit staff specifically for these roles.  However all line managers in the Department, and across in the NICS carry responsibilities in each of these areas as part of the wider leadership roles they are expected to play.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of staff that are employed in their Department in roles related to (i) human resources; (ii) employee relations; (iii) staff development; and (iv) staff well-being, broken down by grade.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Civil Service Human Resources (HR) function, which sits within the Department of Finance, provides operational HR advice and services to The Executive Office (TEO).  There is therefore no dedicated resource in TEO that manages these functions.  However, staff in the Department’s Business Services team would have some minor involvement in these four functions. The team consists of 1 Grade 7, 2 Deputy Principals, 1 part-time Staff Officer and 1 part-time Executive Officer 2, overseen by the Grade 5, Director of Corporate Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of staff that are employed in his Department in roles related to (i) human resources; (ii) employee relations; (iii) staff development; and (iv) staff well-being, broken down by grade.
answer_text: HR functions were centralised within Department of Finance in April 2017.  Departments are supported by NICSHR in the Department of Finance which is responsible for the operational delivery of a comprehensive range of HR services for the NI Departments including resourcing, employee relations, learning and development, occupational health and wellbeing, pensions and HR business partnering.People and Organisational Development (also in the in the Department of Finance) is responsible for HR/Civil Service people strategy; all the Civil Service’s HR, industrial relations and pay policies; and provision of strategic HR advice and support to the NICS Board, the Departments and the Public Prosecution Service.  Furthermore, following the Organisation, Development and Transformation programme, DfE prioritised roles to embed Workforce Management (WFM) and Learning and Culture (L&C).  The make-up of those teams is:WFM - 1 x Grade 7, 2 x Staff Officers, 1 x Executive Officer I and 1 x Executive Officer II; and L&C - 1 x Grade 7, 2 x Deputy Principal, 1 x Staff Officer, 2 x Executive Officer II and 1 x Administrative Officer

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of staff that are employed in her Department in roles related to (i) human resources; (ii) employee relations; (iii) staff development; and (iv) staff well-being, broken down by grade.
answer_text: Human Resources functions were centralised within the Department of Finance (DoF) in April 2017.  The DoF is responsible the operational delivery of a comprehensive range of HR services for all Departments including human resources, employee relations, learning and development, occupational health and wellbeing, industrial relations and pay policies.All line managers in the Department carry responsibilities in each of these areas as part of their wider leadership roles.   Within the Northern Ireland Prison Service, however, 17 staff are employed in a full-time or part-time basis where their sole role is staff development as follows:1 x Unit Manager8 x Senior Officer Tutors1 x EO14 x Part-Time AO staff   3 x Full -Time AO staffThere are no other staff within the Department for which HR, employee relations, staff development or staff well-being is their full-time role.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff that are employed in her Department, outside of the Northern Ireland Civil Service Shared Service on HR, in roles related to (i) human resources; (ii) employee relations; (iii) staff development; and (iv) staff well-being, broken down by grade.
answer_text: Departments in the Civil Service are supported by a centralised HR function, NICSHR and People and Organisational Development, both of which sit in my Department.NICSHR is responsible for the operational delivery of a range of shared HR services including resourcing, employee relations, learning and development, occupational health and wellbeing, pensions and HR business partnering.People and Organisational Development is responsible for the Civil Service People Strategy; all HR, industrial relations and pay policies, diversity and inclusion, and the provision of strategic HR advice and support to the NICS Board, departments and the Public Prosecution Service. All departments are supported by an outsourced HR Service, HRConnect, which provides payroll and other HR services to the Civil Service.In my Department, there are no staff outside the centralised HR function provided by NICSHR and People and Organisational Development employed to work on HR matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide, (i) whether its development included consultation with (a) staff; (b) unions; and (c) stakeholders; and (ii) whether staff will be referred to a disciplinary process if they use terminology within the guide, such as asking someone their Christian name.
answer_text: In developing the Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide, there was consultation with a range of internal and external stakeholders including the Civil Service Diversity Champions Network, Civil Service staff networks (LGBTQ+, Race and Ethnicity, Disability, and Women’s Networks), the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland and Employers for Disability Northern Ireland.As the Inclusive Language Guide is not policy, formal consultation with trade unions was not required under the established Whitley procedures. My Department regularly engages with Trade Unions on diversity and inclusion initiatives being developed by the Civil Service through its annual Diversity Action Plans, which included the Inclusive Language Guide.The Inclusive Language Guide is guidance only. It provides guiding principles and suggestions to encourage civil servants to be more conscious of the language they use and to better understand how the language they use can impact others and make them feel included or excluded.If civil servants witness or experience offensive language or behaviour in the workplace, they are encouraged to call it out, speak to management within their team or, where appropriate, use the procedures in the Civil Service Dignity at Work Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her Department’s engagement with the Department of Levelling Up, Housing and Communities on reviewing Energy Performance Certificates to improve the energy efficiency information provided to tenants, homeowners and other stakeholders.
answer_text: My officials have been liaising on an ongoing basis with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG), on matters relating to Energy Performance Certificates (EPCs). While the pace and detail of the review of EPC fitness is principally a matter for MHCLG to lead, my officials have engaged and provided feedback on proposals and I would encourage local interested groups and individuals to respond to any consultations which, in due course, MHCLG may issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of (i) the uptake and application of the Scoring Social Value policy approved by the Executive, which mandated that from June 2022 tenders must include a minimum of 10 per cent of the total award criteria to social value; (ii)	the quantification of the value of social value award in the 2023-24 financial year; and (iii) the details of the process in place for her Department to monitor the delivery of the social value clause in the procurement and contract management process.
answer_text: Scoring social value has been mandatory in above threshold services and works contracts since June 2022.  In this period, 429 contracts have included social value as a scored criteria.  The majority of these contracts have scored social value at the minimum 10% with a small number going above this figure.The table below outlines the social value delivered on contracts either awarded or live between 1 April 23 and 31 March 2024.  The data does not include details of the social value intended to be delivered in future years. Theme 1 – Secure Employment and SkillsInitiativeQuantityPaid employment for people who are unemployed, disadvantaged in the labour market or from deprived areas669 people who were disadvantaged in the labour marketSkills Development and Education Attainment1984 hoursDigital Inclusion initiatives592 hoursDonation of IT Equipment10 devicesEquality Diversity and Inclusion Strategy9 strategiesIn-work progression and skills development action plan for employees who are disadvantaged30 action plansWork experience80 weeksTheme 2 – Building Ethical and Resilient Supply ChainsInitiativeQuantityBusiness Development and Knowledge Sharing1687 hoursFair Work/Ethical Supply Chain/Human Rights 24 action plansMicro enterprises in the supply chain17Social enterprises in the supply chain42MSAT Tool3 completed Modern Slavery Assessment ToolTheme 3 – Delivering Zero CarbonInitiativeQuantityEnvironmental action plan for the contract38 action plansEnvironmental awareness initiatives1401 hoursTheme 4 – Promoting Well-beingInitiativeQuantityHealth and Wellbeing Initiatives2386 hoursHealth and Wellbeing Action Plan for staff on the contract18 action plansThe Strategic Investment Board’s Social Value Unit has developed a monitoring system which is capturing the social value delivered across all public sector contracts.  Relevant suppliers are given access to this monitoring system after the contract is awarded.  A report is generated from the system detailing what social value is delivered to date and is issued to the supplier and contract manager.  The Procurement Policy Note (PPN 01/21) includes details of the contract management arrangements to ensure social value is delivered in the same way as any other aspect of the contract.  An annual report is published which includes details on the implementation of the Scoring Social Value Policy. The report is also used to identify an contracts where departments or their Arms-Length bodies have sought Ministerial approval to opt out of the policy. To date, all bodies have adhered to the Policy and no Ministerial ‘opt outs’ have been reported.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the number of Penalty Charge Notices that made up the £1,306,562 written-off in 2023-24 as claims waived or abandoned; and (ii), of this total, to detail the number of individuals that were issued these claims.
answer_text: The Department has robust debt recovery procedures in place and will only consider a write-off debt when all of these procedures have been exhausted.  The PCN recovery process involves the issuing of three statutory pieces of correspondence under the terms of the Traffic Management Order (Northern Ireland) 2005 and in cases of non-payment may be followed up with clamping or collection of the debt using the Enforcement of Judgement Office. The level of debt write-off may fluctuate annually depending upon the number of cases where recovery procedures have been fully exhausted and is subject to DoF approval.In 2023/24 a total of £1,306,562 was written off equating to 11,183 PCNS. It is not possible to quantify how many individuals received these PCNs as personal data is no longer retained as per my Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the adequacy of the process for the collection of Penalty Charge Notices issued, following the increase in write-offs from £1 million in 2022-23 to £1.3 million in 2023-24.
answer_text: The Department has robust debt recovery procedures in place and will only consider a write-off debt when all of these procedures have been exhausted.  The PCN recovery process involves the issuing of three statutory pieces of correspondence under the terms of the Traffic Management Order (Northern Ireland) 2005 and in cases of non-payment may be followed up with clamping or collection of the debt using the Enforcement of Judgement Office. The level of debt write-off may fluctuate annually depending upon the number of cases where recovery procedures have been fully exhausted and is subject to DoF approval.In 2023/24 a total of £1,306,562 was written off equating to 11,183 PCNS. It is not possible to quantify how many individuals received these PCNs as personal data is no longer retained as per my Department’s Retention and Disposal schedule.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department’s progress against targets in the current year on the commitment in the 2020-2030 Forests for the Future Programme by planting 600 hectares of new woodland.
answer_text: My Department has current plans and grant agreements in place with private landowners at this stage of the year, to plant approximately 525 hectares of new woodland against the 600 hectare target in the 2024/25 business plan. Forest Service is continuing to engage with land owners and the forestry sector with a view to taking further projects forward in the forthcoming planting season to achieve the target by the end of the business year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the challenges in addressing homelessness in rural areas.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring the vitality and viability of our rural areas and to addressing rural housing need. One of my key priorities as Minister is delivery of social housing, and I know that delivery in rural areas presents significant challenges and requires different solutions than urban areas.My Department has set up a Rural Housing Steering Group which includes representatives from Department for Communities, Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, NI Housing Executive, and Land & Property Services.The overall aim of the Steering Group is to explore the barriers to rural housing development and develop, agree, and oversee the implementation of a plan which will aim to address them. These barriers include availability of land, grant, need, infrastructure, and planning.The Housing Executive has acknowledged there are particular challenges associated with rural homelessness and these are outlined in key strategies including the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 and the Rural Strategy 2021-2025.The Rural Strategy 2021-2025 emphasises that rural housing is interlinked with many other policy areas such as demography, access to employment, and infrastructure such as effective transport networks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what actions are being taken to address rising levels of bovine TB.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis is one of the most challenging issues faced by the cattle farmers in Northern Ireland.  I share Members’ concerns regarding the current high rate of TB incidence and the impact this is having on hard working farm families.  With herd incidence currently at over 10%, one farm in ten has experienced the difficulties associated with a new TB breakdown in the past year. I have heard directly from farmers the impact this is having both from a financial perspective but also, importantly, on their mental and physical health.Tackling this disease is also having a significant impact on my Department’s budget.  Almost 56 million pounds was spent on the control of TB in the last year, whilst current estimates indicate a rise in programme costs to approximately 60 million pounds for this financial year.  The high cost of controlling TB is not sustainable- not to farm businesses nor to my Department. To address the rising levels of bTB, I have asked Brian Dooher, Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO), to undertake a thorough review of matters relating to our TB programme and policy, including the actions contained in the Bovine TB Eradication Strategy. This review will include an assessment of possible enhanced measures to address cattle to cattle spread and outline next steps pertaining to our stakeholder engagement and participation.  The review will also plot the path ahead in determining the most appropriate means to address the role played by badgers in the spread and endurance of the disease and provide associated timelines.   The Chief Veterinary Officer has engaged with a wide range of stakeholders to hear their views and the latest evidence on how bovine TB can be successfully tackled here.  The CVO has also consulted with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute and colleagues in neighbouring jurisdictions to ensure that we are fully up to date on work currently taking place and that our TB Programme is based on the latest evidence and best practices.I am looking forward to considering the CVO’s findings and I intend to publish a new delivery plan before the end of the year.  I anticipate that this plan will set out our immediate and medium-term priorities and will seek to reinvigorate our efforts to support farmers in tackling this costly disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the recovery of fraudulent payments made under the Omicron Hospitality Payment scheme.
answer_text: Following PSNI investigation of five suspected cases of fraud in respect to the Omicron Hospitality Payment (OHP) scheme, the PSNI advised that the cases did not merit criminal investigation. Therefore, Land and Property Services does not consider any fraudulent payments to have issued in respect of the OHP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of Project Bank Accounts in existence where the Department of Finance is a trustee; and (ii) the number of these accounts that have been used in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: There are two Project Bank Accounts in existence where the Department of Finance is a trustee and both these accounts have been used in each of the last three financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of full time equivalent staff vacancies in the Department of Finance in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Details of the total number of vacant posts is not held centrally within the Department of Finance. Each business area is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each business area determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a business area within the Department of Finance identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all business areas on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. My Department reports vacancies in terms of headcount. The table below gives the number of staff vacancies in the Department of Finance at the beginning of each of the last three financial years.Financial Year Number of vacancies22/2341823/2435424/25373	  Source: NICSHR

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the progress of the class action case Public Sector Pensions – Injury to Feelings Claims, in which the Department of Finance is a name respondent in a class action affecting employers across the public sector and is managing claims on behalf of the Northern Ireland Civil Service departments.
answer_text: These claims are being managed by my Department on behalf of the NICS by the HR Litigation Unit (HRLU) in NICSHR, in co-ordination with Civil Service Pensions and the Departmental Solicitors Office (DSO). Given the very significant number of cases lodged, the Office of the Industrial Tribunals is still processing claims. However, responses have been served by DSO on behalf of the NICS Departments for those claims received to date. Proceedings are therefore still in the initial stages of case management by the Tribunal and there are no substantive developments to report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) staffing; and (ii) vacant posts in maternity services in (a) Daisy Hill Hospital; and (b) Craigavon Area Hospital.
answer_text: Southern Trust is currently funded for 243.06 whole time equivalent (WTE) Midwives Band 5 – 7.  There are 231.10 WTE midwives in post based in  Daisy Hill Hospital, Craigavon Hospital & Community localities (Armagh & Dungannon, Craigavon & Banbridge & Newry & Mourne). question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the nature of the £128,000 loss described as fruitless payments in her Department's Annual Reports and Accounts 2023-24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the direction given to withdraw medical staff from special schools in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the steps his Department is taking to support children with medical needs in special schools.
answer_text: No such direction was issued by my Department. My Department does not commission Community Children’s Nurses (CCN) in special educational needs schools. However, as the result of legacy issues within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust there are five Band 5 CCNs and three Band 3 Health Care Assistants working across five special needs schools.  Additionally, one school in the South-Eastern Health and Social Care Trust area has continued to have a nurse present throughout the school day. Within the other three trust areas CCNs do not work within special schools.    My Department is legally required to work together with the Department of Education to support children with additional needs within schools.   The Education Authority (EA) is commissioned to and responsible for providing the necessary care to allow children to attend special school.  This care is delivered through the employment of Classroom Assistants.Some children that attend special schools in Northern Ireland require additional support to allow them to access the curriculum.  Each child will have specific, individual needs and therefore the support for each child is assessed through their personal Special Educational Needs (SEN) assessment and that individual support is delivered to the child.CCNs provide the required training to those Classroom Assistants to enable them to provide child specific, timely care through the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council delegation framework, in line with each child’s individual care plan. While the time commitment to transition the necessary skills and confidence to Classroom Assistants is variable, the goal for them to lead the delivery of the child’s individual support remains the same.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps she has taken to address the legal practitioner fraud and error reported in the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland, which led to the Northern Ireland Audit Office qualification of its 2023-24 annual accounts.
answer_text: The Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) has qualified the financial statements of the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland (LSANI) since its creation in respect of fraud and error. In 2019, the LSANI introduced a significant work programme to address the qualification and has worked in collaboration with the Standards Assurance Unit (SAU) within the Department for Communities (DfC) to design and implement methodologies for measuring fraud and error within the Northern Ireland legal aid system. There are a number of reasons why legal aid expenditure may not be applied for the purposes intended by the Assembly or conform to the authorities which govern them: Official error – where an error can be attributed to the actions or inactions of LSANI; Legal practitioner fraud and error; and Applicant fraud and error.From 2019 SAU have completed several years of substantive assessment of the level of official error. The approach is similar to that used by DfC to measure fraud and error within the wider benefits system in Northern Ireland. The official error rate has continued to show improvement with the 2023 rate being the lowest official error rate within the last five years. In 2023, SAU commenced independent measurement of practitioner fraud and error to allow a baseline to be established for the first time, the NIAO welcomed this development. Compliance with the scheme has been very positive. Testing is carried out by SAU by reviewing practitioner files for evidence to substantiate legal aid payment claims on sampled cases. Payments are withheld until practitioner files are submitted to the Agency. SAU’s findings have highlighted issues relating to the way in which practitioners record the work undertaken in their files. To address these findings, the Agency regularly issues guidance to the legal profession on evidence requirements and has suggested the use of templates to support the preparation of bills. These initiatives are designed to support in the reduction of the level of practitioner fraud and error. Examples of issues highlighted in the Agency’s guidance include:recording the start and end times of telephone calls; recording the length of time recorded for consultations; reading time; court preparation; advocacy; waiting time; and evidence of letters or emails held in file, to match against the dates that are recorded on the bill for when such items were issued. The Agency has recently introduced a fee adjustment and recoupment policy.  Any payment claims submitted by practitioners from 1 July 2024 onwards may be subject to recoupments where an error has been identified by SAU as part of the Practitioner File Review, including inadequate supporting evidence within the file. To complement the review of practitioner files, which looks at individual files, the Agency has recently established a data analytics unit. The new unit will analyse patterns of claiming across all practitioner cases. The Agency will continue to assist the legal

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether each local council area has a Local Development Plan in place; and (ii) to detail the dates for which the current plans cover.
answer_text: All Councils are currently preparing new Local Development Plans (LDPs) to provide the spatial framework for development for their areas over a 15-year period. Four Councils have adopted their Plan Strategies to date which is the first stage of LDP preparation, and they continue to work towards their Local Policies Plan preparation which is the second stage of the LDP process. Fermanagh and Omagh District Council were the first Council to adopt their Local Development Plan Plan Strategy in March 2023 and the plan covers a period up to 2030. Belfast City Council Plan Strategy was adopted shortly thereafter in May 2023 and provides strategic policy direction up to 2035. Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council Plan Strategy was adopted in September 2023 and runs to 2032 and Mid and East Antrim Borough Council Plan Strategy was adopted in October 2023 and covers a period up to 2030.The timing of Local Development Plans is primarily a matter for each Council to decide in line with its priorities and the resources available to it. My officials continue to support the remaining Councils as they work towards their draft Plan Strategy publication. While this work is being taken forward, planning decisions in these Council areas will be made on the basis of extant Development Plan documents and retained planning policy together with the Strategic Planning Policy Statement. Any relevant supplementary and best practice guidance will also continue to apply in this transitional period. Details of extant Development Plans can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/topics/planning/departmental-development-plans and current planning policy can be accessed via the following link: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/planning-policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the current environmental safety of the Aughrim River, Kilkeel.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) currently monitors the Aughrim River at Kilkeel as part of its routine monitoring programme for the Water Framework Directive (WFD). The most recent WFD assessment of water quality status published in 2021 showed that the Aughrim River is at “Moderate” ecological status for soluble reactive phosphorous and diatoms. This assessment indicates that there is nutrient enrichment within the water body. In respect of the Aughrim River (also known as the Little Kilkeel River), there have been 14 confirmed water pollution incidents in the inclusive period 2019 to 2023. No incidents were assessed as High Severity, three were categorised as Medium Severity which were related to Moderate fish kills and 11 as low severity.  From investigation, no definitive sources were identified for the medium incidents, however sampling indicated a domestically available chemical as the source. A pollution prevention leaflet drop was completed to raise awareness of the potential environmental damage such products can cause. Formal action is not normally instigated for low severity incidents, however actions are taken to stop the source and prevent a recurrence.NIEA operates an Incident Hotline, on 0800 807060, that is available 24 hours per day, 365 days per year to the reporting of water pollution and other environmental incidents.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has considered transferring the legacy civil law responsibilities in her Department from before Justice was devolved to Northern Ireland to the Department of Justice.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer provided for AQW 12346/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of why the cost of a provisional driving licence in Northern Ireland is higher than the rest of the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is responsible for the administration of driver licensing for those drivers who are resident in the north and the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Britain is responsible for the administration of driver licensing for drivers who are resident in England, Scotland and Wales. The National Driver License Service (NDLS) is responsible for those drivers who are resident in the south (RoI). You are correct that the fee charged for a first provisional licence in the north is more expensive than in Britain.  This difference in fees is largely attributable to the economies of scale in terms of volumes of licences issued.  Novice drivers in the south pay a provisional licence fee and a further fee for their first full licence when they pass their driving test.  This makes their combined licensing fee higher than for someone in the north, where they do not have to pay for converting their provisional licence into a full licence. Following the recent introduction of a range of online driver licensing services, the DVA has commenced a review of driver licensing fees and will consult as required on any proposed changes to the existing fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) completed surveys in the last three years; and (ii) planned surveys, of women on (a) perinatal mental health; and (b) maternal morbidity.
answer_text: Perinatal Mental HealthTrustCompleted SurveysPlanned SurveysBelfastIn the last 3 years the Perinatal Mental Health Service have gathered pre and post intervention questionnaires for the following groups:Postnatal Support GroupEmotional Regulation Group Incredible Babies GroupIn addition, they collected pre and post activity questionnaires for their Peer-led Co-Produced Service Improvement Project, and questionnaires to partners in relation to partners support for the purpose of service improvement.  The service complete intervention -based outcome measures when a patient enters the service, during treatment and on discharge.On discharge, the service asks patients to complete a qualitative questionnaire to gain feedback on the patient’s experienceSouth Eastern NoNoNorthernNoNoSouthernSouthern Trust Perinatal Mental Health Team have completed the following surveys with women on their caseload in 23/24 period:Survey of whole caseload on what groups they would like the team to developSurvey of partners / next of kin to establish what support they would likeSurvey of women on caseload, Maternity staff & Mental Health staff on PEPP plans for a quality improvement projectAll women complete a CORE 34 survey on admission and discharge to the team, and a CORE 10 is completed when various work is completed for example in groups, OT sessions, anxiety work.A Health Visitor completes Karatane with women, specific to Mother and Baby interactions during Incredible years baby groupAll women complete a POEM on discharge from the teamHealth Visitor are planning to submit survey to Core Health Visiting Teams and women on caseload on how to develop the Health Visiting role within the teamWesternThe Perinatal Team use the outcome measure Core 34 survey at the start and the finish of a women’s Care Journey and the Core 10 survey is issued at points throughout their journey to measure any improvement in their mental health.   The Trust send questionaries’ to both the professionals and the Service Users asking how they found their experience of the PEPP Planning and the outcome. The Trust midwife developed a satisfaction questionnaire regionally with midwifery colleagues in Perinatal Mental Health Teams in relation to their experience of engaging with Midwives in the team.  The Trust also use Care Opinion were families are given a QR Code Questionnaire for both themselves and their families to complete.Currently our Health Visitor and her Regional colleagues, similar to midwifery are developing a survey to send out to ascertain the impact of the Perinatal Specialist Health Visitor.Maternal morbidityTrusts provide data which includes surveys to MBRRACE-UK: Mothers and Babies: Reducing Risk through Audits and Confidential Enquiries across the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the actions he intends to take forward to address child poverty require supporting measures from the Department of Education and the Department of Health.
answer_text: It is essential that the Executive works together to support individuals and families as they work to escape poverty. My officials are currently working on the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy designed to minimise the risks and impact of poverty and to help all in our communities, including our young people, to avoid the risk factors that lead to experiencing poverty. My Department is currently engaging with officials from other Departments including Health and Education to discuss the development of the Strategy and the delivery of a range of keystone actions which will help mitigate impacts, reduce the risk of falling into poverty and support all people including children to exit poverty.Dealing with poverty means not only dealing with low incomes. It will also involve us tackling issues such as educational attainment gaps and health inequalities, as well as building a society and economy that provide opportunities for the future of children and young people in all our communities in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the targeted business support scheme for childcare providers, announced in May 2024.
answer_text: Question 12 – AQO 940/22-27The Department for Education has responsibility for the targeted business support scheme for childcare providers. However, as my Department has expertise in delivering business support, my officials are assisting DE with this work by leading on a market assessment of the childcare sector. This exercise will identify the key issues impacting the financial sustainability of childcare providers and develop options for a government intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of enforcement proceedings invoked against developers who failed in their responsibility to provide road bonds before commencement of work on site, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: There have been no instances in the last three years where my Department has needed to carry out enforcement proceedings against developers who failed to provide a road bond prior to commencement of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Northern Ireland Civil Service employees currently on a Home Working contract, broken down by (i) Department; and (ii) grade.
answer_text: Hybrid working is a non-contractual arrangement enabling a mix of workplace, home and remote working subject to business needs.  Civil servants seeking to avail of hybrid working complete a work style agreement (setting out the agreed split between workplace, home or remote working) for approval by their line manager. The Department of Finance does not hold information centrally on the total number of civil servants availing of hybrid working across the Service. It may assist you to know however that a service wide mapping exercise of job roles was undertaken at the time of developing the Hybrid Working policy. As of October 2021, 24,710 roles had been categorised, encompassing 213 locations, indicating that approximately 70% of Civil Service roles, could be carried out on a hybrid basis; 14% were mobile roles (i.e. which must be undertaken at a variety of locations away from a specific workplace base); and 16% were workplace based.  The hybrid working policy is currently being reviewed.  Consideration will be given to monitoring arrangements and minimum requirements for workplace attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 15297/22-27, for further details on the steering group on the childcare business support scheme, including (i) whether the membership of the steering group has now been allocated; and (ii) the date he has requested for the completion of the market assessment exercise.
answer_text: The membership for the steering group includes officials from:Department of EducationDepartment of HealthDepartment for the EconomyInvest NIThe terms of reference for the market assessment have been agreed. Officials are working to finalise the details.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 14423/22-27, to detail (i) any steps he has taken to address the barrier of GP sign-off to accessing further education and training places in the childcare sector; and (ii) any steps he plans to take in the future.
answer_text: I can advise that the guidelines regarding General Practitioners’ sign-off for training places in the childcare sector are produced by the Department of Health, rather than by my Department.I am aware that the Department of Health has taken the step to introduce an updated process in September 2024 whereby students are to complete a medical self-certification not requiring a General Practitioners’ sign-off. The requirement for sign-off will now only be necessary where any vetting checks reveal information of concern. It is my understanding that Further Education colleges are adhering to these updated guidelines which should address the barrier of a General Practitioners’ sign-off for accessing childcare courses within Further Education colleges in the majority of cases.Whilst I currently have no plans to take any further steps in the future, as this is a change to the previous policy, my officials will continue to liaise with Further Education colleges on any issues arising from revised guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of additional revenue his Department has raised in 2024-25 from the increase in public contributions to domiciliary care services; and (ii) the direction that was provided on the policy and quantity of increases applied to patients accessing services.
answer_text: Domiciliary or home care is defined as the range of services put in place to support an individual in their own home. There is currently no charge for domiciliary care packages in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of increased revenue raised in the 2024-25 financial year, broken down by revenue stream.
answer_text: Whilst the exact amount of revenue raised in 2024-25 will not be known until the conclusion of the financial year, current projections are indicating that approximately £80 million additional income will be raised in 2024-25. The detail is set out in the Budget Sustainability Plan published on 03 October 2024.https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/budget-sustainability-plan

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department has undertaken to develop a scheme to support childcare providers, as included as part of the Early Years package announced by the Minister of Education in May 2024.
answer_text: The Department of Education has responsibility for the targeted business support scheme. My Department does not have the statutory responsibility for, or policy expertise in, this specific sector. It does, however, have considerable experience of delivering business support and therefore is well placed to provide advice on the issue. I have therefore directed my officials to chair a steering group with representation from DE, DoH and Invest NI, which will oversee a market assessment exercise. This will identify the key issues impacting the financial sustainability of providers within the childcare sector and the implications for the supply of childcare places. This evidence will then be used to inform the development of options for an appropriate government response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the cost to her Department to complete Access NI checks for those applications which require referrals to the UK Central Authority for the exchange of criminal record information in respect of EU nationals seeking to work with children, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: There are no costs to the Department in conducting AccessNI checks as all associated costs are included in the fees charged within its overall cost-recovery model.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the 6.4 per cent increase to the Supporting People scheme applied this year will be recurrent.
answer_text: I am fully committed to improving the quality of life for vulnerable people in our society and made it my priority at the beginning of this mandate to visit schemes that deliver housing support through the Supporting People Programme.Accordingly, I decided to allocate an additional £3m of funding to the Programme for last financial year and an additional £4.8m this financial year equating to a 6.4% increase above the final EQIA for 2023/24.The funding available for the Department in 2024/25 was set within the context of a constrained financial position and the overall Departmental budget position was considered against competing priorities.  Due to the financial constraints on my Department’s budget, it is not possible to consider a recurrent funding model at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of tuck money given to prisoners each week, broken down by prison facility.
answer_text: The weekly allowance granted to prisoners is a regime payment based on their current level of behaviour and positive engagement.Standard RegimeThose who have attained the Standard Regime will receive payment of £11.00 per week.Enhanced RegimeThose who have attained the Enhanced Regime will receive payment of £20.00 per week.Basic RegimeThose on the Basic regime will receive payment of £4.00 per week.Separated prisoners Those on the Tier 2 regime will receive payment of £15.00 per week.The last finance report for August shows an average weekly allowance of £14.69This applies across all Prisons: however, Magilligan does not hold Separated prisoners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Northern Ireland Dermatology Photo Triage Pathway, for his assessment of (i) how much time on a waiting list has been saved on average per patient by using the technique; and (ii) the savings to his Department by adoption and use of the pathway.
answer_text: Patients who are referred using the Dermatology Photo Triage pathway will normally have their image reviewed within 72 hours, with a clinical decision regarding the care plan made at that time. However, patients who are referred directly to secondary care can wait over 15 weeks for a red flag appointment and over a year for an urgent appointment.There has been a total of 15,645 red flag and urgent referrals managed through the Photo Triage pathway from March 2022 to July 2024. Of the 15,645 patients referred during this period, 1,005 have been managed within primary care avoiding the need for an outpatient attendance. Based on an average new outpatient unit price the saving has been approximately £190,000. A further 995 referrals have been redirected straight to biopsy or excision, again avoiding the need for an outpatient attendance and saving approximately £189,000.  For those patients requiring an outpatient assessment in secondary care, the consultant is able to effectively manage the patient in a one stop appointment as the images and other essential clinical information have been provided. This again frees up capacity for other dermatology patients including red flag and urgent patients. Further savings will be made as more GPs are trained on the Photo Triage pathway.It should also be welcomed that this new pathway has recently been recognised at the National Quality in Care Dermatology Awards. The pathway received two awards including Patient Care Pathway - Primary, Secondary and Community and Dermatology Digital and Technology Solutions for the Treatment of Skin Conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients accessing free local healthcare, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust areas for (i) 2022-23; and (ii) 2023-24 that were resident (a) in the Republic of Ireland; (b) elsewhere within the UK; and (c) outside the UK and Ireland
answer_text: The following information was supplied by the HSC Trusts in response to this question.Belfast HSC TrustBelfast Health and Social Care Trust does not record patients who access treatment that is exempt and technically ‘free’ of charge at the point of care (these include services such as Emergency Department, Infectious Diseases etc.) regardless of their residence outside Northern Ireland. Patients accessing services who are from outside Northern Ireland would be chargeable and recouped by an agreement, for example EHIC (European Health Insurance Card), Healthcare Insurance etc., unless they meet an exemption, as per Provision of Health Services to Persons Not Ordinarily Resident Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (legislation.gov.uk)Southern HSC TrustThe Trust does not record patients who access Treatments that are exempt from charges at the point of care such as Emergency Department, Infectious Diseases etc. as these categories are not charged for healthcare regardless of ordinarily resident in NI.The Trust does not charge for treatment to non-residents who would be recovered under an agreement e.g. EHIC, Healthcare insurance etc unless they meet an exemption eg Provision of Healthcare to Persons not Ordinarily Resident in Northern Ireland Regulations 2015. Any patients who do not meet the above criteria are charged for treatment received.Western HSC TrustThe Trust does not record patients who access treatments that are exempt from charges and therefore ‘free’ at the point of care regardless of ordinarily resident in NI.The Trust could only provide numbers for chargeable patients from these areas as it does not routinely record country of origin for patient who meet with an exemption. As this information is not captured on the systems, the Trust is  unable to provide the information requested.South Eastern HSC TrustThe Trust does not charge for treatment to non-residents who would be recovered under an agreement e.g. EHIC, Healthcare insurance etc unless they meet an exemption eg Provision of Healthcare to Persons not Ordinarily Resident in Northern Ireland Regulations 2015. Any patients who do not meet the above criteria are charged for treatment received.Confirmed in 2022, there were 540 patients from outside NI who accessed free healthcare at the point of delivery and 490 in 2023.  This information is not readily available in any greater detail.In 2024 the Trust changed how it recorded this activity and it  will be available broken down by country going forward.Northern TrustThe UK government has several international healthcare arrangements with partner countries referred to as reciprocal arrangements, including with the Republic of Ireland (ROI). Each case of attendance where the patient is identified as a non-NI resident is reviewed on an individual basis and patients have to either provide proof of eligibility for health care or details of insurance, if not they are charged.Northern Trust provided the table below to illustrate its breakdo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the criteria for the pricing of land which is acquired by Government departments; and (ii) the rationale behind decision-making between agriculture land or developmental land.
answer_text: the criteria for the pricing of land which is acquired by Government departmentsDepartments will generally acquire property at market value reflecting a wide variety of factors including sales evidence, planning permission, quality of the land, location, access, use and legal title.  Officials in LPS are accredited by the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS) and provide expert valuations and advice to departments in such cases. They adhere to the RICS Valuation – Global Standards which defines Market Value as “The estimated amount for which an asset or liability should exchange on the valuation date between a willing buyer and a willing seller in an arm’s length transaction, after proper marketing and where the parties had each acted knowledgeably, prudently and without compulsion (see International Valuation Standards 104 paragraph 30.1).”Where land is being acquired by vesting or under the threat of vesting the procedure for assessing compensation is based on what is commonly referred to as the “Compensation Code”, which can include additional payments made to the land/property owner.  The Code is made up of Acts of Parliament, case law and established practice including The Lands Tribunal and Compensation Act (NI) 1964, the Planning and Land Compensation Act (NI) 1971, the Land Acquisition and Compensation (NI) Order 1973, the Land Compensation (NI) Order 1982 and the Planning Blight (Compensation) (NI) Order 1981. In such cases compensation will include the landowner’s reasonable cost of employing an agent to negotiate the level of compensation with LPS valuers.the rationale behind decision-making between agriculture land or developmental land.Land is designated as agricultural or development land under either an area plan for that location or by a planning permission for the specific land being acquired. This planning designation will be one of the considerations impacting the value of the land.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how much their Department has invested in events for Good Relations Week 2024.
answer_text: Good Relations Week 2024 focused on the theme of ‘Opportunity’ – a call to action to create a brighter, inclusive future for all.Over 200 events were delivered - showcasing the creativity and dedication of local groups, organisations and individuals who are working together to address challenges such as sectarianism, racism, inequality, and well-being.  Events and activities included workshops, lectures, panel discussions, feature talks, podcasts, films, music performances, and exhibitions.We were delighted to provide ongoing support to the Week through Ministerial involvement in the Good Relations Week launch event earlier this year; the recording of a promotional video to mark the launch of the week; and through Ministerial attendance at a number of events taking place during Good Relations Week, including the T:BUC Engagement Forum.   Delivered by the Community Relations Council on behalf of The Executive Office, they allocated £27,000 to support Good Relations Week 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the cost of temporary and agency staff to cover sickness absence in the Northern Ireland Civil Service in each of the last three years.
answer_text: NICS Agency workers are recruited for finite, special pieces of work or projects, servicing the contract with the Department of Work and Pensions (DWP) and temporary pressures arising from EU Exit and Covid. NICSHR does not hold any information on the use of agency workers to cover sickness absence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action she has taken to address the reported increase in Northern Ireland Civil Service sickness absence rate to 13.8 days per staff member in 2023-24.
answer_text: Reducing sickness absences is a priority for the Civil Service. Anxiety, stress, depression and other psychiatric illnesses are the largest reason for absence. Given the prevalence of mental health issues, both in the workplace and wider society, the Civil Service has taken a proactive approach to promoting and supporting colleagues, offering a comprehensive range of interventions. This includes a welfare support service, access to a free 24-hour counselling service, alongside wellbeing, mental health awareness and sickness absence training interventions for staff and managers.  The Civil Service has also recently developed a new overarching NICS Health and Wellbeing Framework to support staff and managers. The Civil Service is also placing an increased focus on our occupational health and wellbeing service and promoting the preventative role that it can play to support the health and wellbeing of staff.With over four fifths (82.6%) of all working days lost through long-term absence, the NICS will continue to work jointly with colleagues, Trade Unions and other partners to support people to stay in work and return to work as soon as they are able to provide the best possible public service.
