question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of facilities in Northern Ireland Civil Service accommodation that has childcare provision onsite; and (ii) whether she is giving consideration to the need and impact of having childcare provision in place.
answer_text: (i) At present there are no onsite childcare facilities within the Civil Service Office Estate. (ii) The draft Programme for Government 2024-2027 includes a commitment to deliver more affordable childcare and to develop the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  This will be supported by this year’s immediate action for a £25 million package of childcare support allocated from the 2024/25 budget.The Executive recognises the difficulties faced by the childcare sector and families.  Despite the very challenging budget, addressing the issue of appropriate and affordable childcare that meets the needs of parents and children is a priority issue for the Executive.Options for childcare provision will be explored and will inform the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy to be considered by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current number of staff delivering (a) the spinal monitoring process; and (b) surgeries to treat scoliosis patients; and (ii) the number of posts vacant in these categories.
answer_text: Treatment for scoliosis involves the attachment of metal rods to the vertebrae which are subsequently adjusted to straighten the spine over a period of months.  Belfast Trust provides scoliosis surgery for adults and children in Northern Ireland with the procedure undertaken by specialist spinal surgeons in the Royal Victoria Hospital and the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. The Belfast Trust Scoliosis service have advised that  (a) The current number staff delivering the spinal monitoring process is one consultant Neurophysiologist. (b) The current number of staff delivering surgeries to treat scoliosis patients is six Spinal Consultant Surgeons. Spinal monitoring is a very niche area of neurophysiology and not all Consultant Neurophysiologists will undertake such monitoring.   There are 3 Consultant vacancies across the Neurophysiolgy speciality that are covered by locum Neurophysiologists, although they are not trained to deliver spinal cord monitoring.  This area is difficult to recruit to and there have been multiple failed attempts to secure substantive post holders.  There is one Spinal Consultant Surgeon post vacant within staff delivering scoliosis surgeries.   I hope the details provided will address your information request.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 13897/22-27, to detail the value of early payment discount received by domestic ratepayers for the rating years (i) 2021-22; (ii) 2022-23; and (iii) 2023-24.
answer_text: The value of early payment discount received by domestic ratepayers for the rating years (i) 2021-22 was £7,273,629; (ii) 2022-23 was £7,487,283; and (iii) 2023-24 was £8,096,259.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the uptake of the Back in Business rates support scheme since it was reintroduced.
answer_text: In the financial year 2023/24, 99 businesses benefitted from the Back in Business rate support scheme.The Back in Business rate support scheme has been well received since it was reintroduced and opened for applications in May 2024. A further 18 businesses have received a 50% reduction in business rates for up to two years providing almost £41,000 in rates support. I was really pleased to visit Coleraine last month and meet two businesses who have received support from the scheme. One of these businesses occupied a building which had been vacant for 10 years right in the heart of the town. It was really encouraging to hear how the scheme is supporting new businesses while giving premises which had been vacant for a long time a new lease of life.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14073/22-27, for an update on when the road between Hilltown and Kilkeel will be reopened to two lanes following subsidence outside Hilltown.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road Hilltown due to a verge slip caused by the unprecedented weather in November 2023. Unfortunately, the scale and complexity of the work required at Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, together with many competing demands placed on my Department’s limited resources means that it will take time to deliver a safe and cost-effective scheme.  My Department will however, continue to prioritise this project to have the B27 Kilkeel Road, Hilltown opened to two way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 12296/22-27, (i) for his assessment, separate to the specific SPRING Social Prescribing project in 2022, of the use and value of social prescribing across Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) how often social prescribing was used in 2023-24, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting time for a patient to be seen following a red flag breast cancer assessment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any action that is being taken to address regional disparity.
answer_text: The latest Northern Ireland waiting time statistics for cancer waiting times covering the period April - June 2024 were published on 26 September 2024.  The full report and tabular data detailing validated statistics for red flag breast cancer assessment broken down by Health and Social Care Trust can be found on the Department’s website at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-cancer-waiting-times-april-june-2024.  To address the regional disparity, I have commissioned work to establish a regional waiting list in the coming months. This will enable people from across the region to access the earliest appointment available to them. Work is also reviewing the way in which breast cancer services are delivered, with view to ending the long waits which people in some Trust areas are experiencing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 681/22-27, for an update on when the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, will fully reopen following last year's landslide.
answer_text: My Department recognises the inconvenience caused with the closure of one lane on the A2 Shore Road in Rostrevor, due to a landslide which occurred last October following the unprecedented weather event.  Since the landslide occurred, officials have been engaging with DAERA, Forestry Service and NIEA to bring forward an appropriate, safe and sustainable resolution to this issue.  Unfortunately, due to the scale and complexity of the work required, combined with the need to comply with the necessary environmental requirements, it may take some time to implement a permanent solution however it is hoped that an alternative temporary solution can be implemented in the near future that will allow for the road to be reopened to two way traffic until a permanent solution can be developed.   Officials will continue to work to have the road fully reopened as soon as safely possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of students that were unable to take up places in childcare courses in further and higher education in 2024 due to the absence of, or a delay in, obtaining a GP sign off.
answer_text: Northern Regional College has reported that 30 students were unable to take up places in childcare courses in further education in 2024 due to the absence, or delay in, obtaining a GP sign off.		The other five further education colleges and the higher education institutions have reported that no students were unable to take up places in childcare courses for this reason.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that moved from securing Right to Remain status straight to the homeless list requiring housing in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024, published on 13 June 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024.Table 2.1 provides details on households accepted as homeless by reason.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) when Tourism NI annual accounts 2022-23 be published; and (ii) to detail the reason for the delay.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office’s (NIAO) audit of Tourism NI’s 2022-23 Annual Report and Accounts is not complete. The accounts will be published as soon as the audit concludes and NIAO certification obtained.Tourism NI and the NIAO are still working through a number of issues identified during the audit process. My Department continues to work with NIAO to reach a conclusion of the audit at the earliest juncture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her work to strengthen protections against online abuse.
To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the performance targets that have been set for Tourism NI in (a) 2023-24; and (b) 2024-25; and (ii) where these targets are published.
answer_text: Tourism NI’s performance targets are detailed in the annual Operating Plan. The Operating Plan for 2023-24 is published on the Tourism NI website: Strategic Documents (tourismni.com)The 2024-25 Operating Plan is being finalised. It will be published on Tourism NI’s website when it has been approved by my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will answer AQW 13528/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 13528/22-27 was answered on 5 July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the road between Hilltown and Kilkeel will be reopened to two lanes following subsidence outside Hilltown a number of months ago.
answer_text: I recognise the inconvenience that has been caused by the temporary closure of one lane on the B27 Kilkeel Road Hilltown due to a verge slip caused by the severe weather in November 2023. Unfortunately, the scale and complexity of the work required at Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, together with the demands placed on my department’s limited resources because of similar events in the area means that it will take time to deliver a safe and cost-effective scheme. My Department will continue to work hard to have the B27 opened to two way traffic as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review the need for urgent works on the sewerage infrastructure at Annalong Harbour to stop the recurring overspill of raw sewage.
answer_text: The management of public sewerage network is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has completed a Drainage Area study of the sewerage network in Annalong, and developed an option to resolve an Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharge at the Main Street Harbour Storm Overflow, which, when completed, will reduce the likelihood of flooding in the vicinity.   The project is currently at design stage.  Delivery of this project is dependent on planning permission, land acquisition and the availability of  funding. In the interim, NI Water operational staff will continue to maintain the wastewater system in the area, to help ensure that it continues to operate at optimum capacity.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of people that commenced further and higher education courses in childcare in each of the last three academic years.
answer_text: Based on the published data available, the number of students commencing first year in childcare related courses at North of Ireland higher education institutions was 220 for the academic year 2019/20, 220 for 2020/21, and 195 for 2021/22. 		Data is not held for those commencing childcare related courses in Further Education colleges, but rather held for total students enrolled. The number of students enrolled on childcare related courses at Further Education colleges over the same period was 3,095 for the academic year 2019/20, 3,205 for 2020/21 and 3,510 for 2021/22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Women’s Health Strategy will be published.
answer_text: Work to develop a women’s health action plan is currently in a research and data-gathering phase, with stakeholder engagement on an outline plan to follow in due course. The plan will highlight the wide range of policy and service development initiatives that are in progress within my Department and across the Health and Social Care system, as well as any additional initiatives that can be delivered within available resources over the next three years. By focusing on deliverable high priority short-to-medium term actions, alongside ongoing efforts to stabilise the Health and Social Care system during the remainder of this mandate, it is envisaged that the action plan will help improve services and pave the way for a longer-term women’s health strategy in the future, should the Executive decide to prioritise this.  I hope to be able to provide more information on the development of the plan following the summer recess.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the Strategic Framework Ending Violence Against Women and Girls will be published.
answer_text: We remain committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls and associated action plan for consideration by the Executive as soon as possible. We are actively considering a number of options for delivery in consultation with officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 11884/22-27, where the remainder of the £10 million flooding support package, which was not awarded to businesses, will be allocated to.
answer_text: My officials are currently exploring options for utilising any remaining funds to assist those local economies impacted by the flooding in October/November 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the fishing industry’s workforce.
answer_text: I have met with representatives from the fishing industry and I am aware of the problems that they are facing in relation to access to labour, and I am concerned about the challenges associated with the sector’s reliance on foreign crew. I am also aware of the significant impact that some of the changes implemented by the Home Office will have on the industry. I have written to the Secretary of State for the Home Department to outline the issues for fishing and the wider agri-food sector in Northern Ireland and requested a meeting on this.  The Home Secretary did not agree to meet with me.  I have met with the Economy Minister and discussed the matter and a potential approach to be made after the general election on 4 July 2024. My officials have been engaging with the DEFRA and the Migration Advisory Committee on these issues and will continue to do so.Immigration is an excepted matter and a policy area that the UK Government retain exclusive control over. While this is extremely important, I plan to focus on the areas that I do have influence over including the development of policies to increase the resilience of the workforce and make fishing a more attractive career for current and future generations.I will be introducing a funding package for marine and fisheries that will include funding to support the industry in bringing forward initiatives that will incentivise new local entrants to the industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the complex technical format of the consultation document, Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI; and (ii) whether the consultation will receive responses from average householders and businesses most affected from this consultation.
answer_text: The detail of the consultation document is complex and technical in nature. To help stakeholders to respond, the consultation has been designed in such a way that respondents only need to answer the questions they feel are relevant to them or their organisation. We have been encouraging respondents to take this approach through our communications activities and there is further explanation on the DAERA website. My department has also held stakeholder engagement sessions and Q&A information sessions to allow any questions around completing responses to be raised. The Q&As from these sessions have subsequently been published on the DAERA website. 		My Department has already received responses from householders and businesses that may be impacted, and I have no doubt that these stakeholders will provide valuable input.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his assessment of the Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI consultation will take into account previous surveys of householders completed by local councils, including Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council.
answer_text: In my assessment of Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI, I will consider responses to this consultation made between 7th March 2024 and 27th June 2024. I intend to use the best available published research and evidence to inform my decision making, whilst balancing what is right for NI both environmentally and economically.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the proposals in the Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI consultation, whether his Department has estimated (i) the cost of changing the waste system over seven to ten years to implement and operate; (ii) the environmental impact of disposing of over 750,000 plastic bins and containers the new system will replace; and (iii) the cost of proposing a system that does not use robust collection containers, including substantial new triple stack boxes creating plastic wast
answer_text: The costs associated with changing the waste system have been calculated in the regulatory impact assessment which accompanies the consultation. This is available on the DAERA website for review. It outlines the capital costs of transition and the ongoing annualised savings that can be made for each of the options presented within the consultation. There will of course also be the economic and environmental value added to the NI economy by retaining these materials within NI for reprocessing, rather than simply exporting them elsewhere. With all the options outlined my intention would be to implement a system that retained, as far as possible, the current provision, only upgrading or adding new receptacles for new materials. Innovation in collection systems means that new containers have been developed to minimise the number of recycling containers that people use and the footprint that they occupy for households. In the instance that new receptacles should need to be purchased the environmental impact is mitigated by the excellent closed loop recycling system that exists in the UK for wheelie bins. There is no implication within the consultation that new containers will be a triple stack box system. My Department is working closely with stakeholders to research and develop best available techniques for further separation at the kerbside. There are examples of different types of receptacles within the consultation document. The type of receptacles required will be dependent on the consultation outcome.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the use and value of social prescribing across Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) how often social prescribing was used in 2023-24, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: An evaluation of SPRING Social Prescribing Project in 2022 reported that participants benefitted in health and wellbeing improvements such as reduced isolation, increased activity levels, improved confidence, and reduced anxiety and that their participation in the projects also had a positive impact on their family and friends.  The report was limited in that there was little reference to the use of social prescribing across Health and Social Care Trusts and to my knowledge there is currently no routine monitoring of the quantity of Trust social prescribing referrals or their outcomes.  The SPRING evaluation report did however state the difficulty involved in developing metrics for evaluating outcomes.  Other reported benefits were in enabling referral options for GPs which led to reduced visits to GPs especially for issues such as loneliness and isolation and that this reduced GP referrals to secondary care providers for low level mental health issues.  The C&V sector have had an important role in the Primary Care MDT programme to date and where clinically appropriate, the services that the Community and Voluntary sector provide can have a positive impact of the physical mental social wellbeing of the patient population and provide a viable alternative to a prescription or an onward referral.Innovative use of the MDT Social Work Seed Funding has meant closer engagement with the local Community and Voluntary sector and has been at the heart of the roll out of the MDT model. Across the region during 23/24, in excess of 10,000 patients have benefited from nearly 270 targeted community initiatives delivered alongside C&V colleagues with total contacts in excess of 46,000.  These community initiatives were developed in partnership with our local community & voluntary colleagues based on local population need and focussed on Isolation / Loneliness, Wellbeing, Dementia, Bereavement, Frailty / Falls Prevention, Neurodiversity, Menopause, Anxiety, Diabetes, People Seeking International Protection and Poverty.By proactively managing patient need though a multi-disciplinary early intervention approach, we can improve patient outcomes, reduce the need for referrals and appointments elsewhere, and ultimately ease pressure on GP and hospital services.As part of our planning for further roll out the potential to develop a new social prescribing role within the MDT Programme is being considered in relation to the important role MDTs could have in the delivery our Live Better initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of applications for Dial-a-Lift transportation for access to hospital appointments were refused due to the appointment being outside the person’s local area, in each of the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 119/22-27, why details of major roadworks in South Down are still not reported on the TrafficwatchNI website or publicly, including the roadworks at Shore Road, Rostrevor, which have been ongoing for a number of months.
answer_text: As highlighted in my previous reply, due to the large number of road restrictions occurring across the North, only those that are considered to cause significant disruption are shown on the TrafficWatchNI website. A review of the process is currently ongoing to ensure there is a consistent approach being taken, and one that maximises the benefit of the website to the travelling public.  In relation to the temporary lane closure on the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, due to the embankment slip, this was not previously included on the TrafficWatchNI website as it was not considered to cause significant delays. However I have asked my officials to include it on the website as it is on the main A2 road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of people that have had their medical circumstances reviewed for the issuing of a driving license in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) holds information on the number of driving licence renewals issued where a medical condition had been declared but does not hold a central record of the number of licence holders whose medical circumstances have been reviewed.  Whilst the information you requested is held in the driver licensing database, providing the level of detail requested would necessitate a check on each record to determine whether it had been subject to a medical review or investigation.  Extraction of the information to answer your question is therefore both time and cost prohibitive.  The DVA publishes official statistics on a quarterly basis, including information on the number of medical renewal and provisional vocational licences issued, all of which would have been medically assessed.  Please find a link to this information below (DN – next publication is due later this week – link below will need to be updated).https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/system/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-driver-vehicle-operator-and-enforcement-statistics-2023-24-q3-tables-section-5-driver-licensing.ods

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the total annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes to her Department.
answer_text: The total annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes to the Department of Justice including its Agencies and ALB’s in the period 2023/24 was £1,596,682.36.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes to her Department.
answer_text: Procurement spans the whole life cycle of a purchase, from the initial conception and definition of what is needed through to the successful completion of a contract or the end of the useful economic life of a purchased asset. The annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes includes project management; business case preparations and approvals, specification development for the goods, services and construction projects delivered by the Department; pre-tender engagement with suppliers and markets before the tender is advertised; the sourcing and evaluation of tenders; and contract management. The services and processes can be an individual's main role (e.g. Economist, Procurement or Project Advisor, Project Director, Project or Contract Manager or Senior Responsible Owner) or part of an individual's role.It is therefore difficult to identify the precise costs of procuring the goods, services and works required by the Department. It is however possible to detail the costs for the two Centres of Procurement Expertise (CoPEs) within the Department’s Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD). The two CoPEs; Construction Division and Supplies and Services Division; provide procurement services to departments. Construction Division delivers an end to end project advisory service for construction projects using a range of construction professionals (e.g. Architects, Engineers, Building and Quantity Surveyors etc). There are 125 staff in Construction Division. Supplies and Services Division provides procurement advisory services to departments assisting them to develop specifications, carry out pre-market engagement; manage the tendering process and facilitates the evaluation and award stage. Contract management is taken forward by the relevant department/budget holder. Supplies and Services Division also procures and monitors spend on collaborative contracts on behalf of the NICS. There are 43 staff in Supplies and Services Division. The 2023/24 operational costs for Construction Division and Supplies and Services Division were £9.8m. The charges for the procurement shared services are paid by the Clients who commission the services. CPD also funds and manages the eTendersNI system which is used by 146 public bodies. The 2023/24 cost to operate eTendersNI was £149,000

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes to (i) his Department; and (ii) other health bodies managed through the Centre of Procurement Expertise.
answer_text: The Department of Health’s annual cost of procurement for the last financial year (2023/24) was £1.34 million.The annual cost of procurement for other health bodies for the last financial year (2023/24) was £11.27 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes to (i) his Department; and (ii) arm’s-length bodies under his Department’s remit managed through the Centre of Procurement Expertise.
answer_text: The total annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes in my Department and its arm’s length bodies are set out below.  Costs provided cover procurements managed through my Department’s own Centre of Procurement Expertise (the DfI CoPE), the Department of Finance (DoF) CoPE (CPD), the Translink CoPE and the NI Water CoPE.Procurement costs include the cost of dedicated procurement staff, their associated expenses, other procurement related costs (including system costs) and charges levied by DoF.Costs have been based on the 2023-24 financial year. Please note that these figures have not been audited. The audit is underway but is not yet complete.2023-24£’000Department1,837NI Water1,258Translink1,600Waterways Ireland (proportion of costs funded by DfI)13TOTAL4,708

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes to (i) his Department; and (ii) the Education Authority.
answer_text: My Department does not have its own dedicated procurement function. As a business area within the Department of Finance (DoF), CPD (Construction and Property Division) acts as my Department’s Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE), providing procurement and property services to the Department of Education. In its capacity as a CoPE, CPD provides my Department with a range of services designed to ensure procurement processes, tender documentation, evaluations and contract awards comply with Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and the UK Procurement Regulations. Throughout the procurement lifecycle, CPD advises the Department on the exercise of its responsibilities.The annual cost of procurement services, systems and processes charged to the Department of Education for 2023-24 is as follows: Goods and Services£61kConstruction Services£2.1mStrule Shared Education Campus Programme£73kTotal£2.2mThe Education Authority (EA) is the designated Centre of Procurement Expertise for the wider education sector.  This means the EA is directly responsible for the procurement of goods, services and works on behalf of schools.  The 2023-24 annual cost of procurement services in the EA is approximately £3.7m. This includes tendering, contract management, statutory reporting to CPD and ensuring compliance with the Public Contract Regulations 2015, Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy Notes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her work to improve protection against online harm.
answer_text: As you are aware I previously held a roundtable forum to take views on tackling online abuse and intimidation, particularly in light of incidents during the last Assembly elections.  I know you attended both sessions and are aware that telecommunications legislation (including the regulation of social media platforms) is a reserved matter.  I had sought to influence the content of the Online Safety Act 2023, urging more to be done to address the lack of accountability of anonymous account holders which is a key issue when seeking to prevent and detect online harms and offences.  The Act establishes a new regulatory regime to address illegal and harmful content online, imposing legal requirements on providers of internet services.  Services within the scope of the Act will have to follow certain rules, including protecting users from illegal content and activity online, as well as protecting children from harmful content. My Department is also actively working with the online regulator, Ofcom, around the practical outworkings of the Act.  I have also recently met with Ofcom representatives to get an update on the implementation of the Online Safety Act and their plans for it to be put into practice.  Ofcom expects the Act to deliver four outcomes of stronger safety governance in online firms; online services designed and operated with safety in mind; choice for users so they can have meaningful control over their online experiences; and transparency regarding the safety measures services use, and the action Ofcom is taking to improve them.  Ofcom will also give guidance and set out codes of practice on how in-scope companies can comply with their duties.However, a criminal justice response alone cannot improve online safety. We must take a holistic approach that is focussed on prevention and education, as set out in the cross Executive Online Safety Strategy and Action Plan.  The Online Safety Strategy – which is co-ordinated by the Department of Health and involves my Department and the Department of Education amongst others – aims to educate, support and involve a wide range of key stakeholders, including, parents, carers and the wider family as well as schools, colleagues and youth organisations and practitioners working with children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7499/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the programmes receiving funding in South Down.
answer_text: The Department does not hold or collate information by constituency area, however, I can confirm that a resurfacing scheme was completed at Grove Road Ballynahinch, which is in South Down, as a result of the additional in year funding I made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much his Department spent on temporary traffic management systems for road works in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide a cost for the provision of temporary traffic management (TTM) for road works as this information is not recorded in our finance systems. While TTM may be priced under a ‘traffic management’ heading in a tender for larger contracts, it is not separated out when payments are being input to our financial system. For smaller works, such as patching, the cost of TTM is included in the rate for the provision of the repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department's road resurfacing plans for South Down for the 2024-25 financial year will be published.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact of poor quality of road surfaces in relation to disadvantages faced by rural communities in accessing work, school and healthcare.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. While my officials have worked very hard to maintain the road network, they have nonetheless been forced to concentrate only the highest priority road repairs.  As a result, we have all seen a continued deterioration in our infrastructure with large numbers of potholes and other defects developing across the road network, especially after periods of particularly cold or wet weather. I am well aware of the frustrations and, at times, inconvenience this has caused. People and communities rely on the road network to do business, go to work, go to school, and connect to one another.I am keen to invest in our rural road network and now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across various functions. I will be giving careful consideration to the funding for rural roads as part of my deliberations.The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of illegal puppy smuggling detected at Northern Ireland ports in each of the last three years.
answer_text: I can advise that during the period 2021 to date, there have been 164 improvement notices issued by DAERA to individuals transporting dogs through Northern Ireland ports, as part of the Paws for Thought initiative. 		In the same time frame, my Department also revoked one transporter authorisation, issued eight fixed period transporter authorisation suspensions for infringements related to the movement of dogs, secured one formal caution and obtained one conviction in court. 		In addition to action progressed by my Department, the other enforcement bodies working under the Paws for Thought initiative (local councils and the PSNI) have also pursued enforcement action and secured custodial sentences for offences related to dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to progress a windfall tax on energy firms.
answer_text: Taxation is a reserved matter and as such, any assessment of a windfall tax would be a matter for Treasury.However, I am aware that on 1 January 2023, an Electricity Generator Levy was introduced by the British Government which will remain in place until 31 March 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the frequent announcements of closures of hospitality businesses, what plans he has to support the hospitality sector.
answer_text: I understand the importance of the hospitality sector for both our tourism industry and the high street economy. 		Addressing these challenges cuts across several areas of government, including the Department for Communities and local councils.  My Department is taking steps to support such businesses through delivery of the Executive’s Path to Net Zero Energy Strategy, a key objective being to ensure that all consumers, including small businesses, have access to essential and affordable energy.		The level of VAT is an additional issue for the sector.  The Department of Finance has repeatedly raised with the Treasury the negative impact of high levels of VAT on the hospitality sector, and these representations will continue.  		While town centre regeneration is a matter for the Department for Communities, the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce are also relevant as successful hospitality businesses are important features of vibrant town centres.  I therefore intend to work with Executive colleagues to consider the High Street Task Force recommendations to move this agenda forwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to deliver energy efficiency schemes to support working families to achieve better insulated homes.
answer_text: Improving the energy efficiency of our homes is a vital part of addressing fuel poverty and carbon reduction. The Department’s Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS) aims to assist low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with an annual household income of less than £23,000. The Scheme provides a range of energy efficiency measures including loft, cavity wall and solid wall insulation, replacement of inefficient heating systems and replacement windows where appropriate.  My Officials are currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty energy efficiency scheme that will replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends in March 2026. This new scheme will align with the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, Climate Change Act and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy. Energy efficiency and decarbonisation measures will be considered within the design of the new scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he plans to help make going green more affordable for homes in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources in the north of Ireland by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity. Departmental officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects ensuring consumers pay a fair price for electricity produced locally. Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.As you may be aware, the Windsor Framework enables the British government to extend the VAT relief for Energy Saving Materials, previously available in Britian to the north. This means that people here will also be able to apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials such as solar panels.Department officials are preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.My department is also currently preparing a consultation on evidence-based options for a domestic energy efficiency programme in line with Action 7 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2024. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support.  In the interim, the department is working with partners such as the Utility Regulator to assess the options for further scaling of up existing energy efficiency programmes. I have also liaised with the Department for the Communities for an update as it is leading on the Residential Buildings Sector of the Climate Action Plan. Part of this work will consider how future Energy Efficiency Schemes can employ affordable options to decarbonise, including improved insulation and low carbon heating solutions. Work has begun on developing a new Fuel Poverty Strategy which will provide a long-term framework for addressing fuel poverty and its impacts with intensive pre consultation stakeholder engagement currently underway. This engagement includes development of the successor to the current Affordable Warmth Scheme which assists those low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with the costs of energy efficiency measures. The new fuel poverty energy efficiency scheme will align with the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, Climate Change Act and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy.Department for Communities officials are also working on developing the NIHE revitalisation programme and a potential future retrofit funding model for registered Housing Associations, which will aid in the funding of decarbonisation of socia

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has conducted a review into the number of accessible free cash machines.
answer_text: According to the Financial Conduct Authority, as of Q1 2023, 81% (1,417) of ATMs in the North were free-to-use. Although this compares more favourably to 78% in Britain, customers should not have to pay to access their own money. Given my broader concerns around maintaining access to cash locally, I hosted a roundtable meeting with senior representatives from the local banks on 1 May 2024. I pressed the banks on the need to protect the branch network and maintain access to vital banking services in the North, including free-to-use cash machines. Neither my department nor the Assembly has any legislative authority in relation to banking, this sits with the British Treasury and regulatory authorities in London. However, in designated rural areas, my department has made and laid legislation to restore the exemption from rates for ATMs, to encourage and sustain access to cash. This legislation will be debated in the Assembly later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the number of women attending their routine cervical smear test in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust has reduced following the errors in testing being made public.
answer_text: As a large proportion of cervical screening does not occur in direct response to an invitation letter, data on uptake rates for this programme can be misleading. The figure is dependent on the number of women due for screening at a given time, the availability of appointments at GP practices, and the uptake by women, so it is subject to fluctuation.  As such, the number of samples received by the laboratories can provide an indication of comparative activity of women being screened (number of cervical screening samples taken).All Trust areas have seen a reduction in the number of samples taken during April to December 2023. This is an expected pattern for women aged between 25 and 49 years old (who are invited for screening every 3 years), given the pause of the programme in 2020 due to COVID-19. This was followed by an increase in the number of samples taken in all Trusts during January to March 2024.  The PHA will continue to monitor participation in the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment he has completed on how many (i) health outcomes are delivered; and (ii) patients are served by voluntary and community sector organisations who have had the core grant funding removed by his Department.
answer_text: Whilst the Department’s Core Grant Scheme has never directly funded service delivery, funding has been awarded on the basis that the activities of the organisation are in line with DoH strategic and policy objectives and, in particular, the vision for the HSC articulated in Delivering Together. The primary purpose of departmental policy and strategy is to deliver improved health and social wellbeing outcomes for the people of Northern Ireland. It is expected that core-grant recipients will more precisely measure the extent to which they will deliver the outcomes being sought and this is taken into account when processing applications.  The Department will be publishing an Equality Impact Assessment on the 2024/25 budget allocation in the coming weeks. As part of that process, we will be inviting those impacted by reductions in funding to set out how those reductions will impact upon service users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7275/22-27, what work has been carried out in the last two months to progress the issue of baby loss certificates to parents.
answer_text: My Department continues to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England to consider options and cost implications for introducing a similar scheme for Northern Ireland, including the possibility of linking with the English Service once embedded.Following a recent debate in the Assembly on 15 April regarding baby loss certificates and the paediatric pathology service, it has been agreed that my officials will work with their counterparts in Department of Finance’s General Register Office to consider the introduction of a scheme in Northern Ireland. Officials have had an initial meeting, and a further meeting is scheduled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he has taken to rebuild the confidence of women in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust area in relation to the cervical smear process.
answer_text: Primary HPV testing was introduced into the NI Cervical Screening Programme from 11 December 2023, in line with the recommendations of the UK National Screening Committee, the NI Screening Committee and the Cancer Strategy for NI (2022-32).  Nearly all cervical cancers (99.7%) are thought to be linked to persistent infection with high-risk HPV and HPV testing has been shown to be more effective than cytology at identifying those most likely to have cervical cell changes which could develop into cancer.My Department has established the Cervical Screening Oversight and Assurance Group to receive assurances on the NI Cervical Screening Programme, to include maintaining and building on public confidence in the cervical screening programme. The Public Health Agency (PHA)  has assured the Department it will continue to monitor participation in the cervical screening programme and take action as appropriate going forward. In addition, PHA is taking forward measures to highlight the importance of cervical screening to include media engagement and social media campaigns. PHA has asked the Women’s Resource and Development Agency (WRDA), for example, to increase its focus on cervical screening sessions within the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) area,  for 2024/25, promoting informed choice and awareness, via a peer led community-based programme, to target groups where rates of participation in screening is likely to be lower, including those impacted by health inequalities and living in deprived areas. As of 29 April 2024, the Cervical Screening Review in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) has now been completed for 11,470 women, which represents 65% of the total women to be reviewed. While no screening programme is 100% effective, I urge women to continue to come forward for their cervical smear tests when invited. While screening is not a diagnostic test, it aims to detect early changes which could go on to develop into cervical cancer if left untreated, and  evidence shows that cervical screening is still the best way to prevent cervical cancer. Anyone with symptoms should always seek advice from their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he made his officials aware that he did not intend to hold the Ministerial role for the full term of the Assembly.
To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to progress a ban on the sale of energy drinks to people under the age of 16.
answer_text: In the UK, there are no legal age restrictions on the sale of caffeine containing food and beverages, including energy drinks. In 2018, the UK Government consulted on the introduction of a proposed ban on selling energy drinks to children. The policy and the results of the consultation have not been published to date.Food Standards Agency guidance on healthier and more sustainable vending (available online at: https://www.food.gov.uk/business-guidance/healthier-and-more-sustainable-vending) recommends that energy drinks should not be available for sale in vending machines. The Food Standards Agency is working with local government and wider public sector officials to implement the guidance in vending machines in public sector sites that children regularly attend, including leisure centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he will take as a priority to ensure people can access driving tests in their local or nearest test centre.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducts Category B (private car) driving tests at 15 test centres throughout the North.  Their booking system for driving tests is open and available to all eligible customers on a ‘first come, first served’ basis providing everyone with equal access and opportunity to book an appointment.  As the DVA does not operate a priority system, and there has been no policy change, an equality impact assessment is not required.In terms of the Downpatrick test centre specifically, the table below shows that there has been a consistent number of driving tests conducted over the last three years.YearNumber of driving tests conducted2018-192,0262021-222,3022022-232,2902023-242,310The number of driving test conducted at Downpatrick test centre in 2023-24 has increased by 14% on the equivalent pre-Covid period in 2018-19.Over the last three years, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has conducted an average of 65,500 driving tests each year, which is approx. 15% more than the three-year average prior to the pandemic.  This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional driving examiners and the use of overtime to rota off-shift dual role examiners to provide further capacity and cover for leave.  They also offer driving test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test. The DVA has recently recruited a number of new full time driving examiners.  The first two tranches of new examiners were appointed to their test centres in March and April respectively and the next tranche will commence training in May 2024 and, on successful completion of their training, will be allocated to their test centres by mid-end June 2024. The DVA releases Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month. Additional test slots will also be released as resources become available. Due to the demand for services the advice to customers, is to book and pay for the earliest date available and check the booking system regularly for an earlier date as additional appointment slots are added when resources become available and when cancellations are made.Whilst the DVA endeavours to ensure that sufficient tests are available at all test centres, due to factors beyond their control, the availability of driving test appointments at some test centres may, at times, be impacted.  The DVA appreciates that learner drivers are keen to pass their test at the earliest opportunity and while there remains a high demand for driving tests, candidates should consider all the options available to them to secure an early appointment, including choosing an alternative location to take their test.The DVA will continue to work hard to increase their capacity to address the high demand for driver testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has engaged with the childcare sector to identify (i) industry pressures; (ii) training needs; and (iii) economic needs to sustain them.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is leading on the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  My department is committed to working with the Minister for Education and his officials as they take this important work forward.
