question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why there are little or no driving tests currently available to book on any given day.
answer_text: Over the last three years, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has conducted an average of 65,500 driving tests each year, which is approx. 15% more than the three-year average prior to the pandemic.  This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional driving examiners, use of overtime to rota off-shift dual role examiners to provide further capacity and cover for leave, they also offer driving test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test. The DVA has recently recruited a number of new full time driving examiners.  The first tranche of new examiners were appointed to their test centres in March and the second tranche are nearing the completion of their training and will be appointed to their test centres by the end of April.  The next tranche will commence training in May 2024 and, on successful completion of their training, will be allocated to their test centres by mid-end June 2024. The DVA releases Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month.  Additional test slots will also be released as resources become available.  The number of available of appointments constantly changes and as of 19 April there were over 1,100 driving test appointments available to book online.  Due to the demand for services the advice to customers, is to book and pay for the earliest date available and check the booking system regularly for an earlier date as additional appointment slots are added when resources become available and when cancellations are made.The DVA appreciates that learner drivers are keen to pass their test at the earliest opportunity and while there remains a high demand for driving tests, candidates should consider all the options available to them to secure an early appointment, including choosing an alternative location to take their test.The DVA will continue to work hard to increase their capacity to address the high demand for driver testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale on the full reopening of the two lanes on the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, following a partial closure as a result of a landslide at the end of October 2023.
answer_text: Last October was the wettest October on record and we are beginning to see the impacts of climate change with increased flooding. You can be assured that addressing this and reducing the impacts of heavy rainfall, such as flooding, is a priority for my Department. Since the landslide occurred, following the period of significant rainfall at the end of October and start of November 2023, the Department has been engaging with the landowner and other relevant bodies to bring forward an appropriate, safe and sustainable resolution to this issue. Unfortunately, the scale and complexity of the work required, combined with the need to comply with the necessary environmental requirements, will mean that it may take some time to implement a permanent solution. My Department recognises the inconvenience caused as a result of the landslide and worked very quickly to reopen the road to one-way traffic to minimise this. You can be assured that my officials will continue to work with those responsible for the adjacent land and other stakeholders to have the road fully reopened as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered the introduction of toll charges on any roads in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made. However, I currently have no plans to introduce toll charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has conducted an appraisal of the costs and savings to the Executive realised by the move from 26 local councils to 11.
answer_text: Post-project appraisals are the responsibility of the department undertaking the project. The move from 26 to eleven councils took place in 2015 under the management of the previous Department for the Environment prior to the reorganisation of government departments in 2016.Local Government is currently the responsibility of the Department for Communities albeit that the amalgamation of councils took place before the Department was formed. My understanding is that a draft report examining the impact of local government reform on service delivery and cost effectiveness in Northern Ireland is currently being finalised by the Department for Communities following final input from the Council Working Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much the partial lane closure on the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, has cost his Department from October 2023, broken down to include and isolate the cost of (i) remedial works; (ii) engineer work; (iii) installation of concrete safety blocks; and (iv) temporary traffic lights.
answer_text: The information requested is commercially sensitive and disclosure may allow the public, in particular economic competitors, to identify commercial rates/costs for carrying out these activities which may damage the Department’s and contractor’s legitimate economic interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has conducted an appraisal of the costs and savings to the Executive realised by the move from Education and Library Boards to the establishment of the Education Authority NI and Libraries NI.
answer_text: The appraisal and evaluation of the establishment of arm’s length bodies, such as the Education Authority (EA) and Libraries NI are the responsibility of the relevant sponsor department.  The EA is an arm’s length body of the Department of Education, and Libraries NI is an arm’s length body of the Department for Communities.In 2021, the Department of Education commissioned Baker Tilly Mooney Moore to carry out a Landscape Review of the EA, which was published in 2022. This review provides a comprehensive review of the EA’s overall operation and governance, and identified 13 recommendations, including the need for a review of the EA’s budget and affordability, with the opportunity to right size the organisation with a refreshed budget.It is for the Education Minister to progress actions against these recommendations and the budget management of the EA, you may wish to contact Minister Givan in relation to this. Regarding Libraries NI, the Department for Communities has responsibility for the completion of an evaluation. You may therefore wish to contact the Communities Minister in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in order to help fill temporary vacancies in daycare or crèche facilities rather than having to close, whether he will consider the introduction of a database for registered childminders who are AccessNI cleared, similar to that for substitute teachers.
answer_text: There is currently no registering body available to childcare staff in Northern Ireland which could carry out AccessNI checks on behalf of childcare providers. Each check is specific to one setting or employer at a single point in time and cannot be transferred between employers even if recently completed. The Minister for Justice has advised that it remains a key objective of AccessNI to deliver a portable disclosure solution, and that engagement with the Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS) is continuing with a view to AccessNI joining a new and modernised DBS Update Service solution. If delivered, a portable disclosure solution would remove the need to obtain a new Enhanced Disclosure Certificate each time an individual applies for work with children or vulnerable adults and would provide the outcome being sought by way of the introduction of a database.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his long-term plans for the management of Kilkeel Medical Centre; and (ii) how long the Southern Health and Social Care Trust will continue to manage the practice on a temporary basis.
answer_text: I can provide the following information in answer to your question:We are currently working with key stakeholders to put plans in place for the long term management of Kilkeel Medical Practice. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust are continuing to manage Kilkeel Medical Practice until 30th September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of cyber attacks on her Department's IT systems in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The cyber threat for government organisations remains high and the Department of Finance has a range of cyber security technical controls and measures in place to prevent cyber criminals from gaining access to or disrupting the Civil Service shared IT infrastructure and the IT systems hosted within it.The specific information you have requested, if available in the public domain, could be utilised by an attacker to build up a picture of the cyber resilience of the NICS and as such it is best not to publicly disclose this information.I can however offer you the opportunity to meet with senior staff from my Department's Digital, Security and Financial Shared Services team who can provide you with information on the cyber threat facing the NICS, an overview of the mitigations in place to prevent cyber attacks as well as detail on the nature of the attacks my Department is witnessing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff vacancies in the Northern Ireland Civil Service, broken down by (i) grade; and (ii) pay band.
answer_text: (i)	Details of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As at the end of March 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 3147 vacancies across the Civil Service. The breakdown by grade is shown in Table 1 below:Table 1: Vacancies in NICS by Analogous GradeNICS Analogous GradeNumber of vacanciesG22G35G514G628G7106DP217SO293EO1341EO2650AO1134AA177Ind 245Ind 135Uniformed 100Grand Total3147Source: NICSHR(ii)	Vacancies are not categorised by pay band; however, each grade has its own pay scale and the vacancies can be broken down by pay scale as detailed in Table 2 below:Table 2 Vacancies in NICS by Pay ScaleGradeNumber of vacanciesGrade 22Grade 35Grade 514Senior Civil Service Total21Grade 6 and analogous28Grade 7 and analogous106Deputy Principal and analogous 213Agricultural Inspector Grade II 4Deputy Principal Total217Staff Officer and analogous 234Agricultural Inspector Grade III 3Area Inspector3Auditor Staff Officer (CIA or Equivalent)1Forest Officer II3Staff Officer (Accountant)49Staff Officer Total293Executive Officer 1 and analogous 322Forest Officer Grade III5Graduate Trainee Civil/Electrical Engineer8Trading Standards Inspector6Executive Officer 1 Total341Executive Officer 2 and analogous Total650Administrative Officer and analogous Total1134Administrative Assistant and analogous Total177Industrial 2 Total45Industrial 1 Total35NIPS Uniformed GradesCustody Prison Officer30Custody Prison Officer (Trades Department)2Night Custody Officer30Prisoner Custody Officer (30 Hour) Escort24Prisoner Custody Officer (40 Hour) Driver/Escort13Prisoner Escorting & Court Custody Services Manager1Uniformed Grades Total100Grand Total3147The pay scales can be found in the following links:SCS pay scalesNon-industrial pay scalesIndustrial pay scalesThe equivalent pay scales for the Uniformed grades are shown in Table 3 below:Table 3: Uniformed Grade Pay ScalesUniformed Grade2023 Pay ScaleCustody Prison Officer£29,773 - £39,273Custody Prison Officer (Trades Department)Night Custody Officer£27,809 - £31,295Prisoner Custody Officer – (30 hour) Escort£18,961 - £21,338Prisoner Custody Officer – (40 hour) Driver / Escort£25,281 – £28,450Prisoner Escorting & Court Custody Services Manager£36,286 - £39,646

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many; and (ii) which waiting lists he has taken steps to reduce since February 2024.
answer_text: Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  It is, however, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.  I published The Elective Care Framework in 2021, which sets out a detailed plan for addressing hospital waiting lists across a range of adult and paediatric elective specialities. Much good work is already underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and mega clinics.  In addition, my Department is working with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of best practice service improvements.  My Department commissioned Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) independent external reviews of the regional orthopaedic, urology and gynaecology services in Northern Ireland to identify areas of improvement and reduce backlogs in these specialities. Work is well underway to take forward implementation of the recommendations. In addition, my Department has commissioned a similar review of the children’s orthopaedic service to assist with prioritisation of demands within the service.This month I made the decision to transfer vasectomy services from secondary care to primary care. This decision is firmly in line with the important principle of providing the right care in the right place and at the right time and will help to tackle lengthy waits.Whilst waiting lists for inpatient and day case admission are still at unacceptable levels there has been a sustained reduction in the number of patients waiting.   This is the sixth quarter in a row where the treatment waiting lists have reduced. This is the longest sustained reduction since at least 2008.  At December 2023, inpatient or day case treatment waiting lists have seen promising improvements. Whilst still at unacceptable levels, there has been an overall 12.7% (14,259) decrease in numbers waiting since the same month the previous year.At December 2023, when compared to December 2022 figures, there have been the following reductions in patients waiting for Inpatient/Daycase surgery:General Surgery – a reduction of 20.8%Trauma and Orthopaedic surgery – a reduction of 7.6%ENT - a reduction of 15.9%Gynaecology - a reduction of 21.6%Urology - a reduction of 9.6%The Framework is, however, clear that, in addition to the multi-year funding needed to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, sustained recurrent investment is required to address the central issue of demand outstripping the current capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many new build properties have appealed their completion notices, broken down by appeals that were (i) successful; and (ii) unsuccessful, in the last five years.
answer_text: A total of 327 completion notice appeals have been received in respect of new build properties within the last five years i.e. from 1 March 2019 to the present day.  Of these 327 appeals, a total of 24 were successful. A total of 285 were unsuccessful and the remaining 18 appeals were withdrawn prior to being considered.  A full breakdown is provided in the table below. Property TypeTotal Number of AppealsAppeal SuccessfulAppeal UnsuccessfulAppeal WithdrawnDomestic3131727818Non-domestic14770

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the estimated value of rates collected on vacant properties, including empty homes, per year.
answer_text: The estimated value of rates receipts on properties that were recorded as vacant on the LPS rating system during 2022/23 amounted to approximately £16 million for Domestic properties and £14.1 million for Non-Domestic properties.The data for the rating year 2023/24 is not available until after 31 March.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is engaging with the Education Authority to provide a joined up approach to delivering a wide range of sports in school settings.
answer_text: The provision of sports in the school setting is a responsibility for the Department of Education, as physical education is a compulsory part of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. However, I can confirm that officials from my Department currently engage with a range of stakeholders including the Department of Education, the Education Authority, the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools, the Department for Agriculture, the Environment and Rural Affairs, local councils and Sport NI (my Department’s arm’s length body) through the Your School Your Club initiative. The purpose of this initiative is to help promote community use of school sports facilities outside of normal school hours. Investment is disbursed to revitalise existing sports facilities or to develop new/additional provision on school sites whilst ensuring access and use for the local community users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5907/22-27 and AQW 7144/22-27, when he plans to approach the Minister for Infrastructure on this matter and commence actions to reduce the administrative burden on GP practices.
answer_text: As outlined in my response to AQW 5907/22-27, in recognition of the  workload challenges facing General Practice and the demand for GP services, my Department is taking forward a range of work to support the sustainability of General Practice. This has included engagement with relevant Northern Ireland Civil Service Departments and public bodies to explore ways to reduce the administrative burden on GP practices of completing non-statutory administrative tasks and the unnecessary directing of patients to GPs on the part of other Government Departments and public bodies, to help to reduce the impact of these on General Practice.  This work has been informed by GP representatives.  A number of issues that were identified have been considered and as a result processes have been reviewed and/or clarified as appropriate. Policy responsibility in relation to driving licencing and the processes that underpin this rests with the Department for Infrastructure.  Officials in my Department will continue to explore how the administrative burden on GPs might be reduced and as part of this will engage with the Department for Infrastructure as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have completed any analysis of regional disparities in public service delivery.
answer_text: We are committed to delivering real change and prosperity for all our communities. That means tackling barriers to inclusion and prosperity and addressing the crisis in public services, wherever people live and work, to ensure that all our communities thrive, regardless of where they are. The Executive Office has mapped public services delivered through the Good Relations programme. In addition, our department has developed a Wellbeing Dashboard which includes mapping of a number of wellbeing indicators at Local Government District and Assembly Area level.While we are currently in discussion about what our Executive’s priorities will be, ensuring that all areas are supported to achieve economic growth and prosperity is a key priority. We will continue to investigate and adopt new techniques to help us make this a reality.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what assurance she can provide that the existing £53 million UK Shared prosperity funds provided for vital economic inactivity programmes will be protected and a plan put in place to support the projects in the long-term.
answer_text: Economic Inactivity programmes have been funded and delivered directly by the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) through the Shared Prosperity Fund. The funding for these will continue as planned until March 2025. DLUHC has not yet set out its plans or intentions for this funding after March 2025. Clearly it is vital that funding for these important interventions continues, and my officials are pressing their counterparts in DLUHC for substantive engagement and clarity on this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the damage and deterioration from road diversions put in place in November 2023, when road remediation works will commence on (i) Aughnaloopy Road; (ii) Aughnahoory Road; and (iii) Ballinran Road, Kilkeel.
answer_text: During the emergency works carried out on A2 Newcastle Road, Kilkeel during November 2023, I can advise that the Aughnaloopy Road, Aughnahoory Road and Ballinran Road were not part of the official signed diversion route.  Unfortunately, it is a common occurrence, that traffic will often use other local roads instead of the official diversion of their own volition, and while the Department discourages this practice, cannot prevent traffic from using these other routes.  There are currently no plans to undertake remedial works on these routes beyond what is required to meet my Department’s current limited service maintenance policy. However, these roads will be considered for inclusion in future programmes of work subject to funding and prioritisation against other competing schemes. In the meantime, my Department will continue to inspect these roads and prioritise any defects meeting current intervention levels for repair in accordance with the current maintenance standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether there have been any expressions of interest in taking on the GP contract at Rathfriland Health Centre; and (ii) for his assessment of whether it will fall under Health and Social Care Trust control and management following August 2024.
answer_text: On 28 February 2024, Drs Wade and Toner Practice notified the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH) that they were withdrawing from their contract to deliver General Medical Services.  Following completion of the notice period, their contract will end on 31 August 2024. The SPPG has commenced the process to secure alternative arrangements for these services.  The preferred option is to secure a GP contractor or grouping of GPs to take over the practice through a formal recruitment process.  This is in the early stages and the outcome is unknown at present.  Drs Wade and Toner Practice will continue to deliver GP services and patients do not need to take any action.  They should continue to contact their practice as normal.   SPPG has commenced the process of informing patients and they should be receiving an information letter week commencing 11 March 2024.  To ensure stability for all GP practices in the area, the SPPG has temporarily suspended the ability of the patients of Drs Wade and Toner Practice to change GP practices.  This is only a temporary move until a new contractor is appointed and services are stabilised.  Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts, the Department has been able to ensure that no practice has closed and that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.   Where no applicants have been received for these contracts, the Trusts and GP Federations have supported the Department in taking on contracts ensuring patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will provide business support to the childcare sector.
To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on repair work to the roof of Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Health what plans are in place to procure or introduce a specialist therapeutic service for victims of sexual violence and abuse after 31 March 2024.
answer_text: On 4 March 2024 in my statement to the Assembly, I advised that funding for this specialist counselling service for victims of sexual abuse would be extended for a further 12 months.I advised that the one-year extension would allow proper consideration and a decision on and implementation of the best way forward for counselling of this nature.  The planned ending of this contract was not a cost saving measure.  The intention has always been to use the funding to continue to support people who are victims and survivors of sexual abuse in line with the draft domestic and sexual violence strategy 2023-2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of (i) recurrent funding; and (ii) capital grant funding provided by his Department to non-statutory bodies in the museum and library sectors in each year between 2015 and 2024.
answer_text: Resource and capital funding provided by my Department to non-statutory bodies in the museums and libraries sectors is as tabled below.YearResource FundingCapital Funding2015/16£476,618£251,6472016/17£434,117£81,0732017/18£411,000£20,0002018/19£376,000£37,5002019/20£478,000Nil2020/21£406,000£223,5502021/22£416,000Nil2022/23£421,675£5,0002023/24 (as of 6/03/24)£283,750Nil

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he is considering the provision of business support to childcare providers in the short-term, given the current financial pressures the sector is experiencing.
answer_text: The Early Years and Childcare Strategy is being developed by the Department of Education and is an Executive priority.  I recognise the need for high quality affordable childcare to enable parents to work, and I am committed to working with the Department of Education as they develop and implement this strategy to deliver a childcare solution which align with my economic goals of good jobs, increased productivity and regional balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline when he will publish the outcome of the consultation on the Voluntary and Community Sector Infrastructure Support Policy Framework.
answer_text: The public consultation on proposals to support future investment in the infrastructure of the Voluntary and Community Sector, ended on 9th February 2024. Development of the consultation proposals was participative and inclusive, with my officials standing up a formal Policy Reference Group, made up of a wide range of sectoral interests, to act as a sounding board at all stages of the process.This was supplemented by a series of targeted engagement events at pre consultation stages and again during the formal consultation period; and I am pleased that organisations have taken the time to participate and share their views.  We now have almost 200 formal written responses to the proposals.My officials are now analysing the responses to inform final policy proposals, which will be discussed with me in the coming weeks.I anticipate that I will publish a departmental response to the consultation before the summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her plans to improve the justice system's approach to address anti-social behaviour in rural areas.

To ask the Minister of Finance for each of the last three years, please detail (i) the number of properties who have not paid their rates; and (ii) the value of unpaid rates, broken down by (a) council area; and (b) domestic or non-domestic.
answer_text: ANSWERLand & Property Services produces annual statistics showing the amount of Rating Debt and the count of occupancies with Rating Debt at the end of each rating year, by District Council and Sector (Domestic/Non-Domestic).https://admin.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/annual-end-of-year-rating-debt-by-district-council-and-sector

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to conduct an urgent review into the management of capital projects following the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office Major Capital Projects report.
answer_text: Officials are working on a Terms of Reference for the review of Public Procurement which will address some of the issues identified in the Audit Office Major Capital Projects report.  I understand that Strategic Investment Board (SIB) has undertaken a review of the root causes for delays and cost overruns in the projects highlighted in the NIAO’s 2019 Report on Major Capital Projects. I look forward to the outcome of this review.I also understand the Head of the Civil Service has established an Infrastructure Sub Group to identify the work needed to improve the delivery of major capital projects and programmes here and build on the findings from SIB’s review of the root causes for project delays and cost overruns. Officials from my Department are represented on this Sub Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5908/22-27, how his position requiring GP sign-off for childcare workers aligns with his response to AQW 5907/22-27, in which he outlined his work to reduce administrative burden on GP practices.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to undertake a consultation exercise in relation to the requirement for GP-countersigned health assessments on individuals seeking to work in childcare and on current policy relating to staff:child ratios within childcare.  This will be the first stage of a wider review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care, which is due to commence in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 53/22-27, (i) whether he has any plans to review the process for childcare students in Further Education who must receive GP sign-off and a medical assessment before commencing studies and placements; and (ii) whether he will commit to removing this administrative process from GPs.
answer_text: The first stage of the review of the Minimum Standards, a public consultation on adult-child ratios and the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration countersigned by their GP, was launched on Monday 24 March 2025.Following the close of the consultation on 20 June 2025, all responses will be collated for review by the Department, and a consultation report will be produced.  If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these changes will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to change the car and trailer towing test and licensing requirements to bring it into line with the rest of the UK.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500kg Maximum Authorized Mass (MAM).  This change was made in an effort to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales.At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed.In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the additional £8.1 million for road resurfacing, (i) to detail the areas which will benefit from this funding, broken down by constituency; and (ii) for a breakdown of how the funding will be allocated in South Down.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to introduce baby loss certificates for parents in Northern Ireland who experienced a loss in pregnancy before 24 weeks, as these are now available in England.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that pregnancy loss at any stage can have on parents and welcome the recent introduction of pre-24-week pregnancy loss certificates in England, as recommended by the Independent Pregnancy Loss Review commissioned by the Westminster Government. My Department will continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the new system is embedded there, with a view to understanding whether it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland, including any associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why the TrafficWatchNI website does not display roadworks for the South Down area.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to make a decision on changes to rates relief measures before the 2024-25 rates bills are issued, following her Department’s recent consultations.
answer_text: It was noted in the consultation processes that the revenue raising measures would require further impact assessment and consultation on options appraisal before they could be implemented. It would not be possible for this work to be completed by my Department ahead of any bills that issue in April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5907/22-27, whether he is reviewing the requirements for GPs to medically sign-off on driving licenses as a matter of urgency, in light of the administrative challenges facing the General Medical Services.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure part of my responsibility is promoting and improving road safety. I take this responsibility very seriously and believe that it is essential that the driving licence application process, including any required medical assessment, provides unequivocal assurance of an applicant’s fitness to drive. DVA have a process in place to assess each driving licence application/renewal to ensure that driving licences are issued only to those drivers who meet the relevant standards in relation to medical fitness to drive. For some applicants this will require them to provide DVA with additional medical information from their GP in the form of a completed medical questionnaire or report.Current legislation only permits a registered medical practitioner to provide information to DVA to allow them to assess an individual’s fitness to drive. A medical practitioner is a person who is qualified as a doctor in accordance with the Medical Act 1983. To date, we have not received any requests from the Department of Health to review the current requirements for GPs to medically sign-off on driving licences. My officials will, however, consider this position should the need arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how long it takes, on average, for a patient's medical information to be passed to their own health trust following a visit to an Emergency Department in another health trust.
answer_text: Patients attending an Emergency Department will have their episode of care details automatically uploaded to the Northern Ireland Electronic Care Record (NIECR) This includes: Date of Episode, Route of attendance, assessing professional, Date of Discharge Home, Main Diagnosis /Referral Reason on discharge / referral for admission.This can be viewed in summary form, via NIECR by clinicians providing care in any Trust in NI.  The new system, Encompass, is live in the South Eastern HSCT.  More detailed ED episode information is automatically and instantly available across all SEHSCT sites when it is recorded.  This information is also available to healthcare staff working outside SEHSCT via EPIC Care Link portal, but staff need reason to check it for information as an alert is not recorded in NIECR. The rest of the Emergency Departments will Go Live with Encompass with their Trusts over the next 12-18 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much extra it costs the Department per household to process rates payment by monthly payments rather than paying in full up front, as referenced in the consultation paper as a reason for the discount in place.
answer_text: The consultation document referred to the intent of the policy when the discount was first introduced, ie in the past tense, not the current policy position in 2023/24. The Department’s preferred method of payment is by Direct Debit.Information on the cost of collection for various payment methods is not held within the Department at a household level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of registered childcare providers in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The total number of childcare providers registered in Northern Ireland was 3859 in 2021, 3648 in 2022 and 3514 in 2023.I am aware of the challenges currently being experienced within the childcare sector, and the review of the Minimum Standards that my Department will be taking forward in the coming months is intended to make improvements for childcare providers and families where possible, within the context of ensuring that childcare remains a safe, high quality experience for children. I will also continue to engage with my Executive colleagues on the development of a new Early Learning and Childcare Strategy aimed at further supporting child development and enabling parental employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when he plans to introduce primary legislation on coastal change risk management; and (ii) , in the interim, what steps are being taken to address the issue of collapsing cliffs (a) across Northern Ireland; and (b) in Kilkeel.
answer_text: Coastal Change Risk Management is not the responsibility of any department in Northern Ireland.  The legislative gap was recognised by previous Ministers and I will be discussing this issue with the Infrastructure Minister and Executive colleagues.Under the existing policy and legislative framework, my Department has no role in addressing the issue of collapsing cliffs in Kilkeel or elsewhere.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has plans to invest in staff training and qualifications for the childcare sector.
answer_text: I recognise the rising cost of childcare and the impact this has on families who are already struggling with the cost of living. I also recognise that the high cost of childcare prevents many parents, particularly mothers, from working.	My Department currently provides a suite of pathways for childcare training and qualifications through Further and Higher Education, and through vocational qualifications like apprenticeships and traineeships. I have asked my officials to carry out an audit of what we have to offer in this area. This audit will help us determine any gaps in provision or if there is a need for additional financial assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline plans to increase funded special school places in the South Down area for the upcoming academic year.
answer_text: The demand for specialist education provision has increased significantly in recent years.  The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed the demand was 37% higher in 2023/24 than for 2022/23 and it currently estimates that over 1,000 additional places will be required across Northern Ireland for September 2024, with South-Down identified as an area of particular pressure. In line with their current planning assumptions, the EA is proposing to create approximately 15-25 additional special school places and 30-40 additional specialist provisions in mainstream places in the South Down area for September 2024.I recognise that specials schools are operating at full, and in most cases over-capacity, and that brings with it challenges with school accommodation.  In an effort to address medium- and longer-term issues in the South Down area, my department has approved a land purchase adjacent to Rathore School.  This will enable the current school site to be extended which will facilitate the provision of additional accommodation to meet the increased demand for places at the school. My officials are also due to meet with the Principal and Board of Governors (BoG) of Knockevin Special School (SS) (which is currently operating across three sites) to have initial discussions about the possibility of repurposing the Down High School (HS) site when that school decants to its new premises in 2025.Any expansion or move to new accommodation will require a level of capital investment.The EA is working at pace for September 2024 to confirm the need for specialist education provision to meet the learning profile of pupils and seek solutions to create additional capacity in the system.  All schools have been written to and schools in the South Down area will be contacted over the next few weeks to explore the possibility of establishing additional specialist provision classes to meet the demand for September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department plans to review Article 42, Paragraphs 2A & 2B of the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977 for potential reform.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department has been consulting on Non-domestic and Domestic rating measures including the exemption for student halls of residence managed and operated by universities (Article 42, Paragraphs 2A & 2B). The consultation closed on 13 February and I will be assessing the findings of the consultation, alongside a number of other strategic areas that I wish to consider within the rating system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the review of Minimum Standards for Childcare will take place.
answer_text: It is intended that the review will commence in April 2024.  Key stakeholders, including parents and childcare providers, will all have an opportunity to participate in the review process.  Officials will also be liaising with colleagues in other UK jurisdictions in relation to their respective registration and inspection arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to protect and safeguard the fishing industry, following the Home Office's announcement of an increase to the minimum salary required for people arriving on the Skilled Worker visa.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why childcare sector workers are required to undertake a medical assessment and obtain a GP sign-off before commencing work or training; and (ii) whether he plans to review this.
answer_text: I am aware that this issue has been raised with my officials by group setting childcare providers as a potential barrier to recruitment. In response, the Department sought advice from HSC Trust Directors of Children’s Services. The considered view is that having a GP countersign a prospective childcare provider’s health declaration is a necessary safeguarding measure, particularly in connection with home-based childcare. A review of the Department’s Minimum Standards for childcare provision is due to commence in April and the need for countersigned health declarations will be reconsidered as part of that process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is an ongoing or planned review of the process of essential GP medical sign-off of driving licenses and educational forms and the associated processing times.
answer_text: I recognise the increasing workload challenges facing General Practice and the demand for GP services.  The issue of sustainability of General Medical Services is a key priority for my Department.    My Department has been engaging with GP representatives to progress a number of actions in the short and medium term to help address some of the workload challenges identified.  This includes work my Department has been taking forward with relevant Northern Ireland Civil Service Departments and public bodies to explore ways to reduce the administrative burden on GP practices of completing non-statutory administrative tasks and the unnecessary directing of patients to GPs on the part of other Government Departments and public bodies, to help to reduce the impact of these on General Practice.  In terms of the processes associated with essential GP medical sign-off of driving licenses and educational forms, responsibility for these lies with the relevant Government Department/Agency or public body.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of properties exempt from paying rates due to being designated as for the housing of students; and (ii) the value of the rates deemed exempt for this reason for the 23/24 financial year.
answer_text: A total of 33 properties were exempt (or partially exempt) from paying rates in the 2023/24 rating year, as they benefit from a Non-Domestic Exemption due to being designated as eligible under Article 42 of the Rates (NI) Order 1977 and occupied for the housing of students. The total value of the exemption is £2,036,403.My Department has just concluded a consultation on Non-domestic and Domestic rating measures including the exemption for student halls of residence. I will be assessing the findings of the consultation, alongside a number of other strategic areas that I wish to consider within the rating system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the level of reported hate crimes across Northern Ireland, over the last three years, broken down by policing district.
answer_text: The current legislation, which allows me to remain as Minister, will expire on 28 October 2022 due to the continued absence of a functioning Executive. Unfortunately, my officials will be unable to provide the information sought by the Member before this date to enable me to respond to this AQW.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 2154/22-27, for an update on progress to develop measures to monitor the level of backlog on MOT waiting times.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps are being taken to improve equal access for Northern Ireland students to third level student finance loans in England.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the Executive's £961 million projected overspend in 2022-23 financial year, (i) to detail the overspend by each Department; and (ii) what action he has taken to address this projection in terms of advised efficiency targets or spending cuts.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 4174/22-27, why the Southern Trust has the longest waiting list for Speech and Language Therapy in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why this year's roads resurfacing programme for South Down has not yet been finalised and published; (ii) when the programme will be published; and (iii) whether the resurfacing programmes for each other regional area are at the same stage.
answer_text: With the continued DUP boycott of the Executive, budgets have not been agreed for the 2022-23 financial year. However, to ensure that capital spending is maximised to deliver much needed investment in our infrastructure, I have agreed capital allocations for my Department which includes funding for resurfacing programmes for all Council areas which, in turn, has allowed the finalisation of my Department’s Annual Council Reports. Details of the resurfacing programme for the South Down area will be included in my Department’s 2022-23 Annual Report for Newry, Mourne and Down District Council which is due to be presented on 24 October 2022.  The Report will be published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library shortly after the Council meeting. A web-link to the published Report will also be issued to all MLAs in the respective Council area. My Department’s Reports to the respective Councils are either at the latter stages of finalisation or have been recently presented.  Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the planning decision on Kilkeel's Mourne Park House development, which has been called in for determination.
answer_text: The planning applications for Mourne Park House remain under consideration by my officials and will be progressed to a decision point as soon as possible. When the planning assessment is completed, a recommendation will be made to me for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1283/22-27, to confirm whether the funding which has been unspent, due to the absence of a resurfacing contract for the Newry, Mourne and Down area, has been ring-fenced and carried forward to be spent on resurfacing in the area when a contract is awarded.
answer_text: In line with public expenditure requirements, Departments cannot carry forward unspent budget into a new financial year. In ensuring that my Department’s capital budget was efficiently and effectively managed, these funds were redirected to other priority areas within my Department. In 2021-22, my Department spent 99.8% of its capital allocation of £754.7m.I can confirm, however, that two separate resurfacing contracts have recently been awarded within the Newry, Mourne and Down Council area, with resurfacing works expected to commence by August. The majority of schemes that have been on hold since the previous contract expired will be progressed as soon as possible and within the limits of internal and external capacity.Details of works programmes for the 2022-23 financial year will be published within the Department’s Council Report for Newry, Mourne and Down Council area which is now being finalised. In due course the Report will be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1542/22-27, to detail (i) how his Department is monitoring the number of people who are unable to get an MOT appointment on time and currently driving without a valid MOT certificate; (ii) any evidence that a decrease in the backlog level is being achieved; and (iii) whether there are operational targets to reduce the backlog.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) booking system only records details of actual vehicle tests booked and conducted and does not hold information on vehicles that have not made an application for a test.  Therefore, it is not possible for DVA to monitor and collate information about vehicles that are unable to get an MOT appointment on time or are currently driving without a valid MOT certificate. The DVA closely monitors the number of vehicle test applications received and tests conducted and expects waiting times to reduce over time. The actions being taken to reduce vehicle test waiting times are set out in AQW 71/22-27 and referenced in AQW 1542/22-27. The DVA expects that these actions will, over time, increase vehicle testing capacity across the network and reduce waiting times which are key operational targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 1543/22-27, to detail the total number of Civil service and public sector employees who were employed on furlough by Northern Ireland public sector bodies, broken down by Department and Local Authority in (i) 2020-21; and (ii) 2021-22.
answer_text: No civil servants were furloughed in their capacity as civil servants during 2020-21 and 2021-22 under the Coronavirus Job Retention Scheme which was administered directly by Revenue and Customs.My Department does not hold information on public sector employees on furlough in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total value of furlough payments received from the UK government by Northern Ireland public sector bodies in (i) 2020-21; and (ii) 2021-22, broken down by Department and Local Authority.
answer_text: The Coronavirus Job Retention Scheme was administered directly by Revenue and Customs and therefore my department does not hold information on payments received by public sector bodies in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 71/22-27, to detail (i) how the backlog in MOT testing is being monitored; and (ii) how the effectiveness of additional efforts to provide timely MOT testing slots are being measured.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is closely monitoring the number of vehicle test applications received and tests conducted and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions it has taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network, as set out in my response to AQW 71/22-27.As of 21 June 2022, the DVA has confirmed that just over 70,000 vehicle test appointments were available across its network of centres up to 31 October 2022 and more appointments are added daily at all test centres as resources are confirmed.In May 2022, the DVA conducted 84,103 full vehicle tests, which is in line with pre-pandemic levels of vehicle testing for the month of May. The DVA will continue to work hard to reduce vehicle testing waiting times as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on work to resurface and repair the road at Greencastle Street, Kilkeel including (i) the expected timescale for repair work to be completed; and (ii) whether a health and safety assessment of the current condition of the road has been completed.
answer_text: My Department had planned to complete the resurfacing of Greencastle Street, Kilkeel during the 2021/22 financial year, however, the resurfacing contract for the Newry Mourne and Down expired in early 2021 and due to legal challenges a replacement contract could not be awarded. As a result the planned resurfacing works in the area could not be progressed.  My Department is aiming to have a replacement contract awarded for this area shortly which will allow the resurfacing work at Greencastle Street to proceed. With regard to a safety assessment, my Department will continue to carry out monthly routine maintenance inspections of Greencastle Street and all defects meeting current intervention levels will be noted and prioritised for repair in accordance with our current maintenance policies. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current target waiting time for patients to get an appointment in their local GP Surgery; and (ii) how GP surgeries in South Down are performing against this target.
answer_text: As independent contractors, GP practices are responsible for managing their own appointment systems and waiting times. Targets for waiting times are not embedded within the GP Contracts.I recognise, however, that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us.  It is therefore important to highlight that GP practices are working hard to provide services to those who need them and are continuing to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current level of GP Out Of Hours provision in South Down; and how this compares to targets for service provision.
answer_text: The service performance and targets are measured as a Trust wide service, rather than locality specific.  Figures are therefore at Trust level.GP Out of Hours (OOHs) services deliver urgent primary care services to those patients who perceive a need to access GP services when their own surgery is closed, from 6.30pm to 8am the next morning, as well as at weekends and during public holidays. GP OOHs provision for the South Down area may be provided by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) or Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) depending on where the patient lives.The GP Out of Hours service in the SEHSCT operates from three centres in Ards, Lagan Valley and Downpatrick and is a phone-based service Patients can be triaged from any of the three bases. There were approximately 88,800 contacts to SEHSCT GP OOHs in 2021-22.  Of the urgent calls received, 84% (target is 95%) were either given an appointment or triage was completed within 20 minutes, whilst 66% (target is 95%) of less urgent calls were triaged within 1 hour.  The SHSCT Urgent Care OOHs service is also a telephone-based service.    Most people will receive telephone advice and a small number may require a home visit or be offered a consultation at an OOHs base.In 2021-22, there were approx. 60,000 contacts to the Southern GP OOHs Service.  Of the urgent calls received 65% were either given an appointment or triage completed within 20 minutes, whilst 42% of less urgent calls were triaged within 1 hour.
